topics.title: Application Areas of Robot Motion Planning - Personal Transport Vehicles, Planetary Exploration, Demining, Fixed-Base Robot Arms in Industry, Search and Rescue Robots, Surgical Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application Areas of Robot Motion Planning - Personal Transport Vehicles, Planetary Exploration, Demining, Fixed-Base Robot Arms in Industry, Search and Rescue Robots, Surgical Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Robot Motion Planning

**Robot Motion Planning** is the computational process of determining a safe, collision-free, and goal-directed trajectory for a robot from an initial configuration to a final configuration, while satisfying kinematic, dynamic, and environmental constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"Robot Motion Planning is the problem of computing a continuous sequence of valid configurations that moves a robot from a start state to a goal state through a workspace, while avoiding collisions with obstacles and respecting motion constraints imposed by the robot's mechanics and the task."*

The **configuration space** (C-space) is the central mathematical abstraction in motion planning. Every possible pose of the robot maps to a single point in C-space, transforming the geometric problem of moving a body into the simpler problem of moving a point.

- **Configuration $q \in \mathcal{C}$** — A single point in C-space describing the robot's pose
- **Free Space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$** — The set of all collision-free configurations
- **Obstacle Space $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$** — The set of configurations causing collisions
- **Path** — A continuous curve $\tau: [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{free}$
- **Trajectory** — A path parameterized by time

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS for Robots"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are blindfolded in an unfamiliar house and told to reach the kitchen. You must:
> 1. Build a mental map of the rooms (workspace mapping → C-space)
> 2. Identify walls and furniture as obstacles ($\mathcal{C}_{obs}$)
> 3. Plan a walking route avoiding collisions ($\mathcal{C}_{free}$ path)
> 4. Adjust your steps in real-time if a door closes (re-planning)
>
> Robot motion planning is exactly this — but the robot uses sensors, algorithms, and maps to perform the same reasoning at millisecond speeds with centimeter (or sub-millimeter) precision.

### 1.3 Why Application-Specific Planning Matters

Motion planning is **not a one-size-fits-all** discipline. The planner's complexity, sampling resolution, real-time requirement, and safety criticality change dramatically across application domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Path Planning** = Geometric "where to go" problem
> - **Motion Planning** = Geometric + Kinematic + Dynamic + Temporal "how to move" problem
> - **Trajectory Planning** = Time-parameterized motion (velocity & acceleration profiles)

### 1.4 Overview of the Six Major Application Areas

| # | Application Domain | Environment Type | Planning Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Personal Transport Vehicles | Structured urban | High (dynamic obstacles, pedestrians) |
| 2 | Planetary Exploration | Unstructured, extreme | Very High (communication delay) |
| 3 | Demining | Hazardous, unstructured | High (uncertain terrain) |
| 4 | Fixed-Base Robot Arms in Industry | Highly structured | Low-Medium (known CAD model) |
| 5 | Search and Rescue Robots | Cluttered, unknown | Very High (collapsed structures) |
| 6 | Surgical Robots | Constrained, patient-specific | Extreme (sub-mm precision) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Personal Transport Vehicles (PTVs)

#### 2.1.1 Operational Concept

Personal Transport Vehicles include self-driving cars, autonomous wheelchairs, and Segway-type mobility platforms. Motion planning here must operate in dynamic, partially-observable, mixed-traffic urban environments.

**Key Planning Inputs:**
- HD map of road network
- Real-time LiDAR/Radar/Camera perception
- Traffic rules (right-of-way, stop signs)
- Prediction of other agents' trajectories

**Key Planning Outputs:**
- Lane-level path
- Speed profile
- Safe gap distances
- Behavioral intent (yield, overtake, merge)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Planning Stack (used in autonomous vehicles):**
> 1. **Route Planning** — Graph search on road network (Dijkstra / A*)
> 2. **Behavioral Layer** — High-level decisions (lane change, stop)
> 3. **Motion Planning** — Continuous trajectory in Frenet frame
> 4. **Control** — Steering, throttle, brake commands

The **Frenet Frame** is widely used to decouple longitudinal ($s$) and lateral ($d$) motion:

$$
s(t) = s_0 + v \cdot t + \frac{1}{2} a \cdot t^2
$$

$$
d(t) = d_0 + d_1 t + d_2 t^2 + d_3 t^3 + d_4 t^4 + d_5 t^5
$$

where $s$ is arc-length along the lane centerline and $d$ is the lateral offset.

### 2.2 Planetary Exploration Robots

#### 2.2.1 Operational Concept

Rovers such as **NASA's Mars rovers (Sojourner, Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity, Perseverance)** must navigate rocky, sandy, and cratered terrain with **communication round-trip delays of 4–24 minutes**. Real-time teleoperation is impossible.

**Planning Challenges:**
- Slippery, deformable regolith (soil)
- Rocks, craters, sand traps
- Energy constraints (solar/battery limited)
- No global positioning (use visual odometry)
- Communication blackout periods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Onboard Autonomy Stack (Perseverance):**
> - **AEGIS (Autonomous Exploration for Gathering Increased Science)** — Auto-target selection
> - **AutoNav** — Stereo-vision based terrain assessment and path planning
> - **ENABLE (Enhanced Navigation & Autonomous Landing Engine)** — Hazard avoidance during descent

**Path Planning Approach:** Slip-aware traversability analysis + energy-cost map + D* / Field D* re-planning.

The traversability cost function typically uses:

$$
C_{traverse} = w_1 \cdot \text{slope} + w_2 \cdot \text{roughness} + w_3 \cdot \text{slip} + w_4 \cdot \text{energy}
$$

where $w_i$ are empirically-tuned weights.

### 2.3 Demining Robots

#### 2.3.1 Operational Concept

Demining (or humanitarian demining) involves detecting and neutralizing landmines and unexploded ordnance (UXO). The workspace is hazardous, GPS-denied, and contains metal clutter that confuses sensors.

**Two Main Robot Classes:**
1. **Detection Platforms** — Sweep the field with metal detectors, GPR (Ground Penetrating Radar), or thermal sensors.
2. **Neutralization Platforms** — Manipulator arms that carefully expose and detonate/disarm mines.

**Planning Challenges:**
- Soft, deformable ground (vegetation, mud)
- Cannot trigger pressure fuses → ultra-conservative motion
- GPS unavailable under tree canopy
- Sensor false positives from shrapnel

> [!NOTE]
> **Coverage Planning for Demining:**
> The complete coverage path planning (CCPP) problem is solved using boustrophedon (lawn-mower) patterns with obstacle-aware back-off maneuvers. The coverage rate $R_c$ is:
>
> $$R_c = \frac{A_{covered}}{T_{mission}} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{hr}]$$

### 2.4 Fixed-Base Robot Arms in Industry

#### 2.4.1 Operational Concept

Industrial manipulators (e.g., **6-DOF arms from KUKA, ABB, FANUC, Yaskawa**) are the workhorses of manufacturing. Their motion planning is comparatively easier because the workspace is fully known from CAD models.

**Planning Approaches:**
- **Joint-space planning** — Polynomial interpolation between joint angles
- **Task-space planning** — Cartesian straight-line motion (linear segment with parabolic blends, LSPB)
- **Sampling-based** — RRT, PRM for cluttered cells
- **Optimization-based** — CHOMP, STOMP for smooth trajectories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Configuration Space of an n-DOF Arm:**
>
> $$\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{S}^1 \times \mathcal{S}^1 \times \cdots \times \mathcal{S}^1 \quad (n \text{ times})$$
>
> For a 6-DOF arm: $\mathcal{C} = T^6$ (a 6-torus), with $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 6$.

**Joint Trajectory (Cubic Polynomial):**

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_0 + (\theta_f - \theta_0)\left[3\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^3\right]
$$

**Slew Rate Limitation:**

$$
|\dot{\theta}(t)| \leq \dot{\theta}_{max}, \quad |\ddot{\theta}(t)| \leq \ddot{\theta}_{max}
$$

### 2.5 Search and Rescue (SAR) Robots

#### 2.5.1 Operational Concept

SAR robots operate in collapsed buildings, earthquake rubble, and disaster zones. They include tracked ground robots, snake robots, quadrotor drones, and humanoid rescue platforms.

**Key Planning Requirements:**
- Operate in unknown, partially-mapped environments
- Climb stairs, crawl through gaps
- Multi-robot coordination (heterogeneous teams)
- Victim localization (thermal, audio, CO₂ sensors)
- Battery-life-aware mission planning

> [!NOTE]
> **SLAM-based Planning Loop:**
>
> $$z_t = h(x_t) + v_t \quad \text{(observation model)}$$
>
> $$x_{t+1} = f(x_t, u_t) + w_t \quad \text{(motion model)}$$
>
> The EKF-SLAM state vector grows as features are added, while the planner constantly re-evaluates feasible paths.

**Planning Algorithms Used:** ARA* (Anytime Repairing A*), RRT*, Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) for local obstacle avoidance.

### 2.6 Surgical Robots

#### 2.6.1 Operational Concept

Surgical robots such as the **da Vinci Surgical System**, **MAKOplasty**, and **Rosa Robot** perform minimally-invasive surgery with sub-millimeter precision. Motion planning here is safety-critical and subject to **strict regulatory constraints (FDA, CE)**.

**Unique Planning Characteristics:**
- Patient-specific anatomy from pre-op CT/MRI
- **RCM (Remote Center of Motion)** constraint — tool pivots about a fixed entry port
- Haptic feedback in research systems
- Real-time tissue deformation modeling
- No-go zones around critical structures (nerves, arteries)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remote Center of Motion (RCM) Constraint:**
>
> A virtual spherical joint is enforced at the trocar entry point $\mathbf{p}_{rcm}$:
>
> $$||\mathbf{p}_{tip}(q) - \mathbf{p}_{rcm}|| = 0$$
>
> $$\dot{\mathbf{p}}_{tip} \cdot (\mathbf{p}_{tip} - \mathbf{p}_{rcm}) = 0$$
>
> This reduces the surgical tool's effective DOF from 6 to 4 at the entry point, preserving dexterity inside the body.

**Tissue Safety Margin:**

$$
d_{tissue} = ||\mathbf{p}_{tool} - \mathbf{p}_{critical}|| \geq d_{safe}
$$

where $d_{safe}$ is the patient-specific safety threshold (often 1–5 mm).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frenet Longitudinal | $s(t) = s_0 + vt + \frac{1}{2}at^2$ | Self-driving cars | m, m/s, m/s² |
| Traversability Cost | $C = \sum w_i \cdot f_i$ | Planetary rovers | dimensionless |
| C-space Volume | $\mathcal{C} = T^n$ | Robot arms | rad$^n$ |
| Cubic Joint Trajectory | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \Delta\theta(3\tau^2 - 2\tau^3)$ | Industrial arms | rad |
| RCM Constraint | $||\mathbf{p}_{tip} - \mathbf{p}_{rcm}|| = 0$ | Surgical robots | m |
| Safety Margin | $d \geq d_{safe}$ | All safety-critical | m |
| Coverage Rate | $R_c = A_{covered}/T$ | Demining | m²/hr |
| SLAM Observation | $z_t = h(x_t) + v_t$ | SAR | varies |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Application Comparison

### 3.1 Comparative Analytical Matrix

Since the topic is application-oriented, the analytical depth is delivered through a rigorous comparative framework rather than algebraic derivations.

#### 3.1.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Analysis

| Application | Typical DOF | Holonomic? | Redundancy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Self-driving car | 3 (x, y, θ) | Non-holonomic (cannot move sideways) | None |
| Mars rover | 6 (chassis 3 + arm 3) | Non-holonomic | Arm redundancy |
| Demining manipulator | 5–7 | Holonomic (if tracked base) | Often redundant |
| Industrial arm | 6 (or 7 for collaborative) | Holonomic (joints independent) | 7-DOF arms redundant |
| Snake SAR robot | 10–20+ | Hyper-redundant | Highly redundant |
| Surgical robot | 6 + RCM | Constraint-holonomic | RCM enforces 4-DOF tool |

> [!NOTE]
> **Holonomic vs Non-holonomic Distinction (frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Holonomic** = Controllable in all C-space directions equal to DOF
> - **Non-holonomic** = Fewer controllable velocities than DOF (e.g., car cannot translate sideways)
>
> A car with 3 configuration variables (x, y, θ) has only 2 controllable velocities (v, ω), so it is **non-holonomic**.

#### 3.1.2 Environmental Knowledge Spectrum

$$
\text{Known CAD model} \xrightarrow{\text{Industrial}} \text{Semi-structured} \xrightarrow{\text{PTV}} \text{Unknown} \xrightarrow{\text{SAR/Surgical}} \text{Dynamic}
$$

| Application | Map Knowledge | Obstacle Type | Dynamics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Industrial arm | Perfect (CAD) | Static fixtures | Stationary |
| Surgical | Pre-op (CT/MRI) | Deforming tissue | Slowly deforming |
| PTV | HD map + SLAM | Pedestrians, cars | Highly dynamic |
| Planetary | Sparse orbital + terrain mesh | Rocks, craters | Quasi-static |
| Demining | Local GPR scans | Mines, vegetation | Static |
| SAR | Built online via SLAM | Rubble, fire | Unknown & dynamic |

#### 3.1.3 Planning Algorithm Selection Matrix

| Algorithm | Industrial | Surgical | PTV | Planetary | Demining | SAR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A* / D* |  |  | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| RRT / RRT* | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |  | ✓ |
| PRM | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |  |  |
| Potential Fields | ✓ |  | ✓ |  |  |  |
| DWA |  |  | ✓ |  |  | ✓ |
| Optimization (CHOMP) | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |  |  |
| Behavior Trees |  |  | ✓ |  |  | ✓ |

#### 3.1.4 Real-Time & Safety Criticality Analysis

| Application | Cycle Time | Latency Tolerance | Safety Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Industrial arm | 1–10 ms | Low | ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066 |
| Surgical | 1–100 ms | Very low | IEC 60601, FDA Class II/III |
| PTV | 50–100 ms | Medium | ISO 21448 (SOTIF), ISO 26262 |
| Planetary | Seconds to minutes | Very high | NASA NPR 8705.2C |
| Demining | 100 ms | High | MIL-STD-810 |
| SAR | 50–200 ms | Medium | NFPA / INSARAG guidelines |

#### 3.1.5 Worked Example — Choosing a Planner for a Mars Rover

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Reasoning (KTU Exam Style):**
>
> **Step 1 — Identify constraints:** Communication delay = 20 min one-way → cannot rely on ground control in real-time.
>
> **Step 2 — Identify environment:** Unknown rocky terrain with slip-prone slopes.
>
> **Step 3 — Choose map representation:** Elevation grid (2.5D) + traversability cost map.
>
> **Step 4 — Choose global planner:** D* Lite (efficient re-planning when new obstacles are sensed).
>
> **Step 5 — Choose local planner:** Vector Field Histogram (VFH) for real-time obstacle avoidance.
>
> **Step 6 — Add safety layer:** Slip detection triggers emergency stop.
>
> **Conclusion:** Field D* (global) + VFH (local) + slip-monitor (safety) is the canonical Mars rover stack.

#### 3.1.6 Worked Example — RCM Constraint in Surgical Robot

A surgical tool is inserted through trocar at $\mathbf{p}_{rcm} = (0, 0, 100)$ mm. The tool tip is currently at $\mathbf{p}_{tip} = (10, 0, 150)$ mm.

**Step 1 — Verify RCM violation:**
>
> $$||\mathbf{p}_{tip} - \mathbf{p}_{rcm}|| = \sqrt{(10-0)^2 + (0-0)^2 + (150-100)^2} = \sqrt{100 + 2500} = \sqrt{2600} \approx 50.99 \text{ mm}$$
>
> Since this is not zero, RCM is violated. The robot must re-pose joints so the tool tip remains at distance $L = 50$ mm from the trocar pivot.

**Step 2 — Required tool length:**
>
> $$L = ||\mathbf{p}_{tip} - \mathbf{p}_{rcm}|| = 50.99 \text{ mm}$$
>
> The robot must lock the tool insertion depth to this length.

**Step 3 — Allowed motion:** The tool tip can now move on a sphere of radius $L$ centered at $\mathbf{p}_{rcm}$, but constrained to pass through the trocar → effectively a 4-DOF motion inside the patient.
