> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Configuration Space: Specifying a Robot's Configuration, Obstacles and the Configuration Space - Circular Mobile Robot, Two-Joint Planar Arm, C-Obstacles, Topology of the Configuration Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1 — Configuration Space (C-Space) Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This is the foundational topic of Robot Motion Planning. Every subsequent module — roadmaps, cell decomposition, potential fields, sampling-based planners — is built upon a clear understanding of **Configuration Space (C-space)** and **C-obstacles**. Mastery of this module is mandatory.

---

## 1.1 What is a Robot's *Configuration*?

In motion planning, when we ask *"Where is the robot?"*, we do not mean the Cartesian coordinates of every link and joint. We mean a single, complete mathematical description that, once known, allows us to compute the position of **every point** on the robot.

### 🎯 Formal KTU Definition

A **configuration** `q` of a robot is a **minimal set of parameters** that completely specifies the position and orientation of every point on the robot relative to a fixed world (inertial) reference frame.

A **configuration space** `C` is the set of all possible configurations the robot can attain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum specification rule:** A configuration uses the *fewest* independent parameters needed. For a planar arm with 2 revolute joints, `q = (θ₁, θ₂)` — only 2 numbers, even though each link has hundreds of points. This minimalism is what makes planning tractable.

### 🍕 Real-World Analogy — The "Pizza Delivery Robot"

Imagine a small circular robot (`R`) delivering pizza in a cluttered restaurant. Instead of tracking every atom on the robot (impossible!), we just track **two things**:
1. The `(x, y)` coordinates of its **center** on the floor.
2. Its **heading angle** `θ` (where its front faces).

So `q = (x, y, θ)`. Knowing these three numbers instantly tells us where every wheel, sensor, and pizza-holder is. Change any one number, and the robot's pose changes — that is a new configuration.

---

## 1.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

The dimension of the configuration space equals the **number of degrees of freedom (DOF)** of the robot.

| Robot Type | DOF | Configuration `q` |
|---|---|---|
| Point in 2D plane | **2** | `(x, y)` |
| Circular disc in 2D plane | **3** | `(x, y, θ)` |
| Free-flying rigid body in 3D | **6** | `(x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw)` |
| Two-joint planar arm (RR) | **2** | `(θ₁, θ₂)` |
| Humanoid (simplified) | **20–30+** | `(q₁, q₂, …, qₙ)` |

> [!TIP]
> **Gruebler's Formula (for DOF counting):**
>
> $$\text{DOF} = m(N-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{J} c_i$$
>
> where `m` = mobility per link (3 for planar, 6 for spatial), `N` = number of links, `J` = number of joints, `c_i` = DOF removed by joint `i` (1 for revolute/prismatic, 2 for cylindrical, etc.).

---

## 1.3 Workspace vs. Configuration Space — A Critical Distinction

| Concept | Definition | Geometry |
|---|---|---|
| **Workspace `W`** | The 2D/3D physical space in which the robot operates (the "real world"). | The room, factory floor, terrain. |
| **Configuration Space `C`** | The abstract, mathematical space whose points are robot configurations. | Often a torus, sphere, cylinder, or cube. |
| **Workspace Obstacle `WO`** | A physical object in the world the robot must avoid. | The wall, pillar, table. |
| **C-obstacle `COᵢ`** | The set of all configurations `q` where the robot *collides* with `WOᵢ`. | A region (often curved) in C-space. |
| **Free Space `C_free`** | `C \ (∪ COᵢ)` — all collision-free configurations. | Where the robot is allowed to be. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The central abstraction of motion planning:** *Convert a complex geometric problem in the workspace into a simpler point-motion problem in C-space.* A complex rigid robot becomes a **single point** moving through `C_free`, avoiding the **C-obstacle regions**.

---

## 1.4 Topological Nature of C-Space

The topology of `C` depends on the joint types and limits:

- **Continuous joints (no limits):** `C` is typically a Cartesian product of circles `S¹` (for revolute) or lines `ℝ` (for prismatic).
  - 2R planar arm (no limits): `C = S¹ × S¹ = T²` (a **torus**).
- **Joint limits present:** `C` becomes a closed, bounded subset of a torus.
- **Holonomic constraints:** Reduce the dimension.
- **Non-holonomic constraints:** Reduce the *admissible velocities*, not the dimension.

---

## 1.5 The Motion Planning Problem — Restated in C-Space

Given:
- A start configuration `q_start ∈ C_free`
- A goal configuration `q_goal ∈ C_free`
- The obstacle set `CO = ∪ᵢ COᵢ`

**Find:** A continuous path `τ : [0, 1] → C_free` such that `τ(0) = q_start` and `τ(1) = q_goal`.

This single sentence is the heart of every planner you will study in this course.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Formalism — Sets and Mappings

Let:
- `A ⊂ ℝ²` (or `ℝ³`) = the **robot** (a closed set of points in the workspace).
- `x ∈ ℝ²` = a workspace point.
- `WO ⊂ ℝ²` = a workspace obstacle.
- `q ∈ C` = a configuration.
- `A(q)` = the set of workspace points *occupied by the robot* when it is in configuration `q`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forward Kinematics as a Mapping:** Every configuration `q` corresponds to a transformed copy of the robot body `A`. Formally, `A(q) ⊂ ℝ²` is the rigid-body image of `A` under the placement induced by `q`.

### 2.1.1 Defining C-obstacle and C-free

$$
CO_i = \{\, q \in C \;\mid\; A(q) \cap WO_i \neq \varnothing \,\}
$$

$$
C_{\text{free}} = C \setminus \bigcup_{i} CO_i
$$

A configuration `q` is **collision-free** iff `A(q) ∩ WO = ∅`. This is the fundamental collision-check predicate used by all planners.

---

## 2.2 Configuration Space of a Circular Mobile Robot

### Setup

- Robot `A`: a **circular disc** of radius `r` centered at the origin.
- Position reference: the **center** of the disc at `(x, y) ∈ ℝ²`.
- Orientation `θ`: irrelevant due to circular symmetry (the robot is rotation-invariant).
- Therefore: **`C = ℝ²`**, and `q = (x, y)`.

### C-obstacle Derivation (Minkowski Sum Idea)

The disc collides with obstacle `WO` iff there exists a point `a ∈ A` and a point `w ∈ WO` such that, after placement, `a + q = w`. Equivalently:

$$
q \in WO \ominus A \;=\; \{\, q - a \;\mid\; q \in WO,\ a \in A \,\}
$$

This is the **Minkowski difference** `WO ⊖ A`. Geometrically, we "shrink" the robot to a point and "grow" each obstacle by the robot's radius `r`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualizing Minkowski Sum for a Disc:**
> For a circular robot of radius `r` and a polygonal obstacle `P` with vertices `(v₁, v₂, …, vₙ)`, the C-obstacle is simply the polygon `P` "inflated outward" by `r`. Every edge is shifted parallel outward by distance `r`. Every vertex becomes a circular arc of radius `r` centered at that vertex.

---

## 2.3 Configuration Space of a Two-Joint Planar Arm (2R Arm)

### Setup

- Two revolute joints, both unconstrained: `θ₁ ∈ [0, 2π)`, `θ₂ ∈ [0, 2π)`.
- Link lengths: `L₁` (shoulder-to-elbow) and `L₂` (elbow-to-end-effector).
- Configuration: `q = (θ₁, θ₂) ∈ S¹ × S¹`.
- **C-space topology:** A 2-torus `T²` — a square with opposite edges identified.

### Forward Kinematics of the End-Effector

The position of the end-effector `p = (p_x, p_y)` in the workspace is:

$$
p_x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
p_y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

### C-obstacle Mapping

Given a workspace point obstacle `WO`, its C-obstacle is:

$$
CO = \{\, (\theta_1, \theta_2) \in T^2 \;\mid\; \text{link}_1 \text{ or } \text{link}_2 \text{ hits } WO \,\}
$$

For a **point** workspace obstacle (zero radius), the C-obstacle is found by inverting the kinematics. The mapping is **not injective** — generally there are up to **two** configurations `(θ₁, θ₂)` that place the end-effector at the obstacle point (elbow-up and elbow-down solutions).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the most-tested content of Module 1. Memorize every row.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbol Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Configuration of rigid body in 2D | `q = (x, y, θ)` | `(x,y)` ∈ ℝ², `θ` ∈ `S¹` |
| 2 | Configuration of rigid body in 3D | `q = (x, y, z, φ, ψ, θ)` | Z-Y-X Euler angles |
| 3 | DOF (Gruebler) | `m(N−1) − Σ cᵢ` | `m=3` planar, `m=6` spatial |
| 4 | DOF of `n`-revolute planar arm | `n` | One per revolute joint |
| 5 | C-space of 2R arm (no limits) | `T² = S¹ × S¹` | A torus |
| 6 | C-space of `n`R arm (no limits) | `Tⁿ = S¹ × ⋯ × S¹` (n times) | n-torus |
| 7 | C-obstacle definition | `{q ∈ C : A(q) ∩ WO ≠ ∅}` | Forward definition |
| 8 | C-obstacle (disc robot) | `CO = WO ⊕ B(0,r)` | Minkowski sum; `B` = ball of radius `r` |
| 9 | End-effector (2R planar) | `p_x = L₁cosθ₁ + L₂cos(θ₁+θ₂)` | Forward kinematics |
| 10 | End-effector (2R planar) | `p_y = L₁sinθ₁ + L₂sin(θ₁+θ₂)` | Forward kinematics |
| 11 | Joint-angle inversion | `θ₂ = acos((pₓ² + pᵧ² − L₁² − L₂²)/(2L₁L₂))` | Plus minus for elbow |
| 12 | Free space | `C_free = C \ ⋃ COᵢ` | Implicit complement |
| 13 | Connected components | Count via BFS/DFS over `C_free` | Roadmap, cell decomp. |

> **Note on Table Symbols:** The vertical bar symbols in the row for C-obstacle (disc robot) have been deliberately avoided to preserve markdown table integrity. Use `\oplus` for the Minkowski sum operator in LaTeX expressions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of C-Space Abstraction

| Engineering Field | Use of C-Space |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | `(x, y, θ, v, δ)` for car-like non-holonomic vehicles. |
| **Robotic Surgery (Da Vinci)** | High-DOF manipulators; C-space is 7–10 dimensional. |
| **Computer-Aided Manufacturing** | Milling-tool path planning in joint space. |
| **Drone Swarms** | Each drone's SE(3) configuration; planning becomes multi-modal. |
| **Molecular Docking** | Ligand orientations in 6-DOF space. |

The **dimensionality explosion** is the chief challenge — this motivates sampling-based methods (RRT, PRM) covered later in the syllabus.

# 📘 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation — C-Obstacle of a Circular Mobile Robot

**Given:**
- Robot `A`: closed disc of radius `r` centered at origin in its own body frame.
- Workspace obstacle `WO`: a closed region in `ℝ²`.
- Configuration `q = (x, y) ∈ ℝ²`.
- Robot placement: the center of the disc moves to `(x, y)`; orientation does not matter (rotational symmetry).

### Step 1 — Formalize the Robot Placement

The set of workspace points occupied by the robot in configuration `q = (x, y)` is:

$$
A(q) = \{\, (x, y) + a \;\mid\; a \in A \,\}
$$

Since `A = {a : ||a|| ≤ r}`, this simplifies to:

$$
A(q) = \{\, p \in ℝ^2 \;\mid\; \|p - (x,y)\| \leq r \,\}
$$

That is, a closed disc of radius `r` centered at `(x, y)`.

### Step 2 — Collision Condition

A collision occurs iff there exists at least one workspace point belonging to both `A(q)` and `WO`:

$$
A(q) \cap WO \neq \varnothing
$$

### Step 3 — Substituting the Placement

$$
\bigl\{\, p : \|p - q\| \leq r \,\bigr\} \cap WO \neq \varnothing
$$

This holds iff there exists `p ∈ WO` such that `‖p − q‖ ≤ r`. In other words:

$$
\| p - q \| \leq r \quad \text{for some } p \in WO
$$

Rearranging, this is equivalent to:

$$
q \in \{\, p - a : p \in WO, a \in A, \|a\| \leq r \,\}
$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Minkowski Sum

The right-hand side is precisely the **Minkowski sum** of `WO` and `A`:

$$
CO = WO \oplus A \;=\; \{\, w + a \;\mid\; w \in WO,\ a \in A \,\}
$$

> Since `A` is a disc of radius `r`, the Minkowski sum is geometrically equivalent to *inflating* `WO` outward by distance `r` in every direction.

### Step 5 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\,CO \;=\; \{\, q \in ℝ^2 \;\mid\; \mathrm{dist}(q,\, WO) \leq r \,\}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** *"The obstacle grows by the robot's size, the robot shrinks to a point."*

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Square Obstacle + Disc Robot

**Setup:**
- `WO = [0, 1] × [0, 1]` (unit square obstacle).
- Robot: disc of radius `r = 0.5`.

**Step-by-step C-obstacle construction:**

```
1. Original square vertices:    (0,0), (1,0), (1,1), (0,1)
2. Inflate each edge outward by r = 0.5 (parallel offset).
3. Round each original vertex into a quarter-circle arc of radius r.
4. The C-obstacle is the closed region enclosed.
```

**Resulting C-obstacle:** A larger square with corners at `(−0.5, −0.5)` to `(1.5, 1.5)`, but with quarter-circle arcs of radius `0.5` at the four corners (instead of sharp 90° corners).

Mathematically:

$$
CO = \{\, (x, y) \in ℝ^2 \;\mid\; -0.5 \leq x \leq 1.5,\ -0.5 \leq y \leq 1.5 \,\} \;-\; \text{(rounded corners)}
$$

This construction generalizes directly to **any polygon**: each edge becomes a parallel-offset edge, each vertex becomes an arc.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Workspace-to-C-space Mapping for 2R Arm with a Point Obstacle

**Given:**
- Two-joint planar arm, link lengths `L₁`, `L₂`.
- A point workspace obstacle at `p_obs = (x_obs, y_obs)`.
- Configuration `q = (θ₁, θ₂)`.

### Step 1 — End-Effector Position

From forward kinematics (Section 2.3):

$$
x_{ee} = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y_{ee} = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Squared Distance from Origin

$$
x_{ee}^2 + y_{ee}^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for θ₂ (Elbow Angle)

Set `x_ee² + y_ee² = x_obs² + y_obs² = d²` where `d` is the distance from origin to the obstacle:

$$
L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 = d^2
$$

Solving for `cos θ₂`:

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{d^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\frac{d^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2L_1 L_2}\right)\,}
$$

> The `±` corresponds to **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** configurations.

### Step 4 — Solve for θ₁ (Shoulder Angle)

With `θ₂` known, use the `atan2` formula:

$$
\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(y_{obs}, x_{obs}) - \mathrm{atan2}\!\bigl(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\ L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2\bigr)
$$

### Step 5 — C-Obstacle of Point Obstacle

The C-obstacle is the set of **all** `(θ₁, θ₂)` solutions, i.e., generally **two curves** on the torus `T²`:

$$
CO = \{\, (\theta_1, \theta_2) \in T^2 \;\mid\; x_{ee}(\theta_1,\theta_2) = x_{obs},\ y_{ee}(\theta_1,\theta_2) = y_{obs} \,\}
$$

For larger workspace obstacles (extended shapes), the C-obstacle is a *swept region* of these inverse-kinematics curves thickened by the obstacle's extent.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a frequent KTU exam comparison question. Know it cold.**

| Property | Circular Mobile Robot | Two-Joint Planar Arm (2R) |
|---|---|---|
| DOF | **3** | **2** |
| `q` | `(x, y, θ)` | `(θ₁, θ₂)` |
| `C` topology | `ℝ² × S¹ = SE(2)` | `S¹ × S¹ = T²` |
| `C` dimension | **3** | **2** |
| Orientation matters? | Yes (only via `θ`) | Yes (only via joint angles) |
| C-obstacle for point obstacle | Single point in ℝ² becomes a region | Curve(s) on the torus |
| Minkowski sum applicable? | **Yes** (simple inflation) | **No** (needs inverse kinematics) |
| Joint limits affect `C`? | No (free in `ℝ²`) | Yes (`S¹ × S¹` → rectangle `T²`) |
| Visualization difficulty | Easy (2D map) | Moderate (must plot on torus) |

# 📘 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a robot's configuration and configuration space. Give one example each for a 2-DOF and a 3-DOF robot.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **configuration `q`** of a robot is a specification of the position of every point on the robot. Mathematically, it is a minimal set of independent parameters that uniquely determines the placement of the robot in its environment. The set of all possible configurations is called the **configuration space `C`**.
>
> **Examples:**
> - **2-DOF:** A point translating in the plane; `q = (x, y)` and `C = ℝ²`.
> - **3-DOF:** A circular disc moving in the plane with orientation; `q = (x, y, θ)` and `C = SE(2) = ℝ² × S¹`.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a C-obstacle? Why is it important in motion planning?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **C-obstacle `COᵢ`** is the set of all robot configurations `q` in which the robot collides with a particular workspace obstacle `WOᵢ`. Formally,
>
> `COᵢ = { q ∈ C : A(q) ∩ WOᵢ ≠ ∅ }`.
>
> **Importance:** C-obstacles allow us to convert the complex geometric problem of *moving a rigid body through obstacles* into a far simpler problem — *moving a single point through `C_free` while avoiding C-obstacle regions*. This abstraction is the cornerstone of virtually every modern motion planning algorithm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** A circular mobile robot of radius `r = 1` unit operates in a 2D workspace containing a **rectangular obstacle** with corners at `(2, 1)`, `(4, 1)`, `(4, 3)`, and `(2, 3)`. **(7 Marks)**

**(i)** Compute the C-obstacle in the configuration space.

**(ii)** If the robot's start configuration is at `(0, 0)` and goal is at `(7, 0)`, comment on whether a straight-line path in C-space is valid.

**(b)** Explain with neat diagrams the **topology of the configuration space** of a two-joint planar manipulator (both joints revolute and unconstrained). What is the dimension and what manifold results? Mention what happens when joint limits are introduced. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a)(i): Computing the C-obstacle**

Since the robot is a **disc of radius 1**, by the Minkowski sum formulation:

$$
CO = \{\, q \in ℝ^2 \;\mid\; \mathrm{dist}(q,\, WO) \leq 1 \,\}
$$

The original rectangle has corners `(2,1), (4,1), (4,3), (2,3)`. Inflating by `r = 1`:

| Original Corner | Inflated Region |
|---|---|
| `(2, 1)` | Quarter-arc of radius 1 (centered at `(2,1)`) |
| `(4, 1)` | Quarter-arc of radius 1 (centered at `(4,1)`) |
| `(4, 3)` | Quarter-arc of radius 1 (centered at `(4,3)`) |
| `(2, 3)` | Quarter-arc of radius 1 (centered at `(2,3)`) |

The four edges are shifted outward parallel by distance 1, becoming:

- Bottom edge: `y = 0`, for `2 ≤ x ≤ 4`
- Top edge: `y = 4`, for `2 ≤ x ≤ 4`
- Left edge: `x = 1`, for `1 ≤ y ≤ 3`
- Right edge: `x = 5`, for `1 ≤ y ≤ 3`

Hence the C-obstacle is a **rectangle with rounded corners** spanning `x ∈ [1, 5]`, `y ∈ [0, 4]`.

**Part (a)(ii): Straight-line path feasibility**

A straight-line path from `(0,0)` to `(7,0)` lies along the x-axis with `y = 0`. The C-obstacle occupies `y ∈ [0, 4]` for `x ∈ [1, 5]`. The x-axis segment from `x = 0` to `x = 7` passes through `x ∈ [1, 5]` where `y = 0` lies within `[0, 4]`. Therefore the **straight-line path is NOT valid** — it collides with the C-obstacle in the range `x ∈ [1, 5]`.

The robot must detour, e.g., via `(0, 5) → (7, 5)` or any path with `y > 4` to clear the obstacle.

**Part (b): Topology of 2R Arm C-Space**

- Each revolute joint `θᵢ` ranges over the circle `S¹ = [0, 2π)` with `0` and `2π` identified.
- Since both joints are independent and unconstrained, the configuration space is the **Cartesian product**:

$$
C = S^1 \times S^1 = T^2
$$

- `T²` is the **2-torus** — topologically equivalent to a square with both pairs of opposite edges glued.
- Dimension = **2** (matches the 2 DOF of the manipulator).
- **With joint limits** (e.g., `θ₁ ∈ [θ₁_min, θ₁_max]`, similarly for `θ₂`), `C` becomes a **closed rectangle** with each side collapsed to a point (a disk-like topology for revolute limits, or a rectangle if limits span less than 2π). The identification `0 ≡ 2π` is removed.
- The end-effector workspace reachable from `C` is the annulus `|L₁ − L₂| ≤ r ≤ L₁ + L₂` (the reachable annulus).

---

### 📝 Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** A **two-joint planar arm** with link lengths `L₁ = 2` and `L₂ = 1.5` has a **point workspace obstacle** at `(2.0, 1.5)`. **(7 Marks)**

**(i)** Determine whether the obstacle is reachable by the end-effector.

**(ii)** Find the two configurations `(θ₁, θ₂)` that place the end-effector at the obstacle (elbow-up and elbow-down).

**(b)** With the aid of a labelled diagram, describe the **Minkowski sum based C-obstacle construction** for a convex polygonal robot translating in a 2D workspace containing a convex polygonal obstacle. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a)(i): Reachability**

Maximum reach: `L₁ + L₂ = 3.5`. Minimum reach: `|L₁ − L₂| = 0.5`.

Distance from origin to obstacle: `d = √(2² + 1.5²) = √(4 + 2.25) = √6.25 = 2.5`.

Since `0.5 ≤ 2.5 ≤ 3.5`, **the obstacle is reachable**.

**Part (a)(ii): Inverse Kinematics**

Using the cosine rule:

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{d^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} = \frac{6.25 - 4 - 2.25}{2 \cdot 2 \cdot 1.5} = \frac{0}{6} = 0
$$

Therefore `θ₂ = +π/2` (elbow-up) or `θ₂ = −π/2` (elbow-down).

**Case 1: Elbow-Up (`θ₂ = +π/2`)**

$$
\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(y_{obs}, x_{obs}) - \mathrm{atan2}(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\ L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(1.5, 2.0) - \mathrm{atan2}(1.5, 2.0)
$$

Since `atan2(1.5, 2.0) ≈ 0.6435 rad (≈ 36.87°)`, we get `θ₁ ≈ 0`. So **`q_up = (0, π/2)`**.

**Case 2: Elbow-Down (`θ₂ = −π/2`)**

$$
\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(1.5, 2.0) - \mathrm{atan2}(-1.5, 2.0)
$$

`atan2(−1.5, 2.0) ≈ −0.6435 rad`, so `θ₁ ≈ 0.6435 − (−0.6435) = 1.287 rad ≈ 73.74°`. So **`q_down ≈ (1.287, −π/2)`**.

**Part (b): Minkowski Sum Construction**

**Block diagram of the procedure:**

```
Input:
   - Convex polygon Robot R with vertices r1, r2, ..., rn
   - Convex polygon Obstacle O with vertices o1, o2, ..., om

Step 1: Reflect R about its origin to obtain R' with vertices -r1, -r2, ..., -rn
Step 2: Compute Minkowski sum O (+) R' by merging edge vectors.
Step 3: The C-obstacle CO is the polygon obtained.
Step 4: Place robot's reference frame at any point p in C.
        Collision-free iff p is NOT in CO.
```

**Geometry:** Each edge of the robot (when reflected) defines a half-plane constraint. The Minkowski sum is the intersection of the half-planes translated by the obstacle vertices, producing a polygon whose number of edges is at most `n + m`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the direction of the Minkowski sum:** Many students inflate the obstacle by `+r` when the correct formulation is `WO ⊕ A` (i.e., grow the obstacle by the robot, NOT shrink the obstacle).
> 2. **Forgetting circular arcs at polygon corners:** When the robot is a disc and the obstacle is polygonal, the C-obstacle has **rounded corners**, not sharp ones. Drawing sharp corners loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring robot orientation for a disc:** For a circular robot, the orientation DOF `θ` is irrelevant — DOF = 2 (only `x, y`), not 3. Writing `q = (x, y, θ)` for a circular mobile robot will lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong C-space topology for 2R arm:** Writing `C = ℝ²` instead of `T²` is a common error. Always remember the identification `θ = 0 ≡ 2π`.
> 5. **Skipping the inverse-kinematics ± for elbow solutions:** Always present BOTH configurations; partial answers lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print or screenshot this section. It is your last-minute revision sheet.**

- ☐ A **configuration** `q` is the *minimal* specification of robot pose; **C-space** `C` is the set of all `q`.
- ☐ The dimension of `C` = the **DOF** of the robot (use **Gruebler's formula**: `DOF = m(N−1) − Σ cᵢ`).
- ☐ **Workspace** `W` is the physical world; **C-space** `C` is the abstract parameter space.
- ☐ A **C-obstacle** `COᵢ` = `{q : A(q) ∩ WOᵢ ≠ ∅}` — configurations that cause collision.
- ☐ **C-free** `C_free = C \ ⋃ COᵢ` — collision-free region; this is where paths live.
- ☐ For a **circular mobile robot** of radius `r`: `CO = {q : dist(q, WO) ≤ r}` (Minkowski inflation).
- ☐ For a **2R planar arm**: `C = T²` (a torus); configuration `q = (θ₁, θ₂)`.
- ☐ End-effector position: `x = L₁ cos θ₁ + L₂ cos(θ₁ + θ₂)`, `y = L₁ sin θ₁ + L₂ sin(θ₁ + θ₂)`.
- ☐ Inverse kinematics yields **two solutions** (elbow-up and elbow-down) for non-singular points.
- ☐ Joint limits **change topology** — `T²` becomes a closed rectangle (or disk, depending on limits).
- ☐ **Minkowski sum** grows obstacles by the robot's geometry; the robot becomes a point.
- ☐ The motion planning problem reduces to: *find a continuous path in `C_free` from `q_start` to `q_goal`.*
- ☐ High-dimensional C-spaces motivate sampling-based planners (RRT, PRM) — covered in later modules.
- ☐ **Common DOF values to memorize:** point in plane = 2; disc in plane = 3; rigid body in 3D = 6; 2R arm = 2; 6R industrial arm = 6.

> **Good luck with your KTU examinations. Master these fundamentals — every planner you study later depends on them.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Robot Motion Planning — The Canonical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Canonical Definition (Syllabus-Exact Terminology)**
> **Robot Motion Planning** is the computational problem of finding a sequence of valid configurations that move a robot from an initial state to a goal state, while satisfying kinematic constraints, dynamic constraints, and obstacle-avoidance constraints within a defined workspace.

Mathematically, it is the search for a continuous mapping

$$
\pi : [0, 1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}
$$

such that $\pi(0) = q_{\text{start}}$ and $\pi(1) = q_{\text{goal}}$, where $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ denotes the **collision-free subset of the configuration space**.

### Key Sub-Definitions Every KTU Examiner Expects

| # | Term | Formal Definition |
|---|------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Path Planning** | Geometric problem of finding a geometric path in $\mathcal{C}$-space ignoring time/velocity. |
| 2 | **Trajectory Planning** | Time-parameterized path with velocity and acceleration profiles. |
| 3 | **Configuration ($q$)** | A complete specification of every degree of freedom of the robot. |
| 4 | **Configuration Space ($\mathcal{C}$)** | The $n$-dimensional manifold of all possible robot configurations. |
| 5 | **Workspace ($\mathcal{W}$)** | The 2D/3D physical space in which the robot operates. |
| 6 | **Free Space ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$)** | Subset of $\mathcal{C}$ where the robot is not in collision with obstacles. |
| 7 | **Holonomic System** | A system where every differentiable constraint is integrable; controls equal DOF. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Driver

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Think Like a KTU Topper**
> Imagine you are **blindfolded inside an unfamiliar room** with furniture (obstacles) scattered around. You are given the room's floor plan, the chair positions, and you must walk from the door (start) to the window (goal). You cannot see, but you *reason* about every step.
> 
> - Your **body's pose** (location + orientation) at any instant = a **Configuration ($q$)**
> - The set of *all possible body poses* you could ever adopt = **Configuration Space ($\mathcal{C}$)**
> - The poses that don't crash you into furniture = **Free Space ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$)**
> - The map you mentally build = a **Roadmap / Path**
> - The actual sequence of timed steps you take = a **Trajectory**
> 
> **Robot Motion Planning is the math-engine behind that mental reasoning.**

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Problem Dimensions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Board Notation You MUST Memorize**
> - $n$ : Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of the robot — **dimension of $\mathcal{C}$**
> - $m$ : Dimension of the Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ (usually **2** or **3**)
> - $q \in \mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ : A single configuration
> - $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ : Obstacle region in $\mathcal{C}$-space
> - $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$
> - **Rigorously:** $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = n$, while $\dim(\mathcal{W}) = m$ with $n \geq m$ in general.

---

## 1.4 Why Module 2 Begins with Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Roadmap**
> Module 2 of PEBRT632 transitions from the *kinematics* taught in Module 1 to the **algorithms** of planning. Before studying Roadmaps, Cell Decomposition, or Sampling-based methods, you must internalize the *objects* these algorithms manipulate — configurations, free space, and paths. This topic is the **axiomatic foundation** of every subsequent planning theorem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Foundational Logical Steps of Motion Planning

The entire discipline of motion planning rests on a five-step logical scaffold. **Every KTU Module 2 question decomposes into these steps:**

### Step 1 — Model the Robot
- Specify the robot's geometric extent (link lengths, joint types).
- Compute $n$ = total Degrees of Freedom.
- $n = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{DOF}_i$ for a $k$-jointed serial chain.

### Step 2 — Model the Workspace
- Define $\mathcal{W} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m$ (with $m = 2$ or $m = 3$).
- Identify every obstacle $\mathcal{O}_j \subset \mathcal{W}$.

### Step 3 — Lift to Configuration Space
- Construct the configuration space $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$.
- The mapping $F : \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{W}$ gives the *swept volume* of the robot at configuration $q$.
- Define $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid F(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$.

### Step 4 — Identify Free Space
- Compute $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.
- This is the **planning universe**; every planner searches here.

### Step 5 — Search & Connect
- Apply a planner (Roadmap / Cell Decomposition / Sampling) to find a continuous curve
$$
\pi : [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}
$$
- Verify $\pi(0) = q_s$, $\pi(1) = q_g$, and $\pi(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \; \forall t \in [0,1]$.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Step 3 is the Hardest Step**
> The function $F$ that maps a configuration to the workspace footprint of the robot is **nonlinear** for revolute and prismatic joints. The obstacle region $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ is therefore typically a **non-convex, curved manifold in $\mathbb{R}^n$** — which is why direct closed-form solutions are rare and why sampling/roadmap methods dominate the field.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Constraint / Domain | Unit / Notes |
|---------------------|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| $q \in \mathcal{C}$ | A configuration | $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ | Dimensionless / rad / m |
| $n$ | DOF of robot | $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | — |
| $m$ | Workspace dimension | $m \in \{2, 3\}$ | — |
| $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Free C-space | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | Subset |
| $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | Forbidden C-space | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = F^{-1}(\mathcal{O})$ | Subset |
| $\pi : [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Geometric path | Continuous, $C^0$ minimum | Function |
| $\tau : [0, T] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Trajectory | $C^2$ for smooth motion | Function |
| $\dot{q}, \ddot{q}$ | Velocity, Acceleration | Kinodynamic constraints | rad/s, m/s |
| $\mathcal{C}_{\text{connected}}$ | Connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Path exists only if $q_s, q_g$ in same component | — |
| $\delta$ | Clearance from obstacle | $\delta > 0$ guarantees safety | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Tip for Tables:** When writing $|q - q_{\text{obs}}|$ inside a markdown table, ALWAYS use $\lvert q - q_{\text{obs}} \rvert$ — the bare vertical bar will break the table parser and the KTU digital portal will reject your answer sheet rendering.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Motion Planning |
|--------|-----------------------|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane-change, parking, intersection negotiation in $\mathcal{C}$ = (position, heading, velocity). |
| **Industrial Robotics** | Welding, painting, pick-and-place path generation. |
| **Aerospace** | Spacecraft rendezvous & docking with kinematic redundancy. |
| **Medical Robotics** | Minimally invasive surgery path planning inside the human body. |
| **Warehouse Automation** | AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots) navigating dynamic shelves. |
| **Humanoid Robotics** | Footstep planning on uneven terrain. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Workspace Obstacles to C-Space Obstacles

This is the **single most-asked derivation in KTU Module 2**, and examiners award 5–7 marks for it. We derive $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ for a **circular robot** (radius $r$) translating in $\mathbb{R}^2$ among circular obstacles.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Let:
- The robot be a **point** of radius $r > 0$ translating in $\mathcal{W} = \mathbb{R}^2$.
- A single obstacle be a closed disk $\mathcal{O}_i$ centered at $c_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$ with radius $R_i > 0$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the swept volume map $F(q)$**

For a translating robot of radius $r$ at configuration $q = (x, y)$, the swept volume is the disk

$$
F(q) = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \lVert p - q \rVert_2 \leq r \}
$$

**Step 2 — Define collision condition**

A collision occurs if and only if $F(q) \cap \mathcal{O}_i \neq \emptyset$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions**

The two disks $F(q)$ and $\mathcal{O}_i$ intersect if and only if the distance between their centers is at most the sum of their radii.

$$
\lVert q - c_i \rVert_2 \leq r + R_i
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the inequality for $q$**

Squaring both sides (valid since both sides are non-negative):

$$
(q - c_i)^{\top}(q - c_i) \leq (r + R_i)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the C-space obstacle region**

This inequality describes a **closed disk in $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$** centered at $c_i$ with radius $(r + R_i)$. Therefore:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}^{(i)} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \lVert q - c_i \rVert_2 \leq r + R_i \}
$$

**Step 6 — Extend to multiple obstacles via union**

For $k$ workspace obstacles, the total obstacle region in C-space is the finite union:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}^{(i)}
$$

**Step 7 — Express the free space**

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}^{(i)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** Notice how the C-space obstacle is the workspace obstacle **grown** by the robot's radius — this geometric operation is called **Minkowski Sum Expansion** and is foundational to all of Module 2's roadmap methods.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Path vs Trajectory vs Motion Plan

| Property | Path $\pi$ | Trajectory $\tau$ | Motion Plan |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|--------------|
| Time parameterized? | **No** (uses $t \in [0,1]$) | **Yes** (uses $t \in [0, T]$) | Yes, with control inputs |
| Considers velocity? | No | Yes ($\dot{q}$) | Yes ($\dot{q}, \ddot{q}$) |
| Considers dynamics? | No | Optional | **Yes** (forces, torques) |
| Considers obstacles? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Output type | Geometric curve in $\mathcal{C}$ | Time-indexed curve in $\mathcal{C}$ | Sequence of $(q, \dot{q}, u)$ |
| KTU Module relevance | Module 2 (Planner core) | Module 3 (Trajectory) | Module 4 (Kinodynamic) |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — DOF Computation for a 6R Manipulator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic KTU 14-Mark Derivative Question**

A robot manipulator has 6 revolute joints, all unconstrained. Compute its configuration space dimension and identify the topological type of $\mathcal{C}$.

**Solution — Line by Line:**

- For a single revolute joint $J_i$, the configuration variable is $\theta_i \in [0, 2\pi)$ (modulo $2\pi$).
- The full configuration is the ordered tuple $q = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6) \in \mathbb{R}^6$.
- The configuration space is the Cartesian product:

$$
\mathcal{C} = S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1 \times S^1 = (S^1)^6 \cong T^6
$$

- Hence $n = 6$ and $\mathcal{C}$ is the **6-Torus** $T^6$.
- Dimensionally: $\dim(\mathcal{C}) = 6$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake Students Make:** Writing $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^6$ instead of $T^6$. Revolute joints are cyclic, not linear — the KTU examiner WILL deduct a mark for this error.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Motion Planning Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram?** The relationships among robot, workspace, configuration space, and planner form a strict pipeline. A block diagram exposes the *flow of information* that a textual definition cannot.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROBOT GEOMETRY MODEL] --> B[WORKSPACE OBSTACLES]
    B --> C[CONFIGURATION SPACE MAP F]
    A --> C
    C --> D[OBSTACLE REGION IN C-SPACE]
    D --> E[FREE SPACE C-FREE]
    E --> F[PLANNER ALGORITHM]
    G[INITIAL CONFIGURATION qS] --> F
    H[GOAL CONFIGURATION qG] --> F
    F --> I[GEOMETRIC PATH PI]
    I --> J[TRAJECTORY TAU]
    J --> K[ROBOT EXECUTION]

    classDef defBox fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef planBox fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outBox fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E defBox
    class F,G,H planBox
    class I,J,K outBox
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy Diagram — Classification of Planners

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MOTION PLANNING] --> B[GEOMETRIC PLANNING]
    A --> C[KINODYNAMIC PLANNING]

    B --> D[COMBINATORIAL]

    D --> F[ROADMAP]
    D --> G[CELL DECOMPOSITION]

    F --> F1[VISIBILITY GRAPH]
    F --> F2[VORONOI DIAGRAM]
    G --> G1[EXACT CELL]
    G --> G2[APPROXIMATE CELL]

    E --> H1[PRM]
    E --> H2[RRT]

    C --> I1[STATE LATTICE]
    C --> I2[TRAJECTORY OPTIMIZATION]

    classDef root fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class B,C,D,E cat
```

## 4.3 C-Space Construction Pipeline (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[INPUT ROBOT LINKS] --> B1[COMPUTE FORWARD KINEMATICS F]
    A2[INPUT WORKSPACE OBSTACLES] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[SAMPLE OR ANALYTICALLY INVERT F]
    C1 --> D1[ENUMERATE COLLISION CONFIGURATIONS]
    D1 --> E1[COBS UNION OF ALL COLLIDING Q]
    E1 --> F1[CFREE = C MINUS COBS]
    F1 --> G1[APPLY PLANNER]
    G1 --> H1[OUTPUT PATH PI]

    classDef inBox fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef procBox fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef outBox fill:#F5DEB3,stroke:#D2691E,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    class A1,A2 inBox
    class B1,C1,D1,E1,F1,G1 procBox
    class H1 outBox
```

## 4.4 Definition Hierarchy — From Atoms to Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[ROBOT] --> L2[CONFIGURATION Q]
    L1 --> L3[WORKSPACE W]
    L3 --> L4[OBSTACLES O]
    L2 --> L5[C-SPACE C]
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6[COBS]
    L5 --> L7[CFREE]
    L7 --> L8[PATH PI]
    L7 --> L9[TRAJECTORY TAU]
    L8 --> L10[PLANNER ALGORITHM]
    L9 --> L10
    L10 --> L11[ROBOT MOTION PLAN]

    classDef base fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **Format:** Two-mark definition + one-mark example. KTU evaluators mark strictly per rubric.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define configuration space. How does it differ from the workspace?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **configuration space $\mathcal{C}$** of a robot is the $n$-dimensional manifold of all possible configurations the robot can attain, where $n$ equals the number of degrees of freedom of the robot. Each point $q \in \mathcal{C}$ uniquely specifies the position and orientation of every link of the robot.

**Distinction (2 Marks):**

- The **workspace $\mathcal{W}$** is the 2D or 3D physical Euclidean space in which the robot moves its end-effector or body.
- The **configuration space $\mathcal{C}$** is a higher-dimensional abstract space (one axis per joint) used purely for planning.
- Example: For a 2R planar arm with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, $\mathcal{W} = $ reachable disk in $\mathbb{R}^2$, whereas $\mathcal{C} = [0, 2\pi) \times [0, 2\pi) = T^2$, a torus.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms *path*, *trajectory*, and *motion plan*. Give a one-line example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Path $\pi$:** A continuous geometric curve in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ from $q_s$ to $q_g$, *without* time information. Example: a sequence of waypoints in joint space.
- **Trajectory $\tau$:** A time-parameterized path $\tau(t)$ specifying position, velocity, and acceleration at each instant. Example: a cubic polynomial interpolation of joint angles over $T = 4$ seconds.
- **Motion plan:** The complete executable specification including actuator commands / forces. Example: the torque sequence $\mathbf{u}(t)$ sent to a robot's motors.

---

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — KTU ESE Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Mandate (KTU 2024):** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Sub-part (a) tests *understanding/derivation*; sub-part (b) tests *application/comparison*. You MUST answer both sub-parts to score full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Define the **motion planning problem** rigorously. State and explain the **five elements** of the problem formulation. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A circular mobile robot of radius $r = 0.5 \text{ m}$ translates in a 2D workspace containing a single circular obstacle of radius $R = 1.0 \text{ m}$ centered at $c = (3, 4)$. **Derive** the C-space obstacle region $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ and find its area. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — The Five Elements (7 Marks)

The motion planning problem is a 6-tuple $(\mathcal{C}, \mathcal{O}, q_s, q_g, F, \pi)$. The **five elements** are:

1. **The robot** — geometric model with $n$ degrees of freedom.
2. **The workspace $\mathcal{W} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m$** — the physical environment.
3. **The configuration space $\mathcal{C}$** — the $n$-dimensional manifold of all $q$.
4. **The obstacle map $F : \mathcal{C} \rightarrow 2^{\mathcal{W}}$** — gives the swept volume of the robot at $q$.
5. **The free space** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid F(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset \}$.

The problem is to find a continuous function

$$
\pi : [0, 1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}
$$

with $\pi(0) = q_s$ (start), $\pi(1) = q_g$ (goal), and $\pi(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \; \forall t \in [0,1]$. **(1 mark per element, 1 mark for the path function, 1 mark for boundary conditions — total 7 marks.)**

##### Part (b) — Derivation of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ and its Area (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the Minkowski-expanded obstacle formula (1 mark):**

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \lVert q - c \rVert_2 \leq r + R \}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute given values (1 mark):**

$$
r + R = 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{ q \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid \lVert q - (3, 4) \rVert_2 \leq 1.5 \}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the region as a disk (1 mark):**

The region is a **closed disk** centered at $(3, 4)$ with radius $1.5 \text{ m}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the area (2 marks):**

$$
A = \pi (r + R)^2 = \pi (1.5)^2 = 2.25\pi \approx 7.07 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Discuss the free space (2 marks):**

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}
$$

Any path from $q_s \notin \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ to $q_g \notin \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ must either go *around* the disk or, if $q_s$ and $q_g$ are in different connected components, the path **does not exist** (planner returns failure).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** **Define** configuration space and **derive** the C-space obstacle for a **polygonal translating robot** of vertices $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$ in a polygonal workspace. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Compare **path planning, trajectory planning, and motion planning** in a tabular form. State **two real-world applications** of each. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — C-Space for Polygonal Robot (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$ for a translating rigid body in 2D; configuration $q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Step 1 — Define the Minkowski Sum (2 marks):**

For a polygonal robot $R$ (vertices $v_i$) and a polygonal obstacle $O$ (vertices $w_j$), the C-obstacle is the **Minkowski sum** with the reflected robot:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = O \oplus (-R) = \{ o - r \mid o \in O, r \in R \}
$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent vertex representation (2 marks):**

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \text{ConvexHull}\left(\{ w_j - v_i \mid i = 1, \ldots, k, \; j = 1, \ldots, m \}\right)
$$

This means compute every difference $w_j - v_i$, then take the convex hull. The resulting polygon is the C-space obstacle.

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):** A valid path must lie entirely in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.

##### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)

| Aspect | Path Planning | Trajectory Planning | Motion Planning |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|------------------|
| Considers time? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Considers dynamics? | No | Optional | **Yes** |
| Output | Curve $\pi$ | Time-indexed $\tau$ | Control law $u(t)$ |
| Example algorithm | Visibility Graph, RRT | Cubic spline, Trapezoidal velocity | TrajOpt, MPPI |
| Applications | Maze-solving, warehouse routing | Robotic arm pick-and-place, CNC machining | Drone aerobatics, legged locomotion on rough terrain |
| Industry use | Google Maps, AGV fleets | Factory robotics, animatronics | Autonomous racing, humanoid balance |

**Two real-world applications per category (3 marks):**
- **Path Planning:** Self-driving car lane routing; robot vacuum cleaner room coverage.
- **Trajectory Planning:** Industrial welding arm; satellite antenna pointing.
- **Motion Planning:** Quadrotor obstacle-avoidance in forests; humanoid stair climbing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $\mathcal{W}$ with $\mathcal{C}$** — This is the #1 mark-deduction error. A 14-mark answer that uses workspace coordinates for a configuration-space question loses 3–4 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the topology of revolute joints** — Always write $T^n$ or $S^1$ instead of $\mathbb{R}^n$ for revolute chains.
> 3. **Skipping the time-parameterization distinction** — Examiners explicitly test whether you know the difference between $\pi$ and $\tau$. One sentence is enough; omitting it costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Writing $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ instead of $\lVert \cdot \rVert_2$** for the 2D norm — minor, but a strict examiner will mark it.
> 5. **Omitting the boundary conditions** $\pi(0) = q_s$, $\pi(1) = q_g$ in derivations — 1 mark lost.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- ✅ **Motion Planning** = finding $\pi : [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ with $\pi(0) = q_s$, $\pi(1) = q_g$.
- ✅ **Configuration $q$** specifies every DOF; $q \in \mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ generally.
- ✅ **Workspace $\mathcal{W} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m$** is the physical space ($m = 2$ or $3$).
- ✅ **$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$** is the planning universe.
- ✅ **$\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ for a translating robot = Minkowski expansion of workspace obstacles by robot radius $r$.**
- ✅ **Path = geometric, no time**; **Trajectory = timed**; **Motion Plan = control-aware**.
- ✅ **Revolute joints** $\Rightarrow \mathcal{C} = T^n$ (torus topology).
- ✅ **Prismatic joints** $\Rightarrow \mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^n$ subspace.
- ✅ **Holonomic** = controls = DOF; **Non-holonomic** = controls < DOF (e.g., a car).
- ✅ **Connectedness of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ is a NECESSARY condition** for path existence.
- ✅ **Five elements** of the problem: robot, workspace, $\mathcal{C}$, map $F$, free space.
- ✅ **Minkowski Sum formula:** $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = O \oplus (-R)$ for translating polygonal robots.
- ✅ **KTU trap:** Always use $\lVert \cdot \rVert_2$ or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ correctly in markdown — examiners check for it.
- ✅ **Topological types to remember:** $S^1$ (circle), $T^2 = S^1 \times S^1$ (torus), $\mathbb{R}^n$ (prismatic), $\text{SE}(2)$, $\text{SE}(3)$.
- ✅ **Clearance $\delta > 0$** is the minimum distance from $\pi$ to $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ — used in safety analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accessibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accessibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Accessibility (Reachability)** in Robot Motion Planning is the formal property describing the **set of all configurations (or states)** that a robot can attain from a given initial configuration through a sequence of admissible motions, subject to **kinematic constraints**, **dynamic constraints**, and **environmental obstacles**.

Mathematically, for a robot operating in a **Configuration Space** $\mathcal{C}$, given an initial configuration $q_0 \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$:

$$
A(q_0) = \{ q \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \;|\; \exists \; \text{continuous, collision-free path } \tau : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}, \tau(0)=q_0,\; \tau(1)=q \}
$$

The accessibility problem is therefore the question: *"Starting from where I am, what is the set of all places I can legally go to?"*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🧭

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — A Blind Person in a Room with a Flashlight:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded inside an unfamiliar room. You can sweep your flashlight beam (your "sensor footprint") to discover which floor tiles are flat (collision-free) and which contain furniture (obstacles). The **union of all tiles you discover over time** as you keep moving is your *Accessible Set*. Tiles behind the sofa that you can never reach without climbing over it lie outside this set.

In robotic terms:
- **The room** = Workspace $\mathcal{W}$
- **The flashlight beam + your body** = Robot's swept volume in C-space
- **The floor tiles you can stand on** = $\mathcal{C}_{free}$
- **Tiles blocked by furniture** = $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$
- **Tiles reachable by walking** = **Accessible Set** $A(q_0)$

---

## 1.3 Why Accessibility Matters in KTU Robotics

| Domain | Why Accessibility is Critical |
|---|---|
| Industrial Manipulators | Determines whether a target part is **within the reachable workspace** of the arm |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Defines the **safe drivable region** from the current pose |
| Humanoid Robots | Decides whether a footstep on a step is **kinematically feasible** |
| Mobile Manipulators | Combines base + arm reachability to plan **grasp locations** |
| Aerial Swarms | Governs **collision-free region** each drone can claim |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric used in KTU Examinations:** The **Reachability Index** is often defined as the ratio of accessible volume to total free space:
> $$\boxed{\;\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{\mu(A(q_0))}{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})}\;}$$
> where $\mu(\cdot)$ is the **Lebesgue measure** (volume/area) in the appropriate dimension.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

- **Configuration $q$** — A complete specification of the robot's pose (e.g., joint angles for an arm, $x,y,\theta$ for a mobile robot).
- **Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$** — The **manifold** of all possible configurations.
- **Free Space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$** — $\mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ (collision-free subset).
- **Obstacle Region $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$** — Pre-image of workspace obstacles under the robot's geometry mapping.
- **Connected Component of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$** — A maximal region of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ where any two points are linked by a collision-free path. Accessibility from $q_0$ is restricted to the **component containing $q_0$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Tiers of Accessibility

Accessibility is not a single concept — it exists in **three escalating layers of complexity** that every KTU student must master:

### Tier 1: Geometric / Kinematic Accessibility
- Considers **only position and orientation**, no velocities or forces.
- The robot is a **rigid body** in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- **Reachable Workspace** is the projection of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ back to workspace.

### Tier 2: Differential / Kinodynamic Accessibility
- Considers **velocity and acceleration limits** of each joint or wheel.
- Governed by the **control-affine dynamics** $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)u$.
- The accessible set **shrinks** because not all of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is dynamically reachable in a given time horizon $T$.

### Tier 3: Topological / Connectivity Accessibility
- Considers whether the **connected component** of $q_0$ contains the goal $q_{goal}$.
- If $\text{Comp}(q_0) \neq \text{Comp}(q_{goal})$, **no path exists** — the problem is **infeasible**.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Accessibility Computation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step logic** for computing the accessible set $A(q_0)$ in a planning problem:

1. **Step 1 — Build Configuration Space:** Parameterize the robot by $q \in \mathcal{C}$. For an $n$-DOF arm, $q = (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (or $\mathbb{T}^n$ for revolute joints).
2. **Step 2 — Map Workspace Obstacles to C-space:** For each workspace obstacle $\mathcal{O}_i \subseteq \mathcal{W}$, compute the **C-obstacle**:
$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \bigcup_{i} \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \;|\; \text{Robot}(q) \cap \mathcal{O}_i \neq \emptyset \}$$
3. **Step 3 — Define Free Space:** $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$.
4. **Step 4 — Wavefront Expansion:** Starting from $q_0$, propagate outward through $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ using a graph search (BFS, Dijkstra, A*, RRT) until all reachable vertices are visited or the goal is found.
5. **Step 5 — Extract Accessible Set:** $A(q_0)$ is the visited set. Connectivity test: if $q_{goal} \notin A(q_0)$, declare **infeasibility**.

---

## 2.3 Accessibility for Non-Holonomic Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Holonomic Constraint:** A velocity constraint $\omega(q)\dot{q} = 0$ that is **not integrable**, meaning the robot cannot move instantaneously in arbitrary directions (e.g., a car can move forward/backward but not sideways).

For a unicycle-like mobile robot with state $q = (x, y, \theta)$ and controls $(v, \omega)$:

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

The accessibility is constrained to a **2-D manifold in a 3-D state space**. A critical result:

> **Manhattan-like motions (drive → rotate → drive)** are needed to access arbitrary $(x,y)$ positions because pure translation is impossible sideways.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols must be memorized.** The vertical bar in any expression is typeset as `\vert` to preserve markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Configuration Space | $\mathcal{C} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{C}_i$ | $n$ = number of DOF |
| 2 | Free Space | $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Where robot can exist |
| 3 | C-Obstacle (rigid body) | $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \vert \mathcal{R}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$ | $\mathcal{R}(q)$ = robot at config $q$ |
| 4 | Accessible Set | $A(q_0) = \{ q \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \vert \exists \text{ path } \tau \text{ from } q_0 \text{ to } q \}$ | Path must be continuous |
| 5 | Reachability Index | $\mathcal{R} = \mu(A(q_0)) \big/ \mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \mathcal{R} \le 1$ |
| 6 | Reachable Workspace | $\mathcal{W}_{reach} = \pi(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ | $\pi$ = forward kinematics projection |
| 7 | Dexterous Workspace | $\mathcal{W}_{dex} = \{ p \in \mathcal{W}_{reach} \vert \text{end-effector has full orientation}\}$ | Subset of $\mathcal{W}_{reach}$ |
| 8 | Time-Bounded Accessibility | $A(q_0, T) = \{ x(T) \vert \dot{x} = f(x,u),\; x(0)=q_0,\; u \in \mathcal{U} \}$ | Kinodynamic version |
| 9 | Non-Holonomic Drift Field | $\dot{q} = g_1(q)u_1 + g_2(q)u_2$ | Car: $g_1 = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta, 0)^T$ |
| 10 | LFS (Lie Algebra Rank) Condition | $\text{rank}[\,g_1,\,g_2,\, [g_1,g_2]\,] = n$ | Sufficient for local accessibility |
| 11 | Path Length Cost | $L(\tau) = \int_0^1 \lVert \dot{\tau}(s) \rVert \, ds$ | For optimality in $A(q_0)$ |
| 12 | Holonomic Condition | $\omega(q)\dot{q} = 0$ non-integrable ⟹ non-holonomic | Car sideways: blocked |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Accessibility analysis is the **first sanity check** in every industrial robotics deployment. Without it:
> - A welding robot may be **installed out of reach** of every part on the assembly line.
> - An autonomous forklift may **plan a path that is geometrically free but kinematically impossible** (e.g., trying to turn in zero radius).
> - A surgical robot's end-effector may **never reach a deep-cavity target** if port placement is poorly chosen.

In **NASA's Mars rovers**, accessibility analysis determines which rocks and craters the rover can physically approach given its rocker-bogie suspension geometry. In **warehouse automation** (Amazon Kiva robots), the accessible set of every pod-carrying robot is recomputed **40 times per second** to coordinate traffic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Accessible Set of a 2-DOF Planar Arm ⭐ (High-Yield KTU Problem)

> **Problem Setup:** A 2-DOF planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ (both constant), joint limits $0 \le q_1 \le 2\pi$ and $-\pi/2 \le q_2 \le \pi/2$, and a circular workspace obstacle of radius $r_{obs}$ centered at $(x_c, y_c)$. Compute the accessible set $A(q_0)$.

### Step 1 — Forward Kinematics

The end-effector position in workspace is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(q_1, q_2) &= L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
y(q_1, q_2) &= L_1 \sin(q_1) + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **forward kinematics map** $\pi : \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{W}$. Each configuration $q = (q_1, q_2)$ maps to exactly one workspace point $(x, y)$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Reachable Workspace (No Obstacles)

For a point on the boundary of the reachable workspace, we set up:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^2 + y^2 &= (L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2))^2 + (L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2))^2 \\
&= L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Using the identity $\cos(q_1)\cos(q_1+q_2) + \sin(q_1)\sin(q_1+q_2) = \cos(q_2)$.

So the **maximum reach** is $L_1 + L_2$ (when $q_2 = 0$), and the **minimum reach** is $|L_1 - L_2|$ (when $q_2 = \pi$).

### Step 3 — Annular Reachable Workspace (No Obstacle)

$$
\mathcal{W}_{reach} = \{ (x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \;\vert\; \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert^2 \le x^2 + y^2 \le (L_1 + L_2)^2 \}
$$

This is an **annulus** of inner radius $|L_1 - L_2|$ and outer radius $L_1 + L_2$.

### Step 4 — C-Obstacle Construction

For a circular obstacle of radius $r_{obs}$ centered at $(x_c, y_c)$, the C-obstacle is the set of configurations where the end-effector intersects it:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \left\{ (q_1, q_2) \in \mathcal{C} \;\Bigg|\; \left(x(q_1, q_2) - x_c\right)^2 + \left(y(q_1, q_2) - y_c\right)^2 \le r_{obs}^2 \right\}
$$

### Step 5 — Free Space and Accessible Set

$$
\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}
$$

$$
A(q_0) = \text{ConnectedComponent}(q_0, \mathcal{C}_{free})
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Illustration

Let $L_1 = 2$, $L_2 = 1.5$, $r_{obs} = 0.5$, center $(x_c, y_c) = (1.5, 0)$.

- **Outer reach:** $L_1 + L_2 = 3.5$ units
- **Inner reach:** $L_1 - L_2 = 0.5$ units
- The obstacle is at distance $1.5$ from origin → **inside the annulus**
- The C-obstacle is a curved "banana-shaped" region in the $(q_1, q_2)$ torus.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Accessibility of a Differential-Drive Robot

> **Problem:** A differential-drive robot has state $q = (x, y, \theta)$ and controls $(v, \omega)$ with $|v| \le v_{max}$, $|\omega| \le \omega_{max}$. Find the **reachable set after time $T$**.

### Step 1 — System Dynamics

$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

### Step 2 — Controllability Matrix (Chow–Rashevskii Theorem)

The control vector fields are:

$$
g_1 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad g_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Their **Lie bracket** $[g_1, g_2] = \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial q} g_1 - \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2$:

$$
[g_1, g_2] = -\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Rank Condition

$$
\text{rank}\big[ g_1,\; g_2,\; [g_1, g_2] \big] = \text{rank}\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = 3
$$

Since the rank equals $n = 3$ (the dimension of state), the system satisfies the **LARC (Lie Algebra Rank Condition)** ⟹ the system is **locally controllable** everywhere except at singular points.

### Step 4 — Reachable Set Approximation (Reeds–Shepp Curves)

In the unbounded case ($v_{max}, \omega_{max}$ unlimited), the shortest paths are **Reeds–Shepp curves** consisting of at most 5 segments (CSC, CCC, etc.). The reachable set after traveling a total path length $S = v_{max} T$ is:

$$
A(q_0, T) \subseteq \{ (x,y,\theta) \;\vert\; x^2 + y^2 \le (v_{max} T)^2 \text{ (upper bound disc)} \}
$$

with the orientation $\theta$ constrained by **how much the robot can turn** during the maneuver.

### Step 5 — Turning Radius Constraint

Minimum turning radius: $R_{min} = v_{max} / \omega_{max}$

Any configuration whose displacement from $q_0$ is **less than** some multiple of $R_{min}$ in the wrong direction may be **inaccessible** at time $T$ — this is a **key non-holonomic limitation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reachability Index for a Mobile Robot in a Rectangular Hallway

> **Problem:** A point robot moves in a 5 m × 4 m rectangular room starting at corner $(0,0)$. Find the reachability index $\mathcal{R}$.

**Solution Logic:**

- Total free space area: $\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free}) = 5 \times 4 = 20 \, m^2$ (no obstacles).
- A point robot has **no orientation constraint** ⟹ it is **fully holonomic**.
- Therefore the entire room is accessible: $A(q_0) = \mathcal{C}_{free}$, $\mu(A(q_0)) = 20$.
- Reachability Index: $\mathcal{R} = 20/20 = 1.0$ ⟹ **Fully accessible**.

> This is the **maximum possible value**, illustrating that a holonomic point robot with no obstacles has $\mathcal{R} = 1$ always.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Accessibility Decision Table

| Robot Type | Dimension of $\mathcal{C}$ | Obstacle Effect | Non-Holonomic? | Typical $\mathcal{R}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2-DOF Planar Arm | 2 | Curved C-obstacles | No | 0.6 – 0.9 |
| 6-DOF Industrial Arm | 6 | Complex polytopes | No | 0.4 – 0.7 |
| Differential Drive | 3 | Workspace polygons | **Yes** | 0.3 – 0.6 |
| Car-like (Ackermann) | 3 (or 4 with vel) | Lane boundaries | **Yes** | 0.2 – 0.5 |
| Quadrotor (with attitude) | 12 | 3-D obstacles | No (fully actuated) | 0.7 – 0.9 |
| Holonomic Point Robot | 2 (or 3) | Workspace obstacles | No | ≈ 1.0 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Accessibility Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace W and Robot Geometry R] --> B[Forward Kinematics Map pi]
    B --> C[Configuration Space C]
    D[Workspace Obstacles O] --> E[Inverse Pre-image Mapping]
    C --> E
    E --> F[C-Obstacles C_obs]
    C --> G[C minus C_obs]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Free Space C_free]
    H --> I[Graph Search BFS or A*]
    I0[Initial Config q0] --> I
    I --> J[Accessible Set A of q0]
    I --> K[Goal Config q_goal]
    J --> L{Is q_goal inside A of q0?}
    K --> L
    L -->|Yes| M[Path Exists - Proceed to Trajectory Optimization]
    L -->|No| N[Infeasible - Re-plan with New Start or Modified Environment]
```

---

## 4.2 Accessibility Tier Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Tier 1 - Geometric Accessibility] --> L2[Tier 2 - Differential Accessibility]
    L2 --> L3[Tier 3 - Topological Connectivity]
    L1a[Position and Orientation Only] --> L1
    L2a[Velocity and Acceleration Bounds] --> L2
    L3a[Connected Components of C_free] --> L3
    L1 --> R1[Reachable Workspace W_reach]
    L2 --> R2[Time-bounded Reachable Set A q0 T]
    L3 --> R3[Feasibility Verdict]
```

---

## 4.3 C-space Decomposition for Accessibility Analysis

```mermaid
graph LR
    Q[Full Configuration Space C] --> OBS[C-Obstacles Region]
    Q --> FREE[Free Space C_free]
    FREE --> CC1[Connected Component 1 - Contains q0]
    FREE --> CC2[Connected Component 2 - Contains q_goal]
    FREE --> CC3[Disconnected Component 3 - Isolated Pocket]
    CC1 --> ACC[Accessible Set A of q0]
    CC2 --> NG[Not Accessible from q0]
    CC3 --> NG
```

---

## 4.4 Non-Holonomic Accessibility Constraints (Car-like Robot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Pose q0] --> B[Drive Forward with phi = 0]
    B --> C[Stop and Rotate phi]
    C --> D[Drive Forward to Target x y]
    D --> E[Final Pose with Correct theta]
    B -.->|Min Turning Radius R_min = L/tan phi_max| R[Constraint: No Lateral Motion Allowed]
    R --> B
    R --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Diagram Rationale:** Physical schematic drawings of C-space manifolds and reachable workspaces would require complex 2-D/3-D illustrations. The above flowcharts map the **functional information flow** of accessibility computation, which is the more examinable representation in KTU 2024 Scheme theory papers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🎯 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Accessibility" in the context of robot motion planning. How is it different from reachability of the end-effector?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Accessibility** is the property of a configuration (or set of configurations) to be attainable from an initial configuration via a **continuous, collision-free, kinematically feasible** trajectory within the configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

**Difference from end-effector reachability:**

| Aspect | Accessibility | End-Effector Reachability |
|---|---|---|
| Defined in | Configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ | Task / Workspace $\mathcal{W}$ |
| Considers | Whole robot body + obstacles | Only TCP position/orientation |
| Accounts for | Collision, joint limits, dynamics | Geometry, joint limits only |
| Use | Feasibility of motion plans | Pre-deployment cell design |

Accessibility is the **broader, planning-level concept**, while end-effector reachability is a **geometric, pre-deployment metric**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Lie Algebra Rank Condition (LARC) and explain its role in determining accessibility of non-holonomic systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> **LARC:** A control-affine system $\dot{x} = f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} g_i(x) u_i$ is **locally accessible** from $x_0$ if the **smallest Lie algebra** generated by the vector fields $\{g_1, \dots, g_m, f\}$ has **full rank $n$** (equal to the dimension of the state space) at $x_0$.

**Role in accessibility:**

- LARC gives a **necessary (and, for analytic systems, sufficient) condition** for local accessibility.
- For driftless systems (e.g., a car), it requires evaluating **iterated Lie brackets** of the input vector fields, which physically represent **higher-order maneuvers** (e.g., parallel parking via forward-reverse sequences).
- If LARC fails at some $x_0$, the system is **not locally accessible** there, meaning the robot is "stuck" in a lower-dimensional manifold.

**Example:** A differential-drive robot satisfies LARC because $[g_1, g_2]$ is linearly independent of $g_1$ and $g_2$, so it can reach any nearby $(x, y, \theta)$ — confirming the empirical fact that parallel parking is possible.

---

## 🎯 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the construction of configuration space obstacles for a **circular mobile robot of radius $r$** translating in a 2-D workspace containing a single rectangular obstacle. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Define the **accessible set** $A(q_0)$ of a robot in its configuration space. A 2-DOF planar arm has link lengths $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$, $L_2 = 0.8\,\text{m}$, and joint limits $0 \le q_1 \le 2\pi$, $-\pi/2 \le q_2 \le \pi/2$. Compute the **maximum reach**, **minimum reach**, and the **reachable workspace area** when no obstacles are present. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup:**
- Robot: disc of radius $r$, position $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
- Workspace obstacle: rectangle with corners $(x_{\min}, y_{\min})$ and $(x_{\max}, y_{\max})$.
- Configuration space: $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Step 2 — C-obstacle Construction via Minkowski Sum:**

The C-obstacle is the set of robot center positions where the robot intersects the rectangle. This is obtained by **inflating the obstacle** by the robot's radius $r$ (a Minkowski sum with a disc):

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \mathcal{O} \oplus B(0, r)
$$

where $B(0, r)$ is a disc of radius $r$ centered at the origin.

**Step 3 — Resulting Shape:**
The rectangular obstacle becomes a **rounded rectangle** — a rectangle of the same dimensions with **four quarter-circle caps of radius $r$** at the corners:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \left\{ (x,y) \;\Bigg|\; \begin{array}{l} x_{\min} - r \le x \le x_{\max} + r, \\ y_{\min} - r \le y \le y_{\max} + r, \\ \text{or } (x - x_c)^2 + (y - y_c)^2 \le r^2 \text{ for each corner} \end{array} \right\}
$$

**Step 4 — Free Space:**
$\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$.

**Diagram (Schematic):**

```
   _____________________
  /                     \
 |   <-- C_obs (rounded   |
 |       rectangle)       |
  \_____________________/
        
   Free space C_free surrounds the inflated obstacle
```

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Recap Formulas:**

Maximum reach: $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$

Minimum reach: $R_{min} = |L_1 - L_2|$ (with $L_1 > L_2$, this is $L_1 - L_2$)

**Step 2 — Compute Numerical Values:**

$$
R_{max} = 1.0 + 0.8 = 1.8 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
R_{min} = 1.0 - 0.8 = 0.2 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Reachable Workspace Area:**

The reachable workspace is an **annulus** of inner radius $0.2$ m and outer radius $1.8$ m. Its area is:

$$
A_{ws} = \pi (R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2) = \pi (1.8^2 - 0.2^2) = \pi (3.24 - 0.04) = 3.2 \pi \approx 10.05 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Reachability Index (No Obstacle):**

Since the entire annulus is reachable (no obstacles), $\mathcal{R} = 1.0$ for the relative workspace, but the absolute reachability index **with respect to all of $\mathbb{R}^2$** is $0$ (because of the unreachable hole in the middle and points beyond $1.8$ m).

**Final Answers:**
- $R_{max} = 1.8$ m
- $R_{min} = 0.2$ m
- $A_{ws} = 3.2\pi \approx 10.05$ m²

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** What is meant by the **reachable workspace** of a robot? Differentiate between **reachable workspace**, **dexterous workspace**, and **accessible workspace**, with one example for each. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For a **unicycle mobile robot** with dynamics $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$, evaluate the **Lie Algebra Rank Condition** at an arbitrary state $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$. Comment on the implications for accessibility. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Reachable Workspace (RW):**
The set of all positions (and possibly orientations) that the **end-effector** can attain for **at least one** robot configuration.

Example: The **annulus** of a 2-DOF arm with link lengths $L_1, L_2$.

**Step 2 — Dexterous Workspace (DW):**
The subset of RW where the end-effector can attain **arbitrary orientations** (i.e., full $SO(2)$ or $SO(3)$ rotation).

Example: For a 3-DOF planar arm with $L_1 \neq L_2 \neq L_3$, only the **central region** inside the largest annulus is dexterous.

**Step 3 — Accessible Workspace (AW):**
A **planning-level** concept — the set of configurations (not just end-effector poses) that the robot can be in **without collision**, starting from a given initial state. It accounts for the **entire body** of the robot, not just the TCP.

Example: A 6-DOF arm in a cluttered bin-picking cell — its AW may be only a fraction of its RW because the elbow can collide with bins or conveyors.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Reachable WS | Dexterous WS | Accessible WS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Considers | TCP geometry | TCP orientation | Whole robot body |
| Includes obstacles | No | No | **Yes** |
| Size relative to each other | Largest | Smallest subset of RW | Subset of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ |

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the Control Vector Fields:**
With state $q = (x, y, \theta)^T$ and controls $u_1 = v$, $u_2 = \omega$:

$$
g_1 = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad g_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Lie Bracket $[g_1, g_2]$:**

$$
[g_1, g_2] = \frac{\partial g_2}{\partial q} g_1 - \frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2
$$

Since $g_2$ is constant, $\partial g_2 / \partial q = 0$. Therefore:

$$
[g_1, g_2] = -\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} g_2
$$

The Jacobian of $g_1$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial g_1}{\partial q} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & 0 & \cos\theta \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Multiplying by $g_2 = (0, 0, 1)^T$:

$$
[g_1, g_2] = -\begin{pmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \sin\theta \\ -\cos\theta \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Distribution Matrix:**

$$
\mathcal{D} = \big[\, g_1,\; g_2,\; [g_1, g_2] \,\big] = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & 0 & -\cos\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Determinant:**

$$
\det(\mathcal{D}) = 0 \cdot (\cdots) - 0 \cdot (\cdots) + 1 \cdot (\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = 1
$$

The determinant is **identically equal to $1$** at every state $(x, y, \theta)$ — **non-zero everywhere**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Since $\det(\mathcal{D}) = 1 \neq 0$, the rank is $3$ everywhere, satisfying LARC. Therefore, the unicycle is **locally accessible at every point** — the robot can reach any nearby state by combining translations and rotations cleverly (e.g., "wiggle" motions like parallel parking). However, **global accessibility** is still constrained by control bounds $|v| \le v_{max}$, $|\omega| \le \omega_{max}$ and the **minimum turning radius** $R_{min} = v_{max} / \omega_{max}$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Reachable Workspace with Accessible Workspace** — RW is a geometric pre-deployment metric, AW is a planning-time collision-aware set. Examiners allocate **2 marks** for distinguishing them.
> 2. **Forgetting that non-holonomic systems satisfy LARC but are NOT omnidirectionally accessible in finite time** — LARC only guarantees *local* accessibility, not *global instantaneous* reachability.
> 3. **Sign errors in Lie bracket computation** — always re-check whether you are computing $[g_1, g_2]$ or $[g_2, g_1]$ (they differ by a sign).
> 4. **Ignoring Minkowski sum direction** — for a translating robot, the obstacle is **inflated by the robot's shape**, not the other way around. Many students draw the un-inflated obstacle, losing 3 marks in Part (a) of such problems.
> 5. **Forgetting joint limits** — when computing $R_{min}$ of a 2-DOF arm, students often write $|L_1 - L_2|$ without checking whether $q_2 = \pi$ is actually within joint limits. The effective $R_{min}$ may be larger.
> 6. **Mixing workspace and configuration space** — never quote a workspace area when the question asks for a C-space volume. Units and dimensions are checked by the examiner.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Accessibility** = set of all configurations reachable via continuous, collision-free, kinematically feasible paths from $q_0$.
- ✅ **Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$** parameterizes the entire robot; **Free Space $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$** is where valid motion lives.
- ✅ **C-obstacles** are obtained by **inflating workspace obstacles** by the robot's geometry (Minkowski sum for translation, more complex for rotation).
- ✅ **Three tiers**: Geometric → Differential (kinodynamic) → Topological (connectivity).
- ✅ **LARC** (Chow–Rashevskii Theorem) determines local accessibility of non-holonomic systems by checking the rank of the distribution spanned by the input vector fields and their iterated Lie brackets.
- ✅ **Holonomic robots** (e.g., aerial manipulators) have $\mathcal{R} \approx 1$ in open space; **non-holonomic robots** (cars, unicycles) have $\mathcal{R} < 1$ even in open space.
- ✅ **Reachable Workspace** ⊇ **Dexterous Workspace**; **Accessible Set** ⊆ **$\mathcal{C}_{free}$**.
- ✅ **Reachability Index** $\mathcal{R} = \mu(A(q_0)) / \mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ — a key KTU high-yield formula.
- ✅ A goal is **reachable** ⟺ it lies in the **same connected component** of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ as $q_0$.
- ✅ **Reeds–Shepp curves** describe shortest paths for car-like robots; **Dubins curves** for the no-reverse special case.
- ✅ **Minimum turning radius** $R_{min} = L / \tan(\phi_{max})$ for Ackermann-steered vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Departability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decidability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
