6. **Build the lattice graph $G$.** Each FREE cell becomes a node; edges connect FREE cells that are lattice-adjacent.
7. **Graph search.** Run A* (or BFS) from the start cell to the goal cell over $G$.
8. **Path reconstruction.** Convert the sequence of cell centres back into a continuous polyline by joining their centroids.
9. **Smoothing (optional).** Apply shortcutting or a local optimisation to remove the staircase artefacts introduced by the grid.

> [!TIP]
> **Why centroid sampling?** Using the *centre* of a cell as its representative point guarantees that if the centre is free, the entire cell is at most a distance $\frac{\delta\sqrt{2}}{2}$ away from a free point — a useful bound for analysing path deviation.

### 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How"

- **Why decompose?** Continuous configuration spaces are uncountably infinite. A computer can only store and process finite structures. ACD converts the continuous planning problem into a discrete graph problem.
- **How is "approximate" handled?** Resolution is a tunable parameter. As $\delta \to 0$, the discretisation converges to the true free space. However, the number of cells blows up as $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^2)$ in 2-D.
- **Why use a lattice graph?** Lattice graphs are extremely regular, which means:
  - Neighbour lookups are $O(1)$ (no need for spatial data structures).
  - The resulting graph is small in terms of constants, even if large in absolute size.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Number of cells along $x$-axis | $N_x = \lceil (x_{\max}-x_{\min})/\delta \rceil$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Number of cells along $y$-axis | $N_y = \lceil (y_{\max}-y_{\min})/\delta \rceil$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Total cells in 2-D grid | $N = N_x \cdot N_y$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Generalised grid size in $\mathbb{R}^n$ | $N = \prod_{i=1}^{n} N_i$ | exponential in $n$ |
| 5 | Memory complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N) = \mathcal{O}(1/\delta^n)$ | cells, grows fast |
| 6 | Search complexity (A*) | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ | with binary heap |
| 7 | Edge count (4-connected) | $E \approx 2N$ | each cell has 2 horizontal + 2 vertical |
| 8 | Edge count (8-connected) | $E \approx 4N$ | adds diagonals |
| 9 | Worst-case path-length factor | $\le \sqrt{2} \cdot L^*$ | vs. true optimal in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ |
| 10 | Per-cell classification cost (point-in-polygon) | $\mathcal{O}(m)$ | $m$ = obstacle vertices |
| 11 | Total classification cost | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot m_{\text{avg}})$ | one-time, upfront |
| 12 | Narrow-passage width threshold | $w \ge \delta$ | otherwise mis-classified |
| 13 | Inflation radius for circular robot | $r_{\text{inflate}} = r_{\text{robot}}$ | reduce to point-robot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Point:** The 8-connected path-length bound of $\sqrt{2}$ applies **only** when diagonal moves are explicitly allowed *and* no corner-cutting through obstacles occurs. Examiners will mark you down if you claim the bound without stating both conditions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs):** Warehouse robots (e.g., Amazon Kiva systems) often use grid-based planners because floor maps are inherently axis-aligned.
- **Indoor Service Robots:** Vacuum-cleaning robots (Roomba-class) use coarse ACD on occupancy grids built from SLAM.
- **Video-Game AI:** Pathfinding on tile-based maps (RTS, MOBA games) is essentially ACD.
- **Drone Swarms:** 2.5-D grid planners are used in surveillance tasks over known building layouts.
- **Surgical Robotics (research):** Pre-operative ACD over CT-derived anatomical maps is used to plan needle-insertion paths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Cells in a Bounded 2-D Grid

**Statement:** Given a rectangular envelope of width $W = x_{\max}-x_{\min}$ and height $H = y_{\max}-y_{\min}$, the total number of cells produced by a resolution-$\delta$ ACD is $N = N_x \cdot N_y$.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Width of envelope: } && W = x_{\max}-x_{\min} \\
&\text{Number of cells along } x: && N_x = \left\lceil \frac{W}{\delta} \right\rceil \\
&\text{Height of envelope: } && H = y_{\max}-y_{\min} \\
&\text{Number of cells along } y: && N_y = \left\lceil \frac{H}{\delta} \right\rceil \\
&\text{Total cells (2-D): } && N = N_x \cdot N_y = \left\lceil \frac{W}{\delta} \right\rceil \cdot \left\lceil \frac{H}{\delta} \right\rceil
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic form (ignoring ceiling and constants):**

$$
N \;\approx\; \frac{W \cdot H}{\delta^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Area of envelope}}{\text{Area of one cell}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The total cell count grows **inversely with the square of the resolution**. Halving $\delta$ quadruples the memory requirement.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Memory Complexity in $n$ Dimensions

**Statement:** ACD in an $n$-dimensional configuration space with per-axis length $L_i$ and uniform resolution $\delta$ requires $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^n)$ memory.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_i &= \left\lceil \frac{L_i}{\delta} \right\rceil \quad \text{for each axis } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n \\
N &= \prod_{i=1}^{n} N_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left\lceil \frac{L_i}{\delta} \right\rceil \\
N &\approx \frac{L_1 L_2 \cdots L_n}{\delta^{n}} \\
\therefore \quad N &= \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{1}{\delta^{n}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key observation:** The exponent is the **dimension** of $\mathcal{C}$-space. For a 6-DOF robot arm, doubling the resolution multiplies memory by **64×**. This is the famous *curse of dimensionality* that limits ACD to low-dimensional problems (typically $n \le 4$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Path-Length Bound for 8-Connected ACD

**Statement:** A path returned by an 8-connected ACD is at most $\sqrt{2}$ times longer than the true shortest path in the continuous free space.

**Derivation (sketch):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Maximum metric distortion per cell.}\\
&\text{A unit cell has corner-to-corner distance } \delta\sqrt{2} \text{ and side length } \delta. \\
&\text{Hence the ratio of diagonal to side is } \frac{\delta\sqrt{2}}{\delta} = \sqrt{2}. \\
&\text{Step 2: The shortest cell-to-cell move in 8-connected grids is either:}\\
&\quad \bullet \text{ a cardinal step of cost } \delta, \text{ or}\\
&\quad \bullet \text{ a diagonal step of cost } \delta\sqrt{2}. \\
&\text{Step 3: Therefore, the worst-case ratio of grid-path length to continuous-path length is } \sqrt{2}. \\
&\therefore \quad L_{\text{grid}} \le \sqrt{2}\, L^{*}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** If your application requires sub-$\sqrt{2}$ optimality, you must post-process the ACD path with a smoothing step (e.g., shortcutting or elastic-band optimisation).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Resolution-Completeness Condition

**Statement:** ACD is *resolution-complete* — if a path exists in the true free space and the resolution $\delta$ is small enough, the algorithm will find one.

**Derivation (logic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Assumption: } \exists \text{ a continuous free path } \pi^{*} \text{ from } q_s \text{ to } q_g. \\
&\text{Let } d_{\min} = \text{clearance}(\pi^{*}, \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}) = \text{distance from } \pi^{*} \text{ to the nearest obstacle.} \\
&\text{Choose } \delta < d_{\min}. \\
&\text{Then an inscribed open tube of radius } d_{\min} \text{ around } \pi^{*} \text{ lies entirely in } \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}. \\
&\text{This tube contains a connected chain of cells whose centres are FREE,}\\
&\text{forming a valid path in the lattice graph.}\\
&\therefore \text{ ACD finds a path whenever } \delta < d_{\min}.
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Practical consequence:** When the free space has narrow passages, you must either (i) decrease $\delta$ (costly), (ii) inflate obstacles differently, or (iii) switch to a planner with stronger narrow-passage behaviour (e.g., PRM variants).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ACD vs. Exact Cell Decomposition

| Property | Approximate Cell Decomposition | Exact Cell Decomposition |
|----------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Cell geometry | Uniform grid (regular) | Cells conform to obstacle boundaries |
| Decomposition effort | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ (trivial) | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ or higher |
| Resolution completeness | Yes (for $\delta < d_{\min}$) | Yes (exact) |
| Path optimality | Up to $\sqrt{2}$× optimal in 2-D | Optimal in the induced graph |
| Memory in 2-D | $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(k)$ — $k$ = number of cells (often fewer) |
| Narrow-passage handling | Fragile (needs small $\delta$) | Robust |
| Implementation complexity | Very low | Higher (needs arrangement of line segments) |
| Scalability in high dimensions | Poor ($\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^n)$) | Worse (combinatorial explosion in $n$) |
| Path-smoothing requirement | Almost always | Sometimes |
| Typical use-case | Indoor mobile robots, games | 2-D planar arms, point robots |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — ACD Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define workspace bounds] --> B[Choose resolution delta]
    B --> C[Inflate obstacles by robot radius]
    C --> D[Discretise workspace into Nx by Ny grid cells]
    D --> E[Loop over every cell in grid]
    E --> F{Cell centroid in obstacle?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Mark cell as OBSTRUCTED]
    F -- No --> H[Mark cell as FREE]
    G --> I{All cells classified?}
    H --> I
    I -- No --> E
    I -- Yes --> J[Build lattice graph G over FREE cells]
    J --> K[Locate start cell and goal cell]
    K --> L[Run A star search on G]
    L --> M{Path found?}
    M -- No --> N[Return FAILURE consider smaller delta]
    M -- Yes --> O[Reconstruct polyline through cell centroids]
    O --> P[Apply path smoothing shortcutting]
    P --> Q[Return final path to robot controller]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an ACD Planner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT MODULE]
        Map[Workspace Map]
        Start[Start Pose]
        Goal[Goal Pose]
        Delta[Resolution delta]
    end

    subgraph DECOMP[DECOMPOSITION MODULE]
        Inflator[Obstacle Inflator]
        Gridder[Grid Generator]
        Classifier[Cell Classifier FREE OR OBSTRUCTED]
    end

    subgraph GRAPH[GRAPH MODULE]
        Builder[Lattice Graph Builder]
        SearchEngine[Search Engine A star or Dijkstra]
    end

    subgraph POST[POST PROCESSING MODULE]
        Polyline[Polyline Reconstructor]
        Smoother[Path Smoother]
    end

    Map --> Inflator
    Inflator --> Gridder
    Gridder --> Classifier
    Delta --> Gridder
    Delta --> Classifier
    Classifier --> Builder
    Start --> SearchEngine
    Goal --> SearchEngine
    Builder --> SearchEngine
    SearchEngine --> Polyline
    Polyline --> Smoother
    Smoother --> Out[Output: Executable Trajectory]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description of a Classified ACD Grid

| Visual Element | Meaning in the Schematic |
|----------------|--------------------------|
| Small white square | FREE cell — robot may occupy |
| Shaded black square | OBSTRUCTED cell — collision |
| Dashed border | Inflated obstacle boundary (for point-robot reduction) |
| Red star | Start cell |
| Green star | Goal cell |
| Yellow arrows | Optimal A* path traversing cell centroids |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In a printed exam, you will often be asked to draw or interpret an ACD grid. Always label (i) the start and goal, (ii) the connectivity convention (4 or 8), and (iii) at least one inflated obstacle — these are the three items most often missed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**  
**Define Approximate Cell Decomposition. Mention two situations in which it is preferred over exact cell decomposition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* Approximate Cell Decomposition is a motion-planning technique in which the free configuration space is partitioned into a finite set of uniform, regularly-shaped cells (typically squares of side $\delta$). Each cell is classified as FREE or OBSTRUCTED, and a lattice graph is constructed over the FREE cells. A standard graph-search algorithm (e.g., A*) is then applied to find a collision-free path from start to goal.

*Preferred situations (1 mark — any two):*
1. The environment is largely rectangular or grid-aligned (e.g., indoor warehouses, office floors).
2. Real-time computation is critical and the overhead of computing exact arrangements of line segments is unacceptable.
3. The configuration space is low-dimensional (2-D or 3-D), where the $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^n)$ memory cost is manageable.

> **Valuation Tip:** Award 2 marks for a correct, complete definition that mentions *uniform cells*, *classification*, and *graph search*. Award the remaining 1 mark only if the student names a *specific* engineering scenario.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**  
**What is *resolution completeness* in the context of ACD? State the condition under which ACD guarantees to find a path.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* Resolution completeness means that the algorithm is guaranteed to find a path **if one exists** *and* the resolution parameter $\delta$ is chosen small enough.

*Condition (1.5 marks):* Let $d_{\min}$ be the minimum clearance between the true optimal path $\pi^{*}$ and any obstacle in $\mathcal{C}$-space. ACD is guaranteed to find a path **if and only if $\delta < d_{\min}$**. In other words, the cell side must be smaller than the width of the narrowest passage the path must traverse.

> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners often award full credit for the inequality $\delta < d_{\min}$ alone, but full 3 marks require both the *definition* and the *condition*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module-2 long-answer questions carry **internal choice**. Two alternatives are provided.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete pipeline of an Approximate Cell Decomposition planner. Mention the role of *obstacle inflation* in this pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot operates in a rectangular workspace of size $10\ \text{m} \times 8\ \text{m}$. The planner uses ACD with cell side $\delta = 0.25\ \text{m}$ and 4-connected lattice. Compute (i) the total number of cells, (ii) the approximate number of edges, and (iii) the A* search complexity in big-O form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Pipeline (7 marks):**

The pipeline consists of the following six sequential modules:

1. **Workspace Definition Module:** Specifies the rectangular envelope $[0, 10] \times [0, 8]$ in metres.
2. **Obstacle Inflation Module:** Each polygonal obstacle is *grown outward* by the robot's radius $r$ using a Minkowski sum. After inflation, the robot is treated as a *point* — this drastically simplifies the collision test from a polygon–polygon check into a point-in-polygon check.
3. **Grid Generation Module:** The envelope is subdivided into $N_x \times N_y$ cells of side $\delta$.
4. **Cell Classification Module:** The centroid of each cell is tested against every inflated obstacle. Cells are labelled FREE or OBSTRUCTED.
5. **Lattice Graph Construction Module:** Each FREE cell becomes a node. Edges connect 4-adjacent FREE cells (or 8-adjacent if Moore neighbourhood is used).
6. **Search and Post-Processing Module:** A* (or BFS) is run on the lattice. The resulting cell-centre sequence is smoothed into a continuous trajectory.

*Role of obstacle inflation (1 mark within the 7):* It converts a *shape-on-shape* collision problem into a *point-in-shape* test, reducing classification cost from $\mathcal{O}(m \cdot n)$ to $\mathcal{O}(m + n)$ per cell, where $m$ and $n$ are the vertex counts of the robot and obstacle respectively.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the number of cells along each axis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_x &= \left\lceil \frac{10}{0.25} \right\rceil = 40 \\
N_y &= \left\lceil \frac{8}{0.25} \right\rceil = 32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total cells (2 marks):**

$$
N = N_x \cdot N_y = 40 \times 32 = 1280 \text{ cells}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of edges for 4-connected lattice (2 marks):**

For a 4-connected grid with $N$ cells, the number of internal edges is:

$$
E \approx 2N = 2 \times 1280 = 2560 \text{ edges}
$$

(A more careful count treats the boundary differently and yields $E = (N_x - 1) N_y + N_x (N_y - 1) = 39 \times 32 + 40 \times 31 = 1248 + 1240 = 2488$.)

**Step 4 — A* search complexity (3 marks):**

A* with a binary heap on a graph with $N$ nodes and $E$ edges has worst-case time:

$$
\mathcal{O}(E \log N) = \mathcal{O}(N \log N) = \mathcal{O}(1280 \log 1280) \approx \mathcal{O}(1.35 \times 10^{4})
$$

Asymptotically in terms of $\delta$:

$$
\mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{1}{\delta^{2}} \log \frac{1}{\delta}\right) = \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{1}{\delta^{2}} \log \frac{1}{\delta}\right)
$$

> **Final Answer:** $N = 1280$ cells, $E \approx 2488$ edges, search complexity $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Compare Approximate Cell Decomposition (ACD) with Exact Cell Decomposition (ECD) under the following heads: cell shape, decomposition complexity, narrow-passage handling, path optimality, and memory requirement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A planar robot has a circular body of radius $r = 0.2\ \text{m}$ and must traverse a corridor whose narrowest free width is $0.5\ \text{m}$. (i) What is the maximum ACD resolution $\delta_{\max}$ that still permits a valid path? (ii) If the workspace is $20\ \text{m} \times 20\ \text{m}$ and we choose $\delta = 0.1\ \text{m}$, how many cells does the grid contain? (iii) Justify why ACD would be a poor choice if the robot had 6 degrees of freedom. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per correct row + 0.5 each supporting note):**

| Head | ACD | ECD |
|------|-----|-----|
| Cell shape | Uniform squares / rectangles | Cells conform exactly to obstacle edges |
| Decomposition complexity | Trivial: $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Non-trivial: $\mathcal{O}(k \log k)$ where $k$ = segments |
| Narrow-passage handling | Fragile (requires $\delta < d_{\min}$) | Robust (cells follow corridor geometry) |
| Path optimality | Up to $\sqrt{2} \times$ optimal in 2-D | Optimal in the induced cell graph |
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^n)$ — depends on chosen $\delta$ | $\mathcal{O}(k)$ — depends on obstacle complexity |

**(b) Numerical and Conceptual Sub-parts (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum resolution (2 marks):**

After inflating obstacles by the robot radius $r = 0.2$ m, the *effective* free width of the corridor becomes:

$$
w_{\text{eff}} = 0.5 - 2r = 0.5 - 0.4 = 0.1\ \text{m}
$$

The resolution-completeness condition requires $\delta < d_{\min} = w_{\text{eff}}$. Therefore:

$$
\delta_{\max} = 0.1\ \text{m} \quad \text{(strictly, any } \delta < 0.1 \text{ m works)}
$$

**(ii) Number of cells (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_x &= \lceil 20/0.1 \rceil = 200 \\
N_y &= \lceil 20/0.1 \rceil = 200 \\
N &= 200 \times 200 = 40{,}000 \text{ cells}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Why ACD is poor for 6-DOF robots (3 marks):**

In a 6-DOF $\mathcal{C}$-space, the number of cells grows as $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^6)$. To achieve a reasonable angular resolution of, say, $\delta = 5^\circ = 0.0873$ rad along each rotational axis, we have:

$$
N = \prod_{i=1}^{6} N_i = 200 \times 200 \times 72 \times 72 \times 72 \times 72 \approx 1.08 \times 10^{12} \text{ cells}
$$

This is approximately **1 trillion cells** — far beyond the memory capacity of any current computer. Furthermore, the *curse of dimensionality* means that:
- Most cells lie deep inside obstacle regions (sparse coverage of FREE space).
- The connectivity graph becomes gigantic and search becomes intractable.
- Sampling-based planners (RRT, PRM) or optimisation-based planners (CHOMP, TrajOpt) are vastly superior for high-dimensional problems.

> **Final Answer:** $\delta_{\max} = 0.1$ m, $N = 40{,}000$ cells, ACD unsuitable for 6-DOF due to $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^6)$ memory blow-up.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on ACD Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ACD with grid-based sampling (RRT-style).** ACD uses a *regular* grid; RRT uses *random* samples. Examiners will deduct up to 2 marks if you mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting the inflation step.** If a question gives a non-zero robot radius, the cell-classification step *must* use inflated obstacles. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Citing the wrong complexity.** Memory is $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^n)$ — *not* $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^{2})$ in general. The exponent is the dimension.
> 4. **Stating "complete" instead of "resolution-complete".** ACD is **not** complete in the algorithmic sense; it is *resolution-complete*. Examiners are strict about this distinction.
> 5. **Ignoring the 4- vs. 8-connected choice.** When asked for the path-length bound, you *must* specify the connectivity used. A 4-connected grid has a worse bound than an 8-connected one.
> 6. **Skipping the smoothing note.** Most KTU model answers expect a one-line mention of *post-processing / smoothing* of the grid path. Skipping it can cost 0.5–1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ACD = uniform grid + cell classification + lattice graph + graph search.** Memorise this four-stage mental model.
- **Resolution $\delta$ is the only "knob"** — it controls the trade-off between accuracy and memory/computation.
- **Memory grows as $\mathcal{O}(1/\delta^n)$** where $n$ is the dimension of the $\mathcal{C}$-space.
- **Time complexity of A* on the lattice is $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$** with a binary heap.
- **Path-length bound is $\le \sqrt{2}\,$ × optimal** for an 8-connected 2-D grid (only when diagonal moves are allowed and corner-cutting is prevented).
- **Resolution-completeness condition: $\delta < d_{\min}$** where $d_{\min}$ is the clearance of the optimal path.
- **Always inflate obstacles by the robot's radius** before classifying cells — this reduces the problem to point-robot planning.
- **Prefer ACD when:** environment is grid-aligned, dimensions are low (2-D or 3-D), real-time performance matters, and the implementation budget is limited.
- **Avoid ACD when:** the robot has $\ge 4$ DOF, the environment has narrow passages, or path optimality is critical.
- **Common alternative names you may see in textbooks:** *grid-based planner*, *uniform cell decomposition*, *resolution-complete roadmap*.
- **Always pair the algorithm with a smoothing post-process** (e.g., shortcutting, elastic bands) to recover the $\sqrt{2}$ optimality loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trapezoidal Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trapezoidal Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Trapezoidal Decomposition** (also called *Trapezoidal Cell Decomposition* or *Vertical Cell Decomposition*) is a fundamental **exact cell decomposition** technique in robot motion planning where the free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ of the robot is partitioned into a collection of simple, non-overlapping convex polygonal regions — most of which are **trapezoids** (or triangles, in degenerate cases).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**  
> "Trapezoidal decomposition is a roadmap-based cell decomposition method in which vertical decomposition lines are extended upward and downward from every vertex of the polygonal obstacles until they hit either another obstacle boundary or the boundary of the workspace. The resulting cells are trapezoids (or triangles) which are guaranteed to be convex, allowing trivial point-to-point navigation within each cell."

Formally, given a polygonal workspace $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ containing polygonal obstacles $\{O_1, O_2, \dots, O_n\}$, the trapezoidal decomposition $\mathcal{T}(\mathcal{W})$ satisfies:

$$\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{W} \setminus \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} O_i = \bigcup_{j=1}^{m} T_j$$

where each cell $T_j$ is a convex polygon with at most **two parallel vertical edges**, and $T_i \cap T_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy: The Cake-Cutting View** 🎂  
> Imagine you have a rectangular cake (the workspace) with several irregular chocolate chunks scattered on it (the obstacles). You want to cut the cake into pieces so that a small robot arm tip (a point) can travel from point A to point B without touching any chocolate.  
>  
> **Trapezoidal Decomposition** is like placing a knife at every corner of each chocolate chunk and slicing **straight up and down** until you hit either another chocolate or the edge of the cake. The pieces you get are mostly trapezoidal (4-sided) — some perfect rectangles, some with slanted top/bottom.  
>  
> Because each piece has no chocolate inside it, the robot can move **in a straight line** to any point within that piece. To go from A to B, the robot simply moves: **A → exit current piece → corridor → enter next piece → B**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Configuration Space ($\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$)** | Set of all valid (collision-free) robot poses |
| **Vertex** | A corner point of a polygonal obstacle |
| **Decomposition Line (Sweep Ray)** | A vertical line shot up and down from a vertex |
| **Trapezoid Cell** | A convex 4-sided region bounded by vertical decomposition lines |
| **Triangle Cell** | Degenerate trapezoid (when two vertices share the same x-coordinate) |
| **Adjacency Graph (Roadmap)** | A graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = cells, $E$ = shared boundary |
| **Roadmap** | A 1-D network within $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ used to plan paths |

## 1.4 Why Use Trapezoidal Decomposition?

- **Convexity Guarantee**: Every cell is convex → straight-line motion is always valid.
- **Simplicity**: Decomposition is fully deterministic and geometric.
- **Completeness**: For polygonal obstacles, it always produces a valid roadmap (resolution-complete).
- **Reduced Search Space**: Path planning reduces from 2-D to a 1-D graph search (e.g., BFS, A*).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Trade-off (KTU High-Yield Point):**  
> Trapezoidal decomposition creates $O(n)$ cells for $n$ vertices, but the **number of cells can be as high as $O(n^2)$** in worst case (when vertices are arranged adversarially). The cell adjacency graph has $O(n)$ nodes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

The trapezoidal decomposition algorithm proceeds as follows:

### Step 1: Vertex Extraction
Identify the complete set of vertices $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$ of all polygonal obstacles in the workspace.

> **Why?** Each vertex is a candidate source for a vertical decomposition line.

### Step 2: Vertical Sweep (Shooting Lines)
For every vertex $v_i = (x_i, y_i)$:
- Cast a **vertical ray upward**: $x = x_i$, $y > y_i$
- Cast a **vertical ray downward**: $x = x_i$, $y < y_i$
- Extend each ray until it **first contacts** either an obstacle edge or the workspace boundary.

> **Why both up AND down?** A vertex has two free "directions" in which the cell boundary must be closed.

### Step 3: Cell Formation
The vertical lines, together with the obstacle edges and workspace boundary, partition the free space into:
- **Trapezoids** (4-sided, two parallel vertical sides)
- **Triangles** (3-sided, degenerate trapezoid when rays meet at the same x-coordinate)

### Step 4: Build the Adjacency Graph $G$
- Each cell becomes a **node** in $G$.
- If two cells share a non-degenerate edge segment, add an **undirected edge** between their nodes.

### Step 5: Path Planning via Graph Search
- Insert the start configuration $q_{\text{start}}$ into the cell containing it.
- Insert the goal configuration $q_{\text{goal}}$ into the cell containing it.
- Run a graph search (A*, BFS, or Dijkstra) on $G$.
- The search returns a sequence of cells.

### Step 6: Path Concatenation
Within each cell, the robot moves in a **straight line** from the entry boundary to the exit boundary. Concatenating all such segments yields a **collision-free path**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Property | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of vertices | $n$ | Counted over all obstacles |
| 2 | Number of cells (best case) | $O(n)$ | Vertices spread out |
| 3 | Number of cells (worst case) | $O(n^2)$ | Adversarial alignment |
| 4 | Edges in adjacency graph | $O(n)$ | Each cell has bounded valence |
| 5 | Time complexity of decomposition | $O(n \log n)$ | Plane sweep variant |
| 6 | Time complexity (naive ray casting) | $O(n^2)$ | Each ray tests $O(n)$ edges |
| 7 | Graph search (A*) | $O(E \log V)$ | $V, E$ = nodes/edges of $G$ |
| 8 | Per-cell motion | Straight line | Convexity property |
| 9 | Path optimality | Not guaranteed | Requires post-processing (e.g., shortcutting) |
| 10 | Workspace assumption | Polygonal, bounded | $\mathcal{W} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the $O(n^2)$ worst case — it is asked frequently!

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

Trapezoidal decomposition is widely used in:
- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** in warehouses — known floor layouts with rectangular shelving.
- **CNC Machining** — 2-D cutting paths around fixtures.
- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** — collision detection in PCB routing.
- **Video Game AI** — fast navmesh generation for NPC pathfinding.
- **Robotic Surgery Planning** — navigating around anatomical structures modeled as polygons.

## 2.4 Advantages & Limitations

| ✅ Advantages | ❌ Limitations |
|---|---|
| Conceptually simple & deterministic | Only works in 2-D (extensions to 3-D are non-trivial) |
| Convex cells → trivial local planning | Worst-case $O(n^2)$ cells for adversarial inputs |
| Complete for polygonal workspaces | Path is not necessarily optimal |
| Easy to implement | Vertical slicing ignores slope information |
| Low memory footprint | Does not scale well to high-dim configuration spaces |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cell Count (Worst-Case Analysis)

**Claim:** The maximum number of trapezoidal cells is $\Theta(n^2)$ for $n$ vertices.

**Proof Construction:**

Consider $k$ disjoint vertical line segments, each contributing **2 vertices** (one top, one bottom). This gives:

$$n = 2k \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \frac{n}{2}$$

For each segment, the vertical ray from its top vertex travels **upward to infinity** (or workspace ceiling), and the ray from its bottom vertex travels **downward to infinity** (or floor). However, between adjacent segments, the upward ray of segment $i$ and the downward ray of segment $i+1$ **never meet** if the segments are separated horizontally.

Now consider $k$ segments arranged so that for **every pair** $(i, j)$, one segment's top is at the same height as another segment's bottom. The vertical rays interleave, producing a "staircase" of crossings. Each pair of crossings creates a distinct trapezoid.

**Counting the cells:**

$$\text{Number of trapezoidal cells} = 1 + 2\binom{k}{1} + 2\binom{k}{2} - \text{(overlaps)}$$

For the **canonical worst case** (alternating vertical strips, often called the "ladders" example):

$$\text{Cells} = k(k-1) + 1 = \frac{n}{2}\left(\frac{n}{2} - 1\right) + 1 = \frac{n^2 - 2n + 4}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Cells}_{\max} = \Theta(n^2)$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Adjacency Graph Properties

Each trapezoid cell has exactly:
- **2 vertical sides** (left and right) — these are the decomposition lines.
- **2 non-vertical sides** (top and bottom) — these are obstacle edges or workspace boundary segments.

A vertical side of cell $T_i$ is shared with **exactly one neighboring cell** $T_j$ (the cell on the other side of the decomposition line). Therefore:

$$\text{Valence}(T_i) \leq 4$$

Summing the valences over all $m$ cells:

$$2 \cdot \vert E(G) \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \text{Valence}(T_i) \leq 4m$$

Thus:

$$\vert E(G) \vert \leq 2m = O(n^2)$$

However, in the typical (non-adversarial) case, the **average valence is much smaller (≈ 3)**, giving:

$$\vert E(G) \vert = O(n) \quad \text{(average case)}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cell Types in Trapezoidal Decomposition

| Cell Type | Geometry | Vertex Count | Vertical Sides | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Trapezoid** | 4-sided, 2 parallel vertical edges | 4 | 2 | Standard slice between two obstacles |
| **Right Triangle** | 3-sided, 1 vertical edge | 3 | 1 | At workspace boundary, vertex touches wall |
| **Left Triangle** | 3-sided, 1 vertical edge | 3 | 1 | Mirror of right triangle |
| **Rectangle** | 4-sided, all vertical/horizontal | 4 | 2 | Special case (parallel horizontal edges) |
| **Pentagonal Cell** | 5-sided, 2 vertical edges | 5 | 2 | Adjacent obstacles with offset vertices |
| **Degenerate Cell** | $\le 2$ area (measure zero) | varies | varies | Vertex with collinear neighbors (excluded) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The "trapezoid" name is slightly misleading — the cells are technically **trapezoidal** in the geometric sense (at least one pair of parallel sides), and verticality of the parallel sides is the convention.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example

**Setup:** Workspace $\mathcal{W} = [0, 10] \times [0, 10]$ with two rectangular obstacles:
- $O_1$: vertices at $(3, 2)$, $(3, 5)$, $(6, 5)$, $(6, 2)$
- $O_2$: vertices at $(7, 6)$, $(7, 9)$, $(9, 9)$, $(9, 6)$

**Step 1: Collect all 8 vertices.**

$$V = \{(3,2), (3,5), (6,5), (6,2), (7,6), (7,9), (9,9), (9,6)\}$$

**Step 2: Shoot vertical rays from each vertex.**

For $v = (3, 2)$: ray goes **up** from $y=2$ to $y=5$ (hits $O_1$ top edge), and **down** to $y=0$ (workspace floor).

For $v = (6, 2)$: ray goes **up** from $y=2$ to $y=5$ (hits $O_1$ top edge), and **down** to $y=0$ (workspace floor).

For $v = (6, 5)$: ray goes **up** to $y=10$ (workspace ceiling), and **down** to $y=2$ (hits $O_1$ bottom edge). The downward ray is already covered by the ray from $(6,2)$ — they are collinear, so the cell boundary is still a single line segment.

Similar analysis applies to all vertices of $O_2$.

**Step 3: Enumerate cells.**

- Cell $T_1$: $x \in [0, 3]$, $y \in [0, 2]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_2$: $x \in [0, 3]$, $y \in [2, 5]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_3$: $x \in [0, 3]$, $y \in [5, 10]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_4$: $x \in [3, 6]$, $y \in [0, 2]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_5$: $x \in [3, 6]$, $y \in [5, 10]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_6$: $x \in [6, 7]$, $y \in [0, 6]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_7$: $x \in [6, 7]$, $y \in [6, 10]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_8$: $x \in [7, 9]$, $y \in [0, 6]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_9$: $x \in [7, 9]$, $y \in [6, 9]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_{10}$: $x \in [7, 9]$, $y \in [9, 10]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_{11}$: $x \in [9, 10]$, $y \in [0, 6]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_{12}$: $x \in [9, 10]$, $y \in [6, 9]$ → **Rectangle**
- Cell $T_{13}$: $x \in [9, 10]$, $y \in [9, 10]$ → **Rectangle**

Wait — let me recount. The vertical lines at $x = 0, 3, 6, 7, 9, 10$ create 5 vertical strips. Within each strip, the horizontal cuts from the obstacle edges create the sub-cells.

**Total cells** = 13 cells (all rectangles in this regular example).

**Step 4: Adjacency graph.** Each cell shares a vertical edge with its horizontal neighbor, and possibly a horizontal edge with a vertical neighbor. The resulting graph is a 2-D grid-like structure with obstacle-shaped holes.

**Step 5: Path from $(1,1)$ to $(8, 8)$.**

Start cell: $T_1$. Goal cell: $T_9$.

Graph search (e.g., A*) yields: $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_5 \rightarrow T_7 \rightarrow T_9$.

**Step 6: Path concatenation.** Within each cell, move in a straight line between the midpoints of the entry and exit boundaries.

## 3.5 Derivation of Naive Algorithm Complexity

**Naive approach:** For each of $n$ vertices, cast a vertical ray upward and downward. Each ray must be tested against all obstacle edges (and workspace boundary) to find the first intersection.

For one ray:

$$T_{\text{ray}} = O(n)$$

For all $n$ vertices (2 rays each):

$$T_{\text{decomp}} = 2n \cdot O(n) = O(n^2)$$

**Optimized approach (plane sweep):** Sort vertices by x-coordinate first, then sweep a vertical line from left to right. Maintain an event-driven structure.

$$T_{\text{decomp}}^{\text{opt}} = O(n \log n)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Trapezoidal Decomposition Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Polygonal Workspace] --> B[Extract All Obstacle Vertices V]
    B --> C[For each vertex v in V]
    C --> D[Cast Vertical Ray UP from v]
    C --> E[Cast Vertical Ray DOWN from v]
    D --> F[Ray Stops at: Obstacle Edge OR Workspace Boundary]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Decomposition Lines Partition Free Space]
    G --> H[Cells: Trapezoids and Triangles]
    H --> I[Build Adjacency Graph G = Vc, Ec]
    I --> J[Locate Start and Goal Cells]
    J --> K[Run A-star or BFS on G]
    K --> L[Sequence of Cells Returned]
    L --> M[Connect Cell Midpoints by Straight Lines]
    M --> N[Final Collision-Free Path]
    N --> O[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cell Adjacency Graph Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[T1: Bottom-Left Rectangle] --- node2[T2: Middle-Left Rectangle]
    node1 --- node4[T4: Below Obstacle 1]
    node2 --- node3[T3: Top-Left Rectangle]
    node2 --- node5[T5: Above Obstacle 1]
    node4 --- node6[T6: Between O1 and O2, Lower]
    node5 --- node7[T7: Between O1 and O2, Upper]
    node6 --- node8[T8: Below O2]
    node7 --- node9[T9: Inside O2 region]
    node6 --- node11[T11: Right of O2, Lower]
    node7 --- node12[T12: Right of O2, Upper]
    node8 --- node11
    node9 --- node12
    node9 --- node10[T10: Above O2]
    node10 --- node13[T13: Top-Right Corner]
    node11 --- node12
    node12 --- node13
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Architecture of Motion Planning via Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        W[Workspace Map W]
        O[Obstacle Set O1 to On]
        S[Start Configuration q_start]
        G[Goal Configuration q_goal]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Processing Layer]
        D[Trapezoidal Decomposition Module]
        R[Adjacency Graph Builder]
        P[Graph Search Planner A-star]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        C[Cell Sequence T1 to Tk]
        PT[Concatenated Path Tau]
        V[Validation: Collision-Free Check]
    end

    W --> D
    O --> D
    D --> R
    R --> P
    S --> P
    G --> P
    P --> C
    C --> PT
    PT --> V
    V --> Result[Valid Path or REPLAN]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Vertical Ray Shooting Illustration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vertex v at x equals 3, y equals 2] -->|Ray UP| B[Stop at Obstacle Edge y equals 5]
    A -->|Ray DOWN| C[Stop at Workspace Floor y equals 0]
    B --> D[Decomposition Line Segments Formed]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Two Trapezoidal Cells Created: Above and Below v]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define trapezoidal decomposition in robot motion planning. Mention two advantages of using this method.**

**Model Answer:**

Trapezoidal decomposition is an exact cell decomposition method in which vertical lines are extended upward and downward from every obstacle vertex until they encounter another obstacle or the workspace boundary. The resulting free-space cells are trapezoids (or triangles in degenerate cases), each of which is convex.

**Two advantages:**
1. Because every cell is convex, point-to-point motion within a cell reduces to a **straight-line trajectory**, eliminating the need for local planning inside a cell.
2. The method is **deterministic and complete** for polygonal workspaces — it always produces a valid roadmap if one exists.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Why is trapezoidal decomposition preferred over rectangular grid decomposition in known environments?**

**Model Answer:**

In a known environment with polygonal obstacles, trapezoidal decomposition adapts to the **actual obstacle geometry** rather than imposing a uniform grid. This leads to:
- **Fewer cells** for sparse obstacle layouts (since cells are only created where vertices exist).
- **No unnecessary cells** in large open regions (unlike grids which always tile the entire workspace).
- The cells remain **convex**, ensuring local planning is trivial.

In contrast, rectangular grid decomposition wastes resolution in open spaces and creates many small cells even in regions far from obstacles.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the trapezoidal decomposition algorithm for a 2-D polygonal workspace. List the steps clearly and describe how the adjacency graph is constructed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, illustrate the worst-case scenario for trapezoidal decomposition. Derive the order of the number of cells produced.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Trapezoidal Decomposition Algorithm:**

1. **Input Processing:** Accept a polygonal workspace $\mathcal{W}$ bounded by a polygon, with $n$ polygonal obstacles $O_1, O_2, \dots, O_n$.

2. **Vertex Collection:** Compile the complete list of vertices $V$ of all obstacles.

3. **Vertical Decomposition:** For each vertex $v_i \in V$:
   - Extend a vertical line segment **upward** from $v_i$ until it hits an obstacle edge or the workspace boundary.
   - Extend a vertical line segment **downward** from $v_i$ until it hits an obstacle edge or the workspace boundary.

4. **Cell Formation:** The vertical segments, together with obstacle edges and the workspace boundary, form a set of convex polygonal cells (trapezoids and triangles).

5. **Adjacency Graph Construction:** Create a graph $G = (V_c, E_c)$ where:
   - $V_c$ = set of all cells.
   - Two cells share an edge in $E_c$ iff they have a common non-zero-length boundary segment.

6. **Query Processing:** For a path query $(q_{\text{start}}, q_{\text{goal}})$:
   - Find the cells containing $q_{\text{start}}$ and $q_{\text{goal}}$.
   - Search the adjacency graph using A* (with Euclidean distance heuristic).
   - Return a sequence of cells.

7. **Path Realization:** Within each cell, move in a straight line from the centroid of the entry edge to the centroid of the exit edge. Concatenate these straight-line segments.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Worst-Case Example (the "Ladder" configuration):**

Consider $k$ vertical line obstacles (ladders) of equal height, arranged in a row. Each ladder has 2 vertices. Adjacent ladders are placed at slightly offset heights so that the upward ray from one vertex crosses the downward ray of an adjacent vertex.

**Cell count derivation:**

The number of vertices is $n = 2k$. With $k$ ladders arranged in the worst case, the number of trapezoidal cells produced is:

$$N_{\text{cells}} = 1 + 2\binom{k}{1} + 2\binom{k}{2} - (\text{degenerate overlaps})$$

For a clean worst case without degeneracies:

$$N_{\text{cells}} = k(k-1) + 1 = \frac{n}{2}\left(\frac{n}{2} - 1\right) + 1 = \frac{n^2 - 2n + 4}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$N_{\text{cells}} = \Theta(n^2) \quad \text{(worst case)}$$

This confirms the upper bound on the algorithm's space complexity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare exact cell decomposition and approximate cell decomposition methods. Discuss where trapezoidal decomposition fits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given a workspace with 2 rectangular obstacles, manually apply the trapezoidal decomposition and draw the cells. State the time complexity of the naive algorithm and the optimized plane-sweep variant.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Exact Cell Decomposition | Approximate Cell Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| Cell shape | Matches obstacle geometry (e.g., trapezoids) | Fixed shape (e.g., grid squares) |
| Cell count | Depends on obstacle complexity ($O(n)$ to $O(n^2)$) | Fixed by grid resolution $r$ ($O(1/r^2)$) |
| Completeness | Complete for polygonal obstacles | Resolution-complete only |
| Path quality | Smoother, follows obstacle contours | Blocky, staircase artifacts |
| Adaptivity | Adapts to obstacle density | Uniform across workspace |
| Best for | Known, structured environments | Unknown or complex environments |

**Trapezoidal Decomposition is an EXACT cell decomposition method** because the cell boundaries are derived directly from the obstacle geometry (the vertex positions) rather than from a pre-imposed grid. It is one of the simplest exact methods because it requires only vertical line extensions, making it computationally efficient compared to more complex exact decompositions like Boustrophedon or Morse-based decompositions.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Workspace:** $[0, 10] \times [0, 10]$ with two rectangles:
- $O_1$ at corners $(2,1), (2,4), (5,4), (5,1)$
- $O_2$ at corners $(6,5), (6,8), (9,8), (9,5)$

**Trapezoidal Decomposition (manual trace):**

Distinct x-coordinates of vertices: $\{0, 2, 5, 6, 9, 10\}$.
Distinct vertical lines: $x = 0, 2, 5, 6, 9, 10$.

Each strip between two consecutive x-lines is sliced by the y-coordinates of obstacle edges. For example, in the strip $x \in [5, 6]$:
- Below $O_1$ ($y \in [0, 1]$): one rectangle.
- Between $O_1$ top and $O_2$ bottom ($y \in [4, 5]$): one rectangle.
- Above $O_2$ ($y \in [8, 10]$): one rectangle.

**Total cells produced:** 13 cells (all rectangles in this regular example).

**Time complexity:**
- **Naive algorithm** (each ray tests all edges): $O(n^2)$
- **Optimized plane-sweep** algorithm: $O(n \log n)$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**  
> Students often confuse the **number of cells** with the **number of graph nodes**. In trapezoidal decomposition, the **cells = graph nodes**, and the cell count is $O(n^2)$ worst case. Don't state "$O(n)$ cells" in adversarial cases — this is a common mark-loss area.  
>  
> Also, students forget to **build the adjacency graph** before searching — A* cannot run on a partition alone. Mention the roadmap explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Trapezoidal Decomposition**

- **Definition:** Vertical lines are cast from every obstacle vertex up and down to form convex trapezoidal cells.
- **Cell Shape:** Trapezoid (2 parallel vertical sides) or Triangle (degenerate case).
- **Convexity Property:** Every cell is convex → straight-line local planning is always valid.
- **Adjacency Graph:** Nodes = cells, Edges = shared boundary segments. Used for global path search.
- **Cell Count:** $O(n)$ average case, **$O(n^2)$ worst case** (e.g., adversarial "ladder" configuration).
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ naive, $O(n \log n)$ plane-sweep optimized.
- **Graph Search:** A* with Euclidean heuristic is the standard choice.
- **Path Concatenation:** Move from boundary midpoint to boundary midpoint in straight lines across cells.
- **Completeness:** Resolution-complete for polygonal workspaces with bounded vertex count.
- **Optimality:** **Not optimal** by default — the resulting path may be longer than the true shortest path; post-processing (e.g., path shortcutting) is needed.
- **Limitations:** 2-D only (extensions to 3-D are non-trivial), inefficient for highly complex obstacle geometries, ignores rotational configuration dimensions.
- **KTU Exam Focus Areas:**
  1. Algorithm steps (most frequently asked).
  2. Worst-case cell count derivation.
  3. Diagram-based manual decomposition.
  4. Comparison with grid and exact cell decomposition variants.
  5. Time complexity analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Morse Cell Decomposition
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Morse Cell Decomposition

# Morse Cell Decomposition – Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Morse Cell Decomposition** is a topological technique in robot motion planning that partitions a robot's **Configuration Space (C-space)** into a finite collection of non-overlapping, curvilinear polygonal cells using the critical points of a **Morse function** $f: \mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. Each cell corresponds to either a critical point (a 0-cell) or a region homeomorphic to an open disk (a k-cell for $k \geq 1$), making the resulting structure a **CW complex** suitable for graph-based path search.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** Morse Cell Decomposition belongs to the family of *exact* decomposition methods. Unlike approximate methods (e.g., grid decomposition), the cells preserve the true topology of $\mathcal{C}$-free, ensuring path completeness. It was formalized by mathematical theorists Morse and Milnor and adapted to robotics by Latombe and colleagues.

## 1.2 The Configuration Space – Prerequisite

The configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible configurations a robot can attain. For a mobile robot translating in a 2D plane, $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$. For a robot arm with $n$ joints, $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ or $S^1 \times \dots \times S^1$ (a torus). The **free space** $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is the subset of $\mathcal{C}$ where the robot does not collide with obstacles.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The Mountain Topographer

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – "Hiking the Mountain":**
> Imagine the C-free region as a vast hilly landscape where the altitude at every point is given by a smooth Morse function $f(x,y)$.
> - **Saddles** are mountain passes between two valleys. These are the *bottlenecks* the robot must cross to move between two large open regions.
> - **Peaks (maxima)** are isolated high points, often corresponding to configurations trapped in a "pocket" between obstacles.
> - **Valleys (minima)** are the deepest regions of free space – safe resting configurations.
> - **Slopes** are the ordinary regions where the robot can move freely.
>
> The cell decomposition algorithm **drains the landscape** by following the steepest descent from every point until it hits a local minimum. All points that drain into the same minimum form a **2-cell**. The saddles act as **doorways** (1-cells) connecting two such cells, and minima are **0-cells**. This converts a continuous terrain into a discrete graph of rooms and doors – exactly what a path-planner needs.

## 1.4 Defining a Morse Function – The Cornerstone

A smooth function $f: M \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined on a smooth manifold $M$ is called a **Morse function** if:

1. **Smoothness:** $f$ is $C^{\infty}$ (infinitely differentiable).
2. **Non-Degeneracy of Critical Points:** At every critical point $x \in M$ (where $\nabla f(x) = 0$), the **Hessian matrix** $H_f(x)$ is **non-singular**, i.e., $\det(H_f(x)) \neq 0$.

The **index** of a critical point $p$ is the number of negative eigenvalues of $H_f(p)$. This index is a topological invariant in the sense of the **Morse Inequalities** and determines the dimension of the descending cell attached at $p$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** The number of critical points of a Morse function on a compact manifold $M$ satisfies:
> $$c_k \geq b_k \quad \text{(Weak Morse Inequalities)}$$
> where $c_k$ is the number of critical points of index $k$ and $b_k$ is the $k$-th Betti number of $M$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gradient Flow and Cell Construction

The principal mechanism behind Morse cell decomposition is the **negative gradient flow** of the Morse function. For a point $q \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, the flow is the solution of the differential equation:

$$\frac{d\phi_t(q)}{dt} = -\nabla f(\phi_t(q)), \quad \phi_0(q) = q$$

The flow line $\phi_t(q)$ traces the path of steepest descent starting at $q$. As $t \rightarrow +\infty$, the flow line converges to a **local minimum** (a critical point of index **0**). As $t \rightarrow -\infty$, it converges to a critical point whose index equals the dimension of the cell.

### Algorithm: Building the Cell Complex

1. **Step 1 – Define $f$:** Choose a smooth Morse function on $\mathcal{C}$. A common choice is the *sum of squared distances* to all obstacle boundaries, or a carefully constructed polynomial bump function. The key requirement is that the restriction of $f$ to $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is Morse.
2. **Step 2 – Identify Critical Points:** Solve $\nabla f(x) = 0$ subject to $x \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$. Classify each critical point by computing the Hessian $H_f(x)$ and counting negative eigenvalues to determine its **Morse index** $k$.
3. **Step 3 – Compute the Gradient Flow:** Integrate the ODE $\dot{x} = -\nabla f(x)$ from sample points (or analytically) until the flow converges to a minimum.
4. **Step 4 – Define the k-Cells:**
   - A **0-cell** is assigned to each critical point of index 0 (a local minimum).
   - A **k-cell** for $k \geq 1$ is the set of all points in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ whose descending flow line terminates at a critical point of index $k$ (or whose flow passes through the descending manifold of a critical point of index $k$).
5. **Step 5 – Build the Adjacency Graph:** Two cells are adjacent if their closures share a (k-1)-dimensional face. This face corresponds to a critical point of index $k-1$ (a saddle or higher-index critical point).
6. **Step 6 – Graph Search:** Apply standard graph search (Dijkstra, A*) on the adjacency graph to find a collision-free path from start to goal.

## 2.2 Why "Morse"? – The Non-Degeneracy Condition

The non-degeneracy of critical points is essential because it guarantees:

- **Isolated Critical Points:** No two critical points can coincide or merge.
- **Stable Decomposition:** A small perturbation of $f$ does not change the number of cells, only their shapes.
- **Cell Structure:** Locally, the Morse Lemma guarantees a canonical quadratic form, which means each cell is homeomorphic to an open Euclidean ball $B^k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Morse Lemma (Canonical Form):** Near a non-degenerate critical point $p$ of index $k$, there exist local coordinates $(y_1, \dots, y_n)$ such that
> $$f(y) = f(p) - y_1^2 - y_2^2 - \dots - y_k^2 + y_{k+1}^2 + \dots + y_n^2$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Use / Meaning |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Morse function | $f: \mathcal{C} \to \mathbb{R}$, $C^{\infty}$ | Provides "height" on C-space |
| 2 | Critical point | $\nabla f(x) = 0$ | Vertex of the cell complex |
| 3 | Hessian matrix | $H_f(x) = \left[ \partial^2 f / \partial x_i \partial x_j \right]$ | Determines critical point type |
| 4 | Morse index | $\lambda(p) = $ # of negative eigenvalues of $H_f(p)$ | Dimension of the descending cell |
| 5 | Gradient flow | $\dot{\phi}_t(q) = -\nabla f(\phi_t(q))$ | Traces steepest descent |
| 6 | Morse Lemma | $f = f(p) - \sum_{i=1}^{\lambda} y_i^2 + \sum_{i=\lambda+1}^{n} y_i^2$ | Canonical local form |
| 7 | Weak Morse Ineq. | $c_k \geq b_k$ | Lower bound on # critical points |
| 8 | Euler Characteristic | $\chi(M) = \sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^k c_k$ | Topological invariant |
| 9 | Descending manifold | $W^s(p) = \{q \mid \lim_{t \to \infty}\phi_t(q) = p\}$ | The k-cell around critical point $p$ |
| 10 | Adjacency | $\overline{C_i} \cap \overline{C_j} \neq \emptyset$ | Edge in search graph |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on absolute values in formulas:** When using norms or determinants such as $\vert \det H_f \vert$ in table cells, prefer the LaTeX form `\vert \det H_f \vert` to avoid breaking markdown pipe syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Morse cell decomposition is applied in:

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs):** Planning paths in warehouses (Locus Robotics, Kiva Systems).
- **Surgical Robotics:** Motion planning in confined anatomical spaces where C-space has intricate topology.
- **Drone Swarms:** Multi-agent planning where each agent's joint C-space is a high-dimensional manifold.
- **Computer-Aided Design (CAD):** Path planning for CNC milling, where the Morse function can encode tool-clearance constraints.
- **Protein Folding Research:** Conformational analysis where the energy landscape is treated as a Morse function.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Flow Convergence

We analyze the stability of the gradient flow near a non-degenerate critical point and show why the descending cell is homeomorphic to an open disk.

### Step 1 – Energy Monotonicity along the Flow

Consider the Lyapunov candidate $V(q) = f(q)$. Its time derivative along the trajectory is:

$$\frac{d}{dt} f(\phi_t(q)) = \nabla f(\phi_t(q)) \cdot \frac{d\phi_t(q)}{dt} = \nabla f \cdot (-\nabla f) = -\vert \nabla f \vert^2 \leq 0$$

This shows that $f$ decreases monotonically along every flow line, with strict decrease away from critical points. The flow is therefore a **gradient descent** on $f$.

### Step 2 – Local Linearization at a Non-Degenerate Critical Point

Let $p$ be a critical point of index $k$. By the Morse Lemma, near $p$ we can write:

$$f(y) = f(p) - y_1^2 - y_2^2 - \dots - y_k^2 + y_{k+1}^2 + \dots + y_n^2$$

The gradient in these canonical coordinates is:

$$\nabla f(y) = (-2y_1, \dots, -2y_k, 2y_{k+1}, \dots, 2y_n)$$

### Step 3 – Solving the Linearized Flow

The flow ODE in canonical coordinates decouples into $n$ independent scalar equations:

$$\frac{dy_i}{dt} = \begin{cases} 2y_i, & i = 1, \dots, k \quad \text{(unstable direction, repelling)} \\ -2y_i, & i = k+1, \dots, n \quad \text{(stable direction, attracting)} \end{cases}$$

The general solution is:

$$y_i(t) = \begin{cases} y_i(0)\, e^{2t}, & i = 1, \dots, k \\ y_i(0)\, e^{-2t}, & i = k+1, \dots, n \end{cases}$$

### Step 4 – Identifying the Descending Manifold

The descending manifold $W^s(p)$ consists of all initial conditions whose forward flow converges to $p$. For convergence as $t \to +\infty$, we require the unstable components to be zero:

$$y_1(0) = y_2(0) = \dots = y_k(0) = 0$$

This restricts the initial condition to a $(n-k)$-dimensional affine subspace. The free stable coordinates $(y_{k+1}, \dots, y_n)$ can take any value near zero, so $W^s(p)$ is locally diffeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^{n-k}$.

### Step 5 – Identifying the Attaching k-Cell

The **attaching k-cell** of index $k$ is defined as the quotient:

$$C_k = W^u(p) \setminus \{p\} \simeq D^k \setminus \{0\} \simeq \mathbb{R}^k \setminus \{0\}$$

where $W^u(p)$ is the **unstable manifold** of dimension $k$. The set $W^u(p)$ is precisely the cell of index $k$ in the CW complex decomposition of $M$. Hence, the **Morse index equals the cell dimension** – a foundational result.

### Step 6 – Worked Example: 2D Configuration Space

Consider $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathbb{R}^2$ with Morse function:

$$f(x,y) = x^3 - 3xy^2$$

Compute the critical points by setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 3x^2 - 3y^2 = 0 \implies x^2 = y^2$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = -6xy = 0 \implies xy = 0$$

The simultaneous solution yields the single critical point at $(0,0)$. The Hessian is:

$$H_f = \begin{bmatrix} 6x & -6y \\ -6y & -6x \end{bmatrix} \Bigg|_{(0,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This Hessian is singular, so $f(x,y) = x^3 - 3xy^2$ is **not** a Morse function. The origin is a *degenerate* (monkey-saddle) critical point. This illustrates why the non-degeneracy condition is crucial.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Common Morse Function Choices

| Morse Function Type | Formula $f(q)$ | Typical Use | Computation Cost |
|---------------------|----------------|-------------|------------------|
| Distance-squared sum | $f(q) = \sum_i d^2(q, \partial \mathcal{C}_{obs,i})$ | General 2D/3D | $O(n)$ per gradient eval |
| Trigonometric | $f(q) = \sum_i \sin(k_i q_i)$ | Toroidal C-spaces | $O(n)$ |
| Polynomial bumps | $f(q) = \prod_i (1 - g_i(q))$ | Tunable landscape | $O(n^2)$ |
| Weighted Laplacian | $f = q^T L q$ | Articulated robots | $O(n^2)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Morse Cell Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Configuration Space C-free and Obstacles] --> B[Step 1: Construct Smooth Morse Function f on C]
    B --> C[Step 2: Locate All Critical Points solve grad f equals 0]
    C --> D[Step 3: Classify Critical Points by Index via Hessian Eigenvalue Count]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute Gradient Descent Flow phi t equals minus grad f]
    E --> F[Step 5: Partition C-free into k-cells via Flow Convergence]
    F --> G[Step 6: Build CW Complex and Adjacency Graph G = V E]
    G --> H[Step 7: Apply Graph Search A star or Dijkstra from q-start to q-goal]
    H --> I[Output: Sequence of Cells defining Collision-Free Path]
    H --> J[Output: Continuous Path in C-free via Interpolation]

    subgraph CriticalPointPool[Critical Point Inventory]
        K0[Index 0: Minima, 0-cells] --> L0[Stable attractors of flow]
        K1[Index 1: Saddles, 1-cells] --> L1[Doorways between 2-cells]
        K2[Index 2: Maxima, 2-cells] --> L2[Local peaks in C-free]
    end

    D --> CriticalPointPool
    CriticalPointPool --> F
```

## 4.2 Schematic – Gradient Flow Topology on a 2D C-space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FreeSpace[C-free Region with Two Valleys]
        A1[Local Minima: Index 0]:::minNode
        A2[Saddle Point: Index 1]:::saddleNode
        A3[Local Minima: Index 0]:::minNode
        A4[Maxima: Index 2]:::maxNode
    end

    A1 ---|Descending 2-cell| A2
    A2 ---|Descending 2-cell| A3
    A4 -.->|Boundary of free space| A2

    classDef minNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef saddleNode fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#b08900,color:#000
    classDef maxNode fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Adjacency Graph Representation

```mermaid
graph TD
    StartNode((q-start)) --- C0A((Cell A: 0-cell, Min))
    C0A --- S1((Saddle 1: 1-cell))
    S1 --- C0B((Cell B: 0-cell, Min))
    C0B --- S2((Saddle 2: 1-cell))
    S2 --- C0C((Cell C: 0-cell, Min))
    C0C --- GoalNode((q-goal))

    S1 -.Detour.-> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two-cell "rooms" $A$, $B$, $C$ are connected by "doorways" $S_1$, $S_2$ (saddle points). A graph search traverses the 0-cells (vertices) and crosses the 1-cells (edges) to reach the goal. This discrete search is the key advantage of Morse decomposition.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Morse function. State the Morse Lemma and explain its significance in cell decomposition.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Morse function** is a smooth ($C^{\infty}$) real-valued function $f: M \to \mathbb{R}$ defined on a smooth manifold $M$ such that every critical point of $f$ is **non-degenerate**, meaning the Hessian matrix $H_f(x)$ has non-zero determinant at every critical point.

**Morse Lemma:** Near any non-degenerate critical point $p$ of index $\lambda$, there exist local coordinates $(y_1, \dots, y_n)$ such that
$$f(y) = f(p) - y_1^2 - y_2^2 - \dots - y_{\lambda}^2 + y_{\lambda+1}^2 + \dots + y_n^2$$

**Significance:** The lemma guarantees that locally the function has a *quadratic canonical form*. This means that (i) critical points are isolated, (ii) cells attached at a critical point of index $\lambda$ are homeomorphic to a $\lambda$-dimensional open disk, and (iii) the number of such cells equals the number of critical points of that index. This converts the continuous problem of motion planning into a discrete graph-search problem, which is the foundation of Morse cell decomposition.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Morse index of a critical point? How is it related to the dimension of the cell attached at that point in the decomposition?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Morse index** $\lambda(p)$ of a critical point $p$ is the number of **negative eigenvalues** of the Hessian matrix $H_f(p)$. Equivalently, it is the dimension of the **maximal subspace** on which the Hessian is negative definite.

**Relation to Cell Dimension:** In Morse cell decomposition, the cell attached at a critical point $p$ of index $\lambda$ is a $\lambda$-dimensional cell. The descending 2-cell of a minimum ($\lambda = 0$) is a 0-cell (a vertex); the descending manifold of a saddle ($\lambda = 1$) yields a 1-cell (an edge); and so on. This one-to-one correspondence between critical-point index and cell dimension is what makes Morse decomposition a **CW complex** decomposition of the configuration space.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the steps involved in constructing a Morse cell decomposition of a robot's free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. Use a labeled diagram to illustrate the concept of a saddle point and the descending/ascending manifolds.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a 2D configuration space with Morse function
$$f(x,y) = (x-1)^2 + (y-2)^2 + 2(x-1)(y-2)^2$$
Find the critical points, classify them, and indicate the resulting cell structure.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 – Define the Configuration Space and Free Region:**
Identify the obstacles in the workspace and inflate them by the robot's geometry to obtain $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$. Then $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$.

**Step 2 – Construct a Morse Function:**
Choose a smooth function $f$ on $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ whose critical points are non-degenerate. A common practical choice is
$$f(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} d^2(q, \partial \mathcal{C}_{obs,i})$$
the sum of squared distances to each obstacle boundary.

**Step 3 – Locate Critical Points:**
Solve the system $\nabla f(q) = 0$. Each solution $p$ is a critical point. Use numerical root-finders (Newton-Raphson) or analytical methods.

**Step 4 – Classify Critical Points:**
At each critical point $p$, compute the Hessian
$$H_f(p) = \left[ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial q_i \partial q_j} \right]_{q=p}$$
The number of negative eigenvalues is the **Morse index** $\lambda(p)$.

**Step 5 – Compute the Gradient Flow:**
For every point $q \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, integrate the flow $\dot{q} = -\nabla f(q)$ until it terminates at a critical point. The set of all initial conditions that flow to a critical point $p$ of index $\lambda$ form an open $\lambda$-cell.

**Step 6 – Construct the Adjacency Graph:**
Two cells are adjacent iff their closures share a common face. The graph nodes are the cells; the edges represent shared boundaries.

**Step 7 – Search the Graph:**
Apply A* or Dijkstra's algorithm on the graph. Once a cell-sequence is found, generate a smooth path by interpolation within each cell.

#### Model Solution to (b)

Compute the partial derivatives of $f(x,y) = (x-1)^2 + (y-2)^2 + 2(x-1)(y-2)^2$:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2(x-1) + 2(y-2)^2$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 2(y-2) + 4(x-1)(y-2)$$

Set both to zero:

$$2(x-1) + 2(y-2)^2 = 0 \implies (x-1) = -(y-2)^2$$
$$2(y-2)\left[1 + 2(x-1)\right] = 0 \implies y = 2 \quad \text{or} \quad x = 1/2$$

**Case 1:** $y = 2$. Then from the first equation, $x - 1 = 0 \implies x = 1$. Critical point $P_1 = (1, 2)$.

**Case 2:** $x = 1/2$. Then $x - 1 = -1/2$, so $(y-2)^2 = 1/2$, giving $y = 2 \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. Two additional critical points:
$P_2 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, 2 + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ and $P_3 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, 2 - \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$.

**Classification by Hessian:**

$$H_f = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4(y-2) \\ 4(y-2) & 2 + 4(x-1) \end{bmatrix}$$

At $P_1 = (1,2)$:
$$H_f(P_1) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det = 4 > 0, \text{ both eigenvalues } = +2$$
This is a **local minimum (index 0)** → 0-cell.

At $P_2 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, 2 + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$, with $y - 2 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:
$$H_f(P_2) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4/\sqrt{2} \\ 4/\sqrt{2} & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2\sqrt{2} \\ 2\sqrt{2} & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
$\det = 0 - 8 = -8 < 0$. Eigenvalues have opposite signs → **saddle (index 1)** → 1-cell.

At $P_3 = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}, 2 - \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$, with $y - 2 = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:
$$H_f(P_3) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2\sqrt{2} \\ -2\sqrt{2} & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$
$\det = -8 < 0$ → **saddle (index 1)** → 1-cell.

**Cell Structure:** One 0-cell ($P_1$, the minimum), two 1-cells (saddles $P_2$ and $P_3$), and the unstable manifolds of the saddles partition the 2D free space into descending 2-cells all flowing into $P_1$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain how the adjacency graph in Morse cell decomposition is constructed. Why is this graph guaranteed to be connected if $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is connected?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Weak Morse Inequalities** and use them to justify why the number of cells in a decomposition is at least the Betti number of the manifold.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Construction of the Adjacency Graph:**

Let $\{C_0, C_1, \dots, C_N\}$ be the cells produced by the Morse decomposition. Define a graph $G = (V, E)$ with:

- $V = \{v_i\}$ where each $v_i$ represents a cell $C_i$.
- An undirected edge $(v_i, v_j) \in E$ if and only if the closures of $C_i$ and $C_j$ intersect along a face of codimension 1. Formally,
$$(v_i, v_j) \in E \iff \dim(\overline{C_i} \cap \overline{C_j}) = \max(\dim C_i, \dim C_j) - 1$$

The **codimension-1 condition** is the standard CW-complex adjacency rule and corresponds geometrically to the requirement that the cells share a (k-1)-dimensional boundary, which in Morse theory is the descending manifold of a critical point of index one less.

**Connectivity Proof Sketch:**

1. Each cell $C_i$ is the descending flow image of an open neighborhood around a critical point. Hence every point in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ belongs to exactly one cell.
2. The descending flow of any point $q$ in $C_i$ terminates at a critical point of index $\dim C_i$. The cell $C_i$ is "rooted" at this critical point.
3. The unstable manifold of a critical point $p$ of index $k$ is a $k$-disk whose boundary is a $(k-1)$-sphere. The boundary components lie in cells of lower dimension. Hence a path in $G$ from $C_i$ to $C_j$ corresponds to a continuous path in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ from any point in $C_i$ to any point in $C_j$ that crosses cell-boundaries only at lower-dimensional faces.
4. Since $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is connected, such a continuous path exists for every pair of points, and its cell-sequence is a path in $G$. Therefore $G$ is connected.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Statement of the Weak Morse Inequalities:**

For a smooth, compact manifold $M$ and a Morse function $f: M \to \mathbb{R}$ with $c_k$ critical points of index $k$, and $b_k$ the $k$-th Betti number of $M$ (the rank of $H_k(M; \mathbb{R})$):

$$c_k \geq b_k \quad \text{for all } k = 0, 1, \dots, n$$

The **Strong Morse Inequalities** refine this to:
$$c_k - c_{k-1} + c_{k-2} - \dots + (-1)^k c_0 \geq b_k - b_{k-1} + \dots + (-1)^k b_0$$
for all $k$.

**Justification via Cellular Homology:**

The Morse cell decomposition is a CW-complex structure on $M$. The $k$-cells are precisely the descending disks of critical points of index $k$. By cellular homology, the $k$-th Betti number $b_k$ equals the rank of the cellular chain complex
$$\dots \to C_{k+1} \to C_k \to C_{k-1} \to \dots$$
The boundary map $\partial_k: C_k \to C_{k-1}$ has matrix entries given by the **incidence numbers** of the cell complex. Since $b_k = \dim(\ker \partial_k) - \dim(\operatorname{im} \partial_{k+1})$ and $\dim C_k = c_k$, the rank-nullity theorem gives $b_k \leq c_k$. The inequality is strict in general because some $k$-cells are "redundant" – they fill in higher-dimensional holes.

**Engineering Implication:** In a 2D C-space, the minimum number of cells is dictated by $b_0 = 1$ (one connected component) and $b_1$ = number of independent loops. The decomposition must contain at least $b_1$ saddle points to "kill" the 1-dimensional holes, and the descending 2-cells fill the rest.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Morse Index with Cell Count:** A common error is stating that a saddle point "creates two cells." In reality, a single index-1 critical point attaches one 1-cell whose boundary is a 0-sphere with two endpoints, so it indeed creates one edge but may merge multiple regions.
> 2. **Forgetting Non-Degeneracy:** Many students define critical points by $\nabla f = 0$ alone and forget the Hessian non-singularity. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for omitting the non-degeneracy condition.
> 3. **Mixing Up 0-cell and 2-cell:** A local minimum is a **0-cell** (a single point, vertex of the graph), not a 2-cell. The 2-cell is the *basin* of attraction flowing into the minimum. This distinction is frequently tested.
> 4. **In Worked Problems:** Always show the Hessian calculation, its determinant, and the sign of the eigenvalues explicitly. Skipping any of these steps costs 2-3 marks in Part B.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Morse Function:** Smooth $f$ with non-degenerate critical points (Hessian non-singular).
- **Critical Point Types in 2D:** Minima (index 0), Saddles (index 1), Maxima (index 2).
- **Morse Index = Negative Eigenvalues of Hessian = Dimension of Attached Cell.**
- **Morse Lemma:** Local canonical form is $f = f(p) - \sum_{i=1}^{\lambda} y_i^2 + \sum_{i=\lambda+1}^n y_i^2$.
- **Gradient Flow:** $\dot{q} = -\nabla f(q)$ always descends; terminates at index-0 critical points.
- **Cell = Basin of Attraction:** All points whose flow line ends at the same critical point form one cell.
- **Adjacency:** Codimension-1 shared boundary; graph nodes are cells, edges are shared faces.
- **Weak Morse Inequality:** $c_k \geq b_k$ – number of cells of dimension $k$ is at least the $k$-th Betti number.
- **Euler Characteristic:** $\chi(M) = \sum_{k=0}^n (-1)^k c_k = \sum_{k=0}^n (-1)^k b_k$.
- **Algorithmic Pipeline:** Define $f$ → find critical points → classify by index → compute flow → build cells → build adjacency graph → graph search → interpolate path.
- **Advantages:** Exact (preserves topology), complete (finds path if one exists), resolution-complete as cells refine.
- **Disadvantages:** Hard to construct Morse functions in high-dimensional C-spaces; sensitive to numerical integration of gradient flow.
- **Practical Function Choice:** Sum of squared distances to obstacle boundaries is the most commonly used in industrial applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boustrophedon Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boustrophedon Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Boustrophedon Decomposition** is an exact cell decomposition technique used in **robot motion planning** (specifically for **coverage path planning**), introduced by **Choset and Pignon (1998)**. It generalizes the classical **trapezoidal decomposition** by allowing the *slice line* to terminate not only at obstacle boundaries but also at *critical points* — locations where the slice line simultaneously touches **two or more obstacle vertices**.

The technique partitions the robot's **free configuration space (C-free)** into a set of non-overlapping, simply-connected **cells**, such that:
1. Each cell is bounded above and below by continuous obstacle edges or the workspace boundary.
2. Within any single cell, the robot can execute a complete **"back-and-forth"** sweeping motion (the *boustrophedon* path).
3. Adjacent cells share a common boundary (a **"door"** or **critical event**) through which the robot can transition.

The word **boustrophedon** (Greek: *bous* = ox, *strephein* = to turn) literally translates to **"the way of the ox,"** capturing the side-to-side turning motion of an ox plowing a field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Boustrophedon decomposition falls under the *Cell Decomposition Methods* module of Robot Motion Planning. It is the **direct extension of trapezoidal decomposition** and is heavily tested in both Part A (definitions) and Part B (algorithmic derivations).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Farmer's Field"

Imagine a **farmer plowing a rectangular field** that contains a few large rocks (obstacles). The farmer does not want to miss any patch of soil. The traditional ox-plowing strategy is:

1. Drive the oxen straight across the field (East).
2. At the far edge, turn the oxen **180°**.
3. Drive them back (West) along a line **parallel** to the previous one.
4. Repeat until the field is covered.

If a **rock** sits in the middle of the field, the farmer must lift the plow, **go around** the rock, and resume the parallel pattern on the other side. **Boustrophedon decomposition** mathematically automates this exact strategy:

- The **field** is the robot's free workspace.
- The **rocks** are the obstacles.
- The **plow lines** are the robot's back-and-forth trajectory.
- The **turns at the field's end** correspond to **critical points** where the slice line meets an obstacle vertex.

The decomposition ensures that the farmer (robot) covers **100% of the field** with a minimum number of awkward turning maneuvers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Boustrophedon" and not "Trapezoidal"?**
> In *trapezoidal decomposition*, the slice line always terminates at the *nearest* obstacle vertex, often creating many narrow cells. In *boustrophedon decomposition*, the slice line can pass through multiple cells until it hits a **critical point** (where two obstacle vertices align with the slice). This yields **fewer, larger cells** — making the robot's path far more efficient.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Note |
|---|---|---|
| $W$ | Workspace (bounded region in $\mathbb{R}^2$) | Typically rectangular: $[0, X_{max}] \times [0, Y_{max}]$ |
