| $C_{obs}$ | Obstacle region (closed set) | Defined by polygonal boundaries |
| $C_{free}$ | Free configuration space | $W \setminus C_{obs}$ |
| $s(x)$ | Vertical slice line at abscissa $x$ | $s(x) = \{(x, y) \mid y \in \mathbb{R}\}$ |
| $x_v$ | Critical abscissa (event point) | $x_v$ = x-coordinate of obstacle vertex $v$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The boustrophedon decomposition algorithm operates as a **sweep-line process**. Below is the rigorous operational logic:

### **Step 1 — Initialization**
Define a vertical slice line $s(x)$ that will sweep the workspace from $x = x_{min}$ to $x = x_{max}$ (left to right). Initially, the slice is positioned at $x = -\infty$ (effectively, the leftmost edge of the workspace).

### **Step 2 — Identify "Critical Points"**
As the slice $s(x)$ moves rightward, monitor where it intersects **obstacle vertices** (corners of polygonal obstacles). A **critical point** $x_c$ is an abscissa where the slice line **simultaneously touches two or more obstacle vertices** — that is, where the topology (connectivity) of the free space intersected by the slice **changes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topological Event Classification:**
> - **Lower Vertex (V)** — both adjacent edges slope *downward*: the free-space connectivity on the slice **decreases** (two free intervals merge into one).
> - **Upper Vertex (Λ)** — both adjacent edges slope *upward*: the free-space connectivity on the slice **increases** (one free interval splits into two).
> - **Middle Vertex (|·|)** — one edge up, one down: connectivity is **preserved** (no event triggered).

### **Step 3 — Form Cells**
Between consecutive critical points (and from the workspace boundary to the first/last critical point), the **topology of the free space is invariant**. This maximal invariant region is declared a **cell**. Formally:

$$
\text{Cell}_i = \{(x, y) \in C_{free} \mid x_{c,i} \leq x \leq x_{c,i+1}\}
$$

Within each cell, the slice line intersects $C_{free}$ in a **constant number of continuous intervals**, meaning the robot's path-planning problem inside a cell reduces to a 1-D line-sweep.

### **Step 4 — Plan the Boustrophedon Path Inside Each Cell**
For a cell whose top boundary is the function $f_{top}(x)$ and whose bottom boundary is $f_{bot}(x)$, the robot executes the following infinite sequence of "passes" (idealized):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pass 1:} \quad & y = f_{top}(x), \quad x \in [x_{c,i}, x_{c,i+1}] \\
\text{Pass 2:} \quad & y = f_{bot}(x), \quad x \in [x_{c,i+1}, x_{c,i}] \\
\text{Pass 3:} \quad & y = f_{top}(x) - \Delta y, \quad \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\Delta y$ is the swath width of the robot's coverage tool.

### **Step 5 — Connect Cells via an "Adjacency Graph"**
Construct an **adjacency graph** $G = (V_G, E_G)$ where:
- $V_G$ = set of cells.
- $E_G$ = set of edges, one per shared boundary (door) between adjacent cells.

The robot's **global path** is computed by:
1. Performing a boustrophedon sweep inside each cell.
2. At the end of a cell's coverage, traversing to the nearest door.
3. Crossing the door into the adjacent cell.
4. Resuming the boustrophedon sweep in the new cell.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s(x) = \{(x, y) \in W \mid y \in \mathbb{R}\}$ | Vertical slice line at abscissa $x$ | — |
| 2 | $C_{free} = W \setminus C_{obs}$ | Free workspace | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 3 | $f_{top}(x), f_{bot}(x)$ | Upper / lower cell boundary functions | $\text{m}$ |
| 4 | $x_c \in \mathbb{R}$ | Critical abscissa (event point) | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | $v = (x_v, y_v)$ | Obstacle vertex coordinates | $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | $A_{cell} = \int_{x_{c,i}}^{x_{c,i+1}} [f_{top}(x) - f_{bot}(x)] \, dx$ | Area of a boustrophedon cell | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 7 | $L_{path} = N_{passes} \cdot L_{cell} + N_{turns} \cdot L_{turn}$ | Total path length inside a cell | $\text{m}$ |
| 8 | $N_{passes} = \left\lceil \dfrac{Y_{cell}}{\Delta y} \right\rceil$ | Number of back-and-forth passes | dimensionless |
| 9 | $N_{turns} = N_{passes} - 1$ | Number of U-turns per cell | dimensionless |
| 10 | $G = (V_G, E_G)$ | Cell adjacency graph | — |
| 11 | $\rho(\text{coverage}) = \dfrac{A_{covered}}{A_{cell}} \cdot 100\%$ | Coverage efficiency | $\% \in [0, 100]$ |
| 12 | $\Delta y$ | Robot's coverage swath width | $\text{m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Boustrophedon decomposition is the **de-facto standard** for autonomous floor-cleaning robots (e.g., *iRobot Roomba*, *Neato*), agricultural harvesting robots, autonomous lawn mowers, mine-sweeping UGVs, and search-and-rescue drones performing **area-coverage missions**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Point Identification

We wish to formally derive the condition that defines a *critical abscissa* $x_c$ for the sweep line $s(x)$.

### **Step 3.1.1 — Define the Workspace and Obstacles**

Let the workspace $W \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a bounded rectangular region:
$$
W = [0, X_{max}] \times [0, Y_{max}]
$$

Let the obstacle region $C_{obs}$ be a finite union of closed, bounded polygonal obstacles:
$$
C_{obs} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{N} P_k
$$

Each polygon $P_k$ has a finite set of vertices $V_k = \{v_{k,1}, v_{k,2}, \dots, v_{k,n_k}\}$, where each vertex has the form $v_{k,j} = (x_{k,j}, y_{k,j})$.

### **Step 3.1.2 — Define the Intersection of the Slice with Free Space**

For any abscissa $x$, the slice line is:
$$
s(x) = \{(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid y \in \mathbb{R}\}
$$

The intersection of $s(x)$ with the free space is the open set:
$$
F(x) = s(x) \cap C_{free} = s(x) \setminus C_{obs}
$$

The function $F(x)$ is a (possibly disconnected) union of open intervals on the y-axis.

### **Step 3.1.3 — Define a Topological Change**

A **critical abscissa** $x_c$ is any real number at which the **topology** (i.e., the number of connected components) of $F(x)$ changes:

$$
x_c \in \mathbb{R} \quad \text{is critical} \iff \lim_{\epsilon \to 0^+} \text{components}(F(x_c - \epsilon)) \neq \text{components}(F(x_c + \epsilon))
$$

### **Step 3.1.4 — Connect Critical Abscissas to Vertices**

By the **topological invariance theorem for slice-line decompositions**, a topological change in $F(x)$ can occur **only** at abscissas coinciding with an obstacle vertex:

$$
x_c = x_{k,j} \quad \text{for some obstacle } k \text{ and vertex index } j
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why?** A polygonal obstacle's interior is locally homeomorphic to an open disk; the only place a vertical line's intersection with the *boundary* of a polygon can change connectivity is at a vertex.

### **Step 3.1.5 — Final Critical Point Set**

Sorting all critical abscissas in increasing order gives the ordered event sequence:
$$
x_{c,0} < x_{c,1} < x_{c,2} < \dots < x_{c,M}
$$

where $M$ is the total number of critical events. The boustrophedon cells are the slabs $C_i = [x_{c,i-1}, x_{c,i}] \times \mathbb{R}$ intersected with $C_{free}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Path Length Inside a Single Boustrophedon Cell

Consider a single boustrophedon cell of width $W_c$ and variable height $H_c(x) = f_{top}(x) - f_{bot}(x)$. The robot must cover this cell with passes spaced $\Delta y$ apart.

### **Step 3.2.1 — Number of Passes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{passes} &= \left\lceil \frac{H_{c,max}}{\Delta y} \right\rceil \\
H_{c,max} &= \max_{x \in [x_{c,i-1}, x_{c,i}]} \left[ f_{top}(x) - f_{bot}(x) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3.2.2 — Length of a Single Pass**

Each pass is approximately a straight line of length $W_c$ (the cell width). For non-rectangular cells, the exact length of the $k^{th}$ pass is:

$$
L_k = \int_{x_{c,i-1}}^{x_{c,i}} \sqrt{1 + \left( \frac{df_k(x)}{dx} \right)^2} \, dx
$$

where $f_k(x)$ is the y-coordinate of the $k^{th}$ pass. In most textbook analyses, we approximate $L_k \approx W_c$, since the boundary functions are piecewise linear.

### **Step 3.2.3 — Total Sweep Length**

$$
L_{sweep} = N_{passes} \cdot W_c
$$

### **Step 3.2.4 — U-Turn Length**

At the end of each pass (except the last), the robot executes a 180° U-turn. The length of a single U-turn (assuming a circular arc of radius $r$) is:

$$
L_{turn} = \pi r
$$

### **Step 3.2.5 — Total Path Length Inside the Cell**

$$
L_{cell} = N_{passes} \cdot W_c + (N_{passes} - 1) \cdot \pi r
$$

### **Step 3.2.6 — Global Path Length**

For $K$ cells in the adjacency graph traversal:
$$
L_{global} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} L_{cell,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{K-1} L_{transit,j}
$$

where $L_{transit,j}$ is the length of the inter-cell transition path (e.g., navigating to a shared door and crossing it).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Trapezoidal vs. Boustrophedon

| Parameter | Trapezoidal Decomposition | Boustrophedon Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| Slice-line termination | At *nearest* obstacle vertex | At *critical* (multi-vertex) abscissas |
| Number of cells | $O(n)$ (high) | $O(n)$ but **fewer in practice** |
| Cell shape | Trapezoid | General convex polygon |
| Topological event considered | Every obstacle vertex | Only vertices with topology change |
| Suitability for coverage | Inefficient (many small cells) | **Optimal** (sparse, large cells) |
| Computational complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Path redundancy | High (many turn-overs) | **Low** (fewer transitions) |
| Inventor / Year | Latombe (1991) | **Choset & Pignon (1998)** |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A robot operates in $W = [0, 10] \times [0, 10]$. Two rectangular obstacles are present:
- $O_1$ with vertices $(2, 2), (4, 2), (4, 6), (2, 6)$.
- $O_2$ with vertices $(6, 4), (8, 4), (8, 8), (6, 8)$.

The robot's swath width is $\Delta y = 1\,\text{m}$. Find:
(a) The critical abscissas.
(b) The number of boustrophedon cells.
(c) The total path length, assuming a U-turn radius $r = 0.5\,\text{m}$.

### **Solution (a) — Critical Abscissas**

The vertex x-coordinates are: $2, 4, 6, 8$. We must check each for topological change.

- $x = 2$: Vertex $(2, 2)$ is a **lower vertex** (both adjacent edges slope *up* as we move right). Topological change? Looking at $F(1.9)$ vs $F(2.1)$ — at $x=2$, the obstacle *starts*, splitting one free interval into two. **YES — critical**.
- $x = 4$: Vertex $(4, 2)$ and $(4, 6)$ — two vertices at the *same abscissa*! The upper obstacle ends and the lower obstacle continues. At $x = 3.9$: two free intervals (above and below $O_1$). At $x = 4.1$: $O_1$ has ended, so the gap merges with the lower part. **YES — critical**.
- $x = 6$: Similar analysis. $O_2$ starts, splitting the free space. **YES — critical**.
- $x = 8$: $O_2$ ends, merging intervals. **YES — critical**.

Critical abscissas: $x_{c} \in \{2, 4, 6, 8\}$.

### **Solution (b) — Number of Cells**

Cells are the slabs:
- $C_1$: $[0, 2]$ (left of $O_1$)
- $C_2$: $[2, 4]$ (split by $O_1$)
- $C_3$: $[4, 6]$ (between $O_1$ and $O_2$)
- $C_4$: $[6, 8]$ (split by $O_2$)
- $C_5$: $[8, 10]$ (right of $O_2$)

**Total: 5 cells** (after accounting for the splitting at $C_2$ and $C_4$).

### **Solution (c) — Total Path Length**

Width of each cell: $W_c = 2\,\text{m}$ (for $C_1, C_5$), or $W_c = 2\,\text{m}$ (for $C_2, C_3, C_4$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{total} &= 5 \cdot L_{cell} + 4 \cdot L_{transit} \\
&\approx 5 \cdot (N_{passes} \cdot 2 + (N_{passes} - 1) \cdot \pi \cdot 0.5) + \text{transit terms}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $C_1$ (full height $10\,\text{m}$): $N_{passes} = 10$. Path $\approx 20 + 9\pi \approx 48.27\,\text{m}$. Similar for $C_5$.

**Final answer: $L_{total} \approx 100\,\text{m} + \text{transit penalties}$** (exact value depends on cell-by-cell computation).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boustrophedon Sweep Algorithm — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize Workspace W] --> B[Define Obstacles and Compute Vertices V]
    B --> C[Sort Vertices by x-coordinate]
    C --> D[Initialize Slice Line s-x at x = 0]
    D --> E[Slide Slice Rightward by delta-x]
    E --> F{Vertices Touched by Slice?}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Check Topology Change in F-of-x]
    G --> H{Topology Changed?}
    H -- No --> E
    H -- Yes --> I[Record Critical Point x-c]
    I --> J[Close Current Cell, Open New Cell]
    J --> E
    E --> K[Reached x-max?]
    K -- No --> E
    K -- Yes --> L[Construct Cell Adjacency Graph G]
    L --> M[For Each Cell: Plan Boustrophedon Path]
    M --> N[Robot Executes Sweep Coverage]
    N --> O[At Cell End: Navigate to Shared Door]
    O --> P{Door Found in Adjacent Cell?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Cross Door and Resume Sweep]
    Q --> N
    P -- No --> R[Coverage Mission Complete]
    R --> S[Stop]
```

## 4.2 Cell Decomposition Topology — Architectural Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph W[Workspace W]
        W0[Free Region: Cell 1] ---|Door D1| W1
        W1[Cell 2 Upper Slab] ---|Door D2| W2
        W1 ---|Door D3| W3[Cell 3 Lower Slab]
        W2[Cell 3 Upper Slab] ---|Door D4| W4
        W3 ---|Door D5| W4[Cell 4]
        W4 ---|Door D6| W5[Cell 5]
    end
    O1[Obstacle O1] -.Blocks.-> W1
    O1 -.Blocks.-> W3
    O2[Obstacle O2] -.Blocks.-> W2
    O2 -.Blocks.-> W4
```

## 4.3 Critical Event Detection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Obstacle Vertex v = x-v, y-v] --> A{Local Edge Slopes?}
    A -- Both Edges Slope Up --> U[Upper Vertex Lambda]
    A -- Both Edges Slope Down --> D[Lower Vertex V]
    A -- One Up, One Down --> M[Middle Vertex No Event]
    U --> C1[Topology Change: Connectivity Increases]
    D --> C2[Topology Change: Connectivity Decreases]
    M --> C3[No Critical Event Triggered]
    C1 --> X[Mark x-c as Critical Point]
    C2 --> X
    C3 --> Y[Continue Sweep]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Note:** The above block diagrams map the **algorithmic architecture** of boustrophedon decomposition. The actual physical coverage trajectory (back-and-forth plow lines) is geometrically dense and best illustrated in a textbook figure, but the **logical flow** is fully captured in the diagrams above — which is the KTU 2024 Scheme examiner's expectation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define Boustrophedon Decomposition. What is the meaning of the term "boustrophedon"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Boustrophedon decomposition is an exact cell-decomposition method for robot motion planning (especially coverage path planning), introduced by **Choset and Pignon (1998)**. It partitions the free workspace into cells by sweeping a vertical line and recording *critical points* — abscissas where the slice line simultaneously touches two or more obstacle vertices, causing a topological change in the free space.
>
> The term "boustrophedon" comes from the Greek words *bous* (ox) and *strephein* (to turn), literally meaning **"the way of the ox."** It refers to the **back-and-forth plowing motion** of an ox in a field, which is precisely the motion pattern executed by a robot covering a cell.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Trapezoidal Decomposition and Boustrophedon Decomposition.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> | Aspect | Trapezoidal | Boustrophedon |
> |---|---|---|
> | Slice-line termination | At *nearest* vertex (every vertex) | Only at *critical* (topology-changing) vertices |
> | Number of cells | Larger (one per vertex) | Smaller (fewer, larger cells) |
> | Path efficiency | Suboptimal (many transitions) | **Optimal** for coverage |
> | Cell shape | Trapezoids | General convex polygons |
>
> In essence, **trapezoidal decomposition is a special case of boustrophedon decomposition** where every vertex is treated as a critical point.

---

## **PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step algorithm for boustrophedon decomposition. Clearly define the terms *slice line*, *critical point*, *cell*, and *door*.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With a suitable diagram, explain how the cell adjacency graph is constructed. How does the robot plan a global coverage path using this graph?

---

#### **Solution A(a) — Algorithm Explanation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Initialization**
Define a vertical **slice line** $s(x) = \{(x, y) \in W \mid y \in \mathbb{R}\}$. Initialize the slice at the leftmost boundary of the workspace $W$. [1 mark]

**Step 2: Sweeping**
Incrementally translate the slice from $x = x_{min}$ to $x = x_{max}$ along the x-axis. [1 mark]

**Step 3: Critical Point Detection**
At each step, identify obstacle vertices lying on the slice. A **critical point** is an abscissa $x_c$ where two or more obstacle vertices align with the slice line, causing a **topological change** in the connected components of $F(x) = s(x) \cap C_{free}$. [2 marks]

**Step 4: Cell Formation**
Between consecutive critical abscissas (and between the workspace boundary and the first/last critical abscissa), the topology of $F(x)$ is constant. Each such maximal invariant region is declared a **cell**. [1 mark]

**Step 5: Door Identification**
The common boundary between two adjacent cells is a **door** — a passage through which the robot can transition. [1 mark]

**Step 6: Output**
The decomposition yields a set of cells, each characterized by continuous upper and lower boundary functions $f_{top}(x)$ and $f_{bot}(x)$. [1 mark]

---

#### **Solution A(b) — Cell Adjacency Graph & Global Path [7 Marks]**

**Adjacency Graph Construction:**
Construct a graph $G = (V_G, E_G)$:
- Each **node** in $V_G$ represents one boustrophedon cell. [1 mark]
- Each **edge** in $E_G$ represents a shared door (boundary segment) between two adjacent cells. The graph is **undirected** since the robot can traverse the door in either direction. [1 mark]

**Global Path Planning:**
1. **Within-Cell Coverage:** For each cell, the robot executes a boustrophedon sweep — a sequence of parallel passes (back-and-forth) covering the entire cell area. [2 marks]
2. **Inter-Cell Transition:** When a cell is fully covered, the robot navigates to the nearest door. It crosses the door into the adjacent cell. [1 mark]
3. **Path Concatenation:** The total global path is the concatenation of all cell-wise boustrophedon paths plus the inter-cell transition segments. [1 mark]

**Diagram (sketch expected in exam):**

```
     +-------+    +-------+
     |  C_1  |----|  C_2  |   ← Cell adjacency graph
     +-------+    +-------+
         |            |
     +-------+    +-------+
     |  C_3  |----|  C_4  |
     +-------+    +-------+
```

[1 mark for the sketch]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A robot operates in a $10 \times 10$ workspace with two square obstacles of side $2\,\text{m}$ placed at positions $(2, 2)$ and $(6, 6)$. Compute the critical abscissas and the number of boustrophedon cells.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the cell formed in the region $x \in [4, 6]$, derive the boustrophedon path length. Assume $\Delta y = 0.5\,\text{m}$ and U-turn radius $r = 0.4\,\text{m}$.

---

#### **Solution B(a) — Critical Abscissas & Cell Count [7 Marks]**

**Obstacle Vertices:**
- $O_1$ at $(2,2)$ to $(4,4)$: vertices at $x = 2, 4$.
- $O_2$ at $(6,6)$ to $(8,8)$: vertices at $x = 6, 8$.

**Critical Abscissa Check:** [1 mark each]

| $x$ | Vertex Type | Topology Change? |
|---|---|---|
| $2$ | Lower-left corner of $O_1$ | **YES** (free space splits) |
| $4$ | Upper-right corner of $O_1$ | **YES** (free space merges) |
| $6$ | Lower-left corner of $O_2$ | **YES** (free space splits) |
| $8$ | Upper-right corner of $O_2$ | **YES** (free space merges) |

**Critical Abscissas:** $x_c \in \{2, 4, 6, 8\}$. [2 marks]

**Cell Formation:** [1 mark each]
- $C_1$: $[0, 2]$ — full height free.
- $C_2$: $[2, 4]$ — split by $O_1$ into upper/lower sub-regions.
- $C_3$: $[4, 6]$ — full height free.
- $C_4$: $[6, 8]$ — split by $O_2$ into upper/lower sub-regions.
- $C_5$: $[8, 10]$ — full height free.

**Total Cells = 5** (or 7 if upper/lower slabs of $C_2$ and $C_4$ are counted separately, depending on convention). [1 mark]

---

#### **Solution B(b) — Path Length Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $W_c = 6 - 4 = 2\,\text{m}$ (cell width).
- Workspace height in this region: $H_{c,max} = 10\,\text{m}$ (no obstacles in $C_3$).
- $\Delta y = 0.5\,\text{m}$.
- $r = 0.4\,\text{m}$.

**Step 1: Number of Passes** [2 marks]
$$
N_{passes} = \left\lceil \frac{H_{c,max}}{\Delta y} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{10}{0.5} \right\rceil = 20
$$

**Step 2: U-Turns** [1 mark]
$$
N_{turns} = N_{passes} - 1 = 19
$$

**Step 3: Sweep Length** [1 mark]
$$
L_{sweep} = 20 \times 2 = 40\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 4: U-Turn Length** [1 mark]
$$
L_{turns} = 19 \times \pi \times 0.4 = 19 \times 1.2566 \approx 23.88\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 5: Total Path Length Inside $C_3$** [2 marks]
$$
L_{cell,3} = L_{sweep} + L_{turns} = 40 + 23.88 = 63.88\,\text{m}
$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing trapezoidal with boustrophedon (–2 marks):** Many students incorrectly list *every* obstacle vertex as a critical point. **Only vertices that change the topology of $F(x)$ are critical.**
> 2. **Ignoring the middle vertex case (–1 mark):** Vertices where one edge slopes up and the other down do *not* trigger a critical event.
> 3. **Forgetting the U-turn term (–2 marks):** The path length $L_{cell}$ is **not** just $N_{passes} \cdot W_c$. U-turns contribute significant length.
> 4. **Omitting the adjacency graph (–2 marks):** In Part B, a question on "global coverage" requires explicit mention of the **cell adjacency graph** and the **door-based transition logic**.
> 5. **Unit errors (–1 mark):** Always state units (m, m², etc.) in numerical answers.
> 6. **Mis-spelling "boustrophedon" (–0.5 mark):** It is a high-value keyword; spell it correctly.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Boustrophedon decomposition** is a cell-decomposition method for **coverage path planning**, introduced by **Choset and Pignon (1998)**.
- ✅ The word **"boustrophedon"** means **"the way of the ox"** — referencing ox-plowing back-and-forth motion.
- ✅ A **slice line** $s(x)$ sweeps the workspace from left to right.
- ✅ A **critical point** $x_c$ is an abscissa where the **topology of $F(x) = s(x) \cap C_{free}$ changes** — i.e., where two or more obstacle vertices align with the slice.
- ✅ **Lower vertex (V)** → connectivity decreases; **Upper vertex (Λ)** → connectivity increases; **Middle vertex** → no event.
- ✅ Cells are maximal regions between consecutive critical points where the topology of $F(x)$ is **constant**.
- ✅ Inside each cell, the robot executes a **boustrophedon sweep** (back-and-forth parallel passes).
- ✅ The **cell adjacency graph** $G = (V_G, E_G)$ captures inter-cell connectivity via **doors** (shared boundary segments).
- ✅ Boustrophedon decomposition produces **fewer, larger cells** than trapezoidal decomposition — making it **superior for coverage efficiency**.
- ✅ Total path length per cell: $L_{cell} = N_{passes} \cdot W_c + (N_{passes} - 1) \cdot \pi r$.
- ✅ Real-world applications: **floor cleaning robots, agricultural automation, mine sweeping, search-and-rescue, autonomous lawn mowers**.
- ✅ Computational complexity: $O(n \log n)$, where $n$ is the number of obstacle vertices.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Field Planning: Potential Field Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Potential Field Planning: Potential Field Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Potential Field Method (PFM)**, originally introduced by **Oussama Khatib in 1986**, is a reactive, real-time motion planning technique in which the robot is modeled as a charged particle moving through a scalar potential field constructed over its configuration (or Cartesian) workspace. The goal configuration acts as a **pole of attraction**, while obstacles act as **regions of repulsion**. The robot's trajectory is generated by following the negative gradient of the total artificial potential function — analogous to an electron navigating an electromagnetic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *Potential Field Planning treats the robot as a point mass in a field of forces. The goal exerts an attractive force, and obstacles exert repulsive forces. The resultant force determines the robot's velocity/acceleration at every instant, producing a smooth, collision-free path.*

## 1.2 Mathematical Construct — The Two Potentials

The total potential $U(q)$ at any robot configuration $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the sum of an **attractive component** $U_{att}(q)$ and a **repulsive component** $U_{rep}(q)$:

$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$$

The force (or equivalently, the negative gradient that gives the desired velocity) is computed as:

$$F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of the Sign Convention:**
> - The **minus sign** before the gradient of $U_{att}$ makes it an *attractive* force (points toward the goal).
> - The **minus sign** before the gradient of $U_{rep}$ makes it a *repulsive* force (points away from obstacles).
> - In motion planning literature, the "force" is often used synonymously with the "velocity command," since velocity $\propto -\nabla U$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — A Hiker in Foggy Mountains

Imagine a hiker standing on a mountainous terrain in thick fog who cannot see the destination clearly. The hiker can only feel the **slope of the ground** under their feet. The mountain landscape is engineered such that:

- The **destination (goal)** is the **lowest valley**. The terrain naturally *slopes downward* toward it, so the hiker intuitively walks toward the valley.
- Every **obstacle** is a **steep upward cliff or pillar**. As the hiker approaches, the ground rises sharply, repelling them away.
- The hiker, by simply always walking **downhill** (negative gradient), will reach the goal while avoiding all obstacles.

This is exactly how the potential field method works. The "downhill direction" at every point is given by $-\nabla U(q)$, and that becomes the robot's instantaneous motion direction.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Potential fields are computationally cheap (closed-form gradient evaluations) and naturally produce **smooth, continuous trajectories** in real time — making them ideal for **dynamic, sensor-driven environments** such as autonomous mobile robots, UAV swarms, and industrial manipulators operating among moving obstacles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Attractive Potential $U_{att}(q)$

The attractive potential is designed to monotonically decrease as the robot approaches the goal $q_{goal}$. Two standard forms are used:

### Form 1: Quadratic Attractive Potential (Conic Well)
For distances greater than a threshold, a **parabolic** potential is used because its gradient is **linear** in the distance, giving bounded control commands.

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}^2(q)$$

where:
- $\zeta > 0$ is the **attractive gain** (scaling factor),
- $\rho_{goal}(q) = \vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert$ is the **Euclidean distance** from current configuration $q$ to the goal $q_{goal}$.

The corresponding **attractive force** is:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta (q - q_{goal})$$

### Form 2: Conic (Linear-Distance) Attractive Potential
For far-field navigation, a conic potential whose gradient is constant magnitude is sometimes preferred:

$$U_{att}(q) = \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}(q)$$

giving $F_{att}(q) = -\zeta \cdot \dfrac{q - q_{goal}}{\vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert}$ — a unit vector with constant magnitude $\zeta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two forms?** The quadratic form yields a force that *grows* with distance (good for far targets) but produces smoother control near the goal. The conic form gives a constant force regardless of distance, which is good for very distant goals but causes overshoot near the goal.

## 2.2 The Repulsive Potential $U_{rep}(q)$

The repulsive potential is **active only within an influence region** $\rho_0$ around each obstacle. Beyond $\rho_0$, the obstacle does not affect the robot.

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \eta \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \text{if } \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where:
- $\eta > 0$ is the **repulsive gain** (scaling factor),
- $\rho(q) = \text{dist}(q, q_{obstacle})$ is the **shortest Euclidean distance** from the robot to the nearest obstacle boundary,
- $\rho_0$ is the **distance of influence** (a designer-set threshold).

The corresponding **repulsive force** (non-zero only inside the influence region):

$$F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \eta \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho^2(q)} \cdot \nabla\rho(q), & \text{if } \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

The vector $\nabla\rho(q)$ is the unit vector pointing **from the obstacle to the robot**, ensuring the force is correctly directed outward.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Range of Validity | Units (SI) |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|------------|
| 1 | Total Potential | $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ | Entire workspace | Joules (J) |
| 2 | Attractive Potential (Quadratic) | $U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\zeta \rho_{goal}^2$ | $\rho_{goal} \ge 0$ | J |
| 3 | Attractive Potential (Conic) | $U_{att}(q) = \zeta \rho_{goal}$ | $\rho_{goal} > 0$ | J |
| 4 | Attractive Force | $F_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -\zeta(q-q_{goal})$ | Quadratic case | N |
| 5 | Repulsive Potential | $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ | $\rho \le \rho_0$ | J |
| 6 | Repulsive Force (magnitude) | $\vert F_{rep} \vert = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^2}$ | $\rho \le \rho_0$ | N |
| 7 | Influence Region Boundary | $\rho_0$ (designer-set) | $\rho_0 > 0$ | m |
| 8 | Resultant Force | $F_{total} = F_{att} + \sum F_{rep,i}$ | All $q$ | N |
| 9 | Robot Velocity Command | $v(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ | Smooth field | m/s |
| 10 | Distance Function | $\rho(q) = \min_i \vert \vert q - q_{obs,i} \vert \vert$ | Workspace | m |

## 2.4 Design Parameters — Choosing $\zeta$, $\eta$, and $\rho_0$

- **$\zeta$ (attractive gain):** Too small → sluggish motion; too large → oscillations near the goal.
- **$\eta$ (repulsive gain):** Must be large enough to overpower attraction near obstacles but not so large that it creates force discontinuities.
- **$\rho_0$ (influence distance):** Should be **larger than the robot's radius** plus a safety margin; if too small, the robot may not have time to react; if too large, the robot detours unnecessarily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Rule of Thumb:** $\eta$ should be chosen such that when the robot is at the closest obstacle boundary ($\rho \to 0$), the repulsive force is **at least as large** as the maximum possible attractive force, otherwise the robot may collide under high goal-pull.

## 2.5 Why Use the Negative Gradient? — Engineering Insight

The gradient $\nabla U$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent** of the potential. Therefore, $-\nabla U$ points in the direction of **steepest descent** — i.e., the most direct way to *minimize* potential. Physically, the robot is performing **gradient descent** on the potential landscape. In real-time implementations, a discrete update is used:

$$q_{t+1} = q_t + \alpha \cdot F_{total}(q_t)$$

where $\alpha$ is a step-size (learning rate analog) chosen for stability. This iterative rule is essentially a **first-order Euler integration** of the dynamics $\dot{q} = F_{total}(q)$.

## 2.6 The Famous Limitation: Local Minima

> [!WARNING]
> **Local Minima Problem (Frequently Asked in KTU Exams):**
> Gradient descent **cannot distinguish** between a global minimum (the true goal) and a local minimum (a spurious trough caused by the geometry of attractive and repulsive fields). At a local minimum, $\nabla U = 0$, the robot stops prematurely and is *not* at the goal. This is the **principal weakness** of the classical potential field method.

**Common remedies** (also high-yield for KTU):
1. **Random walk / Random perturbation** when trapped.
2. **Navigation functions** (Koditschek & Rimon, 1992) — specially constructed potential with a single global minimum.
3. **Harmonic potentials** — solutions to Laplace's equation with no local minima in simply connected workspaces.
4. **Hybrid methods** — use global planner (e.g., A*) to reach a region near the goal, then switch to PFM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Attractive Force from the Quadratic Potential

**Step 1 — State the potential.**
We start with the **quadratic (parabolic) attractive potential** centered at the goal $q_{goal}$:

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \zeta \, \rho_{goal}^2(q)$$

**Step 2 — Express the distance in vector form.**
The Euclidean distance squared between current position $q = (x, y)$ and goal $q_{goal} = (x_g, y_g)$ in 2D is:

$$\rho_{goal}^2(q) = (q - q_{goal}) \cdot (q - q_{goal}) = (x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $q$ (gradient operation).**
Applying the standard gradient identity $\nabla_q \left[ \frac{1}{2}(q-q_{goal})^2 \right] = (q - q_{goal})$:

$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \zeta \cdot \nabla\left[(q-q_{goal}) \cdot (q-q_{goal})\right] = \zeta (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the negative gradient to obtain the attractive force.**

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**
The force is a **linear restoring force** (Hooke's law analog) that always points directly from $q$ toward $q_{goal}$ with magnitude proportional to the distance — analogous to a virtual spring connecting robot and goal with stiffness $\zeta$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this is elegant:** No matter where the robot starts, this force *always* has a non-zero magnitude until $q = q_{goal}$ (where $F_{att} = 0$). This guarantees the goal is a *global minimum* of $U_{att}$ in the absence of obstacles.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Repulsive Force from the Standard Repulsive Potential

**Step 1 — State the potential (active only within influence zone).**

$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, \eta \, \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, \quad \rho(q) \le \rho_0$$

**Step 2 — Introduce a shorthand for the bracketed term.**

Let $\Phi(\rho) = \dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}$. Then $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta\,\Phi^2$.

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule for the gradient.**
Since $U_{rep}$ is a function of $\rho$, and $\rho$ is a function of $q$:

$$\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \frac{d U_{rep}}{d\rho} \cdot \nabla \rho(q)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the scalar derivative.**

$$\frac{d U_{rep}}{d\rho} = \frac{1}{2}\eta \cdot 2\Phi \cdot \frac{d\Phi}{d\rho} = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\rho^2} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$\frac{d U_{rep}}{d\rho} = -\frac{\eta}{\rho^2} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the negative gradient to obtain the repulsive force.**

$$F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \frac{\eta}{\rho^2} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \nabla \rho(q)$$

**Step 7 — Identify the direction vector.**
The vector $\nabla\rho(q)$ is the **unit vector pointing from the obstacle to the robot**, which can be written explicitly as:

$$\nabla \rho(q) = \frac{q - q_{obstacle}}{\vert \vert q - q_{obstacle} \vert \vert}$$

**Step 8 — Final assembled form of the repulsive force.**

$$F_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \dfrac{1}{\rho^2(q)} \cdot \dfrac{q - q_{obs}}{\vert \vert q - q_{obs} \vert \vert}, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 9 — Behavior at the boundary and at the obstacle.**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{At } \rho = \rho_0: \quad F_{rep} = 0 \quad \text{(smooth, continuous transition)} \\
&\text{At } \rho \to 0: \quad F_{rep} \to \infty \quad \text{(strong, diverging repulsion — safety guaranteed)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuity Property:** Because the term $\left(\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)$ vanishes at $\rho = \rho_0$, the repulsive potential and force are **both zero and continuous** at the boundary of the influence zone. This prevents the robot from "feeling" a sudden force discontinuity as it crosses into the influence region.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Verifying the Goal is a Global Minimum (No Obstacles)

We verify mathematically that in a workspace **without any obstacles**, the goal $q_{goal}$ is the **unique global minimum** of the total potential.

**Step 1 — Without obstacles, $U_{rep}(q) = 0$ everywhere.**

$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\zeta (q - q_{goal})^2$$

**Step 2 — Take the gradient and set to zero to find critical points.**

$$\nabla U(q) = \zeta(q - q_{goal}) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q = q_{goal}$$

**Step 3 — Verify second-order condition (Hessian).**

$$\nabla^2 U(q) = \zeta \cdot I \quad (\text{identity matrix}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{positive definite} \; \forall \zeta > 0$$

**Step 4 — Conclude that $q_{goal}$ is a *strict global minimum*** with $U(q_{goal}) = 0$. This is the mathematical guarantee that an unobstructed potential field has only one minimum, located at the goal.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This is the analytical reason why adding *obstacles* (and hence $U_{rep}$) can introduce spurious critical points where $\nabla U = 0$ but $q \ne q_{goal}$ — the mathematical genesis of the local minima problem.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Potential Field Method

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional signal flow** of a real-time potential field controller. The robot's state is sampled, the field is constructed, the gradient is computed, and the resulting velocity command closes the loop with the robot's actuators.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Robot State q_t] --> B[Compute Distance to Goal rho_goal]
    B --> C[Compute Attractive Potential U_att]
    C --> D[Compute Attractive Force F_att = -grad U_att]

    A --> E[Scan Workspace for Obstacles]
    E --> F[Compute Minimum Distance to Nearest Obstacle rho]
    F --> G{Is rho less than or equal to rho_0?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute Repulsive Potential U_rep]
    H --> I[Compute Repulsive Force F_rep = -grad U_rep]
    G -- No --> J[Set F_rep = 0]

    D --> K[Sum Forces: F_total = F_att + F_rep]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Apply Smoothing or Saturation]
    L --> M[Update Robot State: q_t+1 = q_t + alpha F_total]
    M --> N{Goal Reached? rho_goal less than epsilon}
    N -- No --> A
    N -- Yes --> O[Stop: Path Complete]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Visualization of the Combined Field

The following Mermaid diagram is a **block-level representation** of how attractive and repulsive fields superimpose in the workspace, with the robot at the starting position and the goal as the deepest valley. Obstacles act as upward "hills" that the gradient-descent trajectory must flow around.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Workspace_2D["Configuration Workspace C_free"]
        direction LR
        Start((Start Position q_start))
        Goal((Goal q_goal -- Deepest Valley))
        Obs1((Obstacle 1 -- Repulsive Hill))
        Obs2((Obstacle 2 -- Repulsive Hill))
        LM((Local Minimum -- Spurious Trap))

        Start -->|Negative Gradient Descent| Field1
        Field1 -->|Forced to flow around| Obs1
        Obs1 -->|Channeled through saddle| Field2
        Field2 -->|May settle prematurely| LM
        LM -.->|Random Perturbation Restart| Field1
        Field2 -->|Lucky trajectory reaches| Goal
    end
```

## 4.3 Force Decomposition Schematic

This diagram illustrates how the resultant force on the robot at a generic configuration is the **vector sum** of the goal's attractive pull and the obstacle's repulsive push.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Robot at position q] -->|Vector F_att| SumNode
    G[Goal q_goal] -->|Attractive direction| SumNode
    SumNode -->|Vector sum| Ftotal[F_total = F_att + F_rep]
    O[Obstacle] -->|Repulsive direction opposite to gradient rho| SumNode
    Ftotal --> Motion[Robot Motion Direction: argmin gradient U]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** At every configuration, the robot "feels" a force vector $F_{att}$ pointing toward the goal and a force vector $F_{rep}$ pointing away from the closest obstacle. The vector sum $F_{total}$ is the actual motion direction, and gradient descent follows it step by step.

## 4.4 Influence Region Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Global_Workspace["Global Workspace W"]
        direction TB
        FreeRegion["Free Region: U_rep = 0"]
        subgraph Influence_Region["Influence Region: rho less than or equal to rho_0"]
            IR1["U_rep and F_rep are nonzero"]
            IR2["Smoothly decays to zero at boundary rho = rho_0"]
        end
        ObstacleCore["Obstacle Boundary: rho = 0 -- F_rep to infinity"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**Q: Define the potential field method for robot motion planning. What are the two principal components of the total potential function?**

**Model Answer:**

The potential field method, introduced by **Oussama Khatib (1986)**, is a reactive motion planning technique in which the robot is modeled as a point in a scalar potential field $U(q)$ constructed over its configuration space. The robot moves along the **negative gradient** $-\nabla U(q)$ of this field, which generates a continuous, collision-free trajectory in real time.

The total potential is the sum of two principal components:

$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$$

- **$U_{att}(q)$** — the **attractive potential**, centered at the goal configuration $q_{goal}$. It pulls the robot toward the goal (a quadratic or conic function of distance to the goal).
- **$U_{rep}(q)$** — the **repulsive potential**, defined around each obstacle and active only within a distance of influence $\rho_0$. It pushes the robot away from nearby obstacles (an inverse-function of distance to the obstacle).

The robot's velocity command is computed as $v(q) = -\nabla U(q)$, and the discrete update is $q_{t+1} = q_t + \alpha \cdot v(q_t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3-mark question):** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for naming the two components, and 1 mark for the gradient-descent update rule. Students who omit the negative-gradient formulation lose 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**
**Q: List any three major advantages and two major limitations of the classical potential field method.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Computational simplicity** — the potential and its gradient are closed-form expressions; planning reduces to evaluating a few arithmetic operations per cycle, suitable for real-time control.
2. **Smooth, continuous trajectories** — gradient descent naturally generates smooth paths without requiring post-processing.
3. **Reactive and sensor-driven** — the method can be re-evaluated at every control cycle using live sensor data, enabling obstacle avoidance in dynamic environments.

**Limitations:**
1. **Local minima problem** — the robot can become trapped at a configuration where $\nabla U = 0$ but $q \ne q_{goal}$, especially in cluttered workspaces.
2. **No passage between closely-spaced obstacles** — narrow corridors cause opposing repulsive forces that cancel the attraction, producing oscillations or stagnation.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for each correctly stated advantage/limitation. Naming the "local minima" problem alone is sufficient for the 2-mark limitations component; an example or remedy is optional.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the attractive force $F_{att}(q)$ from the quadratic attractive potential $U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\zeta \rho_{goal}^2(q)$. State the assumptions and discuss why a quadratic form is preferred over a conic (linear) form for accurate goal docking. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the repulsive force $F_{rep}(q)$ from the standard repulsive potential $U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$, active only within the influence region $\rho \le \rho_0$. Show that both the potential and the force are continuous at $\rho = \rho_0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the quadratic attractive potential.**

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \zeta \left[ (x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2 \right]$$

where $q = (x, y)$ and $q_{goal} = (x_g, y_g)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $x$.**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2}\zeta \cdot 2(x - x_g) = \zeta(x - x_g)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $y$.**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{2}\zeta \cdot 2(y - y_g) = \zeta(y - y_g)$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the gradient vector.**

$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = \zeta \begin{bmatrix} x - x_g \\ y - y_g \end{bmatrix} = \zeta (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 5 — Apply the negative gradient to obtain the attractive force.**

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 6 — Discussion: Why quadratic over conic?**
- The quadratic form gives a force proportional to distance (vanishes as $q \to q_{goal}$), enabling **smooth docking with zero velocity at the goal**.
- The conic form $U_{att} = \zeta \rho_{goal}$ gives a *constant-magnitude* force $F_{att} = \zeta \cdot \frac{q-q_{goal}}{\vert \vert q-q_{goal} \vert \vert}$ that does not vanish at the goal, causing the robot to **overshoot and oscillate** near the goal.
- Therefore, quadratic is preferred when **precise goal arrival** is required (e.g., manipulator end-effector placement, mobile robot docking).

---

### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the repulsive potential valid only for $\rho \le \rho_0$.**

$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the derivative with respect to $\rho$.**

Let $f(\rho) = \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$. Then $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta f^2$, so:

$$\frac{dU_{rep}}{d\rho} = \eta f(\rho) \cdot \frac{df}{d\rho} = \eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\rho^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the chain rule for the gradient in $q$.**

$$F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep} = -\frac{dU_{rep}}{d\rho} \cdot \nabla\rho(q) = \frac{\eta}{\rho^2}\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \nabla\rho(q)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the gradient of the distance function.**
For a single point obstacle at $q_{obs}$:

$$\nabla\rho(q) = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\vert \vert q - q_{obs} \vert \vert}$$

**Step 5 — Final assembled repulsive force.**

$$F_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\eta}{\rho^2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \dfrac{q - q_{obs}}{\vert \vert q - q_{obs} \vert \vert}, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Verify continuity at $\rho = \rho_0$.**
- At $\rho = \rho_0$: $\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) = \left(\frac{1}{\rho_0} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) = 0$.
- Therefore $U_{rep}(\rho_0) = 0$ and $F_{rep}(\rho_0) = 0$.
- This matches the value of $U_{rep}$ and $F_{rep}$ in the free region (both zero there), so the potential and force are **continuous at the boundary**.

**Step 7 — Verify continuity of the gradient (smoothness of force).**

$$\left.\frac{dF_{rep}}{d\rho}\right|_{\rho = \rho_0} = \text{finite (no impulse)}$$

This guarantees the robot does not experience a sudden force jump as it enters/exits the influence region — a critical property for stable control.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **attractive potential field** with a suitable diagram. Write the mathematical expressions for both quadratic and conic attractive potentials, and compare their behaviors near the goal. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **local minima problem** in the potential field method? Explain with a workspace example. Discuss at least two remedies proposed in the literature to overcome this problem. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Concept of Attractive Potential.**
The attractive potential models the goal $q_{goal}$ as the **global minimum** of a scalar energy landscape. The robot is pulled toward this minimum by a force that points in the direction of steepest descent of $U_{att}$. The potential value increases monotonically with the distance from the goal.

**Step 2 — Mathematical expressions.**

$$\text{Quadratic: } U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\zeta \vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert^2, \quad F_{att} = -\zeta(q - q_{goal})$$

$$\text{Conic: } U_{att}(q) = \zeta \vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert, \quad F_{att} = -\zeta \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison near the goal.**

| Property | Quadratic | Conic |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| Force magnitude near goal | Vanishes linearly with distance | Constant (does not vanish) |
| Goal docking behavior | Smooth, no overshoot | Overshoots, oscillates |
| Gradient continuity at goal | Smooth (zero derivative) | Discontinuous (unit vector) |
| Best use | Accurate docking, manipulators | Distant goal navigation |

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
The quadratic form is universally preferred for **goal-docking precision**, while the conic form is occasionally combined with a quadratic form in a **hybrid attractive potential** to handle both far-field and near-field regimes.

---

### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of the Local Minima Problem.**
The local minima problem occurs when the gradient of the total potential vanishes at a configuration $q_{LM}$ that is **not** the goal:

$$\nabla U(q_{LM}) = 0, \quad q_{LM} \ne q_{goal}$$

Since the robot follows gradient descent, it becomes **trapped** at $q_{LM}$ and fails to reach the goal. This is the principal failure mode of the classical potential field method.

**Step 2 — Workspace Example.**
Consider a U-shaped obstacle (concave obstacle) with the goal placed **inside** the concave region. The robot starting outside experiences:
- Attraction toward the goal (inside the cavity).
- Repulsion from the obstacle walls (the two inner walls of the U).
- The repulsive forces from the two opposing walls **cancel** the attraction at the entrance of the U, creating a **spurious local minimum** at the cavity entrance.

The robot stops at this entrance, never entering the cavity to reach the goal.

**Step 3 — Remedy 1: Random Walk / Best-First Recovery.**
When $|\nabla U| < \epsilon$ and $q \ne q_{goal}$, apply a small random perturbation to $q$ and continue gradient descent. This probabilistically escapes shallow local minima. *Reference: standard recovery strategies in mobile robotics literature.*

**Step 4 — Remedy 2: Navigation Functions (Koditschek & Rimon, 1992).**
A navigation function $\varphi(q)$ is a specially constructed potential that has **exactly one critical point** — the goal — which is a *global* minimum. This is achieved by adding a higher-order repulsive term involving $\rho_0$ to ensure no spurious minima in star-shaped workspaces.

**Step 5 — Remedy 3: Hybrid Global-Local Planning.**
Use a **global planner** (such as A* or RRT) to navigate to a region close to the goal where obstacles are sparse, then switch to the potential field method for fine motion. The global planner provides a coarse path that avoids the local-minimum traps.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "force" with "potential":** Many students write $F = \nabla U$ instead of $F = -\nabla U$. The minus sign is **mandatory** for the correct physical direction (descent). Losing this sign costs 1–2 marks in any derivation.
> 2. **Forgetting the influence region:** The repulsive potential is **zero** outside $\rho_0$. Writing $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^2}$ without the cutoff is a frequent error.
> 3. **Omitting the unit direction vector:** The gradient of $\rho(q)$ must appear explicitly in $F_{rep}$; simply writing magnitudes without directions shows incomplete understanding.
> 4. **Stating "local minima" without an example:** A bare definition is worth 1 mark; a workspace example (U-shaped obstacle, narrow corridor) elevates the answer to 7 marks.
> 5. **Confusing $\rho_{goal}$ with $\rho$:** $\rho_{goal}$ is distance to the **goal**; $\rho$ is distance to the **nearest obstacle**. Mixing these is a recurring mark-loss error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Origin:** Potential field method was introduced by **Oussama Khatib in 1986** for real-time obstacle avoidance in manipulator and mobile robot control.
- **Core equation:** $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ and robot motion follows $q_{t+1} = q_t - \alpha \nabla U(q_t)$.
- **Attractive potential (quadratic, preferred):** $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2}\zeta \vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert^2$, with force $F_{att} = -\zeta(q - q_{goal})$.
- **Repulsive potential (within influence zone only):** $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ for $\rho \le \rho_0$, else $0$.
- **Repulsive force:** $F_{rep} = \dfrac{\eta}{\rho^2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \nabla\rho(q)$ — non-zero only within $\rho_0$.
- **Continuity:** Both $U_{rep}$ and $F_{rep}$ vanish continuously at $\rho = \rho_0$; the force diverges as $\rho \to 0$, guaranteeing collision avoidance.
- **Design parameters:** $\zeta$ (attractive gain), $\eta$ (repulsive gain), $\rho_0$ (distance of influence). $\eta$ must overpower $\zeta$ near obstacles for safety.
- **Principal weakness:** **Local minima problem** — robot can be trapped where $\nabla U = 0$ but $q \ne q_{goal}$. Especially severe with **concave (U-shaped) obstacles**.
- **Key remedies:** random perturbation, navigation functions (Koditschek & Rimon 1992), harmonic potentials, hybrid global–local planning (A*/RRT + PFM).
- **Real-world uses:** real-time mobile robot navigation, dynamic obstacle avoidance, manipulator path planning, UAV swarm control, AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) routing in warehouses.
- **Examiner's golden rule:** Always include the **minus sign** in the gradient; always specify the **influence region**; always include the **direction vector** $\nabla\rho(q)$ in the repulsive force.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attractive Potential (U<sub>att</sub>)** is a scalar energy function defined over the robot's Configuration Space (C-space) that assigns **lower potential energy** to configurations closer to the goal configuration and **higher potential energy** to configurations farther from the goal. The robot is treated as a point that "rolls downhill" on this potential landscape toward the goal.

In the **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method introduced by **Khatib (1986)**, the attractive potential is one of the two principal components (the other being the repulsive potential) that together construct the total potential field:

$$U_{total}(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$$

The attractive potential is a function of the **Euclidean distance** between the robot's current configuration $q$ and the goal configuration $q_{goal}$ in the configuration space.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Magnet and the Hill":**
> Imagine the goal is a **deep valley** and the robot is a **ball placed on a smooth slope**. The ball naturally rolls downhill due to gravity. The attractive potential is like this gravitational slope — it gently (or strongly) pulls the robot toward the goal. The "steeper" the slope (higher gain $\zeta$), the faster the robot approaches the goal, just as a steeper hill causes a ball to roll faster.

In engineering terms:
- The **goal** is the bottom of the energy well.
- The **robot** is the particle experiencing a force field.
- The **force** on the robot is always directed *toward* the goal, with magnitude proportional to how far the robot is from the goal.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value (Range) |
|---|---|---|
| $\zeta$ (zeta) | Attractive potential gain (scaling factor) | **0.5 to 5.0** (dimensionless or per unit distance) |
| $q$ | Current configuration vector | $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $q_{goal}$ | Goal configuration vector | $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $\rho_{goal}$ | Distance to goal: $\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ | $\geq 0$ |
| $d^*$ | Threshold distance (for hybrid potential) | **User-defined** (e.g., 0.5–2.0 units) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The attractive potential **does not depend** on obstacles — it is purely a function of the distance to the goal. Obstacle avoidance is handled separately by the **repulsive potential**, which is covered as a distinct topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Standard Forms of Attractive Potential

The KTU syllabus and the original Khatib formulation recognize **two classical forms** of the attractive potential, each with distinct mathematical properties. The choice of form affects both the convergence behavior and the smoothness of the resulting force field.

### Form 1: Quadratic (Paraboloid) Attractive Potential

The quadratic form is the most commonly used in introductory treatments:

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$$

where $\rho_{goal}(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ is the Euclidean norm of the distance vector.

### Form 2: Conic (Linear / Cone) Attractive Potential

The conic form uses the unsquared distance:

$$U_{att}(q) = \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}(q)$$

### Form 3: Hybrid (Clamped Quadratic-Conic) Attractive Potential

To combine the **smoothness** of the quadratic form (near the goal) with the **bounded force** of the conic form (far from the goal), a hybrid formulation is widely adopted:

$$U_{att}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot d^{*2} & \text{if } \rho_{goal}(q) \leq d^{*} \\ d^{*} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}(q) - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot d^{*2} & \text{if } \rho_{goal}(q) > d^{*} \end{cases}$$

This ensures:
- **Inside** the threshold sphere of radius $d^*$: smooth paraboloid → smooth gradient.
- **Outside** the threshold: linear growth → bounded force magnitude.

## 2.2 The Attractive Force — The Heart of Motion

The attractive **force** is obtained as the **negative gradient** of the attractive potential. The gradient operation transforms the scalar potential into a vector field, and the negative sign ensures the force is **directed toward the goal** (potential descent).

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q)$$

### Gradient for Quadratic Form:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

### Gradient for Conic Form:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\zeta \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the negative sign?** In physics, force always points from high potential to low potential. The gradient $\nabla U$ points in the direction of *steepest increase* of $U$. Since the goal is at the *minimum* of $U_{att}$, the force $F_{att} = -\nabla U_{att}$ correctly points **toward the goal**.

## 2.3 Properties Summary

| Property | Quadratic | Conic |
|---|---|---|
| Continuity of $U_{att}$ | $\mathcal{C}^{\infty}$ (infinitely smooth) | $\mathcal{C}^{\infty}$ (except at goal, not differentiable) |
| Continuity of $F_{att}$ | $\mathcal{C}^{\infty}$ (smooth) | **Discontinuous at goal** |
| Force magnitude as $q \to \infty$ | $\to \infty$ (unbounded) | $\to \zeta$ (bounded) |
| Force magnitude at goal | $0$ | $0$ |
| Computational cost | Low | Low (requires normalization) |
| Best use case | Local refinement, near goal | Far-field planning, bounded actuator effort |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \zeta \rho_{goal}^{2}$ | Quadratic attractive potential | energy |
| 2 | $U_{att}(q) = \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}$ | Conic attractive potential | energy |
| 3 | $\rho_{goal}(q) = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ | Distance to goal | length |
| 4 | $F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q)$ | General force–potential relation | force |
| 5 | $F_{att}^{quad}(q) = -\zeta (q - q_{goal})$ | Quadratic attractive force | force |
| 6 | $F_{att}^{conic}(q) = -\zeta \dfrac{q - q_{goal}}{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert}$ | Conic attractive force (unit vector form) | force |
| 7 | $F_{att}^{hybrid}(q) = \begin{cases} -\zeta(q-q_{goal}) & \rho \leq d^{*} \\ -\zeta d^{*}\dfrac{q-q_{goal}}{\rho} & \rho > d^{*} \end{cases}$ | Hybrid attractive force | force |
| 8 | $\Vert F_{att}^{quad} \Vert = \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}$ | Magnitude of quadratic force | force |
| 9 | $\Vert F_{att}^{conic} \Vert = \zeta$ | Magnitude of conic force (constant!) | force |
| 10 | $\lim_{\rho \to 0} F_{att}(q) = 0$ | Force vanishes at goal | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The attractive potential is the conceptual backbone of every gradient-based robot planner:

- **Mobile robot navigation** (Roomba, AGVs, warehouse robots): the goal dock or charging station acts as the attractive sink.
- **Manipulator inverse kinematics**: a quadratic cost on the error $q - q_{goal}$ is exactly the attractive potential minimized at each iteration.
- **Drone waypoint tracking**: the goal waypoint defines a paraboloid well; the drone's controller descends it.
- **Autonomous vehicle lane centering**: lane centerlines are essentially attractive potential valleys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The attractive potential **alone** cannot avoid obstacles. It is **always paired** with a repulsive potential in any practical APF-based planner. The combination produces a **navigation function** that, in theory, has a global minimum at the goal and local maxima at obstacle centers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quadratic Attractive Force from First Principles

We start from the quadratic potential definition and apply the definition of the gradient operator.

**Given:**

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the squared norm explicitly** for $q = (x, y)$ in 2D with goal $q_{goal} = (x_g, y_g)$:

$$\rho_{goal}^{2}(q) = (x - x_g)^{2} + (y - y_g)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute back into the potential:**

$$U_{att}(x, y) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \left[ (x - x_g)^{2} + (y - y_g)^{2} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot 2(x - x_g) = \zeta \cdot (x - x_g)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $y$:**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot 2(y - y_g) = \zeta \cdot (y - y_g)$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the gradient vector:**

$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \zeta (x - x_g) \\ \zeta (y - y_g) \end{bmatrix} = \zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 6 — Apply the negative sign to obtain the attractive force:**

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 7 — Compute the force magnitude:**

$$\Vert F_{att}(q) \Vert = \zeta \cdot \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert = \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The magnitude of the attractive force grows **linearly with distance** from the goal in the quadratic formulation. Far from the goal, the force can become very large — this is a key practical issue that motivates the **hybrid formulation**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Conic Attractive Force from First Principles

**Given:**

$$U_{att}(q) = \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}(q) = \zeta \cdot \sqrt{(x - x_g)^{2} + (y - y_g)^{2}}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to $x$ using the chain rule:**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \zeta \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{(x-x_g)^{2} + (y-y_g)^{2}}} \cdot 2(x - x_g)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \zeta \cdot \frac{x - x_g}{\sqrt{(x-x_g)^{2} + (y-y_g)^{2}}} = \zeta \cdot \frac{x - x_g}{\rho_{goal}}$$

**Step 3 — Similarly for $y$:**

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = \zeta \cdot \frac{y - y_g}{\rho_{goal}}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the gradient:**

$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = \frac{\zeta}{\rho_{goal}} \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 5 — Apply the negative sign:**

$$F_{att}(q) = -\frac{\zeta}{\rho_{goal}} \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 6 — Note that $\dfrac{q - q_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}$ is a **unit vector** $\hat{e}$ pointing from goal to current position. Thus:**

$$F_{att}(q) = -\zeta \cdot \hat{e}$$

**Step 7 — The force magnitude is therefore constant:**

$$\Vert F_{att}(q) \Vert = \zeta \quad \text{(for all } \rho_{goal} > 0\text{)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The conic force is **undefined at the goal** ($\rho_{goal} = 0 \Rightarrow$ division by zero). At the goal, the force is defined to be zero by convention. This singularity is the principal weakness of the conic form.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 2-DOF mobile robot is at configuration $q = (3, 4)$. The goal is at $q_{goal} = (0, 0)$. The attractive gain is $\zeta = 2$. Compute (a) the attractive potential, (b) the attractive force vector, and (c) the force magnitude. Use the **quadratic** form.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Distance calculation:**

$$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(3-0)^{2} + (4-0)^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

**Step 2 — Attractive potential (quadratic):**

$$U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot (5)^{2} = 1 \cdot 25 = 25 \text{ units of energy}$$

**Step 3 — Attractive force vector:**

$$F_{att} = -\zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal}) = -2 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 3 - 0 \\ 4 - 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -8 \end{bmatrix} \text{ units of force}$$

**Step 4 — Force magnitude (verification using Pythagorean theorem):**

$$\Vert F_{att} \Vert = \sqrt{(-6)^{2} + (-8)^{2}} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ units of force}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using the formula $\Vert F_{att} \Vert = \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}$:**

$$\Vert F_{att} \Vert = 2 \cdot 5 = 10 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Direction:** The force vector $(-6, -8)$ points from $(3,4)$ **toward** $(0,0)$, confirming the goal-seeking behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> The force is **directly proportional to displacement** in the quadratic form. This means that as the robot gets closer to the goal, the force naturally decreases, producing a smooth deceleration — analogous to a critically damped spring pulling the robot home.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Attractive Potential Field as a Topographical Landscape

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Robot Current Configuration q] --> B[Compute Distance to Goal]
    B --> C["rho_goal = ||q - q_goal||"]
    C --> D{Choose Potential Form}
    D -->|Quadratic| E["U_att = 0.5 * zeta * rho^2"]
    D -->|Conic| F["U_att = zeta * rho"]
    D -->|Hybrid| G{"rho <= d* ?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["U_att = 0.5 * zeta * d*^2"]
    G -->|No| I["U_att = zeta*d* * rho - 0.5*zeta*d*^2"]
    E --> J[Apply Negative Gradient]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["F_att = - gradient U_att"]
    K --> L[Output Attractive Force Vector]
    L --> M[Update Robot Configuration]
    M --> N{Reached Goal?}
    N -->|No| A
    N -->|Yes| O[Stop: Planning Complete]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Attractive Field in APF Planner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER"]
        S1[Current Configuration q]
        S2[Goal Configuration q_goal]
        S3[Attractive Gain zeta]
        S4[Threshold Distance d_star]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE_LAYER["POTENTIAL COMPUTATION LAYER"]
        C1[Distance Calculator]
        C2[Quadratic Form Evaluator]
        C3[Conic Form Evaluator]
        C4[Hybrid Form Selector]
    end

    subgraph FORCE_LAYER["FORCE COMPUTATION LAYER"]
        F1[Gradient Operator]
        F2[Negative Gradient Applier]
        F3[Force Magnitude Limiter]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1[Force Vector F_att]
        O2[Potential Scalar U_att]
        O3[Velocity Command to Robot Controller]
    end

    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C1
    S1 --> C2
    S2 --> C2
    S1 --> C3
    S2 --> C3
    S1 --> C4
    S2 --> C4
    S3 --> C2
    S3 --> C3
    S3 --> C4
    S4 --> C4
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4

    C3 --> F1
    C4 --> F1

    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> O1
    F1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Force Behavior Comparison Across Forms

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Quadratic Form] --> P2[Force grows linearly with distance]
    P1 --> P3[Smooth gradient everywhere]
    P1 --> P4[Unbounded force far from goal]

    P5[Conic Form] --> P6[Force is constant magnitude zeta]
    P5 --> P7[Discontinuous at goal]
    P5 --> P8[Bounded force - good for actuators]

    P9[Hybrid Form] --> P10[Quadratic near goal - smooth]
    P9 --> P11[Conic far from goal - bounded]
    P9 --> P12[Best of both worlds]

    P4 --> P13[Risk: actuator saturation]
    P7 --> P14[Risk: jerky motion at goal]
    P12 --> P15[Recommended for practical deployment]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Sketch Description (Text-Based Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualizing the Attractive Field** (2D top-down view):
>
> Imagine a 3D landscape where height = $U_{att}(x, y)$:
> - The **goal** $(x_g, y_g)$ sits at the **bottom of a bowl**.
> - For the **quadratic** form, the bowl is a **smooth paraboloid** — like a satellite dish.
> - For the **conic** form, the bowl is a **sharp cone** with a pointed bottom.
> - The robot is a marble that **rolls downhill** along the gradient direction.
> - The steeper the bowl (higher $\zeta$), the stronger the pull.
> - Equipotential contours are **concentric circles** around the goal.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the attractive potential in the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method. Write the mathematical expression for the quadratic form of the attractive potential.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **attractive potential** $U_{att}(q)$ in the APF method is a scalar function defined over the robot's configuration space that assigns lower energy values to configurations closer to the goal and higher energy values to configurations farther from the goal. The robot descends this potential gradient to reach the goal.

**Quadratic form:**

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}^{2}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}$$

where $\zeta > 0$ is the attractive gain and $\rho_{goal}$ is the Euclidean distance to the goal. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for variable explanation)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**What is the relationship between the attractive potential and the attractive force? Derive the force expression for the quadratic attractive potential.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The attractive force is the **negative gradient** of the attractive potential:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q)$$

For the quadratic form $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} \zeta \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}$:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

This force vector always points **from the current configuration toward the goal**, with magnitude proportional to the distance. **(3 marks: 1 for relation, 1 for derivation step, 1 for interpretation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q9]**

**(a)** With neat mathematical formulation, explain the **quadratic and conic forms** of the attractive potential used in robot motion planning. Compare their properties with respect to smoothness and force boundedness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF planar robot arm is at configuration $q = (2, 1)$ with goal $q_{goal} = (0, 0)$. The attractive gain is $\zeta = 3$. Using the **quadratic** attractive potential, compute:
   - (i) The attractive potential $U_{att}$ at $q$.
   - (ii) The attractive force vector $F_{att}$.
   - (iii) The force magnitude.
   - (iv) The new configuration after one step of size $\Delta t = 0.1$ s (using $q_{new} = q + F_{att} \cdot \Delta t$, assuming unit mass). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The attractive potential $U_{att}(q)$ in the APF method pulls the robot toward the goal. Two standard forms exist:

**Quadratic form** (smooth, unbounded force):

$$U_{att}^{quad}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert^{2}$$

**Conic form** (sharp, bounded force):

$$U_{att}^{conic}(q) = \zeta \cdot \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$$

**Comparison:**

| Property | Quadratic | Conic |
|---|---|---|
| Smoothness of $U$ | $\mathcal{C}^\infty$ | $\mathcal{C}^\infty$ |
| Smoothness of $F$ | $\mathcal{C}^\infty$ | Discontinuous at goal |
| Force as $\rho \to \infty$ | Unbounded | Bounded at $\zeta$ |
| Force at goal | 0 | 0 (by convention) |

The **quadratic form** is preferred for local smoothing and well-behaved gradients, while the **conic form** is preferred when actuator saturation is a concern. A **hybrid form** switches between the two based on a threshold $d^*$. **(7 marks: 2 for quadratic, 2 for conic, 3 for comparison table)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $q = (2, 1)$, $q_{goal} = (0, 0)$, $\zeta = 3$, $\Delta t = 0.1$.

**(i) Attractive potential:**

$$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(2-0)^{2} + (1-0)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$$

$$U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 3 \cdot (\sqrt{5})^{2} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 5 = 7.5 \text{ energy units}$$

**(ii) Attractive force vector:**

$$F_{att} = -\zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal}) = -3 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix} \text{ force units}$$

**(iii) Force magnitude:**

$$\Vert F_{att} \Vert = \sqrt{(-6)^{2} + (-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{36 + 9} = \sqrt{45} \approx 6.708$$

Or equivalently: $\Vert F_{att} \Vert = \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal} = 3 \cdot \sqrt{5} \approx 6.708 \quad \checkmark$

**(iv) New configuration:**

$$q_{new} = q + F_{att} \cdot \Delta t = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ -3 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 0.1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 0.6 \\ 1 - 0.3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.4 \\ 0.7 \end{bmatrix}$$

The robot has moved from $(2, 1)$ to $(1.4, 0.7)$ — closer to the goal, as expected. **(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 1 for (iii), 2 for (iv))**

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 2, Q10]**

**(a)** Explain the **hybrid attractive potential** formulation. Why is it preferred over pure quadratic or pure conic forms in practical robot motion planning? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the attractive force expressions for both **quadratic** and **conic** forms. A robot is at distance $\rho = 10$ units from the goal with $\zeta = 0.8$. Compute and compare the force magnitudes for both forms. Comment on the engineering implications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **hybrid attractive potential** combines the strengths of both quadratic and conic forms by using a threshold distance $d^*$:

$$U_{att}^{hybrid}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}^{2} & \text{if } \rho_{goal} \leq d^{*} \\[8pt] d^{*} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal} - \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot d^{*2} & \text{if } \rho_{goal} > d^{*} \end{cases}$$

**Why preferred:**
1. **Near the goal** ($\rho \leq d^*$): the quadratic form provides a **smooth gradient** that approaches zero gracefully as the robot arrives — avoiding oscillations and overshoot.
2. **Far from the goal** ($\rho > d^*$): the conic form provides a **bounded constant force** magnitude equal to $\zeta \cdot d^*$, preventing actuator saturation and ensuring energy-efficient travel.
3. **Continuity**: the function is $C^1$-continuous (continuously differentiable) at the boundary $\rho = d^*$, ensuring smooth force transitions.
4. **Engineering practicality**: real robots have bounded motor torques and velocities; the hybrid form respects these physical limits while preserving planning accuracy. **(7 marks: 2 for formulation, 5 for justification)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Quadratic force derivation:**

Starting from $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} \zeta \rho^{2}$ and using $\rho^{2} = (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal})$:

$$\nabla U_{att} = \zeta (q - q_{goal})$$

$$F_{att}^{quad} = -\zeta (q - q_{goal})$$

**Conic force derivation:**

Starting from $U_{att} = \zeta \rho = \zeta \sqrt{(q-q_{goal})^{T}(q-q_{goal})}$:

$$\nabla U_{att} = \zeta \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert}$$

$$F_{att}^{conic} = -\zeta \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert} = -\zeta \cdot \hat{e}$$

**Numerical computation** with $\rho = 10$ and $\zeta = 0.8$:

$$\Vert F_{att}^{quad} \Vert = \zeta \cdot \rho = 0.8 \cdot 10 = 8.0 \text{ force units}$$

$$\Vert F_{att}^{conic} \Vert = \zeta = 0.8 \text{ force units}$$

**Engineering implications:**
- The **quadratic force** is **10× larger** than the conic force at this distance.
- For long-range motion, the quadratic form may demand **impractically large actuator effort** (torque, current, thrust), leading to **saturation** or **energy waste**.
- The conic form maintains a **constant, predictable demand** on actuators regardless of distance — ideal for cruise-phase navigation.
- The hybrid form (with appropriately chosen $d^*$, e.g., $d^* = 5$) would apply the conic formula for $\rho > 5$ and quadratic for $\rho \leq 5$, balancing both concerns. **(7 marks: 2 for quadratic derivation, 2 for conic derivation, 2 for numerical computation, 1 for comment)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
