> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$** in the quadratic potential: writing $U_{att} = \zeta \rho^{2}$ instead of $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} \zeta \rho^{2}$. The $\frac{1}{2}$ is essential — it makes the gradient cleanly equal to $\zeta(q-q_{goal})$ without an extra factor of 2.
> 2. **Sign error on the gradient**: writing $F_{att} = +\nabla U_{att}$ instead of $F_{att} = -\nabla U_{att}$. The force must point **toward** the goal (downhill), which requires the **negative** gradient.
> 3. **Confusing the conic force formula**: writing $F_{att}^{conic} = -\zeta(q-q_{goal})$ (which is the quadratic form!) instead of the correct normalized form $-\zeta \cdot \frac{q-q_{goal}}{\rho}$. The conic force is a **unit vector** scaled by $\zeta$.
> 4. **Ignoring units**: the gain $\zeta$ has units of force/length (for quadratic) or force (for conic). Always state units in numerical answers.
> 5. **Not commenting on boundedness**: when asked to "compare," KTU examiners expect a discussion of *why* the conic form is bounded while the quadratic is not — this is a high-value conceptual point worth 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the hybrid threshold $d^*$**: in questions about the hybrid form, failing to mention the role of $d^*$ as the switching threshold will lose at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Attractive Potential in APF**

- **Definition**: $U_{att}(q)$ is a goal-seeking scalar potential; lower energy near goal, higher energy far from goal. **(Must know)**
- **Quadratic form**: $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} \zeta \rho^{2}$ — smooth, unbounded force, linear in distance.
- **Conic form**: $U_{att} = \zeta \rho$ — sharp, bounded force $\zeta$, discontinuous gradient at goal.
- **Hybrid form**: switches at threshold $d^*$; quadratic inside, conic outside; **$C^1$-continuous**.
- **Force–potential relation**: $F_{att} = -\nabla U_{att}$ (negative gradient).
- **Quadratic force**: $F_{att} = -\zeta(q - q_{goal})$; magnitude $= \zeta \cdot \rho$.
- **Conic force**: $F_{att} = -\zeta \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\rho}$; magnitude $= \zeta$ (constant).
- **Gain parameter $\zeta$**: controls the "stiffness" of attraction; higher $\zeta$ = stronger pull.
- **Distance function**: $\rho = \Vert q - q_{goal} \Vert$ is the Euclidean norm.
- **Boundary condition**: $F_{att}(q_{goal}) = 0$ for both forms (robot stops at goal).
- **Conic singularity**: $F_{att}^{conic}$ is undefined at $q = q_{goal}$; define as zero by convention.
- **Practical use**: always combined with a repulsive potential $U_{rep}$ to form $U_{total} = U_{att} + U_{rep}$.
- **Engineering insight**: the quadratic form acts like a **virtual spring** (Hooke's law: $F = -kx$ with $k = \zeta$).
- **KTU exam focus**: formula derivations, comparison tables, numerical computations, hybrid form justification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repulsive Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Repulsive Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method of robot motion planning, the **Repulsive Potential** is a scalar potential energy function defined in the robot's configuration (or Cartesian) workspace that generates a **repulsive vector field** around obstacles. Its negative gradient, $-\nabla U_{rep}(q)$, yields the **repulsive force** that pushes the robot away from obstacle surfaces, ensuring collision avoidance during trajectory generation.

The classical formulation, introduced by **Khatib (1986)** in the context of real-time obstacle avoidance for manipulators, treats each obstacle as a source of positive potential energy that decays with distance. As the robot's pose $q$ approaches the obstacle boundary, the potential grows without bound (theoretically to **infinity**), creating an impenetrable "energy barrier."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Repulsive potential is studied under *Module 2: Potential Field Methods* and forms the cornerstone of reactive/online motion planners. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate both the **piecewise definition** (inside vs. outside the influence region) and the **gradient derivation** clearly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Build-Up)

Imagine a heated metal ball rolling on a smooth tabletop that contains several **"danger zones"** (obstacles). Around each danger zone, we install an invisible **upward air-jet blower**:
- The closer the ball gets to the jet, the **stronger the upward push** it experiences.
- Beyond a certain radius $\rho_0$ (the **influence distance**), the blower is switched **OFF**.
- The ball is also drawn toward a **goal magnet** (the *attractive potential*).

The combined effect of the goal's pull and the obstacles' push guides the ball safely toward the target. Mathematically, the upward blower's "pressure" is the repulsive potential $U_{rep}$, and the "force" on the ball is $-\nabla U_{rep}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The **attractive potential** is typically defined over the *entire* workspace (global), but the **repulsive potential is *local*** — it acts only within a bounded influence radius $\rho_0$ around each obstacle.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **$\rho$**: shortest Euclidean distance from the robot to the nearest obstacle surface.
- **$\rho_0$**: the **distance of influence** (a tunable positive scalar; typically $1.5\times$ to $2\times$ the robot's footprint radius).
- **$\eta > 0$**: the **repulsive gain** (positive scaling constant controlling repulsion strength).
- **$U_{rep}(q) \geq 0$**: always non-negative (potential is defined to be $\geq 0$ everywhere).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

The repulsive potential $U_{rep}(q)$ must satisfy **four engineering requirements**:

1. **Non-negativity**: $U_{rep}(q) \geq 0$ for all $q \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$.
2. **Monotonic decay with distance**: as $\rho \to \rho_0$, $U_{rep} \to 0$ (smooth transition).
3. **Singularity at the boundary**: as $\rho \to 0$ (collision), $U_{rep} \to \infty$ (theoretically impenetrable barrier).
4. **Locality**: $U_{rep}(q) = 0$ whenever $\rho \geq \rho_0$ (no effect outside influence radius).

To satisfy all four properties simultaneously, the **Khatib quadratic-inverse form** is used:

- **Step 1 — Define the "active" region**: identify obstacles whose minimum distance $\rho$ from the robot is less than $\rho_0$.
- **Step 2 — Compute the proximity ratio**: evaluate $\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$. This ratio is **positive** when $\rho < \rho_0$ and **zero** when $\rho = \rho_0$.
- **Step 3 — Square the ratio**: $\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ enforces smoothness at the boundary and a quadratic rise near collision.
- **Step 4 — Scale by $\frac{1}{2}\eta$**: tunes the magnitude to match robot dynamics and obstacle density.
- **Step 5 — Take the negative gradient**: $\vec{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep}(q)$ produces a vector pointing **away** from the obstacle (since the gradient points toward increasing $U$, which is toward the obstacle).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Mathematical Form** | **Units / Range** |
|------------|-------------|-----------------------|-------------------|
| $U_{rep}(q)$ | Repulsive Potential (piecewise) | $\frac{1}{2}\eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2$ if $\rho \leq \rho_0$; else $0$ | Energy (Joules) |
| $\rho(q)$ | Distance to nearest obstacle | $\rho(q) = \min_{o \in \text{Obst}} \Vert q - q_o \Vert$ | metres (m) |
| $\rho_0$ | Influence distance | Tunable, $> 0$ | metres (m) |
| $\eta$ | Repulsive gain | Tunable, $> 0$ | N·m or dimensionless |
| $\vec{F}_{rep}$ | Repulsive Force (vector) | $-\nabla U_{rep}(q)$ | Newtons (N) |
| $\nabla U_{rep}$ | Gradient of $U_{rep}$ (in 2-D, $q=[x,y]^T$) | $\left[\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x},\; \frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial y}\right]^T$ | N |
| $\partial \rho / \partial x$ | Partial derivative of distance w.r.t. $x$ | $(x - x_{obs})/\rho$ | dimensionless |
| $\partial \rho / \partial y$ | Partial derivative of distance w.r.t. $y$ | $(y - y_{obs})/\rho$ | dimensionless |
| $U_{tot}$ | Total potential | $U_{att}(q) + \sum_k U_{rep}^{(k)}(q)$ | Energy |
| $\vec{F}_{tot}$ | Total force on robot | $\vec{F}_{att} + \sum_k \vec{F}_{rep}^{(k)}$ | Newtons (N) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pitfall Note:** When $\rho > \rho_0$, $U_{rep} = 0$ **AND** $\vec{F}_{rep} = \vec{0}$. Many students incorrectly write $\vec{F}_{rep}$ as non-zero outside the influence region.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial robotic manipulators**: used in real-time collision avoidance alongside Cartesian-attractive pull toward the goal pose.
- **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs)**: repulsive potentials from LIDAR-detected obstacles combined with goal attraction form the basis of dynamic-window approaches.
- **Drone/UAV swarms**: inter-agent repulsion prevents mid-air collisions while goal-attraction maintains formation convergence.
- **Surgical robots**: repulsive zones around critical anatomical structures (e.g., nerves, vessels) ensure safe tool-path planning.
- **Warehouse robots (AMRs)**: navigate dynamically changing obstacle fields in fulfilment centres.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Repulsive Force Vector in 2-D Workspace

We consider a robot at position $q = (x, y)$ and a single circular obstacle centred at $q_{obs} = (x_{obs}, y_{obs})$. The squared distance is:

$$\rho^2 = (x - x_{obs})^2 + (y - y_{obs})^2$$

The Euclidean distance is therefore $\rho = \sqrt{(x-x_{obs})^2 + (y-y_{obs})^2}$.

**Step 1 — Repulsive potential definition (piecewise form):**

$$
U_{rep}(x,y) = 
\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{2}\,\eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \rho \leq \rho_0 \\[6pt]
0, & \rho > \rho_0
\end{cases}
$$

Outside the influence zone, the potential is identically zero, so the gradient is also zero. We therefore focus only on the region $\rho \leq \rho_0$.

**Step 2 — Define the scalar auxiliary function $f(\rho)$ to simplify differentiation:**

$$
f(\rho) = \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}
$$

Then the potential is $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2}\eta\, f(\rho)^2$.

**Step 3 — Apply the multivariable chain rule:**

For any coordinate $x_i \in \{x, y\}$:

$$
\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x_i} \;=\; \eta\, f(\rho)\, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate $f(\rho)$ with respect to $x_i$ via chain rule:**

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\!\left(\rho^{-1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\,\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x_i}
$$

(Note that $1/\rho_0$ is a constant, so its derivative vanishes.)

**Step 5 — Compute the partial derivative of $\rho$ w.r.t. $x_i$:**

Differentiate $\rho = \sqrt{(x-x_{obs})^2+(y-y_{obs})^2}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} \;=\; \frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho}, \qquad \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} \;=\; \frac{y - y_{obs}}{\rho}
$$

**Step 6 — Assemble $\partial U_{rep}/\partial x$:**

$$
\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} \;=\; \eta\, f(\rho)\,\left(-\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\right)\,\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}
$$

Substituting $f(\rho)$ and $\partial \rho/\partial x$:

$$
\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} \;=\; \eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\right) \cdot \frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} \;=\; -\,\eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho^{3}}
$$

**Step 7 — Symmetrically obtain $\partial U_{rep}/\partial y$:**

$$
\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial y} \;=\; -\,\eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{y - y_{obs}}{\rho^{3}}
$$

**Step 8 — Form the repulsive force vector (force = negative gradient):**

$$
\vec{F}_{rep} \;=\; -\nabla U_{rep} \;=\; -\begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial y} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting the partial derivatives and absorbing the minus sign:

$$
\vec{F}_{rep} \;=\; \eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{3}} \begin{bmatrix} x - x_{obs} \\[6pt] y - y_{obs} \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The vector $\begin{bmatrix} x-x_{obs} \\ y-y_{obs} \end{bmatrix}$ points **from the obstacle toward the robot**. Multiplying by the positive scalar $\eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{3}}$ (which is positive when $\rho < \rho_0$) yields a force pointing **away from the obstacle**, i.e., repulsive. ✓

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Behavioural Cases

| **Case** | **Condition** | **Value of $U_{rep}$** | **Value of $\vert \vec{F}_{rep} \vert$** | **Physical Meaning** |
|----------|---------------|------------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Safe distance | $\rho > \rho_0$ | $0$ | $0$ | No interaction; free motion |
| Boundary | $\rho = \rho_0$ | $0$ | $0$ | Smooth transition; $C^1$ continuity |
| Mid-influence | $0 < \rho < \rho_0$ | $\frac{1}{2}\eta\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}$ | $\eta\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}$ | Active repulsion |
| Grazing contact | $\rho \to 0^{+}$ | $\to \infty$ | $\to \infty$ | Impenetrable barrier |
| At $\rho_0/2$ | Numerical example | $\frac{1}{2}\eta\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{\!2}$ | $\eta/\rho_0^{3}$ | Reference magnitude |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix Use)

**Given:** Robot at $q=(2,3)$, obstacle centre $q_{obs}=(1,1)$, $\rho_0=2$ m, $\eta=10$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\rho$:**

$$
\rho = \sqrt{(2-1)^2+(3-1)^2} = \sqrt{1+4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Test the condition:** $\rho = 2.236 > \rho_0 = 2$. Hence the robot is **outside** the influence region.

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** $U_{rep} = 0$ and $\vec{F}_{rep} = \vec{0}$. No repulsion acts on the robot from this obstacle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** If the robot were at $q=(1.5, 1.5)$, then $\rho=\sqrt{0.25+0.25}=\sqrt{0.5}\approx 0.707 < 2$, and the full formula would be active, producing a strong outward force.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Repulsive Potential Computation

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of computing the repulsive force on a robot at each planning tick. This avoids raw geometric drawing constraints and instead maps the algorithm's logical architecture — a standard KTU-acceptable schematic style.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[INPUT: Robot Pose q = x,y] --> nodeB[INPUT: Obstacle Database]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Shortest Distance rho to nearest obstacle]
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD{Is rho less than or equal to rho0}
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeE[Set Urep equals 0]
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeF[Compute Proximity Ratio: 1 over rho minus 1 over rho0]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute Urep = half eta times ratio squared]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute partial Urep over partial x]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Compute partial Urep over partial y]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Assemble Repulsive Force Vector Frep]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeK[OUTPUT: Frep equals zero vector]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[OUTPUT: Frep points away from obstacle]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Merge with Attractive Force Fatt]
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[OUTPUT: Total Force Ftot drives robot motion]
```

### 4.2 Influence Region Schematic (Conceptual Block Diagram)

The diagram below depicts the **local influence region** of an obstacle and how the repulsive potential decays.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeO[Obstacle Centre] --> nodeR[Radius rho0 Influence Boundary]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Smooth Transition: Urep equals 0]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Active Region: 0 less than rho less than rho0]
    nodeT --> nodeU[Repulsive Force rises as 1 over rho cubed]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Near boundary rho approaches 0: Urep approaches infinity]
```

### 4.3 Force Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ[Robot Configuration q] --> nodeW[Total Potential Utot = Uatt plus Urep]
    nodeW --> nodeX[Compute Gradient of Utot]
    nodeX --> nodeY[Total Force Ftot = negative Gradient Utot]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Output: Ftot = Fatt plus Frep]
    nodeZ --> nodeAA[Robot Dynamics Integration]
    nodeAA --> nodeBB[New Configuration q new]
    nodeBB --> nodeQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangular node is an algorithmic operation; arrows show the data/control flow. The conditional diamond (`{...}`) represents the piecewise split between the influence region and the safe region.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State and explain the piecewise definition of the repulsive potential $U_{rep}(q)$ used in the artificial potential field method. Highlight the role of the influence distance $\rho_0$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The repulsive potential is defined piecewise as:

$$
U_{rep}(q) = 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{2}\,\eta\left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[4pt]
0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0
\end{cases}
$$

where $\rho(q)$ is the shortest distance from the robot to the nearest obstacle, $\rho_0$ is the **distance of influence** (a tunable positive scalar), and $\eta > 0$ is the **repulsive gain**.

**Role of $\rho_0$:** It localises the repulsive action. When $\rho \geq \rho_0$, $U_{rep}=0$, ensuring the robot is unaffected by distant obstacles. When $\rho < \rho_0$, the potential grows rapidly as the robot approaches the obstacle, producing a repulsive force that scales as $1/\rho^{3}$ — strong enough to prevent collision but smooth at the boundary. *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for parameter explanation, 1 for locality role)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the repulsive force computed as $-\nabla U_{rep}(q)$ and not as $+\nabla U_{rep}(q)$? Justify with the direction of the resulting force vector.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The gradient $\nabla U_{rep}(q)$ always points in the direction of **steepest ascent** of the potential. Since $U_{rep}$ is maximum at the obstacle surface (the "peak" of the energy hill), $\nabla U_{rep}$ points **from the robot toward the obstacle**. The robot, however, must be pushed **away** from the obstacle, i.e., in the direction of steepest **descent**.

Hence, the repulsive force must be the **negative** gradient:
$$
\vec{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep}
$$
This ensures $\vec{F}_{rep}$ points in the direction of decreasing potential — **outward from the obstacle** — thereby achieving collision avoidance. *(3 marks: 1 for direction of gradient, 1 for requirement of outward force, 1 for final vector direction)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, *Module 2* internal-choice questions are presented below. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Derive the repulsive force vector $\vec{F}_{rep}$ in the 2-D Cartesian workspace for a single circular obstacle. Clearly show all steps, the chain rule, and the final vector expression. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robot at $q = (1, 1)$ must navigate around a circular obstacle centred at $(3, 3)$ with influence radius $\rho_0 = 2$ m. Take $\eta = 8$. Compute the repulsive force vector and determine its magnitude and direction. Comment on whether the robot is being repelled. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

*(Derivation follows Section 3.1 of these notes verbatim. Key marks allocation:)*

1. **Statement of the potential** — *1 mark* (piecewise definition).
2. **Definition of $f(\rho)$ and chain rule application** — *2 marks* (intermediate steps).
3. **Computation of $\partial \rho/\partial x$ and $\partial \rho/\partial y$** — *2 marks* (geometric derivation).
4. **Final vector assembly** — *2 marks* (assembling $\vec{F}_{rep}$ with negative gradient sign).

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\rho$:**

$$
\rho = \sqrt{(1-3)^2 + (1-3)^2} = \sqrt{4+4} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Test influence condition:** $\rho = 2.828 > \rho_0 = 2$.

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** Robot is **outside** the influence zone, therefore:

$$
U_{rep} = 0, \qquad \vec{F}_{rep} = \vec{0}
$$

**Comment:** The obstacle does **not** exert any repulsive force on the robot at this configuration; the robot would be guided solely by the attractive force toward its goal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A(b)):** Many students blindly substitute numbers into the active-region formula without **first checking** whether $\rho \leq \rho_0$. This immediately costs **3 out of 7 marks**. Always evaluate the piecewise condition first.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain the four engineering requirements that a well-designed repulsive potential function must satisfy. Show how the Khatib form $\frac{1}{2}\eta(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)^2$ meets each requirement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot operates at $q = (0.5, 0.5)$ with a circular obstacle centred at $(1.5, 1.5)$. Given $\eta = 5$ and $\rho_0 = 2$ m, compute the magnitude of the repulsive force on the robot. State whether the force is sufficient to keep the robot from contacting the obstacle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

The four requirements and how Khatib's form satisfies them:

1. **Non-negativity:** Squaring $\left(\frac{1}{\rho}-\frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)$ guarantees the result is $\geq 0$. The factor $\frac{1}{2}\eta > 0$ preserves sign. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Monotonic decay with distance:** As $\rho \to \rho_0$, the term $\frac{1}{\rho} \to \frac{1}{\rho_0}$, so the bracket tends to zero. Hence $U_{rep} \to 0$. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Singularity at collision:** As $\rho \to 0^+$, the term $\frac{1}{\rho} \to \infty$, so $U_{rep} \to \infty$, creating an impenetrable barrier. *(2 marks)*
4. **Locality:** The piecewise definition sets $U_{rep} = 0$ identically for $\rho > \rho_0$, ensuring the function only acts within a bounded influence zone. *(2 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\rho$:**

$$
\rho = \sqrt{(0.5-1.5)^2+(0.5-1.5)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Check influence condition:** $\rho = 1.414 < \rho_0 = 2$. ✓ Robot is **inside** the active region.

**Step 3 — Compute the scalar coefficient:**

$$
\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} = \frac{1}{1.414} - \frac{1}{2} = 0.7071 - 0.5000 = 0.2071
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of repulsive force:**

$$
\vert \vec{F}_{rep} \vert \;=\; \eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{2}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\vert \vec{F}_{rep} \vert \;=\; 5 \times 0.2071 \times \frac{1}{(1.414)^{2}} \;=\; 5 \times 0.2071 \times 0.5
$$

$$
\vert \vec{F}_{rep} \vert \;=\; 5 \times 0.10355 \;\approx\; 0.518 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Direction:** $\vec{F}_{rep}$ points along the vector $(0.5-1.5,\; 0.5-1.5) = (-1,\,-1)$, i.e., outward along the southwest diagonal.

**Step 6 — Sufficiency comment:** The magnitude (0.518 N) is **non-zero**, confirming the obstacle is actively repelling the robot. Whether this is *sufficient* depends on the robot's mass and current velocity; in typical KTU problems, the non-zero magnitude is sufficient evidence of active avoidance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B(b)):** A common error is computing only the **potential** $U_{rep}$ instead of the **force magnitude**. Remember: force magnitude involves the product $\eta \cdot (1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)/\rho^2$, **not** $U_{rep}$ itself. This mistake costs **2 marks** instantly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Repulsive potential is *local*:** it acts only within the influence radius $\rho_0$ around each obstacle.
- ✅ **Piecewise definition is mandatory:** $U_{rep} = 0$ when $\rho > \rho_0$ — do not omit this case in derivations.
- ✅ **Force = negative gradient:** $\vec{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep}(q)$ points **away** from the obstacle (in the direction of decreasing $U_{rep}$).
- ✅ **Magnitude scaling:** Force magnitude grows as $\eta(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)/\rho^2$, i.e., approximately as $1/\rho^3$ near the obstacle.
- ✅ **Boundary continuity:** $U_{rep}$ and $\vec{F}_{rep}$ both vanish at $\rho = \rho_0$, ensuring smooth handoff to free-space motion.
- ✅ **Singularity at $\rho = 0$:** the potential diverges to $+\infty$, modelling an impenetrable obstacle (collision is impossible in theory).
- ✅ **Tuning parameters:** $\eta$ controls repulsion strength; $\rho_0$ controls the spatial extent of influence. Both must be tuned to avoid *local minima* traps.
- ✅ **Combine with attractive potential:** $U_{tot} = U_{att} + U_{rep}$; total force drives the robot via $\vec{F}_{tot} = -\nabla U_{tot}$.
- ✅ **Common pitfalls:** (1) Forgetting the piecewise split; (2) using $+\nabla U_{rep}$ instead of $-\nabla U_{rep}$; (3) reporting potential energy when the question asks for force; (4) neglecting to verify $\rho \leq \rho_0$ before substitution.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** manipulator collision avoidance, AGV navigation, UAV swarms, surgical robotics, warehouse AMRs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark derivation question, examiners award **partial credit** for each correct intermediate step (chain rule setup, partial derivatives of $\rho$, gradient assembly). Even if the final vector sign is wrong, you can still score 4–5 marks by showing correct intermediate work. **Always show all steps.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Sampling-Based Planners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Sampling-Based Planners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling-Based Motion Planners (SBMPs)** are a class of robot motion planning algorithms that construct a roadmap or tree in the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ by drawing **random samples** (typically from a uniform or biased distribution) and connecting them with **local planners** to form a connected graph. They avoid the explicit construction of $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ and instead rely on **single-query or multi-query collision checking** of edges and vertices.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, sampling-based planners are characterized as the **workhorse of modern high-dimensional motion planning** because they shift the computational burden from explicit geometric reasoning to a large number of simple **probabilistic queries** and **collision checks**.

The two canonical families are:
- **Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM)** – Multi-query, builds a graph beforehand.
- **Rapidly-exploring Random Trees (RRT / RRT\*)** – Single-query, incrementally grows a tree from the start.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Exploring a Dark Maze with a Blindfold**
> 
> Imagine you are dropped blindfolded into a giant, pitch-black maze. You cannot see the walls, but every time you stretch your arm out, a sensor tells you whether you have hit a wall. You randomly swing in different directions, marking each "safe spot" you find, and connect safe spots that are within reach. Over time, your chain of safe spots covers the maze, and you can trace a path from entry to exit.
> 
> This is exactly how a **RRT** works: the *random samples* are your arm swings, the *collision check* is the wall sensor, and the *tree edges* are the connections between safe spots.

## 1.3 The Seven Defining Characteristics (At a Glance)

| # | Characteristic | One-Line Meaning |
|---|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Probabilistic Completeness** | Probability of finding a solution → 1 as samples → ∞ |
| 2 | **Asymptotic Optimality** (RRT\*) | Cost of solution → optimal cost as samples → ∞ |
| 3 | **Resolution Independence** | Does not require a fixed grid resolution |
| 4 | **Any-Time Property** | Can return the best-so-far solution at any time |
| 5 | **Scalability with Dimension** | Performance degrades gracefully in high-D |
| 6 | **Weakness: Narrow Passages** | Can struggle in "tight" corridors of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ |
| 7 | **Holonomic/Non-Holonomic Capable** | Can be extended with steering functions |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Characteristic 1 — Probabilistic Completeness

A planner is **probabilistically complete** if, for any feasible path $\sigma^*$ that exists in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, the probability that the planner returns a solution approaches **unity** as the number of samples $n \to \infty$.

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\text{planner returns a solution} \mid \sigma^* \text{ exists}) = 1$$

**Why this matters (KTU insight):** Unlike *combinatorial* planners (e.g., C-Space exact decomposition) which are *resolution-complete* and *deterministically complete*, sampling planners trade **determinism for tractability** in high dimensions.

## 2.2 Characteristic 2 — Asymptotic Optimality

Introduced formally with **RRT\*** (Karaman & Frazzoli, 2011), a planner is *asymptotically optimal* if the cost $c_n$ of the returned solution converges to the optimal cost $c^*$ in probability:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(c_n \leq (1 + \varepsilon)\, c^*) = 1, \quad \forall \, \varepsilon > 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RRT** is *probabilistically complete* but **NOT asymptotically optimal**.
> **RRT\*** is **both** probabilistically complete **and** asymptotically optimal.

## 2.3 Characteristic 3 — Sample Complexity (Why They Scale Poorly in High-D)

A fundamental theoretical result by Hsu et al. gives the number of samples required to achieve $\varepsilon$-coverage of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ in $d$ dimensions:

$$N(\varepsilon) = O\!\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon^{\,d}}\right) \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{\delta}\right)$$

where:
- $\varepsilon$ = clearance parameter (smallest passage width the planner must thread)
- $\delta$ = failure probability
- $d$ = dimension of $\mathcal{C}$

**Interpretation:** Sample count grows **exponentially with dimension** — this is the *curse of dimensionality* made explicit.

## 2.4 Characteristic 4 — The RRT\* Rewiring Radius

To achieve asymptotic optimality, RRT\* rewires new nodes within a ball of shrinking radius:

$$r_n = \gamma \cdot \left(\frac{\log n}{n}\right)^{\!1/d}$$

where $\gamma$ is a constant depending on the **Voronoi bias** and $d$ is the configuration space dimension. As $n$ grows, $r_n \to 0$ but slowly enough to ensure global connectivity.

## 2.5 Characteristic 5 — Voronoi Bias (Why RRTs "Grow Toward Uncovered Regions")

At each iteration, a new sample $x_{rand}$ is drawn, and the **nearest node** $x_{near}$ in the tree is found. The probability that $x_{near}$ is selected is **proportional to the volume of its Voronoi cell**:

$$P(x_{near}) = \frac{\text{Vol}(V(x_{near}))}{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C})}$$

This causes the tree to grow **toward unexplored regions** — a beautiful self-organizing property.

## 2.6 Characteristic 6 — Narrow Passage Weakness

If the $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ region containing start or goal is separated from the rest by a *narrow passage* of width $w \ll 1$, the probability of drawing a sample inside it is:

$$P(\text{hit narrow passage}) \approx \frac{w^d}{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C}_{free})}$$

This is the primary motivation for **Gaussian sampling**, **bridge sampling**, and **obstacle-based sampling** variants.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---|--------------------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $\lim_{n\to\infty} P(\text{success}) = 1$ | Probabilistic completeness | dimensionless probability |
| 2 | $\lim_{n\to\infty} P(c_n \leq (1+\varepsilon)c^*) = 1$ | Asymptotic optimality | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N(\varepsilon) = O(1/\varepsilon^d)\ln(1/\delta)$ | Sample complexity | samples |
| 4 | $r_n = \gamma(\log n / n)^{1/d}$ | RRT\* rewiring radius | length |
| 5 | $P(x_{near}) = \text{Vol}(V)/\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C})$ | Voronoi bias | probability |
| 6 | $P(\text{hit}) \approx w^d / \text{Vol}(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ | Narrow passage probability | probability |
| 7 | $\eta = \gamma \cdot (\log n / n)^{1/d}$ | Connection radius | length |
| 8 | $\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{plan}}] \propto N \cdot T_{\text{check}}$ | Expected planning time | seconds |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sampling-based planners are deployed in:
- **Autonomous vehicles** (motion planning in parking, lane-change maneuvers)
- **Robotic surgery** (da Vinci system needle/camera paths)
- **Warehouse AMRs** (mobile robots in Amazon/Kiva fulfillment centers)
- **Spacecraft rendezvous** (NASA's next-gen autonomous docking)
- **Quadrotor drone navigation** (Crazyflie, PX4 autopilots)
- **Humanoid robot footstep planning** (Atlas, Digit, HRP series)

> [!NOTE]
> Every industrial robotic system in production today that plans in **more than 4 configuration dimensions** almost certainly uses some form of sampling-based planner.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Probabilistic Completeness of RRT

We formally show that the RRT algorithm finds a solution with probability approaching 1.

**Step 1 — Define the event of failure.**

Let $F_n$ = event that RRT has **not** found a path after $n$ iterations. We want to bound $P(F_n)$.

**Step 2 — Identify the "lucky event".**

A *lucky event* $L$ occurs at iteration $i$ if:
- The random sample $x_{rand}$ lies within the *clearance volume* $\mathcal{B}_{\varepsilon}$ of the optimal path $\sigma^*$, AND
- The nearest tree node $x_{near}$ is within the *connection radius* $\eta$ of $x_{rand}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the probability of the lucky event.**

The probability that a uniform sample lands in the $\varepsilon$-tube around $\sigma^*$ is:

$$p_1 = \frac{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{B}_{\varepsilon}(\sigma^*))}{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C})}$$

The probability that the nearest node can connect (within radius $\eta$) is at least $p_2$ (a geometric constant depending on $\eta$ and the Voronoi cell structure).

Thus:

$$P(L) \geq p_1 \cdot p_2 = p > 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the geometric (independent) lower bound.**

Since each iteration's lucky event is approximately independent:

$$P(F_n) \leq (1 - p)^n$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(F_n) \leq \lim_{n \to \infty} (1-p)^n = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\text{success}) = 1 - \lim_{n \to \infty} P(F_n) = 1$$

This is the formal proof of **probabilistic completeness** of RRT. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Asymptotic Optimality of RRT\*

We now show that the cost converges to the optimum in probability.

**Step 1 — Define the optimal cost and current cost.**

Let $c^*$ = cost of the optimal path, $c_n$ = cost of best path found by RRT\* after $n$ iterations.

We want to show:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(c_n > (1+\varepsilon)c^*) = 0 \quad \forall \varepsilon > 0$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the path into segments.**

The optimal path $\sigma^*$ is discretized into $k = \lceil L(\sigma^*)/\delta \rceil$ subsegments of length $\delta$, where $L$ is path length.

**Step 3 — Required number of samples for $\delta$-coverage.**

By a covering argument, the number of samples needed so that every subsegment has at least one tree node within distance $\delta$ is:

$$N(\delta) = O\!\left(\frac{1}{\delta^{\,d}} \ln\frac{1}{\delta}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Cost of the resulting path.**

When the tree is $\delta$-dense along $\sigma^*$, RRT\*'s rewiring rule (using $r_n = \gamma(\log n / n)^{1/d}$) produces a path of cost:

$$c_n \leq c^* + k \cdot \delta \cdot L(\sigma^*) = c^* + L(\sigma^*)^2 / \delta$$

But as $n \to \infty$, the effective $\delta \to 0$, so:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} c_n = c^*$$

**Step 5 — Express the bound in probability form.**

For any $\varepsilon > 0$, choose $\delta$ small enough that $L(\sigma^*)^2/\delta < \varepsilon \, c^*$. Then:

$$P(c_n \leq (1+\varepsilon)c^*) \geq 1 - P(\text{coverage fails}) \to 1$$

This proves **asymptotic optimality** of RRT\*. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Voronoi Bias Probability

**Step 1 — Setup.**

Let the tree $T = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ partition $\mathcal{C}$ into Voronoi cells $V(x_i)$ where:

$$V(x_i) = \{x \in \mathcal{C} \mid \|x - x_i\| \leq \|x - x_j\|, \forall j \neq i\}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of selection.**

For a uniform random sample $x_{rand}$:

$$P(x_i \text{ is nearest}) = \frac{\text{Vol}(V(x_i))}{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C})}$$

**Step 3 — Extend from $x_{near}$ toward $x_{rand}$.**

The new node is placed:

$$x_{new} = x_{near} + \eta \cdot \frac{x_{rand} - x_{near}}{\|x_{rand} - x_{near}\|}$$

with $\eta$ = step size. This pulls the tree *toward* the boundary of $V(x_{near})$, which is exactly the unexplored region. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sampler Behavior

| Sampling Strategy | $P(\text{coverage})$ | Best Use Case | Drawback |
|-------------------|----------------------|---------------|----------|
| **Uniform** | $\propto \text{Vol}(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ | Open spaces | Fails in narrow passages |
| **Gaussian** | Higher near obstacles | Moderate passages | Needs obstacle-aware |
| **Bridge** | Tunable to passage width | Tight passages | Computational overhead |
| **Obstacle-Based** | $\propto$ surface area | Cluttered scenes | Biased toward surfaces |
| **Goal-Biased** | $\propto \varepsilon_g$ | Known goal | Reduces exploration |
| **Hybrid (RRT\* smarts)** | Maximized | Most real cases | Implementation complexity |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — General Architecture of a Sampling-Based Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input x_start, x_goal] --> B[Initialize Roadmap or Tree T]
    B --> C{Sampling Budget n Exceeded?}
    C -- No --> D[Draw Random Sample x_rand]
    D --> E[Find Nearest Node x_near in T]
    E --> F[Steer: Compute x_new from x_near toward x_rand]
    F --> G{Edge x_near to x_new Collision Free?}
    G -- No --> C
    G -- Yes --> H[Add x_new to T]
    H --> I[Optional: Rewire Neighbors within r_n]
    I --> J{Goal Reached or Connected?}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Extract Path via Graph Search]
    K --> L[Output: Feasible Path sigma]
    C -- Yes --> M[Return Failure or Best So Far]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The loop on the right is the *inner sampling loop*. Every iteration draws one random sample, attempts a *steering* step, performs *collision checking*, and (in optimal variants like RRT\*) performs a *rewiring* step. The planner terminates when either the goal is reached, the sampling budget is exhausted, or an any-time criterion is met.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — RRT vs RRT\* Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RRT[RRT Algorithm]
        A1[Add x_new] --> A2[Done: Return Path]
    end

    subgraph RRTStar[RRT* Algorithm]
        B1[Add x_new] --> B2[Find Near Neighbors in Ball r_n]
        B2 --> B3[Choose Parent Min Cost]
        B3 --> B4[Rewire Neighbors if Cost Improves]
        B4 --> B5[Done: Return Lowest Cost Path]
    end

    RRT -- Probabilistically Complete --> Result1[Suboptimal but Fast]
    RRTStar -- Asymptotically Optimal --> Result2[Optimal but Slower]
```

**Key takeaway:** The *rewiring step* in RRT\* is what converts a probabilistically complete planner into an asymptotically optimal one. The trade-off is increased per-iteration cost (from $O(\log n)$ neighbors in 2D to higher in high-D).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Voronoi Bias Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ConfigSpace[Configuration Space C]
        direction TB
        V1[Voronoi Cell of Node A - Large] --> N1[Node A: Unlikely Selection]
        V2[Voronoi Cell of Node B - Small] --> N2[Node B: Likely Selection]
        V3[Voronoi Cell of Node C - Frontier] --> N3[Node C: Selected - Tree Extends Here]
    end

    N3 --> Extend[New Node D Added Toward Uncovered Region]
    Extend --> Frontier[Frontier Grows Naturally]
```

**Interpretation:** Nodes on the *boundary* of the existing tree (small Voronoi cells on the inside, large cells on the outside) get the most extension because samples are more likely to land far from them. This is the *self-exploring* nature of RRT.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Sampling Strategy Trade-off Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Sampling Strategy] --> Q1{Is Obstacle Density Known?}
    Q1 -- No --> Uniform[Use Uniform Sampling]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Narrow Passages Present?}
    Q2 -- No --> GoalBias[Use Goal-Biased Sampling]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Bridge[Use Bridge Sampling or Gaussian]
    Bridge --> Hybrid[Combine with RRT* Rewiring]
    GoalBias --> Hybrid
    Uniform --> Hybrid
    Hybrid --> Output[Output Optimized Trajectory]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define probabilistic completeness. How does it differ from resolution completeness?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Probabilistic Completeness:** A motion planner is probabilistically complete if the probability that it returns a solution approaches **1** as the number of samples $n \to \infty$, *provided* a solution exists.
> 
> **Resolution Completeness:** A planner is resolution-complete if it finds a solution whenever one exists *at or above* a pre-specified resolution (e.g., grid spacing $h$); otherwise, it may fail deterministically.
> 
> **Key difference:** Probabilistic completeness is a *limit guarantee* with no fixed resolution, while resolution completeness requires a *priori discretization* and gives a *deterministic* yes/no answer.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the Voronoi bias in RRT? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Voronoi bias** is the property of RRT where the probability of extending from a node $x_i$ is proportional to the volume of its Voronoi cell $V(x_i)$. Nodes with *larger* Voronoi cells (typically on the *frontier* of the tree) are more likely to be extended toward. 
> 
> **Importance:** It causes RRT to **automatically explore unvisited regions** of $\mathcal{C}$ without explicit frontier detection, making it both computationally efficient and self-organizing. This is the fundamental reason RRT does not get "stuck" in already-explored areas.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU ESE Module 3 Choice Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the characteristics of sampling-based motion planners. Discuss their **probabilistic completeness** and **asymptotic optimality** with suitable mathematical justification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a):**

Sampling-based motion planners (SBMPs) such as **PRM**, **RRT**, and **RRT\*** share a common set of characteristics that distinguish them from classical combinatorial planners.

**(1) Probabilistic Completeness:**
A planner is probabilistically complete if, given a feasible path $\sigma^*$ exists in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\text{planner succeeds}) = 1$$

The proof proceeds by identifying a "lucky event" — a sample landing in the $\varepsilon$-clearance tube around $\sigma^*$ with probability $p > 0$. Failure probability after $n$ iterations is bounded by $(1-p)^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Hence RRT is *probabilistically complete*.

**(2) Asymptotic Optimality:**
A planner is asymptotically optimal if the returned cost $c_n$ satisfies:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(c_n \leq (1+\varepsilon)c^*) = 1, \quad \forall \varepsilon > 0$$

**RRT** alone is **NOT** asymptotically optimal. **RRT\*** achieves this by **rewiring** newly added nodes within a shrinking radius $r_n = \gamma(\log n / n)^{1/d}$ to ensure local optimality, which provably converges to global optimality as $n \to \infty$.

**(3) Other characteristics:**
- Resolution independence
- Anytime behavior
- Scalability (with $O(1/\varepsilon^d)$ sample complexity)
- Weakness in narrow passages

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the **Voronoi bias probability** for RRT extension and explain how it leads to efficient exploration of the configuration space. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b):**

The RRT tree $T = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ partitions $\mathcal{C}$ into Voronoi cells:

$$V(x_i) = \{x \in \mathcal{C} : \|x - x_i\| \leq \|x - x_j\|, \forall j \neq i\}$$

For a uniform random sample $x_{rand} \in \mathcal{C}$, the probability that $x_i$ is selected as $x_{near}$ is:

$$P(x_i \text{ selected}) = \frac{\text{Vol}(V(x_i))}{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C})}$$

**Implication:** Nodes on the *boundary* of the tree (where Voronoi cells extend into unexplored regions) have **larger** cell volumes and are more likely to be extended. Interior nodes (surrounded by the tree) have **smaller** cells and are rarely chosen. The new node is placed at:

$$x_{new} = x_{near} + \eta \cdot \frac{x_{rand} - x_{near}}{\|x_{rand} - x_{near}\|}$$

which pushes the tree boundary outward. This **self-organizing frontier expansion** is why RRT explores the configuration space efficiently without explicit bookkeeping.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q. (a)** Compare **RRT** and **RRT\*** in terms of their algorithmic steps, completeness properties, and solution quality. Show mathematically how RRT\* achieves asymptotic optimality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a):**

| Aspect | RRT | RRT\* |
|--------|-----|-------|
| Completeness | Probabilistically complete | Probabilistically complete |
| Optimality | **NOT** asymptotically optimal | **Asymptotically optimal** |
| Rewiring step | Absent | Present within radius $r_n$ |
| Cost vs. samples | Converges to *some* feasible cost | Converges to $c^*$ |
| Per-iteration cost | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n) + O(\text{rewire})$ |

**Algorithmic difference:** RRT\* adds, after connecting $x_{new}$:
1. Find all neighbors within $r_n = \gamma(\log n / n)^{1/d}$.
2. Choose the parent that **minimizes** path cost $g(x)$.
3. **Rewire** each neighbor through $x_{new}$ if cheaper.

**Mathematical justification of optimality:** The cost of the RRT\* solution after $n$ iterations is bounded by $c^* + O((\log n / n)^{1/d})$, which tends to $c^*$ as $n \to \infty$. The shrinking but sufficiently large $r_n$ ensures the tree remains connected (completeness) while rewiring continuously improves local sub-paths (optimality).

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **narrow passage problem** in sampling-based planners. Derive the probability of sampling inside a passage of width $w$ in $d$ dimensions, and list two strategies to overcome this limitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b):**

A *narrow passage* in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is a region of width $w \ll 1$ connecting two larger open areas. The probability that a uniform sample $x_{rand}$ lands inside such a passage scales as:

$$P(\text{hit passage}) = \frac{\text{Vol(passage)}}{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C}_{free})} \approx \frac{w^d \cdot L}{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C}_{free})}$$

where $L$ is the passage length. In high dimensions, $w^d \to 0$ extremely fast, making uniform sampling practically useless for tight corridors.

**Two mitigation strategies:**

1. **Gaussian Sampling:** Draw samples from a Gaussian centered on the configuration; density is concentrated near obstacles, increasing the chance of hitting corridors.

2. **Bridge Sampling:** Draw two samples $x_1, x_2$ from $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$, and if their midpoint $x_m = (x_1 + x_2)/2 \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, accept $x_m$ as the sample. This explicitly targets narrow corridors between obstacle samples.

3. **(Bonus) Obstacle-Based Sampling:** Reject samples that are too far from any obstacle surface, forcing the planner to explore cluttered regions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing completeness types:** Students often write "RRT is complete" without specifying *probabilistic*. KTU examiners explicitly look for the word "probabilistic" — losing 1 mark otherwise.
> 
> 2. **Claiming RRT is optimal:** RRT is **NOT** asymptotically optimal. Only **RRT\*** (and PRM\*) are. This is a 2-mark trap.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting units/domain in formulas:** The formula $r_n = \gamma(\log n/n)^{1/d}$ — students often forget to mention that $d$ is the *configuration-space dimension*, not the workspace dimension.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the limit step:** In probabilistic completeness proofs, students jump to "$\to 1$" without showing the $(1-p)^n \to 0$ geometric argument. Examiners expect this step explicitly.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up Voronoi and Roadmap:** Voronoi bias is a property of **RRT**, not PRM. PRM uses random *milestones* and direct connections, not Voronoi-based selection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Characteristics of Sampling-Based Planners**
> 
> - **Definition:** Planners that explore $\mathcal{C}$ via random sampling + collision checking, avoiding explicit $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ construction.
> - **Probabilistic Completeness:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\text{success}) = 1$. Proof uses the $(1-p)^n \to 0$ lucky-event bound.
> - **Asymptotic Optimality:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} P(c_n \leq (1+\varepsilon)c^*) = 1$. Achieved by **RRT\*** and **PRM\*** via rewiring.
> - **Sample Complexity:** $N(\varepsilon) = O(1/\varepsilon^d)\ln(1/\delta)$ — exponential in dimension.
> - **Voronoi Bias:** $P(x_i) = \text{Vol}(V(x_i))/\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C})$ — drives frontier exploration.
> - **Rewiring Radius (RRT\*):** $r_n = \gamma(\log n/n)^{1/d}$ — shrinks but stays connected.
> - **Narrow Passage Probability:** $P \approx w^d / \text{Vol}(\mathcal{C}_{free})$ — main weakness.
> - **Strengths:** Resolution-free, anytime, scalable, supports non-holonomic constraints.
> - **Weaknesses:** Narrow passages, no optimality guarantee (RRT only), stochastic runtime.
> - **Real-world uses:** Autonomous cars, surgical robots, warehouse AMRs, drones, humanoids, spacecraft.
> - **Key Algorithms to Memorize:** PRM, RRT, RRT\*, PRM\*, Informed RRT\*, BIT\*, FMT\*.
> - **Common exam trap:** RRT ≠ optimal. Only RRT\* is asymptotically optimal.
> - **Equation to memorize verbatim:** $r_n = \gamma \cdot \left(\frac{\log n}{n}\right)^{1/d}$
> - **One-line definition to memorize:** "Sampling-based planners are probabilistically complete by the law of large numbers applied to $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ coverage."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Roadmaps: Basic PRM - Roadmap Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Roadmaps: Basic PRM - Roadmap Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — PROBABILISTIC ROADMAPS (PRM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM)** is a **sampling-based motion planning** paradigm that constructs a **graph data structure** (a *roadmap*) in the robot's **Configuration Space (C-space)** by randomly sampling collision-free configurations and connecting nearby samples using a *local planner*. The roadmap encodes the connectivity of the free space, $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, so that subsequent motion planning queries can be answered efficiently by graph search.

The roadmap is formally defined as a graph $G = (V, E)$ where:

- $V \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{free}$ is the set of **roadmap nodes** (collision-free configurations).
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ is the set of **roadmap edges**, where an edge $(q_1, q_2)$ exists iff the local planner successfully connects $q_1$ and $q_2$ with a collision-free local path.

The two algorithmic phases of PRM are:

| Phase | Purpose | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Learning / Construction Phase** | Build the roadmap in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ | Graph $G = (V, E)$ |
| **Query Phase** | Connect start $q_{start}$ and goal $q_{goal}$ to $G$, then search | Collision-free path |

This module focuses on the **Roadmap Construction (Learning) Phase** in detail.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "Lighting up a dark maze with a flashlight"**

Imagine you are dropped into a **completely dark, cluttered warehouse** at night. You cannot see the obstacles, but you have a small flashlight that illuminates only a tiny cone in front of you. To map the warehouse, you do the following:

1. You **randomly throw tennis balls** (nodes) into the warehouse and check, by sweeping your flashlight, whether each ball landed in an open area or on top of a box (collision check).
2. Between any two balls that landed **close together and in open areas**, you **shine the flashlight along a straight line** between them to see if the path is clear (local planner).
3. After repeating this thousands of times, the tennis balls connected by clear flashlight-beams form a **graph of safe corridors** — your *roadmap*.
4. Now, when someone asks "How do I walk from the entrance to the loading bay?", you simply **connect those two endpoints** to your roadmap of tennis balls and **trace a path** along the safe beams.

This is *exactly* how a **Basic PRM** works: the tennis balls are the **sampled configurations**, the flashlight beam is the **local planner**, and the safe corridor network is the **roadmap graph**.

### 1.3 Physical & Mathematical Constants Used

The Basic PRM is governed by the following tunable parameters (the "knobs" of the algorithm):

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Range / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $N$ | Total number of samples | $\mathbf{10^2}$ to $\mathbf{10^6}$ |
| $k$ | Number of nearest neighbors | $\mathbf{5}$ to $\mathbf{20}$ |
| $r$ | Connection radius | $\mathbf{0.1}$ to $\mathbf{10.0}$ (in C-space) |
| $\Delta q$ | Local planner step size | $\mathbf{0.01}$ to $\mathbf{0.1}$ rad / m |
| $d$ | Dimension of $\mathcal{C}$-space | $\mathbf{2}$ to $\mathbf{7+}$ DOF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The **Basic PRM** (also called *classic PRM* or *uniform-PRM*) is a **multi-query** planner. Once the roadmap is built, it can answer many start–goal queries in the **same environment** without re-sampling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Anatomy of Roadmap Construction

The **Basic PRM Roadmap Construction** is a four-stage pipeline. Each stage has a precise computational role.

### Stage 1 — Node Generation (Sampling)

- **Goal:** Place $N$ configurations uniformly (or quasi-randomly) inside the bounded Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$.
- **Method:** Each sample $q_i$ is drawn i.i.d. from a uniform distribution over $\mathcal{C}$:
$$q_i \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C}), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$
- **Why uniform?** In its basic form, the PRM makes **no prior assumption** about the geometry of the free space, so it treats every configuration as equally likely a priori.
- **Why it works probabilistically:** As $N \to \infty$, the density of samples in any measurable region of $\mathcal{C}$ is proportional to its volume, ensuring that narrow passages of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ are *eventually* sampled (with probability approaching 1).

### Stage 2 — Collision Detection (Feasibility Filter)

- **Goal:** Reject samples that lie in the *obstacle region* $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- **Test:** A configuration $q$ is **valid** iff:
$$\mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset \quad \text{and} \quad \mathcal{A}(q) \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{workspace}$$
where $\mathcal{A}(q)$ is the robot's swept volume at $q$, and $\mathcal{O}$ is the obstacle set.
- **Cost:** Collision checking is the **dominant computational expense** (often **>90% of total runtime**). It is typically performed using hierarchical bounding-volume trees (AABBs, OBBs, k-DOPs) on the robot and the environment.
- **Outcome:** After filtering, $|V| = N - N_{coll}$, where $N_{coll}$ is the count of rejected samples.

### Stage 3 — Local Planning (Edge Generation)

The **local planner** is the function that decides whether two nearby collision-free nodes can be joined by a safe path. Two principal strategies exist:

#### (a) k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) Strategy

- For each node $q \in V$, find the $k$ **nearest** neighbors in $V$ using a distance metric (typically Euclidean or weighted):
$$N_k(q) = \arg\min_{S \subseteq V, \vert S \vert = k} \sum_{q' \in S} \rho(q, q')$$
- Attempt a local connection to each $q' \in N_k(q)$.

#### (b) r-Disk (Radius) Strategy

- For each node $q \in V$, connect it to **all** nodes $q' \in V$ within distance $r$:
$$E_{candidate}(q) = \left\{ q' \in V \;\middle|\; 0 < \rho(q, q') \leq r \right\}$$
- The "disk" comes from the **geometry of the ball** $B(q, r) = \{q' \in \mathcal{C} : \rho(q, q') \leq r\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The choice between **k-NN** and **r-disk** is a trade-off: *k-NN* gives a more uniform average degree per node; *r-disk* is more sensitive to the local density of samples (sparse regions yield few edges).

### Stage 4 — Local Path Feasibility Test

For every candidate pair $(q, q')$, the **straight-line local path** is:
$$\tau(s) = (1 - s)\, q + s\, q', \quad s \in [0, 1]$$

The local planner **discretizes** this segment into small steps of size $\Delta q$:
$$\tau_i = q + i \cdot \frac{q' - q}{m}, \quad i = 0, 1, \ldots, m, \quad m = \left\lceil \frac{\rho(q, q')}{\Delta q} \right\rceil$$

The edge $(q, q')$ is **accepted** iff every $\tau_i \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$:
$$\forall i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, m\}:\; \mathcal{A}(\tau_i) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset$$

The accepted edges form the edge set $E$.

## 2.2 The Discretization of the Local Path — Why It Matters

The straight-line in $\mathcal{C}$-space corresponds to a **rational motion** of the robot (or a screw motion in 3D) in the workspace. Discretization introduces an important subtlety:

> [!WARNING]
> **Discretization is sound but incomplete.** A path with a very thin obstacle may pass *between* two collision checks. The smaller $\Delta q$, the higher the probability of detection, but the cost grows linearly with $m$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it contains every formula the examiner can test.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Roadmap graph | $G = (V, E)$ | Nodes + edges |
| 2 | Uniform sample | $q_i \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$ | Random configuration |
| 3 | Feasibility | $\mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset$ | Collision-free check |
| 4 | Straight-line local path | $\tau(s) = (1-s)q + s q'$ | Linear interpolation |
| 5 | Discretization steps | $m = \lceil \rho(q,q') / \Delta q \rceil$ | Resolution |
| 6 | k-NN candidate set | $N_k(q)$, $\vert N_k(q) \vert = k$ | Fixed degree |
| 7 | r-Disk candidate set | $B(q,r) = \{q' \mid \rho(q,q') \leq r\}$ | Fixed radius |
| 8 | Edge acceptance | $\tau_i \in \mathcal{C}_{free}\; \forall i$ | Safe local motion |
| 9 | Connected component | $G_{cc} \subseteq G$ | Same component $\Rightarrow$ path exists |
| 10 | Coverage probability | $\mathbb{P}[\text{no node in }R] = (1 - \mu(R))^N$ | $R \subset \mathcal{C}_{free}$ measurable, $\mu$ = volume |
| 11 | Asymptotic completeness | $\lim_{N \to \infty} \mathbb{P}[\text{solution found}] = 1$ | For solvable queries |
| 12 | Expected node count in $R$ | $\mathbb{E}[n_R] = N \cdot \mu(R \cap \mathcal{C}_{free}) / \mu(\mathcal{C})$ | Poisson process property |

> **Note on units:** Distances are in the units of $\mathcal{C}$-space (radians for revolute joints, meters for prismatic joints, or a normalized mix). $\Delta q$ must be small enough that the swept volume of one step does not "skip" a thin obstacle.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PRM-based planners are deployed in:

- **Industrial robotic manipulators** (e.g., welding, painting) where the same workspace is reused for thousands of trajectories.
- **Autonomous vehicles & drones** navigating pre-mapped urban airspaces.
- **Surgical robotics** for needle steering in constrained anatomical environments.
- **Character animation & game AI** to find collision-free motions of articulated virtual characters.
- **Protein folding simulations** where the conformation space is extremely high-dimensional.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Probability of Coverage (Why More Samples = Higher Success)

**Problem Setup:** Let $R \subset \mathcal{C}_{free}$ be a measurable "narrow-passage" region of the free space, of normalized volume $\mu(R) / \mu(\mathcal{C}) = \alpha$, where $0 < \alpha \ll 1$.

**Goal:** Compute $\mathbb{P}[R \text{ contains at least one node}]$ as a function of $N$ and $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Probability a *single* sample misses $R$**

Since the sampling is uniform over $\mathcal{C}$, the probability that one sample lands outside $R$ is:
$$p_{miss} = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 2 — Probability *all* $N$ samples miss $R$ (independent draws)**

Because samples are i.i.d., the joint miss probability is the product:
$$\mathbb{P}[\text{no node in }R] = (1 - \alpha)^N$$

**Step 3 — Probability at least one sample lands in $R$**

By the complement rule:
$$\mathbb{P}[\text{coverage of }R] = 1 - (1 - \alpha)^N$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic behavior**

As $N \to \infty$, using the limit $(1 - x/N)^N \to e^{-x}$, we obtain the **exponential tail decay**:
$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \mathbb{P}[\text{coverage of }R] = 1 - \lim_{N \to \infty}(1 - \alpha)^N = 1 - 0 = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The probability that the roadmap *misses* a region of relative volume $\alpha$ decays **exponentially** with $N$. This is the mathematical foundation of **asymptotic completeness** of Basic PRM.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Number of Edges per Node (Degree Analysis)

**Setup:** Use the **r-disk** model. Each node attempts connections to all neighbors within radius $r$ in $d$-dimensional $\mathcal{C}$-space.

**Step 1 — Volume of the connection ball**

The volume of a $d$-dimensional ball of radius $r$ is:
$$V_d(r) = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2 + 1)} \, r^d$$

The unit-volume forms are:

- $d=2$: $V_2(r) = \pi r^2$
- $d=3$: $V_3(r) = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$

**Step 2 — Expected neighbors in the unit $\mathcal{C}$-cube**

With uniform density $\rho = N$ (samples per unit volume), the expected number of neighbors within radius $r$ is:
$$\mathbb{E}[\deg(q)] = \rho \cdot V_d(r) = N \cdot \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2 + 1)} \, r^d$$

**Step 3 — Why this matters in practice**

In high dimensions ($d \geq 7$), the **curse of dimensionality** makes the ball volume either too small (most nodes isolated) or too large (too many candidate edges). This is why **Basic PRM struggles in very high-dimensional C-spaces** without variants like Gaussian sampling, bridge-test, or OBPRM.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Path Existence via the Local Planner (Connectivity Condition)

A query $(q_{start}, q_{goal})$ is **solvable** by the roadmap iff there exists a sequence of nodes $q_{start} = v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_m = q_{goal}$ such that:
$$\forall j \in \{0, 1, \ldots, m-1\}: \quad (v_j, v_{j+1}) \in E \quad \wedge \quad v_j \in V$$

**Step 1 — Identify connected components**

Run **BFS/DFS** on $G$ starting from $q_{start}$. The set of reachable nodes is the **connected component** $C(q_{start})$.

**Step 2 — Test reachability of goal**

The query has a solution iff:
$$q_{goal} \in C(q_{start})$$

Equivalently, using the indicator function $\mathbf{1}[\cdot]$:
$$\text{Solvable}(q_{start}, q_{goal}) = \mathbf{1}\big[\, q_{goal} \in C(q_{start}) \,\big]$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is *deterministic given* the fixed roadmap $G$. The randomness in PRM lies in how $G$ was constructed, not in the query phase.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Roadmap Construction Complexity

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Generate $N$ samples | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | Uniform RNG |
| Collision-check samples | $O(N \log M)$ | — | $M$ = # obstacle primitives |
| k-NN search (per node) | $O(k + N)$ naive / $O(k \log N)$ kd-tree | $O(N)$ | Kd-tree preferred |
| r-Disk enumeration (per node) | $O(N)$ naive / $O(N \log N)$ grid | $O(N)$ | Grid accelerates |
| Local planner (per edge) | $O(m)$ = $O(\rho / \Delta q)$ | — | $m$ = discretization steps |
| **Total roadmap build** | $\boldsymbol{O(N \log N + N k m)}$ | $\boldsymbol{O(N)}$ | Dominated by collision checks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, the standard expected answer for "complexity of Basic PRM construction" is: **$O(N \log N + N \cdot k \cdot m)$** time, $O(N)$ space.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Question:** A 2D planar manipulator has a C-space of area $4\,\text{m}^2$. You sample $N = 500$ configurations uniformly. A narrow corridor has area $0.08\,\text{m}^2$. What is the probability the corridor contains *at least one* sample?

**Solution using Derivation 1:**

$$\alpha = \frac{0.08}{4} = 0.02$$

$$\mathbb{P}[\text{coverage}] = 1 - (1 - 0.02)^{500}$$

Applying the approximation $(1 - x)^N \approx e^{-Nx}$ for small $x$ and large $N$:

$$\mathbb{P}[\text{coverage}] \approx 1 - e^{-500 \times 0.02} = 1 - e^{-10}$$

Since $e^{-10} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-5}$:

$$\mathbb{P}[\text{coverage}] \approx 1 - 0.0000454 \approx 0.9999546 \approx 99.995\%$$

> **Answer to write on the exam:** $\boxed{\mathbb{P} \approx 1 - e^{-10} \approx 99.995\%}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Basic PRM Roadmap Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT Bounded Configuration Space C] --> B[Stage 1 Node Generation]
    B --> B1[Uniform Random Sampler]
    B1 --> B2[Generate N Samples q1 to qN]
    B2 --> C[Stage 2 Collision Detection]
    C --> C1[Compute Robot Volume A qi]
    C1 --> C2{Collision Test A qi intersect O}
    C2 -->|Collision Detected| C3[Discard Sample]
    C2 -->|Free| C4[Add to Roadmap Node Set V]
    C3 --> B1
    C4 --> D[Stage 3 Neighbor Selection]
    D --> D1{Connection Strategy}
    D1 -->|Option A| D2[k Nearest Neighbors N sub k of q]
    D1 -->|Option B| D3[r Disk Ball B q r]
    D2 --> E[Stage 4 Local Planner]
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Discretize Straight Line tau of s]
    E1 --> E2[Step size Delta q]
    E2 --> E3{All tau sub i in C free}
    E3 -->|Yes| E4[Add Edge q q prime to E]
    E3 -->|No| E5[Reject Edge]
    E4 --> F[Output Roadmap Graph G equals V E]
    E5 --> E
    F --> G[Roadmap Ready for Query Phase]
```

## 4.2 Roadmap Construction Data-Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sampling[Stage 1 Sampling]
        S1[Uniform RNG] --> S2[N Configurations]
    end
    subgraph Filtering[Stage 2 Filtering]
        S2 --> F1[Collision Check per qi]
        F1 --> F2{Valid}
        F2 -->|Yes| F3[Add to V]
        F2 -->|No| F4[Discard]
    end
    subgraph Neighbors[Stage 3 Neighbor Search]
        F3 --> N1[Choose Strategy kNN or rDisk]
        N1 --> N2[Candidate Pairs]
    end
    subgraph Connection[Stage 4 Local Connection]
        N2 --> L1[Straight Line Interpolation]
        L1 --> L2[Discretize at Delta q]
        L2 --> L3{Feasible}
        L3 -->|Yes| L4[Add Edge to E]
        L3 -->|No| L5[Reject]
    end
    L4 --> OUT[Final Roadmap G]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Graph of the Roadmap (Toy Example)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start Node q1] --- A[Sample q2]
    A --- B[Sample q3]
    A --- C[Sample q4]
    B --- C
    B --- D[Sample q5]
    C --- D
    C --- E[Sample q6]
    D --- E
    D --- F[Sample q7]
    E --- F
    E --- Goal[Goal Node q8]
    F --- Goal
    Isolated[Isolated Sample q9] -.->|No Edge| D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the graph:** Solid lines are accepted local-planner edges. The dotted line to **$q_9$** indicates an isolated node — it is in $V$ but in a different connected component, so it cannot be reached from $q_1$. This is the typical "incompleteness" symptom of *Basic PRM* in narrow-passage scenarios.

## 4.4 Algorithm Pseudocode Block (NOT executable code — for understanding only)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is presented as a *mathematical algorithm description*, not as source code. This complies with KTU's expectation that students describe the algorithm in words and pseudocode on exams.

**Algorithm: Basic PRM — Roadmap Construction**

1. **Input:** Bounded C-space $\mathcal{C}$, obstacle set $\mathcal{O}$, number of samples $N$, neighbor strategy ($k$ or $r$), step size $\Delta q$.
2. **Initialize:** $V \leftarrow \emptyset$, $E \leftarrow \emptyset$.
3. **Repeat** $N$ times:
   a. Draw $q \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$.
   b. If $\mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset$ then add $q$ to $V$.
4. **For each** $q \in V$:
   a. Determine neighbor set $N(q)$ via $k$-NN or $r$-disk.
   b. **For each** $q' \in N(q)$, $q' \neq q$:
      i. Discretize $\tau(s) = (1-s)q + s q'$ into $m$ steps.
      ii. If every $\tau_i \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$ then add edge $(q, q')$ to $E$.
5. **Return** $G = (V, E)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q1.** Define **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM)**. List the two main phases of the algorithm and briefly explain the **Learning Phase**.

**Model Answer:**

A **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM)** is a graph $G=(V,E)$ constructed in the robot's free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ by randomly sampling collision-free configurations and connecting nearby samples with a local planner.

The two main phases are:
1. **Learning (Roadmap Construction) Phase** — Samples $N$ configurations, filters them for collision-freeness, and connects nearby ones with a local planner to build the roadmap graph.
2. **Query Phase** — Connects $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$ to the existing roadmap and performs graph search (e.g., A*) to retrieve a path.

In the **Learning Phase**, the algorithm:
- Generates $N$ samples uniformly from $\mathcal{C}$.
- Performs collision detection and discards invalid samples.
- For each valid sample, finds nearby neighbors (k-NN or r-disk) and uses a straight-line local planner to add valid edges.

**[3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for two phases + 1 for learning phase details]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q2.** What is a **local planner** in PRM? State the condition under which the local planner accepts a candidate edge between two configurations $q$ and $q'$.

**Model Answer:**

A **local planner** is a deterministic (or randomized) subroutine that tests whether two configurations $q$ and $q'$ can be connected by a simple collision-free path — typically a straight-line interpolation in C-space.

The local planner **accepts** the edge $(q, q')$ iff, for every discrete step $\tau_i$ along the straight-line path $\tau(s) = (1-s)q + sq'$ discretized at resolution $\Delta q$:

$$\forall i \in \{0, 1, \ldots, m\}: \quad \mathcal{A}(\tau_i) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset$$

If any $\tau_i$ is in collision, the edge is **rejected**. The number of discrete steps is $m = \lceil \rho(q,q') / \Delta q \rceil$, where $\rho$ is the chosen distance metric.

**[3 marks: 1 for local planner definition + 2 for acceptance condition]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

> **Q(A).** (a) Describe in detail the **algorithm for Roadmap Construction** in a Basic PRM. Mention the role of sampling, collision detection, neighbor selection, and the local planner.
>
> **(b)** A 3-DOF planar robot operates in a 2D workspace with C-space area $5\,\text{m}^2$. You sample $N = 1000$ configurations uniformly. A critical narrow passage of area $0.05\,\text{m}^2$ is required for solvability. Compute the probability that **at least one sample** lands in this passage. Comment on the result.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Roadmap Construction Algorithm (7 marks breakdown):**

1. **Initialization (1 mark):** Begin with an empty graph $G = (V, E)$. Input the bounded C-space $\mathcal{C}$, obstacle set $\mathcal{O}$, and parameters $N$, $k$ (or $r$), $\Delta q$.

2. **Node Generation / Sampling (2 marks):** Generate $N$ configurations $q_i \in \mathcal{C}$ by uniform random sampling. Each $q_i$ is independent: $q_i \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$. The role of sampling is to populate the C-space with candidate "milestones" that will become the nodes of the roadmap.

3. **Collision Detection (1 mark):** For each $q_i$, compute the robot's swept volume $\mathcal{A}(q_i)$ and test $\mathcal{A}(q_i) \cap \mathcal{O} = \emptyset$. Discard collided samples; add valid ones to $V$. This is the *feasibility filter*.

4. **Neighbor Selection (1 mark):** For each $q \in V$, identify candidate neighbors using either:
   - **k-Nearest Neighbors:** the $k$ closest nodes by Euclidean distance, or
   - **r-Disk:** all nodes within radius $r$.

5. **Local Planner / Edge Generation (1 mark):** For each candidate pair $(q, q')$, discretize the straight-line $\tau(s) = (1-s)q + sq'$ into $m$ steps of size $\Delta q$. If all $\tau_i \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, add the edge $(q,q')$ to $E$.

6. **Output (1 mark):** The final roadmap $G = (V, E)$ is a graph encoding the connectivity of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Numerical Computation:**

Given:
- $N = 1000$, $\mu(\mathcal{C}) = 5\,\text{m}^2$, $\mu(R) = 0.05\,\text{m}^2$.

Compute the relative volume:
$$\alpha = \frac{\mu(R)}{\mu(\mathcal{C})} = \frac{0.05}{5} = 0.01$$

**Probability that no sample falls in $R$:**
$$\mathbb{P}[\text{no node in }R] = (1 - 0.01)^{1000} = (0.99)^{1000}$$

Using the exponential approximation $(1 - x)^N \approx e^{-Nx}$:
$$(0.99)^{1000} \approx e^{-1000 \times 0.01} = e^{-10} \approx 4.54 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Probability of at least one sample in $R$:**
$$\mathbb{P}[\text{coverage}] = 1 - (0.99)^{1000} \approx 1 - 4.54 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.9999546$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** With $N = 1000$ samples, the probability that the narrow passage is *populated* is approximately **99.995%** — a near-certainty. This demonstrates the **exponential decay** of failure probability with $N$, which is the theoretical basis of PRM's asymptotic completeness.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget the **complement rule** and incorrectly compute $(0.99)^{1000}$ as the answer. The correct interpretation is **"1 minus the failure probability"**. Marks are deducted if the conclusion doesn't say "at least one sample is in the passage."

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

> **Q(B).** (a) Compare the **k-Nearest Neighbors** and **r-Disk** strategies for neighbor selection in PRM. State the expected number of neighbors per node in each case for a $d$-dimensional C-space with $N$ uniform samples and connection radius $r$.
>
> **(b)** A mobile robot navigates a 2D C-space where $N = 200$ configurations are sampled uniformly. The connection radius is $r = 0.5\,\text{m}$. Compute the **expected number of neighbors** per node. Is this sufficient to expect a well-connected roadmap? Justify briefly.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | k-NN Strategy | r-Disk Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Parameter | Fixed $k$ (e.g., 15) | Fixed $r$ (e.g., 1.0 m) |
| Node degree | Bounded above by $k$ | Variable, depends on local density |
| Sparse regions | Still attempts $k$ edges | Few or no neighbors |
| Dense regions | Restricted to $k$ edges | Many candidate edges (expensive) |
| Scalability | Stable cost per node | Cost depends on density |
| Best use | Variable density C-spaces | Uniform density C-spaces |

**Expected number of neighbors (4 marks):**

- **k-NN:** Always **exactly $k$** by construction (excluding self-loops): $\mathbb{E}[\deg] = k - 1$ (if self is excluded).

- **r-Disk:** In a $d$-dimensional C-space with sample density $\rho = N / \mu(\mathcal{C})$, the expected number of neighbors within a ball of radius $r$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}[\deg(q)] = \rho \cdot V_d(r) = \frac{N}{\mu(\mathcal{C})} \cdot \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2 + 1)} \, r^d$$

For $d = 2$:
$$\mathbb{E}[\deg(q)] = \frac{N}{\mu(\mathcal{C})} \cdot \pi r^2$$

For $d = 3$:
$$\mathbb{E}[\deg(q)] = \frac{N}{\mu(\mathcal{C})} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $N = 200$, $d = 2$, $r = 0.5\,\text{m}$. Assume the workspace C-space is bounded with area $\mu(\mathcal{C}) = 10\,\text{m}^2$ (a common board-exam assumption — students should state this if not given).

**Sample density:**
$$\rho = \frac{N}{\mu(\mathcal{C})} = \frac{200}{10} = 20\;\text{samples/m}^2$$

**Expected neighbors per node:**
$$\mathbb{E}[\deg(q)] = \rho \cdot \pi r^2 = 20 \cdot \pi \cdot (0.5)^2 = 20 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.25 = 5\pi \approx 15.71$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):** An expected degree of $\approx 15$ neighbors is **more than sufficient** for a well-connected roadmap in a 2D C-space. In practice, degrees between 5 and 20 are considered ideal — they provide enough alternative paths for robustness without making local planning excessively expensive. The roadmap is likely to have a single dominant connected component covering most of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget to **subtract self** when computing degree, or fail to mention that this is an *expected* value subject to Poisson fluctuation. Always write the answer as $\mathbb{E}[\deg] \approx 15.7$ and state the qualitative interpretation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- [x] **Basic PRM is multi-query** — build once, query many times.
- [x] **Roadmap construction = Learning phase**; query phase is separate.
- [x] The roadmap is a graph $G = (V, E)$ over $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- [x] **Sampling** is uniform i.i.d.: $q_i \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$.
- [x] **Collision check** is the **dominant cost** — typically > 90% of total runtime.
- [x] **Local planner** connects nodes via straight-line in C-space, discretized at step size $\Delta q$.
- [x] **k-NN strategy**: degree = $k-1$ exactly; **r-disk strategy**: degree varies with local density.
- [x] Edge accepted iff **all** $\tau_i \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$.
