- [x] **Asymptotic completeness:** $\lim_{N \to \infty} \mathbb{P}[\text{solution}] = 1$ for any solvable query.
- [x] **Probability of missing a region of relative volume $\alpha$:** $(1-\alpha)^N \to 0$ exponentially.
- [x] **Connected components**: query solved iff start and goal lie in the **same component**.
- [x] **Curse of dimensionality**: Basic PRM degrades in $d \geq 7$ without enhancements (Gaussian sampling, bridge test, etc.).
- [x] **Soundness caveat**: PRM is **probabilistically complete**, not **resolution-complete** like cell-decomposition methods.
- [x] **Total complexity**: $O(N \log N + N k m)$ time, $O(N)$ space.
- [x] **Connectedness criterion**: $q_{goal} \in C(q_{start})$ — verified by BFS/DFS.
- [x] The roadmap **does not change** between queries in the same environment — it is **precomputed**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Query Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Query Phase # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Query Phase (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):** > The **Query Phase** in robot motion planning is the *online, online-time execution stage* of a motion planning algorithm that follows a one-time **Learning/Preprocessing Phase** (or Construction Phase). Once a roadmap, cell decomposition, or sampled graph has been built over the free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, the query phase takes two input configurations ($q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$) and produces a valid collision-free trajectory by *attaching* the query points to the precomputed data structure and performing a graph search. In formal terms, given a pre-built data structure $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ that approximates $\mathcal{C}_{free}$: $$\text{Query} : (q_{start}, q_{goal}) \;\longrightarrow\; \pi^*(q_{start}, q_{goal}) \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$$ where $\pi^*$ is the optimal (or near-optimal) collision-free path. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Metro Map** > Imagine a city planner has spent months drawing a complete **subway map** of a city (the *Learning Phase* — building the roadmap). The Query Phase is what happens *every morning* when you, a passenger, walk into a station and ask: *"How do I get from Home to Office?"* The transit system does **not** rebuild the map. It simply (a) snaps your home and office to the nearest stations, (b) runs a shortest-path algorithm (like Dijkstra) over the existing map, and (c) hands you a ticket listing the lines to board. The "snap-and-search" is exactly the Query Phase of motion planning. ## 1.3 Three Canonical Sub-Steps of the Query Phase The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus groups the query phase into **three mandatory sub-operations**: 1. **Connection Step** — Attach $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$ to the precomputed graph $\mathcal{G}$. 2. **Search Step** — Run a shortest-path graph search (BFS, Dijkstra, A*). 3. **Path Extraction & Smoothing Step** — Retrieve and post-process the geometric path. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Significance:** The query phase determines the **real-time responsiveness** of a robot. While the learning phase may take minutes or hours (offline), the query phase is what is invoked in the *control loop* of a robot — it must run in **milliseconds to seconds**. ## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics The query phase is judged by the following KTU-mandated metrics: | Metric | Symbol | Typical Target | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Query time complexity | $T_q$ | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert)$ for Dijkstra | seconds | | Path optimality | $\mathcal{L}(\pi)$ | Minimize path length / clearance | meters | | Success probability | $P_s$ | $> 0.95$ | dimensionless | | Path clearance (safety) | $\delta$ | Maximize | meters | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Phase Paradigm Modern motion planners (PRM, Visibility-Graph, Cell-Decomposition planners) are built on a **decoupled architecture**: ``` [OFFLINE] Learning Phase → builds data structure 𝒢 (one-time cost) [ONLINE] Query Phase → answers (q_start, q_goal) queries (repeated cost) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why decouple?** Because the workspace and robot geometry are often **static** (factory floors, indoor rooms), so the expensive C-space analysis is paid once. Only the *intent* (start/goal) changes with each new task. ## 2.2 Detailed Sub-Step Analysis ### Step A — Connection of Query Points For a precomputed graph $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$: 1. **Find nearest neighbor in $\mathcal{G}$** to $q_{start}$: $$q_{start}^* = \underset{v \in \mathcal{V}}{\arg\min} \; d(q_{start}, v)$$ where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is typically Euclidean or weighted-metric distance. 2. **Validate the local planner** (e.g., straight-line or $k$-nearest) between $q_{start}$ and $q_{start}^*$ to ensure it lies in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. 3. Repeat for $q_{goal}$ to obtain $q_{goal}^*$. 4. **Insert** the validated edges into $\mathcal{G}$ temporarily (or virtually). ### Step B — Graph Search Given the augmented graph, the standard KTU-recommended search algorithms are: - **Breadth-First Search (BFS):** Unweighted, optimal in edge count. - **Dijkstra's Algorithm:** Weighted (e.g., by path length), guarantees shortest path. - **A\* Search:** Heuristic-guided, optimal if heuristic is *admissible*. ### Step C — Path Extraction & Smoothing - **Extraction:** Backtrack from $q_{goal}^*$ to $q_{start}^*$ through predecessor pointers. - **Smoothing:** Apply shortcutting or gradient-based smoothing to reduce path length and increase clearance. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master this table — it covers 90% of numerical questions.** | # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_{total} = T_{learn} + N_q \cdot T_{query}$ | Total planner time = offline + N queries | s | | 2 | $d_{Eucl}(q_i, q_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(q_{ik} - q_{jk})^2}$ | Distance in n-dim configuration space | m or rad | | 3 | $T_{BFS} = O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert)$ | BFS time complexity | ops | | 4 | $T_{Dijkstra} = O(\vert \mathcal{E} \vert + \vert \mathcal{V} \vert \log \vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | With binary heap | ops | | 5 | $T_{A^*} = O(\vert \mathcal{E} \vert + \vert \mathcal{V} \vert \log \vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | Same worst-case, faster in practice | ops | | 6 | $f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$ | A* evaluation function | cost units | | 7 | $h(v) \le d(v, q_{goal})$ | Admissibility condition | cost units | | 8 | $\mathcal{L}(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} d(v_i, v_{i+1})$ | Total path length | m | | 9 | $P_s = 1 - (1 - p)^{N_{samples}}$ | Sampling-based success probability | dimensionless | | 10 | $\delta_{min} = \min_{q \in \pi} d(q, \mathcal{C}_{obs})$ | Minimum clearance | m | | 11 | $\pi_{smoothed} = \arg\min_{\pi} \mathcal{L}(\pi)$ | Smoothing objective | m | | 12 | $V_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ | Max joint velocity bound | m/s or rad/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The query phase is the **bottleneck** in industrial robotics — a car assembly robot may need to be re-planned every 0.1 s if a moving obstacle appears. Optimizing $T_q$ to be sub-millisecond enables reactive re-planning. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Cost Trade-off Between Learning and Query Phases The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks students to justify *why* the two-phase paradigm is computationally superior. Let us derive the **break-even point**. ### Setup Let: - $T_L$ = time for one-time learning phase - $T_Q$ = time per single query - $N$ = total number of queries to be answered **Total time** for a **single-phase (lazy)** planner = $N \cdot T_L$ **Total time** for a **two-phase** planner = $T_L + N \cdot T_Q$ ### Derivation The two-phase approach is faster **iff**: $$T_L + N \cdot T_Q < N \cdot T_L$$ Rearranging: $$ \begin{aligned} T_L + N \cdot T_Q &< N \cdot T_L \\ T_L - N \cdot T_Q &< N \cdot T_L - N \cdot T_Q \\ T_L (1 - N) &< N(T_L - T_Q) \\ T_L (N - 1) &> N(T_L - T_Q) \end{aligned} $$ For $N \gg 1$, the condition simplifies to: $$T_Q < T_L \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right) \approx T_L$$ **Interpretation:** The query phase *must* be faster than the learning phase for the two-phase approach to win. Since $T_L$ typically explores the entire $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ and $T_Q$ is just graph search, this is almost always true. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Break-even point** $N^*$ where both are equal: > $$N^* = \frac{T_L}{T_L - T_Q}$$ > For $N > N^*$, always prefer the two-phase planner. ## 3.2 Derivation: A* Optimality from Admissible Heuristic A* selects the next node $v$ from the open set using: $$f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$$ where: - $g(v)$ = actual cost from $q_{start}$ to $v$ (known) - $h(v)$ = heuristic estimate of cost from $v$ to $q_{goal}$ (estimated) **Claim:** If $h$ is *admissible* ($h(v) \le d(v, q_{goal})$), then A* returns an optimal path. ### Proof Sketch (KTU standard answer) 1. Suppose A* terminates with sub-optimal path of cost $C^* > C_{opt}$. 2. Let $v^*$ be the **first** node on the optimal path that is **not** expanded when the sub-optimal path is returned. 3. The predecessor $u$ of $v^*$ on the optimal path was expanded, so: $$f(u) = g(u) + h(u) \le g(u) + d(u, q_{goal}) = C_{opt}$$ 4. Since $v^*$ is a successor and $f(v^*) \ge f(u)$: $$f(v^*) = g(v^*) + h(v^*) \le C_{opt}$$ 5. But the algorithm returned a path of cost $C^* > C_{opt}$, contradicting that $v^*$ would have been expanded first. Hence, optimality is preserved. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Search Algorithms (High-Yield for KTU) | Algorithm | Optimality | Time Complexity | Space | Heuristic | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | BFS | Yes (unweighted) | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert)$ | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | No | Visibility-graph queries | | Dijkstra | Yes (weighted) | $O((\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert) \log \vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | No | Cost-based PRM query | | A* | Yes (admissible $h$) | Same as Dijkstra (best case $<<$) | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | Yes | Real-time mobile robots | | Greedy BFS | No | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert)$ | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | Yes | Fast but sub-optimal | ## 3.4 Worked Example: Query Phase on a 2D Roadmap **Problem:** A planar robot operates in a 5×5 m$^2$ workspace. The PRM learning phase produces a roadmap with $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 50$ nodes and $\vert \mathcal{E} \vert = 120$ edges. $q_{start}$ is closest to node $S$, $q_{goal}$ closest to node $G$, with $d(q_{start}, S) = 0.2$ m and $d(q_{goal}, G) = 0.4$ m. Estimate the query time using Dijkstra's algorithm. ### Solution Given: - $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 50$ - $\vert \mathcal{E} \vert = 120$ - Assume each operation takes $1 \mu s = 10^{-6}$ s. **Step 1 — Connection cost (neighbor search):** $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert) = 50$ operations = $50 \times 10^{-6}$ s = $50 \; \mu s$. **Step 2 — Dijkstra search cost:** $$ T_{Dijkstra} = O((\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert) \log \vert \mathcal{V} \vert) $$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_{Dijkstra} &= (50 + 120) \times \log_2(50) \\ &= 170 \times 5.64 \\ &\approx 959 \text{ operations} \end{aligned} $$ At $1 \mu s$ per operation: $T_{Dijkstra} \approx 0.959$ ms. **Step 3 — Path extraction cost:** $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert) = 50 \; \mu s$ (backtracking). **Total query time:** $$ T_Q \approx 50 + 959 + 50 = 1059 \; \mu s \approx 1.06 \text{ ms} $$ This is **well within the real-time budget** of a typical mobile robot. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Query Phase Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Input: q_start, q_goal] --> B[Nearest Neighbor Lookup in G] B --> C{Local Planner Validates Edge?} C -- No --> D[Try Next Neighbor k=2,3,...] D --> C C -- Yes --> E[Augment G with q_start, q_goal] E --> F[Run Graph Search BFS / Dijkstra / A*] F --> G[Path Exists in G?] G -- No --> H[Return FAILURE] G -- Yes --> I[Backtrack Predecessor Chain] I --> J[Raw Path pi in C_free] J --> K[Path Smoothing / Shortcutting] K --> L[Final Smoothed Trajectory] L --> M[Output to Robot Controller] ``` ## 4.2 PRM Query Phase Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OFFLINE[Offline Learning Phase - Already Done] L1[Sample n configs in C_free] --> L2[Collision Check Each Sample] L2 --> L3[Connect k-Nearest Neighbors] L3 --> L4[Roadmap G = V,E] end subgraph ONLINE[Online Query Phase] Q1[Receive q_start, q_goal] --> Q2[Find nearest nodes S, G in V] Q2 --> Q3[Connect q_start to S, q_goal to G] Q3 --> Q4[Augment G temporarily] Q4 --> Q5[A* Search on G] Q5 --> Q6[Extract Path] Q6 --> Q7[Smooth Path] end L4 --> Q2 Q7 --> R[Robot Motion Command] ``` ## 4.3 Graph Search State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Connecting: Receive Query Connecting --> Searching: Edges Validated Connecting --> Failure: No Valid Connection Searching --> Extracting: Goal Reached Searching --> Failure: Open Set Empty Extracting --> Smoothing: Path Found Smoothing --> Done: Trajectory Ready Failure --> Idle: Return Error Done --> Idle: Reset for Next Query ``` ## 4.4 Cost Trade-off Diagram (Learning vs Query) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Time = T_L + N * T_Q] --> B{N > N*?} B -- Yes --> C[Two-Phase Wins
Use PRM, Cell Decomp.] B -- No --> D[Single-Phase May Be Better
Use RRT, Potential Field] C --> E[Industrial / Repeated Tasks] D --> F[One-off / Dynamic Planning] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the Query Phase in motion planning. Why is it decoupled from the Learning Phase?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Query Phase** is the *online* stage of a two-phase motion planner. It accepts a pair of configurations $(q_{start}, q_{goal})$ and, using a precomputed data structure (e.g., a roadmap graph $\mathcal{G}$), produces a collision-free path $\pi \subset \mathcal{C}_{free}$. It is decoupled from the Learning Phase because: 1. The workspace and robot geometry are typically **static**, so the expensive $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ analysis is amortized. 2. The *intent* (start/goal) changes with every new task but the *environment* does not. 3. Decoupling enables **real-time replanning** — only the cheap graph-search is repeated per query. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List the three mandatory sub-steps of the Query Phase and write the time complexity of A\* search.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three sub-steps are: 1. **Connection:** Attach $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$ to the precomputed roadmap. 2. **Search:** Run a graph search (Dijkstra, A*) to find a path. 3. **Extraction & Smoothing:** Backtrack the path and apply smoothing. **Time complexity of A\*** (with binary heap): $O(\vert \mathcal{E} \vert + \vert \mathcal{V} \vert \log \vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Always draw the **augmented roadmap** showing $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$ connected to the graph — students who skip this lose **2 marks**. > - In A* questions, *explicitly state the admissibility condition* of the heuristic, not just write the formula. Lose **1 mark** if missed. > - Numerical answers **must include units** (meters, seconds). A unitless answer loses **0.5 mark** per instance. > - When asked to "compare" algorithms, use a **table** — KTU examiners award full marks only for structured comparison. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the three sub-steps of the Query Phase in a Probabilistic Roadmap Method (PRM). Discuss the time complexity of each sub-step. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PRM planner has produced a roadmap with $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 200$ nodes and $\vert \mathcal{E} \vert = 500$ edges. A new query is given with $q_{start} = (1, 2)$ and $q_{goal} = (8, 9)$. The nearest roadmap nodes are $S = (1.1, 2.05)$ and $G = (7.9, 9.1)$. Using Dijkstra's algorithm with a binary heap, calculate the total query time assuming each elementary operation takes $0.5 \; \mu s$. State your assumption clearly. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Block diagram (3 marks):** ``` [ q_start, q_goal ] | v [ Nearest Neighbor Lookup ] --(k-NN search) | v [ Local Planner: Validate Edge ] --(collision check) | v [ Augmented Roadmap G' ] | v [ Graph Search: Dijkstra / A* ] --(returns predecessor map) | v [ Path Extraction by Backtracking ] | v [ Path Smoothing ] | v [ Final Trajectory to Robot ] ``` **Time complexity (4 marks):** | Sub-step | Complexity | Reason | |---|---|---| | k-NN lookup | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert \log \vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | k-d tree / brute force | | Edge validation | $O(k \cdot C_{check})$ | k neighbors × collision cost | | Dijkstra search | $O((\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert) \log \vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | Binary heap | | Path extraction | $O(\vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ | Backtracking | | Smoothing | $O(M \cdot \vert \pi \vert)$ | M shortcut iterations | #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** - $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 200$, $\vert \mathcal{E} \vert = 500$ - $T_{op} = 0.5 \; \mu s$ per operation **Step 1 — k-NN connection cost:** For each query point, scanning all 200 nodes: $$T_{NN} = 2 \times 200 \times 0.5 \; \mu s = 200 \; \mu s$$ **Step 2 — Dijkstra search cost:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{Dijkstra} &= (\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert) \log_2 \vert \mathcal{V} \vert \times T_{op} \\ &= (200 + 500) \times \log_2(200) \times 0.5 \; \mu s \\ &= 700 \times 7.64 \times 0.5 \; \mu s \\ &= 700 \times 3.82 \; \mu s \\ &= 2674 \; \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Path extraction cost:** $$T_{extract} = \vert \mathcal{V} \vert \times T_{op} = 200 \times 0.5 \; \mu s = 100 \; \mu s$$ **Step 4 — Total query time:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_Q &= T_{NN} + T_{Dijkstra} + T_{extract} \\ &= 200 + 2674 + 100 \\ &= 2974 \; \mu s \approx 2.97 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** $T_Q \approx 2.97$ ms — feasible for real-time use. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **(a)** What is the Query Phase? Explain with an example why an admissible heuristic in A* guarantees path optimality. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare Dijkstra's algorithm and A* search algorithm for query-phase graph search. Mention the formula for the evaluation function $f(v)$ in A* and explain the admissibility condition. Use a 5-node example graph to illustrate A* in action. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Definition (2 marks):** The Query Phase is the online stage that, given $(q_{start}, q_{goal})$ and a precomputed roadmap, returns a collision-free path by connecting the query points and performing graph search. **Why A* with admissible heuristic is optimal (5 marks):** A heuristic $h(v)$ is **admissible** if $h(v) \le d(v, q_{goal})$ for every node $v$ — i.e., it never overestimates the true cost. **Proof by contradiction:** - Suppose A* returns a sub-optimal path of cost $C^* > C_{opt}$. - Let $v^*$ be the first node on the optimal path that is **not** expanded. - Its predecessor $u$ on the optimal path was expanded, so: $$f(u) = g(u) + h(u) \le g(u) + d(u, q_{goal}) = C_{opt}$$ - Since $f(v^*) \ge f(u)$, we have $f(v^*) \le C_{opt} < C^*$. - So the algorithm should have expanded $v^*$ before terminating — **contradiction**. Hence A* with an admissible heuristic returns an optimal path. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | Dijkstra | A* | |---|---|---| | Uses heuristic | No | Yes, $h(v)$ | | Evaluation function | $f(v) = g(v)$ | $f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$ | | Optimality | Always optimal | Optimal if $h$ is admissible | | Search direction | Uniform (all directions) | Goal-directed | | Speed | Slower in large graphs | Faster (explores fewer nodes) | **Admissibility condition (2 marks):** $$h(v) \le d(v, q_{goal}) \quad \forall v \in \mathcal{V}$$ A common admissible heuristic for grid/roadmap: **Euclidean distance** $h(v) = \sqrt{(x_v - x_{goal})^2 + (y_v - y_{goal})^2}$. **5-node example (2 marks):** Consider nodes $A, B, C, D, E$ with start $A$ and goal $E$. Assume straight-line distances as $h$. A* will expand in the order of $f = g + h$, expanding goal-directed nodes first and reaching $E$ via a smaller node set than Dijkstra. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this before the exam!)** - [ ] **Query Phase = Online stage**; Learning Phase = Offline (one-time). - [ ] **Three sub-steps:** Connect → Search → Extract & Smooth. - [ ] **Dijkstra complexity:** $O((\vert \mathcal{V} \vert + \vert \mathcal{E} \vert) \log \vert \mathcal{V} \vert)$ with a binary heap. - [ ] **A* evaluation function:** $f(v) = g(v) + h(v)$. - [ ] **Admissibility condition:** $h(v) \le d(v, q_{goal})$ — never overestimate. - [ ] **Consistency (monotonicity):** $h(u) \le d(u, v) + h(v)$ — stronger than admissibility, guarantees $f$ is non-decreasing along a path. - [ ] **Break-even point** for two-phase vs single-phase: $N^* = \dfrac{T_L}{T_L - T_Q}$. - [ ] **Success probability of sampling-based methods** grows as $1 - (1 - p)^N$. - [ ] **Always annotate numerical answers with units** (m, s, rad, rad/s). - [ ] **Draw the augmented roadmap** in any query-phase numerical — KTU examiners expect a diagram. - [ ] **Local planner** (collision-checking the connection edge) is the *most expensive* sub-step in dense roadmaps — use lazy evaluation if needed. - [ ] **Path smoothing techniques:** shortcutting, gradient-based, spline fitting — KTU expects at least *one* name. - [ ] **PRM is multiple-query biased**; **RRT is single-query biased** — the query phase is what differentiates them in practice.

topics.title: PRM Sampling Strategies - Sampling Near the Obstacles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** PRM Sampling Strategies - Sampling Near the Obstacles ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM)** for robot motion planning, **Sampling Near the Obstacles** refers to a family of non-uniform sampling strategies that deliberately bias the random sample distribution toward the boundaries of configuration-space obstacles (C-obstacles), as opposed to the classical uniform distribution over the free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. Formally, given the configuration space $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ decomposed as $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \cup \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$, the sampling strategy defines a probability density function $p(\mathbf{q})$ over $\mathcal{C}$ such that: $$p(\mathbf{q}) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{d(\mathbf{q}, \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}})}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ where $d(\mathbf{q}, \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}})$ is the Euclidean distance from configuration $\mathbf{q}$ to the obstacle boundary $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$, and $\sigma$ is a tunable bandwidth parameter. The roadmap nodes are no longer drawn from $\mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$ but from this **obstacle-biased distribution**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 - Sampling Strategies):** > The 2024 scheme emphasizes three primary non-uniform samplers — **Gaussian Sampler**, **Bridge Sampler**, and **Obstacle-Based PRM (OBPRM)** — under the umbrella of "Sampling Near the Obstacles." Each targets the well-known failure mode of uniform PRM: the inability to penetrate **narrow passages**. --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) Imagine you have lost your car keys inside a **large, dark, cluttered garage** (this is your configuration space). You have a flashlight and a sweeping beam. If you sweep the flashlight uniformly across the room, you will spend most of your time looking at empty floor space — exactly what a uniform PRM sampler does with C-space. But what if, instead, you **shine the flashlight along the edges of furniture, walls, and shelves first**? You are far more likely to find the keys because keys tend to roll *next to* obstacles. **Sampling Near the Obstacles does exactly this**: it concentrates sampling effort in the regions bordering obstacles, where narrow passages and critical connectivity often lie. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight (Examiner's Favorite Quote):** > *"Most of the interesting topology of the free space lives on the boundary of obstacles — the obstacles define the connectivity of the world."* — adapted from Hsu et al., *OBPRM (1998)*. ### 1.3 Why Uniform Sampling Fails | Aspect | Uniform Sampling | Sampling Near Obstacles | |--------|------------------|--------------------------| | Coverage of wide-open regions | Excellent | Good (reduced) | | Penetration of narrow passages | **Poor** | **Excellent** | | Computational cost of sampler | $O(1)$ per sample | $O(\log n)$ or $O(k)$ per sample | | Roadmap connectivity in clutter | Frequently disconnected | Highly connected | The empirical probability that a uniform sample lands inside a narrow passage of width $w$ in an $n$-dimensional cube of side $L$ is proportional to $(w/L)^{n-1}$, which decays exponentially with dimension — the famous **"narrow passage curse."** ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Principal Strategies — Logic Decomposition #### 2.1.1 The Gaussian Sampler **Why it works:** Obstacles repel the sampler, so the local density of useful configurations increases near $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. **How it works:** 1. Draw a candidate sample $\mathbf{q}_{\text{cand}}$ uniformly from $\mathcal{C}$. 2. Compute $d = d(\mathbf{q}_{\text{cand}}, \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}})$. 3. Accept $\mathbf{q}_{\text{cand}}$ with probability $\propto \exp(-d^2 / 2\sigma^2)$ using rejection sampling. 4. This biases accepted samples toward obstacle boundaries. #### 2.1.2 The Bridge Sampler (Sun, Reif & Manocha, 2005) **Why it works:** A line segment that "bridges" a narrow passage has both endpoints inside the obstacle and its midpoint inside free space. **How it works:** 1. Draw two points $\mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2 \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$. 2. Reject if $\mathbf{q}_1 \notin \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ or $\mathbf{q}_2 \notin \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ (must lie inside obstacle). 3. Form midpoint $\mathbf{m} = (\mathbf{q}_1 + \mathbf{q}_2)/2$. 4. **Accept** iff $\mathbf{m} \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ — this midpoint is the bridge sample. #### 2.1.3 Obstacle-Based PRM (OBPRM) — Hsu et al., 1998 **Why it works:** Directly projects uniform samples onto the obstacle boundary, guaranteeing a node lies on $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. **How it works:** 1. Draw $\mathbf{q} \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$. 2. Find the nearest obstacle boundary along a random direction $\mathbf{v}$ (ray-casting). 3. Place the new node at the first collision point on $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table compiles **every formula** you may need to solve a board question on this topic. Use `\mid` or `\vert` for absolute values to avoid markdown table breakage. | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Boundary Condition / Units | Strategy | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Gaussian PDF (1-D) | $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\sigma > 0$ (length units) | Gaussian | | 2 | Multivariate Gaussian PDF | $f(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}}\exp\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{q}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{\!\top}\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{q}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$ | $\Sigma \succ 0$ (PSD matrix) | Gaussian | | 3 | Obstacle-biased density | $p(\mathbf{q}) \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{d(\mathbf{q}, \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}})^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $d \geq 0$ (distance) | Gaussian | | 4 | Acceptance probability (rejection) | $P_{\text{acc}} = \min\!\left(1, \dfrac{p(\mathbf{q})}{M}\right)$ | $M = \sup_{\mathbf{q}} p(\mathbf{q})$ | Rejection | | 5 | Bridge test midpoint | $\mathbf{m} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{q}_1 + \mathbf{q}_2)$ | $\mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | Bridge | | 6 | Narrow passage probability (uniform) | $P_{\text{narrow}} \sim \left(\dfrac{w}{L}\right)^{n-1}$ | $0 < w \ll L$ | Failure analysis | | 7 | Configuration space obstacle | $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{\mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{C} \mid A(\mathbf{q}) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset\}$ | Subset of $\mathcal{C}$ | General | | 8 | Path validity | $\tau:[0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, $\tau(0)=\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$, $\tau(1)=\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ | Continuous | All | | 9 | Connection radius | $r = \gamma \cdot (\log n / n)^{1/d}$ | $\gamma$ = tuning constant | Connectivity | | 10 | OBPRM projection | $\mathbf{q}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{q} + t^* \mathbf{v}$, $t^* = \min\{t > 0 \mid \mathbf{q} + t\mathbf{v} \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}\}$ | Ray-cast parameter | OBPRM | --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Sampling Near the Obstacles is the backbone of modern motion planning in: * **Autonomous Vehicles** — negotiating lane changes, parking in tight bays. * **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci, ROSA spine)** — threading instruments through confined anatomy. * **Warehouse Robotics (Kiva/Amazon)** — navigating cluttered shelves. * **Drone Swarms** — flying through truss-like environments (e.g., SubT Challenge). * **Digital Character Animation** — generating collision-free paths for virtual humans. The principle generalizes beyond robotics into **computational geometry**, **molecular docking**, and **VLSI routing**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Multivariate Gaussian Distance Decay We want to show that if $p(\mathbf{q}) \propto \exp(-d^2 / 2\sigma^2)$ where $d$ is the perpendicular distance to a planar obstacle boundary, then $p$ reduces to a **univariate Gaussian** along the normal direction. **Step 1 — Parametrize the configuration.** Let $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^n$ and let the obstacle boundary $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ be the hyperplane $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} = \{\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid \mathbf{n}^{\top}\mathbf{q} = c\}$, where $\mathbf{n} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the unit outward normal ($\Vert\mathbf{n}\Vert = 1$) and $c$ is a scalar offset. **Step 2 — Express the signed distance.** The signed distance from any point $\mathbf{q}$ to this hyperplane is given by the standard projection: $$d(\mathbf{q}, \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}) = \mathbf{n}^{\top}\mathbf{q} - c$$ This is positive outside the obstacle and negative inside, by convention. **Step 3 — Substitute into the exponential.** Inserting this into the obstacle-biased density: $$p(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{Z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\mathbf{n}^{\top}\mathbf{q} - c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ where $Z$ is the normalization constant ensuring $\int_{\mathcal{C}} p(\mathbf{q})\,d\mathbf{q} = 1$. **Step 4 — Decompose into tangential and normal parts.** Choose an orthonormal basis $\{\mathbf{n}, \mathbf{e}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{e}_n\}$ for $\mathbb{R}^n$ and write $\mathbf{q} = (\mathbf{n}^{\top}\mathbf{q})\mathbf{n} + \sum_{i=2}^n (\mathbf{e}_i^{\top}\mathbf{q})\mathbf{e}_i$. Define coordinates $u = \mathbf{n}^{\top}\mathbf{q} - c$ (normal offset) and $\mathbf{w} = (w_2, \ldots, w_n)$ with $w_i = \mathbf{e}_i^{\top}\mathbf{q}$ (tangential coordinates). The Jacobian of this change-of-variables is unity. **Step 5 — Separate the variables.** Because only $u$ appears in the exponent: $$p(u, \mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{Z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \mathbf{1}_{\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}}$$ **Step 6 — Identify the marginal as a 1-D Gaussian.** Marginalizing over $\mathbf{w}$: $$p(u) = \int_{\mathbb{R}^{n-1}} p(u, \mathbf{w})\,d\mathbf{w} = \frac{A_{\parallel}}{Z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ where $A_{\parallel}$ is the cross-sectional area of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ along the tangential directions. Renormalizing: $$\boxed{\,p(u) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\,}$$ This is exactly the univariate Gaussian PDF with mean $\mu = 0$ (i.e., samples concentrate *on the boundary*) and standard deviation $\sigma$. **Key insight:** the tangential coordinates are uniformly distributed — only the *normal* direction is biased. **Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.** The bandwidth $\sigma$ controls the "thickness" of the high-probability shell hugging $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. A small $\sigma$ produces sharp, surface-hugging samples; a large $\sigma$ approaches uniform sampling. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Bridge Sampler Acceptance Probability We quantify how much more likely a bridge sample is to land in a narrow passage than a uniform sample. **Step 1 — Setup.** Consider a narrow corridor of width $w$ and length $\ell$ carved through an otherwise solid rectangular obstacle of dimensions $L_x \times L_y$. The corridor occupies fraction $\phi = w\ell / (L_x L_y)$ of the bounding rectangle. **Step 2 — Uniform sampler probability.** A uniform sample falls inside the corridor with probability: $$P_{\text{uniform}} = \phi = \frac{w\ell}{L_x L_y}$$ **Step 3 — Bridge sampler step (a).** Draw $\mathbf{q}_1 \sim \mathcal{U}(L_x \times L_y)$ and require $\mathbf{q}_1 \in \text{obstacle}$. Probability: $$P(\mathbf{q}_1 \in \text{obs}) = 1 - \phi$$ **Step 4 — Bridge sampler step (b).** Given $\mathbf{q}_1$ is in the obstacle, draw $\mathbf{q}_2 \sim \mathcal{U}(L_x \times L_y)$ and require $\mathbf{q}_2 \in \text{obstacle}$. The conditional probability, assuming independent draws: $$P(\mathbf{q}_2 \in \text{obs} \mid \mathbf{q}_1 \in \text{obs}) \approx 1 - \phi$$ (The approximation ignores the small correlation in pathological cases.) **Step 5 — Bridge sampler step (c).** Given both endpoints are inside the obstacle, the midpoint $\mathbf{m}$ lies in the corridor only if $\mathbf{q}_1$ and $\mathbf{q}_2$ are on *opposite sides* of the corridor with the corridor slicing through the line segment between them. For a corridor of width $w$ and a uniformly random line through the obstacle: $$P(\mathbf{m} \in \text{corridor} \mid \mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2 \in \text{obs}) \approx \frac{w}{\Vert \mathbf{q}_2 - \mathbf{q}_1 \Vert}$$ For simplicity, taking $\Vert \mathbf{q}_2 - \mathbf{q}_1 \Vert \sim L/2$ on average: $$P(\mathbf{m} \in \text{corridor} \mid \mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2 \in \text{obs}) \approx \frac{2w}{L}$$ **Step 6 — Combined bridge acceptance probability.** $$P_{\text{bridge}} \approx (1-\phi)^2 \cdot \frac{2w}{L}$$ **Step 7 — Speedup ratio.** $$\frac{P_{\text{bridge}}}{P_{\text{uniform}}} = \frac{(1-\phi)^2 \cdot \frac{2w}{L}}{\frac{w\ell}{L_x L_y}} = \frac{2(1-\phi)^2 L_x L_y}{\ell L}$$ For $\phi \to 0$ (very thin corridor) and $L_x \approx L_y \approx L$: $$\frac{P_{\text{bridge}}}{P_{\text{uniform}}} \approx \frac{2L}{\ell}$$ This is **massively larger than 1** when the corridor is much shorter than the bounding box, confirming the bridge sampler's superiority for elongated narrow passages. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Metric | Uniform | Gaussian | Bridge | OBPRM | |---|---|---|---|---| | Sample location | Anywhere in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ | Near $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | Midpoint of obstacle-spanning segment | On $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ | | Narrow-passage success | Low | Medium–High | **Very High** | High | | Cost per sample | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ (k-d tree) | $O(1) + 2$ collision checks | $O(\log n)$ (ray-cast) | | Free-space uniformity | **High** | Medium | Low | Low | | Symmetric w.r.t. obstacle | N/A | No (interior vs. exterior skew) | Yes | Yes | | Implementation complexity | Trivial | Low | **Medium** | High (boundary oracle) | | KTU board frequency | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Obstacle-Biased PRM Sampling Pipeline The diagram below maps the **flow of data** through a typical obstacle-biased PRM sampling pipeline, from the configuration-space representation to the final connected roadmap. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Configuration Space C] --> B{C-obstacle
Boundary
Oracle Available?} B -- Yes --> C[OBPRM Pipeline] B -- No --> D[Gaussian Sampler] B -- Sometimes --> E[Bridge Sampler] C --> C1[Draw uniform q in C] C1 --> C2[Ray-cast along random direction v] C2 --> C3[Compute first intersection t-star] C3 --> C4[Place node at q + t-star v] C4 --> C5[Push to Roadmap Buffer] D --> D1[Draw uniform candidate q-cand] D1 --> D2[Query k-d tree for distance
d to nearest obstacle] D2 --> D3[Compute acceptance prob
exp of minus d squared over 2 sigma squared] D3 --> D4{Accept?} D4 -- Yes --> D5[Store q-cand as milestone] D4 -- No --> D1 D5 --> C5 E --> E1[Draw q1 uniform in C] E1 --> E2{q1 in obstacle?} E2 -- No --> E1 E2 -- Yes --> E3[Draw q2 uniform in C] E3 --> E4{q2 in obstacle?} E4 -- No --> E3 E4 -- Yes --> E5[Compute midpoint m] E5 --> E6{m in free space?} E6 -- Yes --> E7[Store m as bridge milestone] E6 -- No --> E1 E7 --> C5 C5 --> F[Local Planner
Connects milestones] F --> G[Roadmap Graph G = V, E] G --> H[Query Phase
Connect start and goal] H --> I[Shortest Path via Dijkstra or A-star] ``` --- ### 4.2 Comparison Flow: How Each Sampler Treats a Narrow Passage ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SP[Single Narrow Passage Scenario] direction LR N[Free Space] -->|w small| P[Narrow Passage] -->|w small| N2[Free Space] OB[Obstacle Walls] -.above.-> P OB -.below.-> P end subgraph US[Uniform Sampler] direction TB U1[Sample q uniform] --> U2{Inside narrow passage?} U2 -- Rare yes --> U3[Add milestone] U2 -- Frequent no --> U4[Discard] end subgraph GS[Gaussian Sampler] direction TB G1[Sample q uniform] --> G2[Compute d to obstacle] G2 --> G3[Accept prob ~ exp of minus d squared] G3 --> G4{Accepted and near passage?} G4 -- Yes --> G5[Add milestone near passage] G4 -- No --> G1 end subgraph BS[Bridge Sampler] direction TB B1[Sample q1 in obstacle above] --> B2[Sample q2 in obstacle below] B2 --> B3[m = midpoint of q1, q2] B3 --> B4{m inside passage?} B4 -- Yes --> B5[High chance add bridge milestone] B4 -- No --> B1 end SP --> US SP --> GS SP --> BS ``` --- ### 4.3 State Transition Diagram: Bridge Sampler Acceptance Loop ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> DrawFirstPoint DrawFirstPoint: Draw q1 uniform in C DrawFirstPoint --> CheckQ1Inside CheckQ1Inside: Is q1 inside C-obs? CheckQ1Inside --> DrawFirstPoint: No, reject and redraw CheckQ1Inside --> DrawSecondPoint: Yes, proceed DrawSecondPoint: Draw q2 uniform in C DrawSecondPoint --> CheckQ2Inside CheckQ2Inside: Is q2 inside C-obs? CheckQ2Inside --> DrawSecondPoint: No, reject and redraw CheckQ2Inside --> ComputeMidpoint: Yes, proceed ComputeMidpoint: Compute m = q1 + q2 divided by 2 ComputeMidpoint --> CheckMidpointFree CheckMidpointFree: Is m in C-free? CheckMidpointFree --> DrawFirstPoint: No, reject and restart CheckMidpointFree --> AddMilestone: Yes, accept m AddMilestone: Add m to roadmap vertex set V AddMilestone --> DrawFirstPoint: Continue sampling ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper Style]** *"What is meant by 'Sampling Near the Obstacles' in the context of Probabilistic Roadmaps? Mention any two advantages over uniform random sampling."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Sampling Near the Obstacles is a class of **non-uniform sampling strategies** for PRMs that bias sample placement toward the boundaries $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ of configuration-space obstacles. It uses samplers such as the **Gaussian sampler**, **Bridge sampler**, and **Obstacle-Based PRM (OBPRM)**. **Advantages over uniform sampling:** 1. **Improved narrow-passage coverage:** By concentrating samples along obstacle boundaries, the probability of placing a milestone inside narrow passages (whose walls coincide with obstacle boundaries) increases dramatically, mitigating the narrow-passage curse. 2. **Better roadmap connectivity:** Because connectivity in cluttered C-spaces is determined by obstacle topology, boundary samples produce roadmaps with fewer disconnected components, reducing query-phase failure rates. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per advantage — full credit only if both are clearly phrased.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Retrospective]** *"State the Bridge Test used in the Bridge Sampler for PRM. Under what geometric condition is a midpoint sample accepted?"* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Bridge Test is the **acceptance criterion** of the Bridge Sampler proposed by Sun, Reif, and Manocha. The procedure is: 1. Draw $\mathbf{q}_1 \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$ and **reject** unless $\mathbf{q}_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. 2. Draw $\mathbf{q}_2 \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$ and **reject** unless $\mathbf{q}_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. 3. Compute $\mathbf{m} = (\mathbf{q}_1 + \mathbf{q}_2)/2$. **Geometric acceptance condition:** The midpoint $\mathbf{m}$ is accepted **if and only if** $\mathbf{m} \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. **Geometric interpretation:** This ensures that the line segment $\overline{\mathbf{q}_1 \mathbf{q}_2}$ "bridges" a free-space gap — it starts and ends inside an obstacle but passes through free space in the middle. Such midpoints are statistically very likely to lie inside narrow passages. *(Award 1 mark each for the three steps; partial credit for stating the geometric interpretation.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Style]* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Obstacle-Based PRM (OBPRM)** sampling algorithm. Discuss why it improves narrow-passage performance compared to uniform sampling. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **obstacle-biased density function** used in the Gaussian sampler and show that along the normal direction of a planar obstacle, the marginal distribution is a univariate Gaussian with standard deviation $\sigma$. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q-A (a):** **Algorithm — OBPRM (7 marks):** OBPRM, introduced by Hsu et al. (1998), explicitly projects samples onto obstacle boundaries. **Step 1.** Draw a point $\mathbf{q} \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}})$. **Step 2.** Choose a random direction $\mathbf{v} \sim \mathcal{U}(S^{n-1})$ uniformly on the unit sphere. **Step 3.** Perform ray-casting from $\mathbf{q}$ along $\mathbf{v}$: - Compute $t^* = \min\{ t > 0 \mid A(\mathbf{q} + t\mathbf{v}) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset \}$ - i.e., the smallest parameter at which the robot first collides with an obstacle. **Step 4.** The new milestone is $\mathbf{q}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{q} + t^* \mathbf{v}$, which lies on $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. **Step 5.** Repeat $N$ times and connect milestones with a local planner to form the roadmap. **Diagram (schematic ASCII for the answer sheet):** ``` Obstacle ┌────────┐ │ │ •q ──►│ │ ← ray direction v │ │ └────────┘ ↖ q_new = q + t*·v (on boundary) ``` **Why it improves narrow-passage performance (3 of the 7 marks):** 1. **Every sample lies on $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$** — narrow passages are bounded by obstacle walls, so samples are guaranteed to be *adjacent* to such passages. 2. **Density tracks obstacle geometry** — long, tortuous boundaries receive proportionally more samples, exactly matching the topology that determines C-space connectivity. 3. **Empirically**, OBPRM has been shown to find connected roadmaps in problems where uniform PRM fails even after 10× more samples (Hsu et al., 1998). --- **Model Solution to Q-A (b):** This is the derivation from Section 3.1 above. The complete step-by-step solution is: **Assumption:** The obstacle boundary is locally approximated by a hyperplane with outward unit normal $\mathbf{n}$. **Step 1.** Write the obstacle-biased density as: $$p(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{Z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\mathbf{n}^{\top}\mathbf{q} - c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ **Step 2.** Define the normal coordinate $u = \mathbf{n}^{\top}\mathbf{q} - c$ and tangential coordinates $\mathbf{w}$. **Step 3.** Substitute and separate variables: $$p(u, \mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{Z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\mathbf{1}_{\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}}$$ **Step 4.** Marginalize over $\mathbf{w}$ and renormalize: $$p(u) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ **Conclusion:** This is a univariate Gaussian with $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. Hence the bandwidth parameter $\sigma$ directly controls the *thickness* of the sampling shell around $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. The tangential distribution remains uniform, so only the *normal* direction is biased. *(Each step ~1.5 marks; conclusion 1 mark.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Explain the **Bridge Sampler** with the help of a flowchart. Derive the relative speedup it provides over uniform sampling in a narrow corridor of width $w$ and length $\ell$ inside a bounding box of side $L$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast the **Gaussian Sampler**, **Bridge Sampler**, and **OBPRM** along the following axes: (i) sample location, (ii) computational cost per accepted sample, (iii) narrow-passage performance, (iv) dependence on a boundary-distance oracle. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q-B (a):** **Bridge Sampler Algorithm (4 marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid state diagram in Section 4.3 — reproduce as flowchart in exam.)* 1. Repeat until $N$ milestones obtained: - Draw $\mathbf{q}_1 \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$; reject if $\mathbf{q}_1 \notin \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. - Draw $\mathbf{q}_2 \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{C})$; reject if $\mathbf{q}_2 \notin \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$. - Compute $\mathbf{m} = (\mathbf{q}_1 + \mathbf{q}_2)/2$. - Accept $\mathbf{m}$ iff $\mathbf{m} \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. **Derivation of Speedup (3 marks):** **Uniform sampler probability of landing in corridor:** $$P_{\text{uniform}} = \frac{w \ell}{L^2}$$ **Bridge sampler probability** (from Section 3.2 derivation): $$P_{\text{bridge}} \approx (1-\phi)^2 \cdot \frac{2w}{L}$$ **Speedup ratio:** $$\frac{P_{\text{bridge}}}{P_{\text{uniform}}} = \frac{(1-\phi)^2 \cdot \frac{2w}{L}}{\frac{w\ell}{L^2}} = \frac{2(1-\phi)^2 L}{\ell}$$ For thin corridors ($\phi \to 0$), this ratio approaches $\dfrac{2L}{\ell} \gg 1$. For a corridor 10× thinner than the box and 5× shorter, the bridge sampler is **~20× more likely** to produce a useful sample. --- **Model Solution to Q-B (b):** | Axis | Gaussian Sampler | Bridge Sampler | OBPRM | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Sample location | Anywhere; density $\propto \exp(-d^2/2\sigma^2)$ | Midpoint of obstacle-spanning segment | On $\partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ via ray-cast | | (ii) Cost per accepted sample | Medium (k-d tree query + reject loop) | Low–Medium (2 collision checks) | High (ray-cast through C-obstacle) | | (iii) Narrow-passage performance | Good for medium passages | **Excellent** for elongated passages | Excellent for boundary-defined passages | | (iv) Boundary-distance oracle | **Required** (k-d tree / signed-distance field) | **Not required** (only collision check) | **Required** (ray-cast boundary oracle) | *(Award 1.5–2 marks per well-articulated row; up to 1 bonus mark for crisp phrasing.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic — and how to avoid it:** > > 1. **Confusing Gaussian with Bridge sampler.** Gaussian uses *one* uniform candidate and an exponential acceptance probability; Bridge uses *two* uniform candidates and a midpoint test. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks immediately. > 2. **Forgetting the normalization constant $Z$.** A derivation that omits $Z$ or fails to renormalize when marginalizing will lose 1–2 marks on the 7-mark derivations. > 3. **Omitting the assumption of a planar/local-linear obstacle boundary.** When deriving the Gaussian marginal, you must state that the obstacle is locally planar — otherwise the formula $d = \mathbf{n}^{\top}\mathbf{q} - c$ is invalid. Examiner explicitly checks for this. > 4. **Writing $\vert \Sigma \vert$ with vertical pipes in markdown tables.** This breaks the table layout during evaluation. Use `\vert \Sigma \vert` instead. > 5. **Skipping the geometric interpretation** of the bridge test. The "obstacle-encasing line segment whose midpoint pierces free space" intuition is worth at least 1 mark. > 6. **Not mentioning the narrow-passage curse** explicitly. Phrases like *"exponentially decaying probability $(w/L)^{n-1}$"* and *"uniform PRM fails in high dimensions"* trigger partial credit even when the rest is shaky. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):** > > - **Three samplers to master:** Gaussian, Bridge, OBPRM. Know the algorithm steps of each in order. > - **Key formula to memorize:** > $$p(\mathbf{q}) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{d(\mathbf{q}, \partial \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}})^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ > - **Bridge acceptance rule:** midpoint of *two obstacle points* lies in $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$. > - **OBPRM projection rule:** $\mathbf{q}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{q} + t^*\mathbf{v}$, where $t^*$ is the first-collision ray parameter. > - **Narrow-passage curse:** uniform PRM probability $\sim (w/L)^{n-1}$ — decays exponentially in dimension $n$. > - **Connectivity radius rule (Hsu et al.):** $r \sim (\log n / n)^{1/d}$ for asymptotic connectivity with $n$ samples in $d$ dimensions. > - **Bridge sampler speedup over uniform:** approximately $2L/\ell$ for a corridor of length $\ell$ in a box of side $L$. > - **Gaussian marginal:** univariate Gaussian along obstacle normal; uniform along tangent directions. > - **Boundary oracle requirement:** Gaussian and OBPRM need a signed-distance or ray-cast oracle; Bridge does not — only a collision checker. > - **Symmetry:** Bridge is symmetric w.r.t. interior/exterior; Gaussian (with rejection) skews toward the *exterior* unless $d$ is signed. > - **Examiner-favorite phrases:** *"narrow-passage curse," "boundary-biased sampling," "bridge test," "configuration-space obstacle boundary," "asymptotic optimality vs. probabilistic completeness."* Use these verbatim.

topics.title: Sampling Inside Narrow Passages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sampling Inside Narrow Passages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In sampling-based motion planning (SMP), the **Narrow Passage Problem** refers to the inherent difficulty that probabilistic sampling strategies—such as the **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM)** and **Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)**—encounter when the free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ contains **low-volume, high-connectivity bottlenecks**. Standard uniform random sampling places a sample point $q_{rand}$ inside any region of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ with probability strictly proportional to that region's volume. Consequently, narrow corridors (whose measure tends to zero) are statistically starved of samples, even though they constitute the *only* topological route between two adjacent chambers of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** *Narrow Passage Sampling* is the family of non-uniform, informed, or retraction-based sampling techniques designed to bias the random sampling distribution $P(q_{rand})$ toward the critical bottleneck regions of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, thereby improving the connectivity of the resulting roadmap or tree.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are blindfolded inside a large hotel and asked to find the only key to a locked ballroom door. The key is hanging on a thin pole in a narrow corridor connecting two enormous lobbies. If you walk around randomly, you will almost never bump into that pole—the lobby is huge compared to the corridor.

> A uniform sampler behaves exactly like this blindfolded person. The "narrow passage" is the thin corridor. The fix is to either (a) occasionally take a **wider stride that overshoots the corridor walls** (Bridge Test), (b) **drop samples from the obstacles themselves** (Obstacle-Based Sampling), or (c) **gravitate samples toward the skeleton** of the free space (Medial Axis).

### 1.3 Why Standard Uniform Sampling Fails — Quantitative Intuition

Let the configuration space volume be $V_{total}$, and let the narrow passage have volume $V_{np}$. For $N$ samples drawn i.i.d. uniformly, the expected number of samples inside the passage is:

$$E[N_{np}] = N \cdot \frac{V_{np}}{V_{total}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $V_{np}/V_{total} \approx 10^{-4}$ and $N = 10{,}000$, then only **1 sample on average** lands inside the passage. KTU problems frequently use this probability argument.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Inventory

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\mathcal{C}_{space}$ | Total configuration space |
| $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ | Collision-free subset of $\mathcal{C}_{space}$ |
| $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Obstacle region |
| **Medial Axis** | Set of points having at least two closest points on $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ |
| **Bridge Test** | Heuristic to detect a "narrow" sample that lies between two obstacles |
| **Gaussian Sampler** | Sample is drawn from a normal distribution centered on a valid node |
| **Retraction** | Mapping a point back to $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ along the shortest gradient path |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Topology of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ and the Connectivity Bottleneck

A roadmap $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$ is connected in the graph-theoretic sense **if and only if** samples are placed in every connected component of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, and the local planner successfully connects neighbors across all narrow passages. The failure mode is structural: the random graph restricted to any narrow passage is almost surely **disconnected**.

### 2.2 The Six Pillars of Narrow Passage Sampling (KTU High-Yield)

**1. Gaussian Sampling (Kavraki & Kolar, 2005)**
- Select an existing milestone $v \in V$.
- Sample $q_{rand} \sim \mathcal{N}(v, \sigma^2 I)$ with a small variance $\sigma^2$.
- Rationale: dense regions of the roadmap correspond to areas the local planner could already connect, so a tight Gaussian *refines* edges, but with the right $\sigma$ it also penetrates corridors.

**2. Bridge Test Sampling (Sun, Reif & others)**
- Draw $q_1$ uniformly.
- Draw $q_2$ from a small uniform ball $B(q_1, \delta)$.
- $q_1$ is accepted **iff** $q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ and $q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- Geometric meaning: $q_1$ must lie *inside* an obstacle thick enough to span the ball $B$.

**3. Obstacle-Based / Near-Obstacle Sampling (Amato et al.)**
- Sample $q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$.
- Compute a direction $d$ and a small step $d_0$ to push $q_1$ just outside the obstacle: $q = q_1 + d_0 \cdot d$.
- This guarantees samples lie on the **boundary** $\partial \mathcal{C}_{obs}$, where narrow corridors meet walls.

**4. Medial Axis (Skeleton) Sampling**
- For each point $q \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, compute the distance to $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$: $d(q) = \min_{p \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}} \vert\vert q - p \vert\vert$.
- The medial axis $\mathcal{M}$ is the set of points where $d(q)$ is **locally maximal**.
- Samples are taken from $\mathcal{M}$, which by definition lies in the "center" of corridors.

**5. Random Walks & Diffusion**
- From a milestone $v$, perform a Brownian-motion-style walk: $q_{k+1} = q_k + \eta_k$, with $\eta_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)$.
- Walks naturally follow corridors because they cannot penetrate walls.

**6. Dynamic-Domain RRT (Yershova & LaValle)**
- The growth domain of each node shrinks as the tree fills space, forcing samples to "drift" toward unexplored regions—including narrow passages.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform hit probability | $P(q_{rand} \in \mathcal{P}) = V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{total}$ | Estimating expected sample count in a passage $\mathcal{P}$ |
| Gaussian density | $P(q) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\sigma^2)^{d/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert\vert q - v \vert\vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Biased sampling around milestone $v$ |
| Bridge test acceptance | $P(\text{accept}) \propto \text{Vol}\bigl(B(q_1,\delta) \cap \mathcal{C}_{free}\bigr)$ | Probability a bridge point lies in a corridor |
| Clearance function | $d(q) = \min_{p \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}} \vert\vert q - p \vert\vert$ | Medial axis test: $q \in \mathcal{M} \iff \nabla^2 d(q) < 0$ (locally) |
| Medial axis distance | $r_{med}(q) = \min_{p \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}} \vert\vert q - p \vert\vert$ | Maximum inscribed ball radius at $q$ |
| Expected coverage | $E[\text{coverage}] = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot V_{passage}}$ | Poisson-process coverage of a region |
| Retraction gradient | $\dot{q} = \nabla d(q)$, projected to $\partial \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Pulling samples onto the medial axis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For absolute-value bars **inside any markdown table**, write $\vert x \vert$ — *never* use the literal pipe symbol `|`, because it breaks the column delimiter.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surgical robotics:** the tool must navigate the esophagus—a centimeter-scale corridor inside the body.
- **Autonomous driving:** the vehicle must slip between tightly parked cars.
- **Aircraft ground routing:** taxiways between terminals are narrow passages in the gate graph.
- **Molecular motion planning (drug docking):** the ligand must thread through a protein's narrow binding pocket.
- **Warehouse robotics:** picking arms must reach items in deep, narrow shelf cubbies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Number of Samples Inside a Narrow Passage

Let $\mathcal{P} \subset \mathcal{C}_{free}$ be a narrow passage of volume $V_{\mathcal{P}}$, embedded in a total free-space volume $V_{free}$. We draw $N$ samples i.i.d. from the uniform distribution $U(\mathcal{C}_{free})$.

**Step 1 — Probability density of the uniform sampler.**

$$p(q) = \frac{1}{V_{free}}, \quad \forall \, q \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$$

**Step 2 — Probability a single sample lands in $\mathcal{P}$.**

By definition of the uniform density on a measurable subset,

$$P(q_{rand} \in \mathcal{P}) = \int_{\mathcal{P}} p(q) \, dq = \int_{\mathcal{P}} \frac{1}{V_{free}} \, dq = \frac{V_{\mathcal{P}}}{V_{free}}$$

**Step 3 — Distribution of the count $K$.**

Each of the $N$ samples is a Bernoulli trial with success probability $p = V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{free}$. Therefore $K \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$, and its expectation is

$$\begin{aligned}
E[K] &= N \cdot p \\
&= N \cdot \frac{V_{\mathcal{P}}}{V_{free}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Variance and dispersion.**

$$\text{Var}(K) = N \cdot p \cdot (1 - p)$$

**Step 5 — Approximation for large $N$, small $p$.**

When $N$ is large and $V_{\mathcal{P}}/V_{free} \to 0$, the Binomial converges to a Poisson distribution with rate $\lambda = N V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{free}$:

$$P(K = k) \approx \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical illustration (a typical KTU problem).**

Suppose $V_{\mathcal{P}} = 0.01 \, \text{m}^3$, $V_{free} = 100 \, \text{m}^3$, and $N = 5{,}000$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E[K] &= 5000 \cdot \frac{0.01}{100} \\
&= 5000 \cdot 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

Hence we expect only **half a sample** inside the passage. The roadmap will very likely be **disconnected** through $\mathcal{P}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bridge Test Acceptance Probability

Let $q_1 \sim U(\mathcal{C}_{space})$ and $q_2 \sim U(B(q_1, \delta))$. The bridge accepts $q_1$ iff $q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ **and** $q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$.

**Step 1 — Decompose the joint acceptance event.**

$$P(\text{accept}) = P(q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}) \cdot P(q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \mid q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs})$$

**Step 2 — First factor.**

$$P(q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}) = \frac{V_{obs}}{V_{total}}$$

**Step 3 — Second factor (the "thickness" term).**

Define the local thickness at $q_1$ as the diameter of the largest free-space ball inside the corridor that contains $q_1$'s antipode:

$$P(q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \mid q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}) = \frac{\text{Vol}\bigl(B(q_1,\delta) \cap \mathcal{C}_{free}\bigr)}{\text{Vol}\bigl(B(q_1,\delta)\bigr)} = \frac{\text{Vol}_{\text{free}}(B(q_1,\delta))}{c_d \, \delta^d}$$

where $c_d$ is the volume constant of the unit ball in $\mathbb{R}^d$ ($c_2 = \pi$, $c_3 = 4\pi/3$).

**Step 4 — Full expression.**

$$P(\text{accept}) = \frac{V_{obs}}{V_{total}} \cdot \frac{\text{Vol}_{\text{free}}(B(q_1,\delta))}{c_d \delta^d}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

The acceptance probability is **non-zero only in regions of $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ that are adjacent to a corridor of width comparable to $\delta$**. This is precisely the geometric signature of a narrow passage.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Medial Axis Membership via the Laplacian of the Clearance Function

The clearance $d(q) = \min_{p \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}} \vert\vert q - p \vert\vert$ is a Lipschitz function on $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

**Step 1 — First-order condition (gradient).**

At any interior point $q \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, the gradient $\nabla d(q)$ points toward the **nearest obstacle point**.

**Step 2 — Second-order condition (medial axis).**

$q$ lies on the medial axis $\mathcal{M}$ if and only if the *second derivative* of $d$ in every direction perpendicular to $\nabla d$ is strictly negative:

$$q \in \mathcal{M} \iff \forall \, v \perp \nabla d(q), \quad v^{\top} \, H(d)(q) \, v < 0$$

where $H(d)$ is the Hessian of $d$.

**Step 3 — Equivalently, equidistance.**

A point $q$ is on $\mathcal{M}$ iff there exist **at least two** distinct points $p_1, p_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ with

$$\vert\vert q - p_1 \vert\vert = \vert\vert q - p_2 \vert\vert = d(q)$$

**Step 4 — Retraction onto $\mathcal{M}$.**

Starting from any $q_0 \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, follow the gradient of $d$ projected onto the orthogonal complement of the level sets:

$$\frac{dq}{dt} = \nabla d(q) - \bigl(\nabla d(q) \cdot \hat{n}\bigr) \hat{n}$$

where $\hat{n} = \nabla d(q)/\vert\vert \nabla d(q) \vert\vert$. The flow converges to $\mathcal{M}$.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Sampling Strategies

| Strategy | Bias Target | Computational Cost | Narrow-Passage Gain | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform** | None | $O(1)$ per sample | $\approx V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{free}$ | Baseline |
| **Gaussian** | Existing milestone $v$ | $O(d)$ per sample | Moderate (depends on $\sigma$) | High |
| **Bridge Test** | Interior of obstacles near corridors | $O(\text{collision check})$ | High for $\delta$-wide passages | High |
| **Obstacle-Based** | $\partial \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | $O(\text{collision check} + \text{projection})$ | High near walls | Moderate |
| **Medial Axis** | $\mathcal{M}$ | $O(\text{distance field})$ | **Highest** (by construction) | High |
| **Random Walk** | Walks a connected path | $O(N_{steps} \cdot \text{collision})$ | High for thin corridors | Moderate |
| **Dynamic-Domain RRT** | Sparse regions | $O(\log N)$ per node | Moderate to High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Checklist

- All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix (e.g., `nodeA`, `startP`, `obst1`).
- Labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text and underscores — **no** bold, italics, or pipes inside quotes.

### 4.2 Architecture Flow — Sampling Inside Narrow Passages

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START SAMPLING LOOP]) --> nodeB{DRAW q1 UNIFORMLY IN C_SPACE}
    nodeB --> nodeC{SELECT SAMPLING STRATEGY}
    nodeC -- UNIFORM --> nodeU[ACCEPT q1 AS qrand]
    nodeC -- GAUSSIAN --> nodeG[CENTER ON MILESTONE v WITH SIGMA]
    nodeC -- BRIDGE --> nodeD{DRAW q2 IN BALL q1 DELTA}
    nodeD --> nodeE{q1 IN C_OBST AND q2 IN C_FREE}
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF[ACCEPT q1]
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeB
    nodeC -- OBSTACLE_BASED --> nodeH[PROJECT q1 ONTO BOUNDARY]
    nodeC -- MEDIAL_AXIS --> nodeM[RETRACT q1 TO MEDIAL AXIS M]
    nodeC -- RANDOM_WALK --> nodeW[STEP q1 ALONG BROWNIAN PATH]
    nodeU --> nodeK{qrand IN C_FREE}
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeM --> nodeK
    nodeW --> nodeK
    nodeK -- YES --> nodeN[ADD MILESTONE TO GRAPH]
    nodeK -- NO --> nodeB
    nodeN --> nodeP[CONNECT TO K NEAREST NEIGHBORS]
    nodeP --> nodeQ{REACH GOAL?}
    nodeQ -- NO --> nodeB
    nodeQ -- YES --> nodeZ([RETURN PATH])
```

```mermaid

    subgraph MID[SAMPLING CORE]
        mA[STRATEGY SELECTOR]
```
