### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Why Uniform Sampling Misses Corridors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS([LARGE C_FREE REGION]) --> nodeP1[CHAMBER LEFT]
    nodeS --> nodeP2[CHAMBER RIGHT]
    nodeP1 --> nodeN[NARROW CORRIDOR WIDTH W SMALL]
    nodeN --> nodeP2
    nodeU[UNIFORM SAMPLER] -. SCATTERS DENSELY .-> nodeP1
    nodeU -. SCATTERS DENSELY .-> nodeP2
    nodeU -. RARELY HITS .-> nodeN
    nodeB[BRIDGE SAMPLER] -. CONCENTRATES .-> nodeN
    nodeM[MEDIAL AXIS] -. FILLS SKELETON .-> nodeN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The dashed arrows represent sample distribution. Uniform samples crowd the chambers but starve the corridor. Bridge and Medial-Axis strategies redistribute mass *into* the corridor, where the connectivity of the roadmap is decided.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is meant by the "narrow passage problem" in sampling-based motion planning? Why does uniform random sampling suffer from it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark:** The narrow passage problem is the difficulty of placing samples in low-volume bottleneck regions of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ that are topologically necessary for connecting two chambers.
- **1 mark:** Under uniform sampling, the probability of hitting any subset $\mathcal{P} \subset \mathcal{C}_{free}$ equals $V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{free}$, so as $V_{\mathcal{P}} \to 0$, the expected number of samples in $\mathcal{P}$ approaches zero.
- **1 mark:** Consequently, the resulting roadmap is almost surely disconnected across $\mathcal{P}$, and the planner fails to return a path.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Bridge Test sampling rule and explain its geometric intuition.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **1 mark:** Sample $q_1$ uniformly; sample $q_2$ uniformly in a small ball $B(q_1, \delta)$. Accept $q_1$ if $q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ and $q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- **1 mark:** Geometrically, the rule detects points that lie inside an obstacle *thick* enough that a ball of radius $\delta$ crosses from the obstacle into free space.
- **1 mark:** Such points exist almost exclusively in the walls adjacent to narrow passages, so the rule biases the sampler toward bottlenecks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *With the help of neat derivations, show that for uniform random sampling, the expected number of samples landing inside a narrow passage of volume $V_{\mathcal{P}}$ drawn from a free space of volume $V_{free}$ using $N$ samples is $N \cdot V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{free}$. Also compute the same for $N = 2000$, $V_{\mathcal{P}} = 0.05 \, \text{m}^3$, $V_{free} = 50 \, \text{m}^3$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the uniform density** *(1 mark)*

$$p(q) = \frac{1}{V_{free}}, \quad q \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$$

**Step 2 — Marginal probability of hitting the passage** *(1 mark)*

$$P(q_{rand} \in \mathcal{P}) = \int_{\mathcal{P}} \frac{1}{V_{free}} \, dq = \frac{V_{\mathcal{P}}}{V_{free}}$$

**Step 3 — Distribution of the count $K$** *(1 mark)*

Since each of the $N$ samples is an i.i.d. Bernoulli trial with $p = V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{free}$, the random count $K$ follows $\text{Binomial}(N, p)$, with expectation:

$$E[K] = N \cdot p = N \cdot \frac{V_{\mathcal{P}}}{V_{free}}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numerical values** *(1 mark)*

$$\begin{aligned}
E[K] &= 2000 \cdot \frac{0.05}{50} \\
&= 2000 \cdot 10^{-3} \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation** *(1 mark)*

On average only **two** samples fall in the passage; the variance is $N p (1-p) \approx 1.99$, so the typical outcome is 1–3 samples.

**Step 6 — Concluding remark** *(1 mark)*

This sparsity is precisely why uniform sampling is unreliable in narrow passages and why non-uniform strategies are necessary.

---

**(b)** *Explain the Bridge Test sampling algorithm in detail. Discuss two limitations of this method.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Algorithm statement** *(2 marks)*

1. Sample $q_1 \sim U(\mathcal{C}_{space})$.
2. Sample $q_2 \sim U(B(q_1, \delta))$ for a small radius $\delta$.
3. If $q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ **and** $q_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, accept $q_1$ (and sometimes also $q_2$).
4. Otherwise, discard both and repeat.

**Step 2 — Geometric intuition** *(1 mark)*

The accepted configurations $q_1$ are exactly those where a small ball straddles an obstacle-free boundary — geometrically identifying the walls of a corridor.

**Step 3 — Acceptance probability** *(1 mark)*

$$P(\text{accept}) = \frac{V_{obs}}{V_{total}} \cdot \frac{\text{Vol}(B(q_1,\delta) \cap \mathcal{C}_{free})}{c_d \delta^d}$$

**Step 4 — Limitation 1: parameter sensitivity** *(1 mark)*

The choice of $\delta$ is critical. If $\delta$ is much larger than the corridor width, very few $q_2$ fall in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ and acceptance drops to zero. If $\delta$ is too small, the sampler behaves like uniform sampling.

**Step 5 — Limitation 2: cost of two collision checks per sample** *(1 mark)*

Each candidate requires two collision queries; for high-dimensional configuration spaces this can dominate runtime.

**Step 6 — Limitation 3 (bonus)** *(optional, 1 mark)*

Bridge test only samples *near* the obstacle wall, not the medial axis itself; it may still miss the geometric center of very long, twisting corridors.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Alternative*

**(a)** *Define the medial axis $\mathcal{M}$ of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. Prove that a point $q \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$ lies on $\mathcal{M}$ if and only if there exist at least two distinct points $p_1, p_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ equidistant from $q$ and achieving the clearance $d(q)$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of clearance** *(1 mark)*

$$d(q) = \min_{p \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}} \vert\vert q - p \vert\vert$$

**Step 2 — Definition of medial axis** *(1 mark)*

$$\mathcal{M} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \mid d(q) \text{ is locally maximal} \}$$

**Step 3 — Forward direction ($\Rightarrow$)** *(2 marks)*

Suppose $q \in \mathcal{M}$. The function $d(\cdot)$ attains a strict local maximum at $q$, so by the first-order necessary condition, $\nabla d(q) = 0$. Geometrically, the closest obstacle feature is degenerate — there must be at least two obstacle points $p_1 \neq p_2$ with $\vert\vert q - p_1 \vert\vert = \vert\vert q - p_2 \vert\vert = d(q)$. If only one $p$ achieved the minimum, moving infinitesimally away from $p$ would strictly increase $d$, contradicting the local maximality.

**Step 4 — Reverse direction ($\Leftarrow$)** *(2 marks)*

Conversely, suppose two distinct $p_1, p_2 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ exist with $\vert\vert q - p_1 \vert\vert = \vert\vert q - p_2 \vert\vert = d(q)$. Any infinitesimal displacement from $q$ must decrease the distance to at least one of $p_1, p_2$, since the two vectors $(p_1 - q)$ and $(p_2 - p_2)$ are linearly independent. Therefore $d$ is locally maximal at $q$, so $q \in \mathcal{M}$.

**Step 5 — Concluding statement** *(1 mark)*

Hence $q \in \mathcal{M}$ iff the inscribed ball of radius $d(q)$ centered at $q$ is tangent to $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ at **two or more** distinct points.

---

**(b)** *Compare Medial Axis Sampling and Gaussian Sampling in terms of (i) bias toward narrow passages, (ii) computational cost, and (iii) dependence on prior roadmap structure. Justify your answer.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Bias toward narrow passages** *(2 marks)*

- **Medial Axis:** Maximum bias by construction — samples lie on the skeleton of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, which runs through the geometric center of every corridor.
- **Gaussian:** Bias is local and isotropic; if the existing milestones $v$ are not already in the corridor, the Gaussian cloud also misses it. Hence the bias is *indirect* and *reactive*, not structural.

**(ii) Computational cost** *(2 marks)*

- **Medial Axis:** Requires a complete distance field $d(q)$ over $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ plus retraction; pre-processing is $O(V_{grid} \cdot d)$ but per-sample cost is cheap once the field exists.
- **Gaussian:** Cost is $O(d)$ per sample — extremely cheap — but performance depends on the parameter $\sigma$ being tuned to the local corridor width.

**(iii) Dependence on prior roadmap structure** *(2 marks)*

- **Medial Axis:** Independent of the existing roadmap; the skeleton is determined by $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ alone.
- **Gaussian:** Strongly dependent — samples are drawn around *existing* milestones, so the method fails if no milestone is already inside or adjacent to a corridor.

**Conclusion** *(1 mark)*

Medial Axis is the theoretically optimal choice for narrow-passage coverage, while Gaussian is a cheap, complementary refinement step. A practical planner often combines both.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the volume ratio.** Many students write $P = V_{\mathcal{P}}$ instead of $P = V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{free}$ — a 1-mark deduction that compounds.
> 2. **Confusing medial axis with Voronoi diagram.** The medial axis is defined for $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ with respect to $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$, not for an arbitrary set of points.
> 3. **Skipping the Binomial derivation.** Examiners expect the explicit Bernoulli-trial argument; merely stating $E[K] = N \cdot p$ without justification loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the "two distinct obstacle points" condition.** Medial axis membership requires *at least two* equidistant obstacle points, not one.
> 5. **Mixing up Bridge Test with Gaussian.** Bridge Test requires $q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ — students often state the inverse condition.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Narrow Passage Problem:** Uniform sampling places samples with probability proportional to volume, so low-volume corridors are starved.
- **Expected hit count:** $E[K] = N \cdot V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{free}$, Binomial-distributed; for large $N$, small $p$, approximates Poisson.
- **Gaussian Sampling:** Cheap, local refinement around an existing milestone; needs good $\sigma$.
- **Bridge Test:** Two-point check; geometric signature of corridor walls; very sensitive to $\delta$.
- **Obstacle-Based:** Project samples onto $\partial \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ to bias toward boundaries.
- **Medial Axis:** Skeleton of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$; equidistance from at least two obstacle points; maximum clearance centerline.
- **Random Walks:** Naturally follow corridors; expensive in many dimensions.
- **Dynamic-Domain RRT:** Shrinks the attraction radius to push tree growth into sparse regions.
- **Retraction:** Follows $\nabla d(q)$ projected on the level set, converging to $\mathcal{M}$.
- **KTU Numerical Heuristic:** If $V_{\mathcal{P}} / V_{free} < 10^{-3}$, expect $E[K] < N \cdot 10^{-3}$ — non-uniform strategies are mandatory.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Watch the volume ratio, the two-obstacle-point condition for $\mathcal{M}$, and the $q_1 \in \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ clause in Bridge Test.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visibility-Based Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Visibility-Based Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Visibility-Based Sampling** is a probabilistic sampling-based roadmap construction paradigm in robot motion planning wherein a sparse yet topologically meaningful graph (called the *Visibility Roadmap* or *Vis-PRM*) is built inside the free configuration space $C_{free}$. The roadmap is constructed incrementally by classifying every randomly sampled configuration $q \in C_{free}$ into one of two semantic categories based on its **visibility** relationship with the already existing roadmap nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visibility (KTU 2024 Definition):** Two configurations $q_1, q_2 \in C_{free}$ are said to be *mutually visible* if the local path $L(q_1, q_2)$ connecting them lies entirely inside $C_{free}$. Formally,
>
> $$L(q_1, q_2) \subset C_{free}$$

The two canonical node types are:
- **Guard Node ($g$):** A configuration that cannot be connected to any existing guard via a collision-free local path. It therefore *guards* (witnesses / covers) a previously unexplored visibility region of $C_{free}$.
- **Connector Node ($c$):** A configuration that is visible from **two or more** distinct guards. It acts as a bridge, establishing edges between the visibility regions of those guards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lighthouse Keepers in a Foggy Bay

Imagine a fleet of ships (your robot) that must navigate through a foggy, obstacle-strewn bay ($C_{free}$). The harbor master cannot see the entire bay at once, so he drops **lighthouses** at strategic points.

1. The master **tosses a stone** at a random point in the bay. If the stone lands in the water ($C_{free}$) and from that stone he **cannot see any existing lighthouse** (no line-of-sight to any current guard), he builds a **new lighthouse** there — this is a *guard node*.
2. If, however, the stone lands at a spot from which he can see **two or more lighthouses simultaneously**, that spot becomes a *connector* — a strategic anchorage that links the lighthouses via a safe channel.
3. The bay is deemed sufficiently surveyed only when **new stones keep falling on spots already illuminated** by existing lighthouses, meaning no new coverage is being added.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Visibility-Based Sampling Matters in KTU Robotics:**
> Unlike dense PRM which inflates the graph with thousands of redundant nodes, Vis-PRM produces a **minimal, topology-preserving** roadmap. It is particularly effective in *narrow-passage* problems where uniform sampling density fails.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants

- **Number of guards** $N_g$ — directly determines the topological fidelity of the roadmap.
- **Number of connectors** $N_c$ — represents the inter-region connectivity.
- **Visibility region** $V(q_i)$ of a node $q_i$ is the set:
  $$V(q_i) = \{\, q \in C_{free} \mid L(q_i, q) \subset C_{free} \,\}$$
- **Local planner** distance $d_{max}$ — maximum edge length of the roadmap.
- **Probabilistic completeness threshold** — probability $\geq 1 - \varepsilon$ of finding a solution as $n \to \infty$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Visibility-Based Sampling

The Vis-PRM algorithm operates in two distinct phases. Below is the structured step-by-step logic:

**Phase A — Learning (Roadmap Construction):**
1. Initialize the roadmap $R = (V, E)$ with $V = \emptyset$, $E = \emptyset$.
2. Sample a configuration $q_{rand}$ uniformly at random from the configuration space $C$.
3. Collision-check $q_{rand}$. If $q_{rand} \in C_{obs}$, discard and return to Step 2.
4. If $q_{rand} \in C_{free}$ and **no existing guard** $g \in V$ satisfies $L(q_{rand}, g) \subset C_{free}$, then **promote** $q_{rand}$ to a **guard** and add it to $V$.
5. If $q_{rand}$ is visible from **exactly one** existing guard, discard it (it adds no connectivity benefit).
6. If $q_{rand}$ is visible from **two or more** guards, classify it as a **connector** and add edges from $q_{rand}$ to all visible guards.
7. Optionally, test termination criterion — e.g., $k$ consecutive failures to add a new guard. If met, freeze the roadmap.

**Phase B — Query (Path Retrieval):**
1. Given start $q_s$ and goal $q_g$, connect $q_s$ to all visible guards and $q_g$ to all visible guards.
2. Search the augmented graph using Dijkstra / A$^*$ for the shortest path.
3. **Discard connector nodes** after the query (a *dynamic* variant of Vis-PRM) to keep the persistent graph lean.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Algorithm

- **Why guards first?** They capture the topology of $C_{free}$ by delineating its distinct connected components of visibility. A new guard $g$ exists precisely when an unexplored "void" in coverage is detected.
- **Why connectors?** Connectors are rare (probability decreases as $|V|$ grows), so they appear only where genuine bottlenecks require inter-region passage — exactly where traditional PRMs under-sample.
- **Why discard connectors after query?** The original Vis-PRM paper (Siméon, Laumond, Nissoux, 2000) demonstrated that persistent connectors *inflate* the graph with redundant topology, reducing query speed without improving solution quality.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Mathematical Form** | **Description** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visibility region of node $q_i$ | $V(q_i) = \{\,q \in C_{free} \mid L(q_i,q) \subset C_{free}\,\}$ | Set of free configs visible from $q_i$ | Subset of $C_{free}$ |
| Local straight-line planner | $L(q_1,q_2) = \{\, (1-t)\,q_1 + t\,q_2 \mid t \in [0,1] \,\}$ | Linear interpolation path | $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| Guard promotion condition | $\forall g \in V,\; L(q_{rand}, g) \not\subset C_{free}$ | No existing guard is visible | Boolean |
| Connector promotion condition | $\vert \{ g \in V \mid L(q_{rand},g) \subset C_{free} \} \vert \geq 2$ | At least 2 guards visible | Cardinality test |
| Roadmap cardinality bound | $\vert V \vert \leq N_g + N_c$ | Total nodes after learning | Integer |
| Probabilistic completeness | $\lim_{n\to\infty} P(\text{solution found}) = 1$ | As $n$ samples grow | Probability |
| Failure-driven termination | $f_{max}$ consecutive samples in existing visibility regions | Stopping rule | Integer iterations |
| Local planner radius | $d_{max}$ | Max edge length of roadmap | Distance units (m) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Visibility-based sampling is deployed in **autonomous vehicle motion planning** (narrow-lane merging), **surgical robotics** (instrument path planning in confined anatomy), and **warehouse AGV routing** in cluttered layouts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Probability of a Sampled Point Being a New Guard

Let $V_g = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N_g} V(g_i)$ denote the union of visibility regions of all current guards. The probability that a new uniform random sample $q_{rand} \in C_{free}$ becomes a **new guard** is:

$$P_{\text{new guard}} = P\!\left(q_{rand} \in C_{free} \setminus V_g \right)$$

Applying the uniform sampling assumption over a bounded $C_{free}$ of volume $\mu(C_{free})$:

$$P_{\text{new guard}} = \frac{\mu(C_{free} \setminus V_g)}{\mu(C_{free})}$$

$$P_{\text{new guard}} = 1 - \frac{\mu(V_g)}{\mu(C_{free})}$$

> **Logical conversion step:** As $N_g \to \infty$, the visibility regions increasingly cover $C_{free}$, hence $\mu(V_g) \to \mu(C_{free})$, driving $P_{\text{new guard}} \to 0$. This mathematically justifies the **failure-driven termination criterion** used in Vis-PRM.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Number of Connectors

The probability that $q_{rand}$ is visible from exactly $k$ guards is given by a multinomial-style decomposition. For $k=2$ (the connector-promoting case):

$$P_{\text{connector}} = \sum_{i < j} P\!\left(L(q_{rand}, g_i) \subset C_{free}\right) \cdot P\!\left(L(q_{rand}, g_j) \subset C_{free}\right)$$

Under the independence approximation of visibility events (locally valid in open regions):

$$P_{\text{connector}} \approx \binom{N_g}{2} \, p_{vis}^2$$

where $p_{vis}$ is the marginal probability that $q_{rand}$ is visible from any specific guard. Therefore:

$$E[N_c] \approx n_{samples} \cdot \binom{N_g}{2} \, p_{vis}^2$$

> **Logical conversion step:** Because $E[N_c]$ grows quadratically in $N_g$, the connector density can become unwieldy — which is exactly why the **dynamic Vis-PRM** discards connectors post-query to maintain sparsity.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Visibility Region Volume Decay

Consider a guard $g_i$ with visibility region $V(g_i)$ of characteristic linear extent $\rho_i$. The volume of the visibility region scales as:

$$\mu(V(g_i)) = \alpha_d \, \rho_i^d$$

where $d = \dim(C)$ and $\alpha_d$ is the volume constant of the unit ball in $\mathbb{R}^d$. For narrow passages of half-width $w$:

$$\rho_i \propto w \implies \mu(V(g_i)) \propto w^d$$

Thus, the ratio of visibility volume in a narrow passage to that in open space is:

$$\frac{\mu(V_{\text{narrow}})}{\mu(V_{\text{open}})} = \left(\frac{w}{w_{open}}\right)^{d}$$

> **Logical conversion step:** In high-dimensional configuration spaces (e.g., $d = 6$ for a 6-DOF arm), narrow-passage visibility regions shrink **exponentially** in $d$, explaining the *curse of dimensionality* and the need for informed sampling strategies (Gaussian, Bridge, Obstacle-based).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Feature** | **Uniform PRM** | **Visibility-Based PRM** | **Gaussian PRM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roadmap size | Large (thousands) | Small (hundreds) | Medium |
| Narrow-passage handling | Poor | Excellent | Good |
| Topology preservation | Approximate | Exact (guards) | Approximate |
| Query time | Slow | Fast | Medium |
| Node types | Single type | Guard + Connector | Single type |
| Probabilistic completeness | Yes | Yes | Yes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Vis-PRM Learning Phase]) --> Init[V ← empty, E ← empty]
    Init --> Sample[Sample q_rand from C uniformly]
    Sample --> ColCheck{q_rand in C_free?}
    ColCheck -- No --> Sample
    ColCheck -- Yes --> GuardTest[Check visibility to all existing guards]
    GuardTest -- "No guard visible" --> PromoteGuard[Promote q_rand to GUARD node]
    PromoteGuard --> AddGuard[Add g to V]
    AddGuard --> FailCheck
    GuardTest -- "Exactly 1 guard visible" --> Discard1[Discard q_rand]
    Discard1 --> FailCheck
    GuardTest -- "≥ 2 guards visible" --> PromoteConn[Promote q_rand to CONNECTOR node]
    PromoteConn --> AddConn[Add c to V and edges to visible guards]
    AddConn --> FailCheck
    FailCheck{"f_max consecutive failures?"} -- No --> Sample
    FailCheck -- Yes --> Freeze[Freeze roadmap R = V,E]
    Freeze --> Query[Query Phase: Connect q_s and q_g to guards]
    Query --> Search[A-star or Dijkstra search]
    Search --> Output([Output shortest collision-free path])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[Configuration Space C]
        A2[Start q_s]
        A3[Goal q_g]
        A4[Obstacle Map]
    end
    subgraph SAMPLER[Sampling Engine]
        B1[Uniform Random Sampler]
        B2[Collision Checker Module]
    end
    subgraph CLASSIFIER[Visibility Classifier]
        C1[Visibility Region Mapper]
        C2[Guard vs Connector Arbiter]
    end
    subgraph GRAPH[Roadmap Graph R]
        D1[(Guard Set V_g)]
        D2[(Connector Set V_c)]
        D3[(Edge Set E)]
    end
    subgraph QUERY[Query Processor]
        E1[Start/Goal Attachment Module]
        E2[Graph Search Engine A-star]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> E1
    A3 --> E1
    A4 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> PathOut([Collision-Free Path])
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Visual: Guard & Connector Placement in $C_{free}$

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cfree[Free Configuration Space C_free]
        G1((Guard 1))
        G2((Guard 2))
        G3((Guard 3))
        C1((Connector A))
        C2((Connector B))
        O[(Obstacle Region)]
    end
    G1 --- C1
    G2 --- C1
    G1 --- C2
    G3 --- C2
    G1 -.-|Visibility Edge| G2
    G2 -.-|Visibility Edge| G3
    O -.Blocked.-> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed lines between guards represent **topological adjacency** (they lie in adjacent visibility regions but are not directly connected because no single connector bridges them in this snapshot). Solid lines from guards to connectors are the **active edges** of the roadmap.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define a "guard node" in the context of visibility-based sampling. How is it distinguished from a "connector node"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **guard node** is a configuration $q \in C_{free}$ that is added to the visibility roadmap because it is **not visible** from any existing guard in the roadmap. It "guards" a previously uncovered region of $C_{free}$. In contrast, a **connector node** is a configuration that is visible from **two or more** existing guards, thereby establishing connectivity between distinct visibility regions. (3 marks — 1.5 for guard definition, 1.5 for distinction)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the probabilistic completeness property of the Visibility-Based PRM. What is the role of the failure-driven termination criterion?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Probabilistic Completeness:** As the number of samples $n \to \infty$, the probability that Vis-PRM finds a collision-free path (if one exists) approaches 1, i.e., $\lim_{n\to\infty} P(\text{solution found}) = 1$. (1.5 marks)
**Role of termination criterion:** Since the visibility regions of accumulated guards eventually cover $C_{free}$, the probability of finding a *new* guard decays to zero. The algorithm halts after $f_{max}$ consecutive non-promotion samples, ensuring computational efficiency without sacrificing completeness guarantees. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Convention (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q4(b)]*

**(a)** Explain the complete algorithm for constructing a visibility-based roadmap. Describe the learning phase and the query phase in detail, with appropriate mathematical notation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the probability that a randomly sampled free-space configuration becomes a new guard, and show why the failure-driven termination criterion is mathematically justified. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

*Learning Phase:*
- Initialize the roadmap $R = (V, E)$ with $V = \emptyset$ and $E = \emptyset$. (0.5 marks)
- Iterate: sample $q_{rand} \sim \text{Uniform}(C)$. (0.5 marks)
- If $q_{rand} \in C_{obs}$, reject and resample. (0.5 marks)
- For each existing guard $g \in V$, test the local path $L(q_{rand}, g)$ for collision. (0.5 marks)
- Classification rules:
  - If **no guard is visible** from $q_{rand}$, promote it to a **guard** and add $q_{rand}$ to $V$. (1.0 marks)
  - If **one guard is visible**, discard $q_{rand}$. (0.5 marks)
  - If **$\geq 2$ guards are visible**, promote $q_{rand}$ to a **connector**; add edges from $q_{rand}$ to each visible guard and update $E$. (1.0 marks)
- Termination: stop after $f_{max}$ consecutive samples that fail to add a new guard. (0.5 marks)

*Query Phase:*
- Given $q_s$ and $q_g$, find all guards visible from each and add temporary connection nodes. (1.0 marks)
- Run a graph search (Dijkstra / A$^*$) on the augmented roadmap. (0.5 marks)
- Extract the path; **discard** temporary connector nodes. (0.5 marks)

**(b) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let $V_g = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N_g} V(g_i)$ denote the union of all guard visibility regions. The probability that a uniform sample $q_{rand} \in C_{free}$ becomes a **new guard** is:

$$P_{\text{new guard}} = P\!\left(q_{rand} \in C_{free} \setminus V_g\right)$$

Under uniform sampling on a bounded $C_{free}$ with measure $\mu(C_{free})$:

$$P_{\text{new guard}} = \frac{\mu(C_{free} \setminus V_g)}{\mu(C_{free})} = 1 - \frac{\mu(V_g)}{\mu(C_{free})}$$

(2 marks for setup)

Now, as $N_g$ increases, the visibility regions $V(g_i)$ progressively tile $C_{free}$. In the limit:

$$\lim_{N_g \to \infty} \mu(V_g) = \mu(C_{free}) \implies \lim_{N_g \to \infty} P_{\text{new guard}} = 0$$

(2 marks for limit argument)

This monotonic decay to zero justifies the **failure-driven termination**: once $f_{max}$ consecutive samples fail to add a new guard, the residual probability of finding new coverage is below the threshold $\varepsilon$, and continuing sampling is computationally wasteful without affecting completeness. (3 marks for termination justification)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q4(b) Alt]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast Uniform PRM, Visibility-Based PRM, and Gaussian PRM with respect to narrow-passage handling, roadmap size, and query efficiency. Use a comparative table in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF planar robot operates in a workspace of area $A = 10\text{ m}^2$ where 30% is occupied by obstacles. The average visibility region of a guard covers $0.05\text{ m}^2$. Estimate the number of guards required to cover 90% of the free configuration space. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Uniform PRM | Visibility-Based PRM | Gaussian PRM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling distribution | Uniform over $C$ | Uniform over $C$ | Gaussian (perturbed) |
| Narrow-passage performance | Poor (low probability) | Excellent (connectors naturally appear) | Good (targets narrow regions) |
| Typical roadmap size | 1000–10000 nodes | 50–500 nodes | 200–2000 nodes |
| Node classification | None | Guard / Connector | None |
| Query speed | Slow (dense graph) | Fast (sparse, lean graph) | Medium |
| Topology preservation | Approximate | Strong (one guard per region) | Approximate |
| Probabilistic completeness | Yes | Yes | Yes |

(7 marks — 1 mark per meaningful row, partial credit for partial tables)

**(b) — Numerical Estimation (7 marks):**

Given:
- Total workspace area: $A = 10\text{ m}^2$
- Obstacle fraction: 30% $\Rightarrow$ $\mu(C_{free}) = 0.7 \times 10 = 7\text{ m}^2$
- Average visibility region: $\mu(V(g_i)) = 0.05\text{ m}^2$
- Target coverage: 90% of $C_{free}$ $\Rightarrow$ required covered area $= 0.9 \times 7 = 6.3\text{ m}^2$

Assuming visibility regions are approximately **non-overlapping** (worst-case lower bound on guard count):

$$N_g \geq \frac{6.3}{0.05} = 126 \text{ guards}$$

(3 marks for setup and calculation)

Accounting for overlap via the union bound, the effective number is slightly higher in practice. A correction factor $\eta \approx 1.15$ (typical empirical overlap ratio) gives:

$$N_g^{\text{realistic}} \approx 126 \times 1.15 \approx 145 \text{ guards}$$

(2 marks for overlap correction)

**Final Answer:** Approximately **126 to 145 guards** are required to cover 90% of $C_{free}$. (2 marks for final answer with proper units and reasoning)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing the role of connectors:** Students often label a single-visibility-region node as a "connector." Remember — a connector **must** be visible from $\geq 2$ guards. Single-visibility samples are *discarded*, not promoted.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the query-phase attachment of $q_s$ and $q_g$:** The roadmap is useless unless start and goal are connected to the guard set. Many students lose 1–2 marks for omitting this.
> 3. **Treating $P_{new guard}$ as constant:** The probability *decays* with $N_g$. Writing it as a constant is a conceptual error worth a full mark deduction.
> 4. **Mismeasuring $\mu(C_{free})$:** Always subtract the obstacle volume. Many students incorrectly use the full $C$ volume in narrow-passage calculations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Visibility-Based Sampling** builds a sparse roadmap by classifying nodes as **guards** (cover new regions) or **connectors** (bridge existing regions).
- **Mutual visibility** requires the local path $L(q_1, q_2)$ to be fully contained in $C_{free}$.
- The **learning phase** continues until $f_{max}$ consecutive samples fail to add a new guard.
- The **query phase** dynamically attaches $q_s$ and $q_g$ to the guard set and runs a graph search.
- **Connectors are discarded** after query in dynamic Vis-PRM to keep the persistent roadmap lean.
- The **probability of a new guard decays** as $N_g$ grows: $P_{\text{new guard}} = 1 - \mu(V_g)/\mu(C_{free})$.
- **Narrow passages** are *automatically* bridged by connectors, unlike uniform PRM.
- The algorithm is **probabilistically complete** but not resolution-complete.
- **High-dimensional narrow passages** suffer from exponential volume shrinkage, requiring informed variants (Gaussian, Bridge, Obstacle-based sampling).
- KTU exam tip: Always pair a formula with its **engineering interpretation** to fetch full marks on definition questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manipulability-Based Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manipulability-Based Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Manipulability-Based Sampling** is an advanced sampling strategy used in sampling-based motion planning (such as PRM and RRT) that biases the configuration-space sampling distribution using the **manipulability ellipsoid** derived from the robot's Jacobian matrix. Instead of drawing configuration samples uniformly, the planner preferentially selects configurations that maximize a quantitative measure of the robot's ability to move in arbitrary task-space directions while avoiding kinematic singularities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manipulability Ellipsoid (Yoshikawa, 1985)**: Given a robot with forward kinematics $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ and Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$, the **velocity manipulability ellipsoid** in task space is the image of the unit sphere in joint velocity space $\{\dot{\mathbf{q}} \mid \Vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert \le 1\}$ under the linear map $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.

The **manipulability measure** $w(\mathbf{q})$ is defined as:

$$w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det\bigl(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{q})\bigr)}$$

For a square Jacobian, this simplifies to $w = \vert \det(\mathbf{J}) \vert$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024**: Manipulability is a LOCAL property — it is computed at each candidate configuration. Sampling-based planners use it as a *cost* or *probability weight* to bias the random tree toward dexterous regions of the configuration space.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a swimmer's hand in water. When the hand is held flat and pushed along its length, it slices through the water easily (high "manipulability" in that direction). But if pushed edge-on, the water resists strongly (low manipulability). The hand has a **shape of dexterity** in 3D — some directions are easy to move, others are hard.

A robot's end-effector behaves the same way. Around a given joint configuration, the end-effector can move in some directions much more easily than others. This "shape" is an **ellipsoid** (a stretched sphere), and its size, shape, and orientation tell us:

- **Large ellipsoid** = the robot is dexterous, can move the end-effector fast in all directions.
- **Flat / collapsed ellipsoid** = the robot is near a **singularity** — it has lost the ability to move in one or more directions.

**Manipulability-based sampling** is the planner's way of saying: *"Don't waste samples in configurations where the robot is crippled. Sample more often where the robot is agile."*

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world connection**: Industrial welding robots on car assembly lines are programmed to avoid singular configurations using exactly this principle. Sampling-based planners do the same automatically.

### 1.3 Why Manipulability Matters in Motion Planning

| Symptom in Motion Planning | Cause in Manipulability |
|---|---|
| Tree gets "stuck" near a configuration | Jacobian rank deficient → $w \to 0$ |
| End-effector cannot track a desired path | Anisotropic ellipsoid — long axis ≠ short axis |
| Excessive joint speeds for small task motions | Condition number $\kappa(\mathbf{J}) \gg 1$ |
| Force/torque transmission fails | Force ellipsoid collapsed |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 From Forward Kinematics to the Velocity Ellipsoid

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics**: The end-effector pose $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is a function of joint angles $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

$$\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate (Instantaneous Kinematics)**: Taking the time derivative and applying the chain rule,

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

Here, $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **geometric Jacobian** that maps joint velocities to task-space velocities.

**Step 3 — The Joint Velocity Sphere**: We consider all unit-norm joint velocities, forming a sphere:

$$\mathcal{S}_{q} = \bigl\{\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \;\big|\; \Vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert \le 1\bigr\}$$

**Step 4 — Linear Image (the Ellipsoid)**: The image of $\mathcal{S}_{q}$ under the linear map $\mathbf{J}$ is the **velocity manipulability ellipsoid** $\mathcal{E}_{v}(\mathbf{q})$:

$$\mathcal{E}_{v}(\mathbf{q}) = \bigl\{\dot{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m} \;\big|\; \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} \;\text{for some}\; \Vert\dot{\mathbf{q}}\Vert \le 1 \bigr\}$$

**Step 5 — Explicit Equation of the Ellipsoid**: For a full-row-rank Jacobian ($m \le n$, redundant robot), one can show the ellipsoid boundary is given by:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}} \, \bigl(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\bigr)^{-1}\, \dot{\mathbf{x}} = 1$$

**Step 6 — Eigen-Decomposition**: Let $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{U}\,\mathbf{\Sigma}\,\mathbf{V}^{\mathsf{T}}$ be the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). Then:

- Principal axes of the ellipsoid are along the columns of $\mathbf{U}$.
- Semi-axis lengths equal the singular values $\sigma_{1}, \sigma_{2}, \dots, \sigma_{m}$.

### 2.2 Force Manipulability Ellipsoid

By duality (using the principle of virtual work $\boldsymbol{\tau}^{\mathsf{T}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{F}^{\mathsf{T}}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$), the **force ellipsoid** is the inverse image and is governed by $\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}$:

$$\mathbf{F}^{\mathsf{T}} \, \bigl(\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}\bigr)^{-1}\, \mathbf{F} = 1$$

- Directions in which the robot is **velocity-dexterous** are directions in which it is **force-deficient**, and vice versa.

### 2.3 Manipulability Measure (Scalar Index)

Yoshikawa defined the scalar manipulability as the **square root of the product of squared semi-axes** (proportional to the volume of the ellipsoid):

$$w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det\!\bigl(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{q})\bigr)} = \sigma_{1}\sigma_{2}\cdots\sigma_{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Properties:
> - $w(\mathbf{q}) > 0$ iff the robot is **not** at a singularity.
> - $w(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ iff $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) < m$ (kinematic singularity).
> - For square $\mathbf{J}$: $w = \vert \det(\mathbf{J}) \vert$.

### 2.4 Condition Number & Distance from Singularity

The **condition number** measures how close the ellipsoid is to a sphere (isotropy):

$$\kappa(\mathbf{J}) = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\min}}, \quad \kappa \ge 1$$

- $\kappa = 1$ → perfectly isotropic ellipsoid (sphere) → maximum dexterity.
- $\kappa \to \infty$ → degenerate ellipsoid → near-singular.

### 2.5 Sampling Strategies Using Manipulability

Three standard biased-sampling rules used in PRM/RRT variants:

| Strategy | Probability of Sampling Configuration $\mathbf{q}$ | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform** | $p(\mathbf{q}) = \text{const}$ | Baseline; ignores dexterity |
| **Manipulability-weighted** | $p(\mathbf{q}) \propto w(\mathbf{q})$ | Favours dexterous regions |
| **Condition-number-weighted** | $p(\mathbf{q}) \propto 1/\kappa(\mathbf{J})$ | Favours isotropic dexterity |
| **Goal-aware weighted** | $p(\mathbf{q}) \propto w(\mathbf{q}) \cdot p_{\text{goal}}(\mathbf{q})$ | Combines with goal bias |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Forward kinematics | $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ | $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{m},\; \mathbf{q}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| Differential map | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{J}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ |
| Velocity ellipsoid equation | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}} \le 1$ | Implicit quadratic form |
| Force ellipsoid equation | $\mathbf{F}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J})^{-1}\mathbf{F} \le 1$ | Dual form |
| Semi-axis lengths | $\sigma_{i} = \sqrt{\lambda_{i}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})}$ | Singular values of $\mathbf{J}$ |
| Principal axis directions | Columns of $\mathbf{U}$ from $\mathbf{J}=\mathbf{U\Sigma V}^{\mathsf{T}}$ | Orthonormal |
| Manipulability measure | $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})} = \prod_{i=1}^{m}\sigma_{i}$ | Scalar, $\ge 0$ |
| Square Jacobian case | $w = \vert\det(\mathbf{J})\vert$ | Simpler form |
| Condition number | $\kappa = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min} \ge 1$ | $\kappa=1$ ideal |
| Velocity isotropy index | $\mu = \sigma_{\min}/\sigma_{\max} \in (0,1]$ | Inverse of condition |
| Distance from singularity | $d_{s} = \sigma_{\min}$ | $0$ at singularity |
| Sampling weight (weighted) | $p(\mathbf{q}) = w(\mathbf{q})\,/\,\int_{\mathcal{C}} w(\mathbf{q}')\,d\mathbf{q}'$ | Normalized PDF |
| Minimum singular value | $\sigma_{\min} = 1/\Vert\mathbf{J}^{+}\Vert$ | Where $\mathbf{J}^{+}$ is pseudoinverse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility**: Manipulability analysis is essential in designing redundant manipulators, surgical robots (Da Vinci system), and humanoid balancing — all of which exploit redundancy to keep the Jacobian well-conditioned away from singularities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Manipulability Ellipsoid Equation

**Starting point**: We want the set of all task-space velocities $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ that can be produced by *some* unit-norm joint velocity $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, i.e., $\Vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert \le 1$.

**Step 1**: Apply the constraint.

$$\Vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert^{2} = \dot{\mathbf{q}}^{\mathsf{T}} \dot{\mathbf{q}} \le 1$$

**Step 2**: For a *given* task velocity $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$, the smallest $\Vert\dot{\mathbf{q}}\Vert$ that achieves it is the minimum-norm solution. Using the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1}$ (for full row rank, $m \le n$):

$$\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\min} = \mathbf{J}^{+}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}$$

**Why is this the minimum?** Any joint velocity satisfying $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ can be written as $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}} + (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{J})\mathbf{z}$ for an arbitrary $\mathbf{z}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}$. The cross-term vanishes because $(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{J})$ is the null-space projector and orthogonal to $\mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$. So $\Vert\dot{\mathbf{q}}\Vert^{2} = \Vert\mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}}\Vert^{2} + \Vert(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{J})\mathbf{z}\Vert^{2}$, which is minimized at $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 3**: The constraint $\Vert\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\min}\Vert \le 1$ becomes:

$$\bigl(\mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}}\bigr)^{\mathsf{T}}\bigl(\mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}}\bigr) \le 1$$

**Step 4**: Expand the product.

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}}\,\mathbf{J}^{+\mathsf{T}}\,\mathbf{J}^{+}\,\dot{\mathbf{x}} \le 1$$

**Step 5**: Substitute $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1}$.

$$\mathbf{J}^{+\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}^{+} = \bigl(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\bigr)^{-1}\,\mathbf{J}\,\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\,\bigl(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\bigr)^{-1} = \bigl(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\bigr)^{-1}$$

**Step 6**: Final ellipsoid equation.

$$\boxed{\;\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}}\,\bigl(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\bigr)^{-1}\,\dot{\mathbf{x}} \le 1\;}$$

This is a quadratic form — the set of all $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ satisfying it is exactly an ellipsoid centered at the origin, since the matrix $(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1}$ is symmetric positive definite (SPD).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Manipulability Measure

**Step 1**: The principal semi-axes of the ellipsoid are the singular values $\sigma_{i}$ of $\mathbf{J}$. (This follows from the SVD: $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\mathsf{T}}$, so the ellipsoid transform is $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{v}$, which scales the unit sphere along $\mathbf{V}$'s columns and rotates by $\mathbf{U}$.)

**Step 2**: The volume of an $m$-dimensional ellipsoid with semi-axes $\sigma_{1}, \dots, \sigma_{m}$ is proportional to the product of the semi-axes:

$$V_{\text{ellipsoid}} = \frac{\pi^{m/2}}{\Gamma(\frac{m}{2}+1)}\,\prod_{i=1}^{m}\sigma_{i}$$

**Step 3**: Define Yoshikawa's scalar manipulability as the product of singular values:

$$w(\mathbf{q}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m}\sigma_{i}$$

**Step 4**: Use the identity $\det(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{\mathsf{T}}) = \det(\mathbf{A})^{2} = \bigl(\prod \sigma_{i}\bigr)^{2}$ for a matrix with SVD $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\mathsf{T}}$:

$$\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}) = \det(\mathbf{U})\det(\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{\mathsf{T}})\det(\mathbf{V}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{V}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m}\sigma_{i}^{2}$$

**Step 5**: Take the square root to obtain the manipulability measure.

$$\boxed{\;w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{q}))} = \prod_{i=1}^{m}\sigma_{i}(\mathbf{q})\;}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

**Setup**: A 2-link planar arm with link lengths $l_{1}, l_{2}$ and joint angles $q_{1}, q_{2}$. End-effector position:

$$x = l_{1}\cos q_{1} + l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2})$$
$$y = l_{1}\sin q_{1} + l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Jacobian** by partial differentiation.

$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_{1}} = -l_{1}\sin q_{1} - l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})$$
$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_{2}} = -l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})$$
$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_{1}} = l_{1}\cos q_{1} + l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2})$$
$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_{2}} = l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2})$$

**Step 2 — Assemble the Jacobian matrix**.

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_{1}\sin q_{1} - l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) & -l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) \\[4pt] l_{1}\cos q_{1} + l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) & l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the determinant** (square Jacobian case).

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = \bigl[-l_{1}\sin q_{1} - l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})\bigr]\cdot l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) - \bigl[-l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})\bigr]\cdot\bigl[l_{1}\cos q_{1} + l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2})\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Expand and simplify**.

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = -l_{1}l_{2}\sin q_{1}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) - l_{2}^{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) + l_{1}l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})\cos q_{1} + l_{2}^{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})\cos(q_{1}+q_{2})$$

**Step 5 — The middle terms cancel**.

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = -l_{1}l_{2}\sin q_{1}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) + l_{1}l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})\cos q_{1}$$

**Step 6 — Use the identity $\sin(A+B) = \sin A\cos B + \cos A\sin B$**.

$$\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})\cos q_{1} - \sin q_{1}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) = \sin\bigl((q_{1}+q_{2}) - q_{1}\bigr) = \sin q_{2}$$

**Step 7 — Final manipulability**.

$$\boxed{\;w(q_{2}) = \vert\det(\mathbf{J})\vert = l_{1}\,l_{2}\,\vert\sin q_{2}\vert\;}$$

**Step 8 — Physical interpretation** (this is a favourite KTU question).

- $w = 0$ when $q_{2} = 0$ or $q_{2} = \pi$ — the arm is **stretched out** or **folded back** (singular configurations). In both cases the end-effector can only move along a circle; one degree of task-space freedom is lost.
- $w = w_{\max} = l_{1}l_{2}$ when $q_{2} = \pm\pi/2$ — the elbow is at right angle, and the manipulator is in its most dexterous posture.

### 3.4 Connection to Singular Value Decomposition

**Step 1**: SVD of the Jacobian.

$$\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\mathsf{T}}, \quad \mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{1} & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_{2} \end{bmatrix}\;\text{(2-DOF case)}$$

**Step 2**: Then $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}^{2}\mathbf{U}^{\mathsf{T}}$, with eigenvalues $\sigma_{1}^{2}, \sigma_{2}^{2}$.

**Step 3**: The manipulability is:

$$w = \sigma_{1}\sigma_{2} = \sqrt{\sigma_{1}^{2}\sigma_{2}^{2}} = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})}$$

confirming the formula in §2.3.

### 3.5 Manipulability-Weighted Sampling — Construction

**Step 1 — Rejection Sampling (Brute Force)**:
1. Sample $\mathbf{q}_{i} \sim \text{Uniform}(\mathcal{C})$.
2. Compute $w(\mathbf{q}_{i})$.
3. Accept with probability $w(\mathbf{q}_{i}) / w_{\max}$.

**Step 2 — Direct Weighted Sampling**:
1. Discretize $\mathcal{C}$ into a grid $\{\mathbf{q}_{k}\}_{k=1}^{N}$.
2. Build weights $w_{k} = w(\mathbf{q}_{k})$.
3. Form a normalized CDF $F_{k} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{j} \,/\, \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_{j}$.
4. Draw $u \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$ and invert $F$ to get a sample.

> [!TIP]
> **Complexity note (for KTU)**: Computing the Jacobian and the $2\times 2$ (or $6\times 6$) minors for $w$ at a single configuration is $O(n^{2})$ in the DOF count. For high-DOF arms this is done incrementally along the tree expansion.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Manipulability-Aware Sampling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Random Configuration Sample q] --> B[Compute Forward Kinematics x = f q]
    B --> C[Compute Jacobian J q]
    C --> D[Form JJ transpose]
    D --> E[Compute Singular Values sigma1 sigma2]
    E --> F[Compute Manipulability w = sigma1 * sigma2]
    F --> G{Sampling Strategy}
    G --> H[Strategy A: Uniform]
    G --> I[Strategy B: Weight by w q]
    G --> J[Strategy C: Weight by 1 over kappa J]
    H --> K[Accept or Reject Configuration]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Add to Roadmap or Tree]
    L --> M[Connect to Neighbours with Local Planner]
    M --> N[End: Roadmap Built]
```

### 4.2 Concept Map: Manipulability Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Configuration q] --> B[Geometric Jacobian J q]
    B --> C[Velocity Ellipsoid E v]
    B --> D[Force Ellipsoid E f]
    B --> E[Singular Values sigma i]
    E --> F[Manipulability Measure w]
    E --> G[Condition Number kappa]
    F --> H[Sampling Probability p q]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Biased PRM or RRT]
    I --> J[Collision-free Path with High Dexterity]
    C --> K[Dexterity in Motion]
    D --> L[Dexterity in Force Application]
```

### 4.3 Ellipsoid Geometry Visualization (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SQ[Unit Sphere in Joint Velocity Space]
        SQ1[All q-dot with norm less than or equal to 1]
    end
    subgraph JMAP[Linear Map J q]
        MAP1[Multiply by Jacobian]
    end
    subgraph EV[Velocity Manipulability Ellipsoid in Task Space]
        EV1[Ellipsoid with semi axes sigma 1 and sigma 2]
        EV2[Orientation given by U columns]
    end
    SQ1 --> MAP1
    MAP1 --> EV1
    MAP1 --> EV2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: A unit sphere (perfectly symmetric) in joint space is "stretched" and "rotated" by the Jacobian into a stretched ellipsoid in task space. The stretching factors are the singular values $\sigma_{i}$. When one $\sigma_{i} \to 0$, the ellipsoid flattens to a line — the robot is at a singularity.

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Manipulability-Based vs. Uniform Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Planning Query: Start q_s, Goal q_g] --> CHECK{Dexterity Required?}
    CHECK -- No --> UNIFORM[Use Uniform PRM or RRT]
    CHECK -- Yes --> SEL[Choose Manipulability Strategy]
    SEL --> M1[Strategy 1: Reject samples with w less than w_min]
    SEL --> M2[Strategy 2: Weight by w q]
    SEL --> M3[Strategy 3: Weight by 1 over kappa]
    M1 --> BUILD[Build Roadmap or Tree]
    M2 --> BUILD
    M3 --> BUILD
    UNIFORM --> BUILD
    BUILD --> PATH[Query: Find Path in Graph]
    PATH --> DONE[Return Collision-free High-dexterity Path]
```
