## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Q1. Define the manipulability ellipsoid of a robotic manipulator and state its significance in motion planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The manipulability ellipsoid is the image of the unit sphere in joint-velocity space under the linear map defined by the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$, i.e., the set of all end-effector velocities $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ reachable with $\Vert\dot{\mathbf{q}}\Vert \le 1$. It is described by the equation

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}}\,\bigl(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\bigr)^{-1}\,\dot{\mathbf{x}} \le 1$$

Its **significance in motion planning** is that it quantifies, at each configuration, *how well* the robot can move the end-effector in arbitrary directions. Planners use it to (i) avoid sampling near singular configurations where the ellipsoid degenerates, and (ii) bias sampling toward dexterous regions, producing more reliable paths.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
**Q2. What is the manipulability measure $w(\mathbf{q})$ proposed by Yoshikawa? Mention two properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Yoshikawa's manipulability measure is defined as

$$w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det\bigl(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{q})\bigr)} = \prod_{i=1}^{m}\sigma_{i}(\mathbf{J})$$

**Two key properties:**
1. **Singularity indicator**: $w(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ if and only if the robot is at a kinematic singularity (rank-deficient Jacobian); $w > 0$ elsewhere.
2. **Volume interpretation**: $w$ is proportional to the volume of the velocity manipulability ellipsoid, hence larger $w$ means greater dexterity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the equation of the velocity manipulability ellipsoid starting from the differential kinematics $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ and the unit-norm joint velocity constraint $\Vert\dot{\mathbf{q}}\Vert \le 1$. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $l_{1}$ and $l_{2}$, derive an expression for the manipulability measure as a function of the joint angles. Identify the singular configurations and the configuration of maximum manipulability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumption*: Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ has full row rank, i.e., $m \le n$ (redundant or square manipulator), so $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}$ is invertible.

1. We seek the set of all $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ such that there exists $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ with $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{\mathsf{T}}\dot{\mathbf{q}} \le 1$.
2. The minimum-norm joint velocity that produces a given $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ is $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\min} = \mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$, where $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1}$ is the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse. This is minimum because adding any null-space component $(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{J})\mathbf{z}$ only increases the norm.
3. The constraint becomes $(\mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}})^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}}) \le 1$.
4. Expanding: $\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}^{+\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}} \le 1$.
5. Substituting $\mathbf{J}^{+}$: $\mathbf{J}^{+\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}^{+} = (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1}\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1} = (\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1}$.
6. Final equation:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}}\bigl(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\bigr)^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}} \le 1$$

This is the implicit equation of an ellipsoid (positive definite quadratic form).

**(b) 2-DOF Manipulability (7 marks):**

1. Forward kinematics: $x = l_{1}\cos q_{1} + l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2})$, $y = l_{1}\sin q_{1} + l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2})$.
2. Jacobian:

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_{1}\sin q_{1} - l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) & -l_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) \\ l_{1}\cos q_{1} + l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) & l_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) \end{bmatrix}$$

3. Determinant (after trigonometric simplification using $\sin(A+B) = \sin A\cos B + \cos A\sin B$): $\det(\mathbf{J}) = l_{1}l_{2}\sin q_{2}$.
4. Manipulability: $w(q_{2}) = \vert\det(\mathbf{J})\vert = l_{1}l_{2}\vert\sin q_{2}\vert$.
5. **Singular configurations**: $w = 0 \Rightarrow \sin q_{2} = 0 \Rightarrow q_{2} = 0$ (arm fully stretched) or $q_{2} = \pi$ (arm folded back on itself).
6. **Maximum manipulability**: $w = w_{\max} = l_{1}l_{2}$ when $\sin q_{2} = \pm 1 \Rightarrow q_{2} = \pm\pi/2$ (elbow at right angle).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **force manipulability ellipsoid** and show how it is the dual of the velocity ellipsoid. Mention the relevant equation and the physical interpretation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to sampling-based motion planning, explain three different strategies for incorporating manipulability into the sampling distribution. Give the corresponding probability density functions and discuss the trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Force Ellipsoid (7 marks):**

The force ellipsoid describes the set of end-effector forces $\mathbf{F}$ that can be generated by joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ bounded by $\Vert\boldsymbol{\tau}\Vert \le 1$, using the relation $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{F}$ (from the principle of virtual work).

Following the same derivation as the velocity ellipsoid (replacing $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ with $\mathbf{F}$ and $\mathbf{J}$ with $\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}$), the force ellipsoid is given by:

$$\mathbf{F}^{\mathsf{T}}\,\bigl(\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}\bigr)^{-1}\,\mathbf{F} \le 1$$

**Duality**: A configuration that is *velocity-dexterous* (large velocity ellipsoid) is *force-deficient* (small force ellipsoid), and vice versa. This is because the principal axes of the two ellipsoids are along the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}$ and $\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}$ respectively, and the squared semi-axes are reciprocals of each other (they are the same $\sigma_{i}^{2}$ for the velocity case and $1/\sigma_{i}^{2}$ for the force case).

**Physical interpretation**: In a direction in which the robot can move the end-effector rapidly, it can apply only small forces; in a direction in which motion is constrained, large forces can be applied.

**(b) Sampling Strategies (7 marks):**

**Strategy 1 — Uniform sampling (baseline):**

$$p_{\text{unif}}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{C})}$$

Ignores manipulability; useful when dexterity is not a primary concern. Trade-off: simple, no extra computation, but may produce samples in poor configurations.

**Strategy 2 — Manipulability-weighted sampling:**

$$p_{w}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{w(\mathbf{q})}{\int_{\mathcal{C}} w(\mathbf{q}')\,d\mathbf{q}'}$$

Favours dexterous regions. Trade-off: requires Jacobian evaluation at every sample ($O(n^{2})$ per sample); over-biasing may cause the planner to miss narrow passages.

**Strategy 3 — Inverse condition-number sampling:**

$$p_{\kappa}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1/\kappa(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}))}{\int_{\mathcal{C}} (1/\kappa(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}')))\,d\mathbf{q}'}$$

Favours configurations where the ellipsoid is closest to a sphere (isotropic dexterity). Trade-off: same complexity as Strategy 2, but produces more uniform task-space coverage.

**Additional remark**: A practical combination used in modern RRT variants is $p(\mathbf{q}) = (1-\alpha)\,p_{\text{unif}} + \alpha\,p_{w}$, with $\alpha \in [0,1]$ controlling the bias strength.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the transpose in the inverse term**: A very common mistake is writing the ellipsoid equation as $\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J})^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}} \le 1$. The correct form is $\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}} \le 1$ for the **velocity** ellipsoid.
> 2. **Confusing the velocity and force ellipsoids**: Mixing up the two is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. Memorize: *velocity uses $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}$; force uses $\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}$*.
> 3. **Skipping the singular-value interpretation**: Examiners specifically look for statements like "*the principal axes of the ellipsoid are along the columns of $\mathbf{U}$ and have lengths $\sigma_{i}$*". Omitting this costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the minimum-norm justification**: When deriving the ellipsoid equation, you must justify why $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\min} = \mathbf{J}^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ is the smallest solution (null-space argument). Simply stating the pseudoinverse formula loses a mark.
> 5. **Confusing manipulability with reachability**: Manipulability is a *local*, *velocity* concept. It is NOT the same as the workspace (global reachability).
> 6. **In the 2-DOF derivation, skipping the trigonometric identity step**: The cancellation of the $l_{2}^{2}$ terms must be shown explicitly. Jumping to $\det(\mathbf{J}) = l_{1}l_{2}\sin q_{2}$ without the intermediate algebra is penalized.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Manipulability-Based Sampling**

- ✅ **Manipulability ellipsoid** = image of unit joint-velocity sphere under $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$; described by the quadratic form $\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{\mathsf{T}}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}} \le 1$.
- ✅ **Velocity vs Force**: velocity uses $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}$; force uses $\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{J}$. They are *duals* (reciprocal semi-axes).
- ✅ **Semi-axis lengths** of the velocity ellipsoid = **singular values** $\sigma_{i}$ of $\mathbf{J}$; directions = columns of $\mathbf{U}$ from SVD.
- ✅ **Yoshikawa's manipulability measure**: $w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\mathsf{T}})} = \prod \sigma_{i}$ — proportional to ellipsoid volume.
- ✅ **Square Jacobian shortcut**: $w = \vert\det(\mathbf{J})\vert$ (e.g., $w = l_{1}l_{2}\vert\sin q_{2}\vert$ for 2-DOF planar arm).
- ✅ **Singularity detection**: $w(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ iff $\mathbf{J}$ is rank-deficient; physically means a loss of task-space DOF.
- ✅ **Condition number** $\kappa = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ — measures isotropy of dexterity; $\kappa=1$ is ideal.
- ✅ **Sampling strategies**:
  - *Uniform*: $p = $ const — baseline.
  - *Manipulability-weighted*: $p \propto w$ — favours dexterity.
  - *Condition-weighted*: $p \propto 1/\kappa$ — favours isotropy.
  - *Combined*: $p = (1-\alpha)p_{\text{unif}} + \alpha p_{w}$.
- ✅ **Computational cost**: $O(n^{2})$ per sample for Jacobian; full SVD is $O(n^{3})$; incremental methods exist for streaming use in RRT.
- ✅ **Algorithm integration**: Manipulability-based sampling is plugged into PRM/RRT by replacing the uniform random configuration generator with the weighted sampler at the *sample* step.
- ✅ **Key trade-off**: heavy bias toward high-manipulability regions can starve the planner of samples needed to escape narrow passages of $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ — a balance $\alpha \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ is common.
- ✅ **Force vs Velocity duality intuition**: "Fast but weak" directions correspond to long velocity-ellipsoid axes; "slow but strong" directions correspond to long force-ellipsoid axes.
- ✅ **History hook for viva**: Yoshikawa, 1985 — original manipulability paper; this is the standard citation KTU examiners expect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single-Query Sampling-Based Planners - Expansive-Spaces Trees (EST)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single-Query Sampling-Based Planners - Expansive-Spaces Trees (EST)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Expansive-Spaces Trees (EST)** is a **single-query**, **sampling-based motion planning algorithm** introduced by Hsu et al. (1997–1998). It is designed to overcome the inefficiency of the Probabilistic Roadmap Method (PRM) in **narrow-passage** and **high-dimensional** configuration spaces. EST grows **two trees** simultaneously — one rooted at the **start configuration** $q_{start}$ and one rooted at the **goal configuration** $q_{goal}$ — and uses an **explicit weight function** to balance exploration between well-explored and poorly-explored regions, thereby achieving the property of *expansiveness* in the free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

In formal KTU 2024 terminology:

$$\text{EST} = \Big( T_s, T_g, \mathcal{C}_{free}, w(v), \text{Select}, \text{Extend}, \text{Connect} \Big)$$

where:
- $T_s$ : Tree rooted at $q_{start}$
- $T_g$ : Tree rooted at $q_{goal}$
- $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ : Free configuration space
- $w(v)$ : Weight function associated with each node $v$
- **Select** : Probabilistic selection operator
- **Extend** : Local planner that generates new valid configurations
- **Connect** : Tree-merging operation

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Two Firefighters in a Burning Building" Analogy**

Imagine a massive, smoke-filled warehouse (the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$). Two firefighters enter from **opposite doors** — one from the **start** door, one from the **goal** door. Each firefighter carries a long rope (the tree). Instead of randomly wandering, each firefighter follows a simple rule:

> *"Always move into the region where **fewer** footprints have been left."*

This rule is the **weight function** $w(v)$. The footprint-count represents how many descendants / neighbors a tree node already has. A node in a *sparsely-explored* region gets a **high priority** (low weight = high priority), forcing exploration into **unexplored valleys and corridors**. When the two ropes finally touch, the escape route is reconstructed by retracing the ropes back to each door.

This is exactly how EST works — two trees growing toward each other, with **intelligent bias toward unexplored regions** to escape narrow passages.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU 2024 Definitions to Memorize**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Expansiveness** | Property of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ that allows efficient sampling-based exploration |
| **Reachability** | Likelihood that a random sample lies in the same connected component as the tree |
| **Weight Function** | $w(v) \propto 1 / n(v)$ where $n(v)$ = number of neighbors of $v$ |
| **Single-Query** | Algorithm solves **one** specific start-goal query (no preprocessing) |
| **Tree Merging** | The final step where $T_s$ and $T_g$ attempt to connect |
| **Narrow Passage** | Region of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ with low volume, difficult to sample |

---

### 1.4 Historical Context

EST was developed at **Stanford University** as a response to two major PRM weaknesses:
1. PRM's uniform sampling wastes samples in large open spaces.
2. PRM's roadmap may be disconnected across narrow passages.

EST's innovation is the **explicit exploration bias** through the weight function $w(v)$, which dynamically re-allocates sampling effort to under-explored regions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition — Step-by-Step Logic

The EST algorithm executes the following **operational pipeline**:

#### **Phase 1 — Initialization**
- Initialize tree $T_s \leftarrow \{ q_{start} \}$
- Initialize tree $T_g \leftarrow \{ q_{goal} \}$
- Set iteration counter $i \leftarrow 0$

#### **Phase 2 — Node Selection (Weighted Probabilistic Sampling)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **heart** of EST. Unlike RRT which uses nearest-neighbor (greedy), EST uses **weighted random selection**.

Given tree $T$ with nodes $V$, choose node $v \in V$ with probability:

$$P(v) = \frac{w(v)}{\sum_{u \in V} w(u)}$$

#### **Phase 3 — Neighborhood Construction**
- Compute $\mathcal{N}(v)$ = the set of nodes in $T$ within distance $r$ of $v$ (or the **k-nearest neighbors**)
- $n(v) = \vert \mathcal{N}(v) \vert$

#### **Phase 4 — Local Extension**
- Sample a random direction or configuration $x_{rand}$
- Generate a new configuration $x_{new}$ in the vicinity of $v$
- Check collision-freeness: $x_{new} \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$
- If valid, **add** $x_{new}$ as child of $v$ in $T$

#### **Phase 5 — Symmetric Growth**
- Repeat Phases 2–4 for tree $T_g$

#### **Phase 6 — Tree Connection**
- After every $\Delta$ iterations, attempt to connect $T_s$ and $T_g$ by running Extend() between the **closest pair** of nodes
- If connection succeeds → **Path Found** → terminate
- If maximum iterations $N_{max}$ reached → return **Failure**

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 Examination**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w(v) = \dfrac{1}{n(v)}$ | Inverse-degree weight (most common form) | $n(v) \geq 1$ |
| 2 | $w(v) = \dfrac{1}{\log(n(v)) + C}$ | Logarithmic weight (Hsu variant) | $C > 0$ |
| 3 | $P(v) = \dfrac{w(v)}{\sum_{u \in V} w(u)}$ | Selection probability | $0 \leq P(v) \leq 1$ |
| 4 | $n(v) = \vert \mathcal{N}_r(v) \vert$ | Count of neighbors within radius $r$ | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| 5 | $d(v_1, v_2) = \Vert v_1 - v_2 \Vert_2$ | Distance metric (typically Euclidean) | Length |
| 6 | $\alpha(T_s, T_g) = \min_{v \in T_s, u \in T_g} d(v, u)$ | Closest distance between trees | Length |
| 7 | $E[V] \leq O(\log N)$ | Expected work per node (for expansive $\mathcal{C}_{free}$) | Computational complexity |
| 8 | $P(\text{success at iter } i) \geq 1 - e^{-\lambda i}$ | Convergence (exponential) | Probability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $n(v) = 0$ is **impossible** (each tree node has at least itself in its neighborhood)
> - $w(v) \to 0$ as tree density increases (saturation)
> - Algorithm assumes $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ satisfies **$(k, \gamma)$-expansiveness**

---

### 2.3 The $(k, \gamma)$-Expansiveness Condition (Advanced)

A configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is said to be **$(k, \gamma)$-expansive** if there exists constants $k > 0$ and $\gamma \in (0, 1]$ such that any subset $S \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{free}$ with $\mu(S) \geq k$ has a "neighborhood" of volume at least $\gamma \cdot \mu(S)$ in $\mathcal{C}_{free} \setminus S$, where $\mu$ denotes the measure (volume).

Formally, the **$\beta$-lookout** of $S$ is:

$$\beta(S) = \Big\{ q \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \setminus S \;\Big|\; \mu(\mathcal{N}(q) \cap S) \geq \beta \cdot \mu(\mathcal{N}(q) \cap \mathcal{C}_{free}) \Big\}$$

> This is the **theoretical guarantee** that makes EST provably efficient under KTU-style proofs.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of EST |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane-change path planning in narrow urban corridors |
| **Surgical Robotics** | da Vinci robot navigating around organs |
| **Warehouse Robotics** | Amazon/Kiva robots in narrow aisle reconfiguration |
| **Drone Swarms** | Forest-canopy navigation with narrow gaps |
| **Space Robotics** | On-orbit servicing in cluttered satellite environments |
| **Industrial Manipulators** | Welding paths around obstacles on shop floors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weight Function Selection Probability

We wish to derive the formal expression for the probability $P(v)$ of selecting node $v$ in tree $T$.

**Step 1: Define the weight of each node**

The weight of node $v \in T$ is given by:

$$w(v) = \frac{1}{n(v)}$$

where $n(v) = \vert \mathcal{N}(v) \vert$ is the cardinality of the neighborhood of $v$.

**Step 2: Construct the probability distribution**

For a valid probability distribution, we require $\sum_{u \in V} P(u) = 1$. Since the selection must be **proportional to $w(v)$**, we set:

$$P(v) = c \cdot w(v)$$

for some normalization constant $c$.

**Step 3: Solve for $c$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{u \in V} P(u) &= 1 \\
\sum_{u \in V} c \cdot w(u) &= 1 \\
c \cdot \sum_{u \in V} w(u) &= 1 \\
c &= \frac{1}{\sum_{u \in V} w(u)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Substitute back**

$$P(v) = \frac{w(v)}{\sum_{u \in V} w(u)}$$

Substituting $w(v) = 1 / n(v)$:

$$P(v) = \frac{\dfrac{1}{n(v)}}{\sum_{u \in V} \dfrac{1}{n(u)}}$$

**Step 5: Consequence — Inverse Density Bias**

This shows that nodes with **small** $n(v)$ (few neighbors = low local density) have **large** $P(v)$ and are thus preferentially selected. This is the **core exploration bias** of EST.

---

### 3.2 Convergence Analysis Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (Hsu et al. 1997):** *If $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is $(k, \gamma)$-expansive, then EST finds a path in expected time polynomial in the "expansiveness constants" $k$ and $1/\gamma$.*

**Sketch of the proof:**

Let $T_i$ denote tree $T$ at iteration $i$. Define the **expected number of new cells covered** per iteration.

**Step 1:** Define a "good" event $A_i$: the iteration $i$ produces a node in an **unexplored** region of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

**Step 2:** From $(k, \gamma)$-expansiveness, $P(A_i) \geq \gamma$ (constant lower bound).

**Step 3:** The number of new cells covered grows linearly with successful events:

$$E[\mu(T_{i+1} \setminus T_i)] \geq \gamma \cdot \Delta\mu$$

**Step 4:** Since total volume of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is finite, the number of iterations to cover it is bounded by:

$$E[N] \leq \frac{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})}{\gamma \cdot \Delta\mu} = O\Big(\frac{1}{\gamma}\Big)$$

**Step 5:** Combining, the **expected running time** is:

$$E[T_{EST}] = O\Big( \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \text{poly}(k) \Big)$$

This is the **key polynomial complexity result** that KTU examiners expect students to recognize.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Style Problem — Illustrative**

**Given:** A 2D configuration space with tree $T$ containing 5 nodes: $A, B, C, D, E$. Their neighbor counts are:

| Node | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $n(v)$ | 2 | 4 | 1 | 3 | 5 |

**Find:** The probability of selecting each node.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute weights.

$$\begin{aligned}
w(A) &= \frac{1}{2} = 0.5000 \\
w(B) &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.2500 \\
w(C) &= \frac{1}{1} = 1.0000 \\
w(D) &= \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333 \\
w(E) &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.2000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Sum of weights.

$$\sum w = 0.5000 + 0.2500 + 1.0000 + 0.3333 + 0.2000 = 2.2833$$

**Step 3:** Compute probabilities.

$$\begin{aligned}
P(A) &= \frac{0.5000}{2.2833} = 0.2190 \\
P(B) &= \frac{0.2500}{2.2833} = 0.1095 \\
P(C) &= \frac{1.0000}{2.2833} = 0.4380 \\
P(D) &= \frac{0.3333}{2.2833} = 0.1460 \\
P(E) &= \frac{0.2000}{2.2833} = 0.0876
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** $0.2190 + 0.1095 + 0.4380 + 0.1460 + 0.0876 = 1.0001 \approx 1$ ✓

> **Observation:** Node $C$ (the *least* crowded node with $n=1$) has the **highest** selection probability of **43.8%**, demonstrating the **inverse-density exploration bias** of EST.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | PRM | RRT | **EST** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Query type | Multi-query | Single-query | **Single-query** |
| Data structure | Graph | Tree | **Two trees** |
| Selection rule | Random | Nearest neighbor | **Weighted probabilistic** |
| Bias | Uniform | Voronoi-bias | **Inverse-density bias** |
| Narrow-passage handling | Poor | Moderate | **Good (theoretically)** |
| Completeness | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | **Probabilistic** |
| Theoretical guarantee | Weak | Weak | **$(k, \gamma)$-expansive** |
| Collision checks | All pairs | Per-extension | **Per-extension** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level EST Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize Ts and Tg] --> B[Iteration Counter Increment]
    B --> C[Compute weights w v for all v in Ts]
    C --> D[Select node vs from Ts by P v]
    D --> E[Sample xrand in neighborhood of vs]
    E --> F{Is xrand in Cfree?}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Add xrand as child of vs in Ts]
    G --> H[Symmetrically grow Tg from qgoal]
    H --> I{Distance alpha Ts Tg less than threshold}
    I -- No --> B
    I -- Yes --> J[Attempt Connection via Extend]
    J --> K{Connection successful?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Path Found - Reconstruct and Return]
    K -- No --> B
    B --> M{Iterations exceed Nmax}
    M -- Yes --> N[Return Failure]
```

### 4.2 Two-Tree Growth Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ts[Tree from Start]
        Qs[qstart] --> V1[v1]
        Qs --> V2[v2]
        V1 --> V3[v3]
        V2 --> V4[v4]
        V3 --> V5[v5]
    end
    subgraph Tg[Tree from Goal]
        Qg[qgoal] --> U1[u1]
        Qg --> U2[u2]
        U1 --> U3[u3]
        U2 --> U4[u4]
    end
    V5 -. Connection Attempt .-> U3
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of EST

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Phase1[Phase 1 Initialization Module]
        I1[Input qstart and qgoal]
        I2[Create Ts containing qstart]
        I3[Create Tg containing qgoal]
        I1 --> I2
        I1 --> I3
    end
    subgraph Phase2[Phase 2 Weighted Selection Module]
        S1[Compute n v for all v]
        S2[Compute w v equals 1 over n v]
        S3[Sample v with probability P v]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3
    end
    subgraph Phase3[Phase 3 Local Extension Module]
        E1[Generate xrand near v]
        E2[Collision Check in Cfree]
        E3[Add xrand to tree if valid]
        E1 --> E2 --> E3
    end
    subgraph Phase4[Phase 4 Tree Merging Module]
        M1[Compute closest pair between Ts and Tg]
        M2[Run local planner between pair]
        M3[Reconstruct final path]
        M1 --> M2 --> M3
    end
    Phase1 --> Phase2 --> Phase3 --> Phase4
```

### 4.4 Weight-Based Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Selection] --> B{Compute n v for candidate v}
    B --> C[Compute w v equals 1 over n v]
    C --> D[Compute cumulative sum of w u]
    D --> E[Draw uniform random number r in 0 1]
    E --> F{Compare r to cumulative thresholds}
    F --> G[Return selected node v]
```

### 4.5 EST vs RRT Exploration Behaviour (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RRT[RRT Behaviour]
        R1[Voronoi Bias] --> R2[Greedy nearest extension]
        R2 --> R3[Strong narrow-passage failure]
    end
    subgraph EST[EST Behaviour]
        E1[Inverse Density Bias] --> E2[Weighted selection of low density nodes]
        E2 --> E3[Active narrow-passage penetration]
    end
    RRT -- Comparison -- EST
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> Define **Expansive-Spaces Trees (EST)**. State the role of the **weight function** $w(v)$ in node selection.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **EST** is a single-query, sampling-based motion planning algorithm that grows two trees — one from the start configuration $q_{start}$ and one from the goal configuration $q_{goal}$ — and connects them to form a path.
>
> The **weight function** $w(v) = \dfrac{1}{n(v)}$ assigns a numerical priority to each node $v$ in the tree, where $n(v)$ is the number of neighboring nodes. Nodes with **fewer neighbors** (i.e., in **sparsely explored** regions) have **larger weights** and are selected with **higher probability** for the next expansion step. This produces an **exploration bias toward narrow passages and under-explored regions** of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> Differentiate between **RRT** and **EST** in terms of node selection strategy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> | Aspect | RRT | EST |
> |---|---|---|
> | Selection rule | **Nearest neighbor** (deterministic, greedy) | **Weighted probabilistic** (stochastic, exploratory) |
> | Bias | Voronoi-region bias | **Inverse-density bias** |
> | Narrow passage | Weak penetration | **Strong penetration** (theoretically proven) |
> | Optimality | Asymptotically optimal (RRT*) | Not asymptotically optimal |
> | Number of trees | One | **Two** (start + goal) |
>
> Hence RRT extends toward random samples greedily, while EST **explicitly prefers under-explored nodes** via $w(v)$. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Expansive-Spaces Trees (EST)** algorithm in detail. Explain the role of the **weight function** and the **neighborhood size** in achieving the property of expansiveness.
>
> **(b) [7 marks)** Derive the **probability expression** $P(v)$ for node selection in EST. Using a numerical example with **5 nodes** having neighbor counts $(2, 4, 1, 3, 5)$, compute the selection probabilities and identify which node is most likely to be chosen.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) EST Algorithm Description [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Initialization** — Two trees $T_s$ and $T_g$ are initialized with $q_{start}$ and $q_{goal}$ respectively.

**Step 2: Node Selection** — In each iteration, a node $v$ is selected from $T_s$ with probability $P(v) = \dfrac{w(v)}{\sum_{u} w(u)}$ where $w(v) = \dfrac{1}{n(v)}$.

**Step 3: Local Extension** — A random configuration $x_{rand}$ is sampled near $v$ using a local planner. If $x_{rand} \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$, it is added as a child of $v$.

**Step 4: Symmetric Growth** — The same procedure is applied to $T_g$.

**Step 5: Tree Connection** — Periodically, the closest pair of nodes between $T_s$ and $T_g$ is identified, and a connection attempt is made. If successful, the path is reconstructed.

**Step 6: Termination** — Algorithm terminates on success or after $N_{max}$ iterations.

**Role of Weight Function:** The weight function $w(v)$ implements an **inverse-density bias**, which directs the search toward **under-explored regions** of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. This is critical for penetrating **narrow passages** where uniform sampling fails.

**Role of Neighborhood Size:** The neighborhood size $r$ (or the $k$-nearest parameter) controls the **resolution** of the bias. A larger $r$ produces a coarser bias (broader exploration), while a smaller $r$ produces a finer bias (more localized exploration).

> This satisfies the $(k, \gamma)$-expansiveness condition and guarantees polynomial expected running time. **(7 marks — full marks distribution: 1 for initialization, 2 for node selection + extension, 1 for symmetric growth, 1 for connection, 2 for weight function + neighborhood discussion)**

---

**(b) Probability Derivation and Numerical Computation [7 marks]**

**Derivation:**

Given weights $w(v)$, the selection probability must satisfy $\sum P(v) = 1$. Setting $P(v) = c \cdot w(v)$:

$$c = \frac{1}{\sum_{u \in V} w(u)} \implies P(v) = \frac{w(v)}{\sum_{u \in V} w(u)}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given $n = (2, 4, 1, 3, 5)$ for nodes $A, B, C, D, E$:

$$w = (0.5, 0.25, 1.0, 0.3333, 0.2)$$

$$\sum w = 2.2833$$

$$P = (0.2190, 0.1095, 0.4380, 0.1460, 0.0876)$$

> **Most likely node: $C$** with $P(C) = 0.4380$ (43.8%) because it has the smallest neighborhood count $n(C) = 1$, hence the largest weight. **(7 marks — full marks: 2 for derivation, 3 for numerical computation, 2 for interpretation)**

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **$(k, \gamma)$-expansiveness** of a configuration space. Why is this property important for the theoretical convergence of EST?
>
> **(b) [7 marks)** Compare EST with PRM and RRT in a **tabular matrix** covering at least 6 parameters. State **two practical scenarios** where EST is preferred over RRT.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) $(k, \gamma)$-Expansiveness [7 marks]**

A free configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is called **$(k, \gamma)$-expansive** if there exist constants $k > 0$ and $\gamma \in (0, 1]$ such that for any subset $S \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{free}$ with measure $\mu(S) \geq k$, there exists a neighborhood $\beta(S) \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{free} \setminus S$ with measure at least $\gamma \cdot \mu(S)$ that is reachable from $S$ through $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

**Importance for EST Convergence:**

1. **Polynomial Complexity:** The $(k, \gamma)$ parameters directly bound the expected running time of EST as $E[T_{EST}] = O(\text{poly}(k, 1/\gamma))$.
2. **Narrow Passage Guarantee:** Expansiveness ensures that the algorithm does **not get stuck** in dead-ends or disconnected components.
3. **Sample Efficiency:** Each iteration has probability $\geq \gamma$ of reaching a new region, yielding exponential convergence: $P(\text{success at iter } i) \geq 1 - e^{-\gamma i}$.
4. **Theoretical Bridge:** It transforms a heuristic algorithm into a **provably polynomial-time** planner under mild assumptions. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Comparative Matrix and Practical Scenarios [7 marks]**

| # | Parameter | PRM | RRT | EST |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Query type | Multi-query | Single-query | Single-query |
| 2 | Data structure | Roadmap (graph) | Tree | **Two trees** |
| 3 | Node selection | Random | Nearest neighbor | **Weighted probabilistic** |
| 4 | Exploration bias | Uniform | Voronoi | **Inverse-density** |
| 5 | Narrow passage handling | Poor | Moderate | **Good** |
| 6 | Theoretical guarantee | Weak | Weak | **$(k, \gamma)$-expansive** |
| 7 | Optimality variant | PRM* | RRT* | **Lazy EST** |
| 8 | Connection step | Roadmap query | None | **Tree merge** |

**Two Practical Scenarios Where EST is Preferred:**

1. **Narrow-Passage Industrial Manipulation:** A 6-DOF robotic arm must thread through a tight aperture (e.g., a peg-in-hole assembly task). RRT often fails because Voronoi bias pulls the tree into wide open regions. EST's inverse-density bias pulls it **into** the aperture.

2. **Surgical Robot Navigation in Cluttered Anatomy:** A laparoscopic instrument must navigate through narrow anatomical corridors. EST's tree-merge approach allows bidirectional planning from incision to target with **explicit narrow-passage penetration**, reducing planning time by 30–50% in published benchmarks. **(7 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Loss of Marks in KTU 2024 EST Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RRT and EST selection rules** — Many students write "nearest neighbor" for EST. The correct rule is **weighted probabilistic selection** using $w(v) = 1/n(v)$. This is a **2-mark trap**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the second tree** — EST is **bidirectional** (two trees: $T_s$ and $T_g$). Forgetting to mention the goal tree loses **2–3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Not stating the $(k, \gamma)$-expansiveness assumption** — Theoretical questions explicitly require this. Omitting it loses **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Numerical computation errors in $P(v)$** — The sum $\sum w$ must be correctly normalized. Marks are awarded for **both** the derivation **and** the final verification ($\sum P = 1$).
>
> 5. **Mixing up EST with Visibility-PRM or other variants** — EST is a **tree-based**, single-query algorithm. Visibility-PRM is a **graph-based**, multi-query algorithm. Confusing them is a **fatal** error in 14-mark questions.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for EST (Module 3, PEBRT632)**

- **EST = Expansive-Spaces Trees**, a single-query sampling-based planner by Hsu et al. (1997).
- **Two trees**: $T_s$ (from $q_{start}$) and $T_g$ (from $q_{goal}$) grow simultaneously.
- **Weight function**: $w(v) = \dfrac{1}{n(v)}$, where $n(v) = \vert \mathcal{N}(v) \vert$ (number of neighbors).
- **Selection probability**: $P(v) = \dfrac{w(v)}{\sum_{u} w(u)}$ — implements inverse-density exploration bias.
- **Core innovation**: Explicit exploration bias toward **narrow passages** and **under-explored regions**.
- **$(k, \gamma)$-expansiveness** is the theoretical foundation guaranteeing polynomial expected running time.
- **Convergence**: $P(\text{success at iter } i) \geq 1 - e^{-\gamma i}$ (exponential).
- **Tree connection step**: Periodically merge $T_s$ and $T_g$ via local planner when their closest pair distance $\alpha < \epsilon$.
- **Difference from RRT**: RRT uses **nearest neighbor** (Voronoi bias), EST uses **weighted probabilistic** (inverse-density bias).
- **Difference from PRM**: PRM is **multi-query** with a roadmap; EST is **single-query** with two trees.
- **Neighborhood parameter**: Either a **radius** $r$ or a **k-nearest** count; controls bias resolution.
- **Practical applications**: Industrial manipulation in narrow apertures, surgical robotics, autonomous driving in cluttered environments, drone navigation in forests, space robotics.
- **Examiner favorites**: derivation of $P(v)$, numerical examples with 5 nodes, comparison with RRT, mention of $(k, \gamma)$-expansiveness.
- **Common pitfalls**: confusing selection rules, forgetting bidirectional growth, omitting theoretical assumptions, computational errors in normalization.
- **Variant to know**: **Lazy EST** postpones collision checks for further speedup.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rapidly-Exploring Random Trees (RRTs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rapidly-Exploring Random Trees (RRTs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree (RRT)** is an **incremental, sampling-based, randomized data structure** designed to efficiently search non-convex, high-dimensional configuration spaces (C-spaces) by incrementally building a space-filling tree rooted at an initial configuration.

Formally, an RRT is a directed graph $\mathcal{T} = (V, E)$ constructed iteratively in the configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ such that:
- $V \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{free}$ (all vertices lie in the obstacle-free region),
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ (edges are collision-free local paths),
- $q_{init} \in V$ (the start configuration is the root),
- The tree is grown by **random sampling** with uniform probability over $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

The algorithm is **probabilistically complete**: the probability that the planner finds a feasible path, if one exists, approaches **1** as the number of samples $n \to \infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Expected):**
> "An RRT is a randomized algorithm that incrementally builds a tree by recursively adding new states via random samples in the configuration space, providing probabilistic completeness for motion planning problems in high-dimensional spaces."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Firefighter in a Smoke-Filled Building"

Imagine a **firefighter** trapped in a large smoke-filled building, who can only see a few meters ahead. To escape, she has two strategies:

1. **Wave the flashlight randomly** in all directions and take one careful step toward whichever beam of light reveals a walkable patch of floor.
2. Each new step forms a **branch** from her current position. The branches together form a **tree of explored paths**.
3. She keeps doing this until she eventually stumbles into the **exit (goal)** or runs out of steps.

This is exactly how an RRT works:
- The **firefighter's position** = the current tree node (vertex).
- The **flashlight beam** = the random configuration sample $q_{rand}$.
- The **careful step** = the *steer* operation of length $\epsilon$ toward the sample.
- The **tree of paths** = the RRT structure $\mathcal{T}$.
- The **exit** = the goal configuration $q_{goal}$.

The brilliance of this analogy is that the firefighter (RRT) does **not** need to know the building's full blueprint (full map) — she just probes locally, making RRT ideal for **unknown or partially known environments**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RRTs were revolutionary (1998, LaValle):** Classical roadmap and cell-decomposition planners suffer an **exponential curse of dimensionality**. RRTs side-step this by trading completeness for **probabilistic completeness**, making them practical for robots with 6+ degrees of freedom.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $\epsilon$ | **Step size (steer length)** | 0.1 – 1.0 (in C-space units) |
| $n$ | Number of samples / iterations | $10^3$ – $10^6$ |
| $k$ | Goal bias probability | 0.05 – 0.20 (**dimensionless**) |
| $d$ | Dimensionality of $\mathcal{C}$ | 2 – 7+ (DOF) |
| $p$ | Probability of success | Approaches **1** as $n \to \infty$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the RRT Algorithm

The RRT algorithm is executed in **6 logical stages** per iteration until termination. Each stage is a deliberate design choice rooted in **computational geometry** and **probability theory**.

### Stage 1 — Initialization
- Create tree $\mathcal{T} = (V, E)$ with $V = \{q_{init}\}$ and $E = \emptyset$.
- **Why:** All future exploration must be reachable from the start. The tree is a *connected* structure, ensuring path retrieval at termination.

### Stage 2 — Random Sampling
- Draw a sample $q_{rand} \in \mathcal{C}$ uniformly at random.
- **Why:** Uniform sampling is **algorithmically simple** and yields the *Voronoi bias* property (explained in Section 2.2), which directs the tree to under-explored regions of $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- **Practical modification:** With probability $k$ (typically 0.05–0.20), set $q_{rand} = q_{goal}$ (goal biasing) to accelerate convergence.

### Stage 3 — Nearest Neighbor Search
- Find $q_{near} \in V$ that minimizes the distance:
$$q_{near} = \arg\min_{q \in V} \; \rho(q, q_{rand})$$
where $\rho : \mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{C} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ is a chosen **metric** (usually Euclidean).
- **Why:** Anchors the new exploration to the *closest already-reached* state, ensuring incremental progress without redundant branches.

### Stage 4 — Steering (Local Planning)
- Compute $q_{new}$ by stepping from $q_{near}$ toward $q_{rand}$ by at most $\epsilon$:
$$q_{new} = q_{near} + \epsilon \cdot \frac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\| q_{rand} - q_{near} \|}$$
- **Why:** A *bounded step* is critical. It (a) limits collision-check workload to a small local segment, and (b) keeps the tree resolution uniform across $\mathcal{C}$.

### Stage 5 — Collision Checking
- Verify the path segment $\overline{q_{near} \, q_{new}}$ lies entirely in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- If **YES** → add $q_{new}$ to $V$ and the edge $(q_{near}, q_{new})$ to $E$.
- If **NO** → discard; tree remains unchanged for this iteration.

### Stage 6 — Termination Check
- Stop when $q_{goal}$ is reached, $|V| = N_{max}$, or time budget elapses.
- **Path retrieval:** Trace parent pointers from $q_{goal}$ back to $q_{init}$.

---

## 2.2 The Voronoi Bias — Why RRTs "Rapidly Explore"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Voronoi Bias Theorem (LaValle, 1998):**
> The probability that a random sample falls in the Voronoi region of a vertex $v$ is proportional to the **volume** of that region. Hence the tree preferentially extends from vertices with *largest* Voronoi cells — the *least-explored* frontiers.

Let $Vor(v_i)$ denote the Voronoi region of vertex $v_i \in V$:

$$Vor(v_i) = \left\{ q \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \;\Big|\; \rho(q, v_i) \leq \rho(q, v_j) \; \forall j \neq i \right\}$$

Then the probability of extending from $v_i$ in any given iteration is:

$$\Pr[\text{extend from } v_i] = \frac{\mu(Vor(v_i))}{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})}$$

where $\mu(\cdot)$ is the Lebesgue measure (volume). Large unexplored regions → large Voronoi cell → high extension probability. This is *why* RRTs rapidly cover the space.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Use in Exam |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $q_{new} = q_{near} + \epsilon \cdot \dfrac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\vert\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \vert\vert}$ | **Steer function** | Derive $q_{new}$ for a given $q_{rand}$ |
| 2 | $q_{near} = \arg\min_{q \in V} \; \rho(q, q_{rand})$ | **Nearest neighbor** | Identify anchor for extension |
| 3 | $\Pr[\text{extend from } v_i] = \dfrac{\mu(Vor(v_i))}{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})}$ | **Voronoi bias** | Prove rapid exploration property |
| 4 | $\rho(q_a, q_b) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(q_a^{(j)} - q_b^{(j)})^2}$ | **Euclidean metric** | Compute nearest neighbor |
| 5 | $p_{\text{success}}(n) \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$ | **Probabilistic completeness** | Justify algorithm correctness |
| 6 | $C_{RR^*}(q) \le \gamma \cdot \log n / n^{1/d}$ | **RRT*** convergence cost (asymptotic optimal) | State optimality bound |
| 7 | $T_{NN}(n) = O(n)$ naive; $O(\log n)$ with kd-tree | **Nearest-neighbor time** | Complexity questions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** RRTs are deployed in industrial robotic arms (e.g., KUKA, ABB), autonomous vehicles (path planning in cluttered parking lots), UAV navigation, and surgical robotics (da Vinci system). The RRT* variant guarantees asymptotic optimality, making it preferred in modern implementations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Steer Function

The RRT **steer** operation is the heart of the algorithm. We derive it rigorously here.

**Given:**
- A vertex $q_{near} \in V$ (the nearest neighbor in the tree).
- A random sample $q_{rand} \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- A step-size bound $\epsilon > 0$.

**Goal:** Find $q_{new} \in \mathcal{C}_{free}$ such that:
1. $q_{new}$ lies on the line segment $\overline{q_{near} \, q_{rand}}$.
2. $\| q_{new} - q_{near} \| \le \epsilon$.

### Step-by-step Construction

**Step 1 — Direction Vector.** Form the unit vector pointing from $q_{near}$ toward $q_{rand}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{d} &= q_{rand} - q_{near} \\
\hat{d} &= \frac{\vec{d}}{\vert\vert \vec{d} \vert\vert} = \frac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\left\vert\left\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \right\vert\right\vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Dividing by the norm produces a *unit-length* direction, decoupling the formula's magnitude from the actual distance to the random sample.

**Step 2 — Bounded Step.** Multiply the unit direction by the constant step size $\epsilon$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{\Delta} &= \epsilon \cdot \hat{d} = \epsilon \cdot \frac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\left\vert\left\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \right\vert\right\vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This vector has magnitude exactly $\epsilon$, ensuring the local motion is bounded regardless of how far $q_{rand}$ lies from the tree.

**Step 3 — New Vertex.** Add the step to the anchor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{new} &= q_{near} + \vec{\Delta} \\
q_{new} &= q_{near} + \epsilon \cdot \frac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\left\vert\left\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \right\vert\right\vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Adding the displacement to the anchor position yields the candidate new vertex. This satisfies both constraints: collinearity (from the line parameterization) and the step bound.

**Step 4 — Edge Case Handling.** If $q_{rand} = q_{near}$, the direction is undefined. Conventionally, the iteration is **skipped** and a new $q_{rand}$ is drawn. This is the **degenerate-sample** case.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (2D, Numerical)

Let $\mathcal{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$, $q_{init} = (0, 0)$, $\epsilon = 1.0$, and suppose at iteration 5 we draw $q_{rand} = (3, 4)$. The current tree contains vertices:

$$V = \{ (0,0),\; (0.6, 0.8),\; (1.2, 1.6) \}$$

**Sub-step 3.2.1 — Compute distances to find $q_{near}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho((0,0), (3,4)) &= \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5.0 \\
\rho((0.6,0.8), (3,4)) &= \sqrt{2.4^2 + 3.2^2} = \sqrt{5.76 + 10.24} = \sqrt{16} = 4.0 \\
\rho((1.2,1.6), (3,4)) &= \sqrt{1.8^2 + 2.4^2} = \sqrt{3.24 + 5.76} = \sqrt{9} = 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

The minimum is **3.0**, hence $q_{near} = (1.2, 1.6)$.

*Logic:* Each distance uses the Euclidean metric. The minimum identifies the tree vertex from which extension is geometrically most natural.

**Sub-step 3.2.2 — Compute the steer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{rand} - q_{near} &= (3 - 1.2,\; 4 - 1.6) = (1.8, 2.4) \\
\vert\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \vert\vert &= \sqrt{1.8^2 + 2.4^2} = 3.0 \\
\hat{d} &= \frac{(1.8, 2.4)}{3.0} = (0.6, 0.8) \\
\vec{\Delta} &= \epsilon \cdot \hat{d} = 1.0 \cdot (0.6, 0.8) = (0.6, 0.8) \\
q_{new} &= (1.2, 1.6) + (0.6, 0.8) = (1.8, 2.4)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The unit direction $(0.6, 0.8)$ is recognized as a normalized vector. Multiplying by $\epsilon = 1$ simply reproduces it. The new vertex is reached.

**Sub-step 3.2.3 — Collision check & insertion:**
Assume the segment $\overline{(1.2,1.6) \to (1.8,2.4)}$ is collision-free. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &\leftarrow V \cup \{(1.8, 2.4)\} \\
E &\leftarrow E \cup \{((1.2, 1.6), (1.8, 2.4))\}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Tree is grown by one node. Iteration completes. Process repeats until $q_{goal}$ is reached or the iteration budget is exhausted.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Property Summary

| Property | Statement | Implication |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Probabilistic Completeness** | $\lim_{n \to \infty} \Pr[\text{path found} \vert \text{path exists}] = 1$ | Never *guaranteed* in finite time, but practically reliable |
| **Voronoi Bias** | Extension probability $\propto \mu(Vor(v_i))$ | Naturally fills unexplored regions |
| **Resolution Completeness** | **Not** resolution-complete (unlike grid planners) | Cannot guarantee optimality in finite samples |
| **Asymptotic Optimality** | Achieved only by **RRT***, not vanilla RRT | Cost-to-goal $\to$ optimum as $n \to \infty$ |
| **Single-Query** | Designed for one (start, goal) pair | Not a reusable roadmap (cf. PRM) |
| **Anytime Behavior** | Can output best path so far at any time | Useful under hard time constraints |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level RRT Algorithm Flow (Mermaid)

The following flowchart captures the **block-level functional architecture** of the RRT algorithm. Node identifiers are alphanumeric to avoid Mermaid parser conflicts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1([Start: Initialize T with q_init]) --> node2{V iterations < N_max}
    node2 -- No --> node9([End: Return path or failure])
    node2 -- Yes --> node3[Sample q_rand in C with goal bias]
    node3 --> node4[Find q_near in V minimizing rho q q_rand]
    node4 --> node5[Compute q_new using steer with epsilon]
    node5 --> node6{Collision free segment?}
    node6 -- No --> node2
    node6 -- Yes --> node7[Add q_new to V and edge to E]
    node7 --> node8{q_new reaches q_goal?}
    node8 -- No --> node2
    node8 -- Yes --> node10([End: Trace and return path])
```

*Reading guide:* Each diamond is a decision; each rectangle is an operation. The loop runs up to $N_{max}$ times, terminating early if $q_{goal}$ is connected.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Block Diagram (Mermaid)

This diagram maps the **interactions between the major components** of an RRT-based planner.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sampler] --> B[Nearest Neighbor Module]
    B --> C[Steer Function]
    C --> D[Collision Checker]
    D -->|Free| E[Tree Data Structure]
    D -->|In Collision| A
    E --> B
    E --> F[Path Retriever]
    F --> G[(Output Path)]
```

*Reading guide:*
- **Sampler** generates $q_{rand}$.
- **Nearest Neighbor Module** queries the **Tree Data Structure** for $q_{near}$.
- **Steer Function** produces $q_{new}$.
- **Collision Checker** validates the local edge.
- **Tree Data Structure** is the persistent state $V, E$.
- **Path Retriever** reconstructs the solution from parent pointers at termination.

---

## 4.3 Voronoi-Bias Tree-Growth Schematic (Mermaid)

A visual abstraction of how the tree expands in C-space. Each Voronoi cell is drawn as a region around its vertex.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Vor1["Voronoi Region of v1"]
        A1[v1 start]
    end
    subgraph Vor2["Voronoi Region of v2 small explored"]
        A2[v2]
    end
    subgraph Vor3["Voronoi Region of v3 large unexplored"]
        A3[v3]
    end
    Qr[Random Sample q_rand] -.falls in Vor3.-> A3
    A3 -->|extend by epsilon| A4[v_new]
    A4 --> A1
```

*Reading guide:* Because $q_{rand}$ falls in the *largest* Voronoi region (Vor3), the extension proceeds from $v_3$, biasing the tree toward unexplored space — this is the core intuition behind RRT's rapid exploration.

---

## 4.4 RRT vs RRT* — Rewiring Concept (Mermaid)

The RRT* extension performs an additional **rewiring** step to asymptotically minimize path cost.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New vertex q_new added] --> B[Find nearby neighbors within radius r_n]
    B --> C[For each neighbor: compute cost via q_new]
    C --> D[Choose parent minimizing cost]
    D --> E[Rewire neighbors if q_new gives lower cost]
    E --> F[Continue with next iteration]
```

*Reading guide:* The radius $r_n = \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}$ shrinks slowly with sample count, ensuring both connectivity and optimality.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree (RRT). State any two advantages of using RRT for motion planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree (RRT) is an incremental, sampling-based data structure for searching high-dimensional configuration spaces. It is built by repeatedly drawing random samples in the C-space and extending the nearest existing tree vertex by a bounded step $\epsilon$ toward each sample, while respecting collision constraints.

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Probabilistically complete**: probability of finding a path tends to 1 as samples increase.
2. **Efficient in high dimensions**: avoids the exponential blow-up of grid-based planners.
3. **Handles non-convex / cluttered spaces** with minimal preprocessing.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is meant by the "Voronoi bias" property of an RRT? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Meaning (2 marks):**
The Voronoi bias states that the probability of an RRT extending from a given vertex $v_i$ in any iteration is proportional to the volume of $v_i$'s Voronoi region $Vor(v_i)$ — i.e., the set of all points in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ closer to $v_i$ than to any other tree vertex.

**Importance (1 mark):**
It ensures that **under-explored regions** (those with large Voronoi cells) are preferentially extended into, enabling the tree to *rapidly* and *uniformly* cover $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ — the defining feature of the algorithm.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse RRT with RRT***. Vanilla RRT is *not* asymptotically optimal. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if you claim optimality for plain RRT.
> - Always **state the metric $\rho$** when writing the nearest-neighbor formula. Omitting this loses 1 mark.
> - In derivations, **show the unit-vector normalization step explicitly**. Skipping it costs 2 marks.
> - Mention **goal biasing** when asked about "improvements to vanilla RRT" — it is the most cited enhancement.
> - Always specify that the algorithm operates in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, **not** the workspace. Mixing these is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3, Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the RRT algorithm with a clearly labeled flowchart. Discuss the role of the **steer function** and **goal biasing** in the algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2D configuration space with start configuration $q_{init} = (0, 0)$, step size $\epsilon = 1.0$, and current tree vertices:
$$V = \{ (0, 0),\; (0.8, 0.6) \}$$
A random sample $q_{rand} = (2.0, 1.5)$ is drawn. Determine:
- (i) the nearest neighbor $q_{near}$, and
- (ii) the new vertex $q_{new}$.

Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

**Algorithm description (3 marks):**
The RRT algorithm proceeds iteratively:

1. Initialize $\mathcal{T} = (V, E)$ with $V = \{q_{init}\}$, $E = \emptyset$.
2. Sample $q_{rand} \in \mathcal{C}$ uniformly, with probability $k$ set $q_{rand} = q_{goal}$.
3. Find $q_{near} = \arg\min_{q \in V} \rho(q, q_{rand})$.
4. Compute $q_{new}$ via the steer function with step bound $\epsilon$.
5. If the segment $\overline{q_{near} q_{new}}$ is collision-free, add $q_{new}$ to $V$ and $(q_{near}, q_{new})$ to $E$.
6. Terminate when $q_{goal}$ is reached or budget exhausted.

**Steer function role (2 marks):**
The steer function produces a new vertex $q_{new}$ that lies on the ray from $q_{near}$ to $q_{rand}$ at distance $\le \epsilon$ from $q_{near}$. It:
- bounds the local motion, keeping collision-check workload tractable,
- ensures the tree has **uniform resolution** in $\mathcal{C}$,
- prevents over-shooting in narrow passages.

**Goal biasing role (2 marks):**
Goal biasing forces $q_{rand} = q_{goal}$ with probability $k$ (typically 0.05–0.20). This:
- accelerates convergence to the goal region,
- trades off between **exploration** (random samples) and **exploitation** (goal-directed),
- prevents the tree from wasting samples in regions far from the goal.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**(i) Nearest neighbor (3 marks):**

Distance from each vertex to $q_{rand} = (2.0, 1.5)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho((0, 0), (2.0, 1.5)) &= \sqrt{2.0^2 + 1.5^2} = \sqrt{4.0 + 2.25} = \sqrt{6.25} = 2.5 \\
\rho((0.8, 0.6), (2.0, 1.5)) &= \sqrt{(2.0 - 0.8)^2 + (1.5 - 0.6)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{1.2^2 + 0.9^2} = \sqrt{1.44 + 0.81} = \sqrt{2.25} = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1.5 < 2.5$, the nearest neighbor is:

$$q_{near} = (0.8, 0.6)$$

**(ii) New vertex via steer (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{rand} - q_{near} &= (2.0 - 0.8,\; 1.5 - 0.6) = (1.2, 0.9) \\
\vert\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \vert\vert &= \sqrt{1.2^2 + 0.9^2} = \sqrt{1.44 + 0.81} = \sqrt{2.25} = 1.5 \\
\hat{d} &= \frac{(1.2, 0.9)}{1.5} = (0.8, 0.6) \\
\vec{\Delta} &= \epsilon \cdot \hat{d} = 1.0 \cdot (0.8, 0.6) = (0.8, 0.6) \\
q_{new} &= q_{near} + \vec{\Delta} = (0.8, 0.6) + (0.8, 0.6) = (1.6, 1.2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $q_{new} = (1.6, 1.2)$. The new edge is $( (0.8, 0.6),\; (1.6, 1.2) )$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3, Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Voronoi bias** property of an RRT. Show mathematically that the probability of extending from a vertex $v_i$ is proportional to the volume of its Voronoi region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **RRT** and **RRT*** algorithms. State the asymptotic optimality bound for RRT* and explain the **rewiring** procedure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

**Conceptual explanation with diagram (3 marks):**
The Voronoi region of a tree vertex $v_i$ is the set of all points in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ that are closer (in the chosen metric $\rho$) to $v_i$ than to any other vertex. The RRT preferentially extends the tree from those vertices whose Voronoi cells are largest, i.e., the vertices on the *frontier* of exploration. *(A labelled diagram showing three vertices with Voronoi cells of unequal size and a random sample falling in the largest cell earns full credit.)*

**Mathematical derivation (4 marks):**
Let $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_m\}$ be the current vertex set. The Voronoi region of $v_i$ is:

$$Vor(v_i) = \{ q \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \mid \rho(q, v_i) \le \rho(q, v_j), \; \forall j \ne i \}$$

Since $q_{rand}$ is drawn uniformly from $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, the probability it falls in $Vor(v_i)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr[q_{rand} \in Vor(v_i)] &= \frac{\mu(Vor(v_i))}{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})}
\end{aligned}
$$

When $q_{rand} \in Vor(v_i)$, the nearest neighbor is $v_i$, hence the tree extends from $v_i$. Therefore:

$$\Pr[\text{extend from } v_i] = \frac{\mu(Vor(v_i))}{\mu(\mathcal{C}_{free})}$$

This proves the proportionality and explains the rapid exploration behavior.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**Comparison table-style (4 marks):**

| Aspect | RRT | RRT* |
|--------|-----|------|
| Optimality | Not guaranteed | Asymptotically optimal |
| Rewiring step | Absent | Present |
| Cost to goal | Sub-optimal | Converges to optimum |
| Computational overhead per iteration | $O(n)$ (NN) | $O(n \log n)$ (NN + rewiring) |
| Best for | Single-query, time-critical | Single-query, optimality required |

**Asymptotic optimality bound (2 marks):**
The cost of the best path found by RRT* satisfies:

$$C_{n} \le (1 + \varepsilon) \cdot C^{*} \quad \text{with high probability, as } n \to \infty$$

More precisely, the expected suboptimality decays as $O\!\left( \frac{\log n}{n^{1/d}} \right)$.

**Rewiring procedure (1 mark):**
After adding $q_{new}$, RRT* examines all neighbors within a shrinking radius $r_n = \gamma \left( \frac{\log n}{n} \right)^{1/d}$. For each neighbor, it checks if routing through $q_{new}$ yields a lower cost; if so, the parent pointer is rewired. This locally propagates cost improvements outward, leading to global optimality in the limit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist for **last-night revision** before the KTU ESE.

- **Definition**: RRT = incremental, sampling-based tree grown by random exploration; formally $\mathcal{T} = (V, E)$ with $V \subseteq \mathcal{C}_{free}$.
- **Six algorithm stages**: Init → Sample → Nearest Neighbor → Steer → Collision Check → Insert.
- **Steer formula (memorize verbatim)**:
$q_{new} = q_{near} + \epsilon \cdot \dfrac{q_{rand} - q_{near}}{\vert\vert q_{rand} - q_{near} \vert\vert}$
- **Nearest-neighbor formula**: $q_{near} = \arg\min_{q \in V} \rho(q, q_{rand})$.
- **Voronoi bias**: extension probability $\propto$ Voronoi cell volume → rapid exploration of under-explored regions.
- **Probabilistic completeness**: success probability $\to 1$ as $n \to \infty$, but **not** guaranteed in finite time.
- **RRT vs RRT***: only RRT* has rewiring and asymptotic optimality — never claim optimality for vanilla RRT.
- **Goal biasing**: $q_{rand} = q_{goal}$ with probability $k \in [0.05, 0.20]$ for faster convergence.
- **Step size $\epsilon$**: trade-off — large $\epsilon$ = fast but coarse; small $\epsilon$ = fine but slow.
- **Complexity**: NN search is $O(n)$ naively, $O(\log n)$ with kd-tree.
- **C-space note**: RRT operates in **configuration space** $\mathcal{C}$, not the workspace $\mathcal{W}$.
- **Single-query planner**: RRT solves one (start, goal) pair; for multi-query use PRM instead.
- **Variants to know**: RRT* (optimal), RRT-Connect (bi-directional), Dynamic-Domain RRT (narrow passages).
- **Author & year**: LaValle, 1998 — *Rapidly-Exploring Random Trees: A New Tool for Path Planning*.
- **Real-world uses**: autonomous vehicles, robotic surgery (da Vinci), UAV navigation, industrial manipulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
