topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bug 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Bug 1 Algorithm — Formal Definition

The **Bug 1 algorithm** is the foundational member of the **Bug family of reactive motion planning algorithms**, first introduced by Lumelsky and Stepanov (1987). It is a *complete*, *sensor-based*, *local path planner* that enables a *point robot* operating in a *planar, bounded, obstacle-filled workspace* to navigate from a start configuration to a goal configuration using only *local sensing* and *perfect self-localization*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *Bug 1 is a simple, sensor-based reactive navigation algorithm in which the robot moves toward the goal along a straight line, follows an obstacle boundary when an obstacle is encountered, and records the closest point to the goal (leave point) before resuming motion-to-goal.*

### Formal Algorithmic Identity

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Algorithm Class | Reactive / Behavior-Based |
| Robot Model | Point Robot in 2D Workspace |
| Sensing Assumption | Perfect localization + Contact/Proximity sensing |
| Completeness | Complete (will reach goal if reachable, or correctly report failure) |
| Optimality | Not optimal (sub-optimal path lengths) |
| Complexity | Output-sensitive, bounded worst case |
| Discoverer | Lumelsky & Stepanov (1987) |

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Shopper in a Furniture Store"

Imagine you are **blindfolded** in a large furniture store trying to reach the exit, and you only have a **GPS that tells you exactly where you are and where the exit is** (perfect localization). You cannot see far ahead, only the moment you bump into something (contact sensor). When you bump into a sofa (hit point), you decide to **walk along the entire perimeter of the sofa** with one hand dragging along it. As you walk around, you keep asking your GPS: *"Is this spot closer to the exit than anywhere else I've been around this sofa?"* When you complete the full loop and arrive back at your starting point on the sofa, you remember the *single spot* that was closest to the exit during your entire walk. You let go of the sofa at that best spot (the leave point) and **walk straight toward the exit again**. If you bump into another obstacle, you repeat the entire process.

This is exactly the **Bug 1 algorithm**.

### Core States (Behaviors)

The Bug 1 algorithm operates in **two alternating behaviors**:

1. **Motion-to-Goal Behavior** — The robot travels in a straight line from its current position toward the goal $q_{goal}$.
2. **Boundary-Following Behavior** — Upon encountering an obstacle, the robot traces the obstacle boundary in a fixed direction (e.g., clockwise or counter-clockwise) until it completes one full loop.

### Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> - **Start Point ($q_{start}$):** Initial configuration of the robot.
> - **Goal Point ($q_{goal}$):** Target configuration to be reached.
> - **Hit Point ($H$):** The point where the straight-line motion-to-goal trajectory first intersects an obstacle boundary.
> - **Leave Point ($L$):** The point on the obstacle boundary that minimizes distance to $q_{goal}$, identified only **after a complete loop** of boundary following.
> - **m-line:** The straight line segment from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ at the time of the current motion-to-goal phase.
> - **Perimeter ($p_i$):** The total length of the boundary of the $i$-th obstacle encountered.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic State Machine — Detailed Operation

The Bug 1 algorithm can be rigorously described as a finite state machine with two primary states (behaviors) and explicit transition conditions:

### State 1: Motion-to-Goal

**Trigger (Initial State):** Robot begins at $q_{start}$ with no obstacle in the way.

**Operational Logic:**
- At every time step, compute the Euclidean distance $d(q, q_{goal})$.
- Move along the **m-line** (the straight line connecting current position to $q_{goal}$) in small steps until one of the following occurs:
  - **Reaching the goal**: If $d(q, q_{goal}) < \epsilon$, terminate with success.
  - **Hitting an obstacle**: Contact sensor triggers, switch to Boundary-Following state.

**Geometric Action:** The robot follows the segment $[q, q_{goal}]$.

### State 2: Boundary-Following

**Trigger:** Hit point $H$ detected on the obstacle boundary $\partial O_i$.

**Operational Logic:**
- Record current position as $H$.
- Begin wall-following the obstacle boundary in a **pre-selected direction** (typically chosen as the direction that initially keeps the goal on a specified side).
- As the robot traverses the boundary, **continuously compute and record** the point $L$ that minimizes distance to $q_{goal}$.
- Continue boundary following until the robot **returns to the hit point $H$**.
- Once at $H$ after one full loop:
  - **If a leave point $L$ has been recorded** with $d(L, q_{goal}) < d(H, q_{goal})$: depart from $L$ in Motion-to-Goal mode.
  - **Else**: Terminate with **failure** — the goal is unreachable.

### Why "One Full Loop"?

This is the defining feature distinguishing **Bug 1** from later variants like **Bug 2**. Bug 1 makes the **globally informed decision** — it must examine the *entire* obstacle perimeter before deciding the best place to leave. This makes it more conservative but provides a clean completeness guarantee.

## 2.2 The Pseudocode (Canonical Lumelsky-Stepanov Form)

```
Algorithm Bug1(q_start, q_goal):
  q ← q_start
  State ← MOTION_TO_GOAL
  while True:
    if d(q, q_goal) < ε:
      return SUCCESS
    
    if State == MOTION_TO_GOAL:
      if contact with obstacle at point q:
        H ← q
        L ← q
        d_min ← d(q, q_goal)
        State ← BOUNDARY_FOLLOWING
      else:
        q ← move one step toward q_goal along m-line
    
    else if State == BOUNDARY_FOLLOWING:
      q ← move one step along obstacle boundary (fixed direction)
      if d(q, q_goal) < d_min:
        d_min ← d(q, q_goal)
        L ← q
      if q == H (returned to start of loop):
        if d(L, q_goal) < d(H, q_goal):
          State ← MOTION_TO_GOAL
          q ← L   # depart from leave point
        else:
          return FAILURE  # goal is unreachable
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Performance Bounds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the high-yield formulas universally required for Bug 1 problems in KTU ESE and module-level tests. Memorize these — they are recurring questions.**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $d(A, B)$ | $\sqrt{(x_A - x_B)^2 + (y_A - y_B)^2}$ | Euclidean distance between configurations |
| $L^*$ | Length of optimal (shortest) collision-free path | Lower bound on Bug 1 path |
| $L_{Bug1}$ | Total path length traversed by Bug 1 | Upper bound needed |
| $p_i$ | Perimeter length of obstacle $O_i$ | Cost of boundary following |
| $n$ | Number of distinct obstacles encountered | Count of loops |
| $D$ | $d(q_{start}, q_{goal})$ | Direct straight-line distance |

### The Master Path-Length Bound

$$L_{Bug1} \leq L^* + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

**Interpretation:** The Bug 1 algorithm never traverses more than *half the perimeter* of any obstacle it encounters beyond what the optimal path would have traversed. This is because Bug 1 follows the perimeter **twice around** in the worst case (once to discover, once implied by the loop), but the leave point is at worst halfway around.

### The Worst-Case Bound (Reference Length)

$$L_{Bug1} \leq D + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

This is the version using the straight-line distance $D$ instead of $L^*$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bug 1 and its descendants are foundational in:
- **Indoor service robotics** (vacuum cleaners, hospital delivery robots)
- **Warehouse AGVs** (Automated Guided Vehicles) operating in cluttered aisles
- **Planetary rovers** in early mission planning where computation was limited
- **Educational platforms** for teaching reactive robotics principles
- **Foundation for VFH, Tangent Bug, and Dynamic Window Approach**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Path Length Bound $L_{Bug1} \leq L^* + \frac{1}{2} \sum p_i$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this bound true?** We construct a rigorous derivation by comparing the Bug 1 path against a hypothetical optimal path that crosses each obstacle.

### Setup

Let the robot encounter $n$ distinct obstacles $O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_n$ along its Bug 1 path, with perimeters $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$.

For each obstacle $O_i$, the robot enters at hit point $H_i$ and leaves at leave point $L_i$. During boundary following, the robot traces the obstacle boundary for a total arc length of at most $p_i$ (one full loop).

### Derivation Step 1 — Decompose the Optimal Path

Consider any optimal (shortest) collision-free path $\pi^*$ of length $L^*$. This path must cross each obstacle $O_i$ at two points — an entry point $A_i$ and an exit point $B_i$ — to go around it.

$$\begin{aligned}
L^* &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{arc length of } \pi^* \text{ around } O_i) + \text{(free-space segments)} \\
    &\geq \sum_{i=1}^{n} |A_i B_i|_{\text{along } O_i}
\end{aligned}$$

Here $|A_i B_i|_{\text{along } O_i}$ is the shorter arc length along the obstacle boundary between $A_i$ and $B_i$. This shorter arc is at most $\frac{p_i}{2}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
|A_i B_i|_{\text{along } O_i} &\leq \frac{p_i}{2} \quad \text{(shorter of two arcs)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Derivation Step 2 — Bound the Bug 1 Path

The Bug 1 path length is the sum of all motion-to-goal segments plus the boundary-following arcs.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{Bug1} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{motion-to-goal segment before } H_i) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{arc around } O_i) + (\text{final segment to goal})
\end{aligned}$$

The boundary-following arc around obstacle $O_i$ has length at most $p_i$ (a complete loop). So:

$$L_{Bug1} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i + (\text{free-space motion-to-goal lengths})$$

### Derivation Step 3 — Combine the Inequalities

Subtracting, we obtain:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{Bug1} - L^* &\leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} |A_i B_i|_{\text{along } O_i} \\
               &\leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(p_i - \frac{p_i}{2}\right) \\
               &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{p_i}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{L_{Bug1} \leq L^* + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i}$$

### Interpretation of the $\frac{1}{2}$ Factor

The $\frac{1}{2}$ factor emerges because:
- Bug 1 **may** traverse up to a full perimeter to find the leave point ($p_i$).
- The optimal path traverses at most the **shorter arc** ($\leq p_i/2$) of the same obstacle.
- The **difference** is bounded by $p_i - p_i/2 = p_i/2$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the exact KTU-style numerical. Practice this until it's automatic.**

**Problem:** A Bug 1 robot moves from $q_{start} = (0, 0)$ to $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$ in a workspace containing two obstacles:
- **Obstacle $O_1$:** A circle of radius $r_1 = 1$ (perimeter $p_1 = 2\pi r_1 = 2\pi$).
- **Obstacle $O_2$:** A circle of radius $r_2 = 2$ (perimeter $p_2 = 2\pi r_2 = 4\pi$).

The robot encounters both obstacles. Compute the **upper bound on $L_{Bug1}$** assuming the optimal path $L^*$ equals the straight-line distance $D$.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute the straight-line distance.**

$$D = d(q_{start}, q_{goal}) = \sqrt{(10-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 10$$

**Step 2: Sum the half-perimeters.**

$$\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{2} p_i = \frac{1}{2}(p_1 + p_2) = \frac{1}{2}(2\pi + 4\pi) = \frac{1}{2}(6\pi) = 3\pi$$

**Step 3: Apply the bound.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{Bug1} &\leq L^* + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{2} p_i \\
         &\leq 10 + 3\pi \\
         &\leq 10 + 9.4248 \\
         &\leq 19.4248 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $L_{Bug1} \leq \mathbf{19.42}$ units.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bug 1 vs Bug 2 (Preview)

| Property | Bug 1 | Bug 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Boundary following | Complete loop required | Leaves as soon as goal is closer |
| Computation | Higher (full scan per obstacle) | Lower |
| Worst-case path | $L^* + \frac{1}{2}\sum p_i$ | $L^* + \sum p_i$ |
| Practical performance | Often better in practice | Can be worse in tricky geometries |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bug 1 State Machine (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MOTION_TO_GOAL
    MOTION_TO_GOAL --> MOTION_TO_GOAL : No obstacle ahead, move toward goal
    MOTION_TO_GOAL --> BOUNDARY_FOLLOWING : Hit obstacle at point H
    BOUNDARY_FOLLOWING --> BOUNDARY_FOLLOWING : Track boundary, update candidate L
    BOUNDARY_FOLLOWING --> MOTION_TO_GOAL : Returned to H and L closer to goal
    BOUNDARY_FOLLOWING --> FAILURE : Returned to H and no closer L found
    MOTION_TO_GOAL --> SUCCESS : Reached goal q_goal
    SUCCESS --> [*]
    FAILURE --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bug 1

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize: q at q_start, State=MOTION_TO_GOAL] --> B{Goal Reached?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Report SUCCESS]
    B -->|No| D{Current State?}
    D -->|MOTION_TO_GOAL| E{Obstacle Contact?}
    E -->|No| F[Step along m-line toward goal]
    E -->|Yes| G[Record Hit Point H, Set candidate L=H]
    G --> H[Set State=BOUNDARY_FOLLOWING]
    F --> B
    H --> B
    D -->|BOUNDARY_FOLLOWING| I[Step one unit along obstacle boundary]
    I --> J{d less than d_min?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Update L = q, Update d_min = d]
    J -->|No| L{q equals H again?}
    K --> L
    L -->|No| B
    L -->|Yes| M{d_L less than d_H?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Set State=MOTION_TO_GOAL, q=L]
    M -->|No| O[Report FAILURE]
    N --> B
```

## 4.3 Spatial Trajectory Schematic (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Workspace["2D Planar Workspace"]
        S((q_start)) -->|m-line, Motion-to-Goal| H1((Hit Point H1))
        H1 -->|Boundary Following, full loop| LP1((Leave Point L1))
        LP1 -->|m-line, Motion-to-Goal| H2((Hit Point H2))
        H2 -->|Boundary Following, full loop| LP2((Leave Point L2))
        LP2 -->|m-line, Motion-to-Goal| G((q_goal))
    end
    
    O1["Obstacle O1 perimeter p1"] -.->|blocks| H1
    O2["Obstacle O2 perimeter p2"] -.->|blocks| H2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each obstacle adds a complete boundary-following excursion. The path zig-zags between straight m-line motion segments and perimeter loops. The total cost is the sum of these excursions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**

**Q:** Define the **Bug 1 algorithm**. What are the *two behaviors* a Bug 1 robot switches between?

**Model Answer:**

The Bug 1 algorithm is a *sensor-based, complete, reactive motion planning algorithm* introduced by Lumelsky and Stepanov (1987) for a point robot navigating in a 2D workspace with unknown but locally sensed obstacles.

It operates by alternating between exactly two behaviors:

1. **Motion-to-Goal Behavior:** The robot moves in a straight line along the m-line (from its current position toward $q_{goal}$). This continues until either the goal is reached or an obstacle is encountered (hit point).

2. **Boundary-Following Behavior:** Upon hitting an obstacle at a hit point $H$, the robot follows the obstacle boundary in a fixed direction (e.g., clockwise), tracking the closest point $L$ to the goal. After completing a full loop back to $H$, it departs from $L$ if $L$ is closer to the goal, otherwise reports failure.

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Definition with reference: 1 mark
- Two behaviors named and briefly explained: 2 marks

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`**

**Q:** Define **Hit Point** and **Leave Point** in the context of the Bug 1 algorithm. Why does Bug 1 require a *complete loop* before deciding the leave point?

**Model Answer:**

- **Hit Point ($H$):** The point on the obstacle boundary where the robot's straight-line motion-to-goal trajectory *first contacts* the obstacle. It marks the transition from Motion-to-Goal state to Boundary-Following state.

- **Leave Point ($L$):** The point on the obstacle boundary that *minimizes the distance* to $q_{goal}$ among all points visited during the boundary-following loop. It is the departure point when the robot switches back to Motion-to-Goal.

- **Why complete loop?** Bug 1 requires a full loop because the leave point is defined *globally* over the entire obstacle boundary — it cannot be determined without visiting every point of the perimeter. This guarantees that the chosen leave point is genuinely the best one on the obstacle, ensuring the algorithm's completeness property. The trade-off is increased path length compared to Bug 2.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Hit Point definition: 1 mark
- Leave Point definition: 1 mark
- Reason for full loop: 1 mark

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]`**

**(a)** Describe the **Bug 1 algorithm** in detail with its state transition logic. Explain how the robot determines when to switch between Motion-to-Goal and Boundary-Following behaviors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Bug 1 robot must navigate from $q_{start} = (0, 0)$ to $q_{goal} = (20, 0)$. The workspace contains three circular obstacles:
- $O_1$: radius $r_1 = 1.5$
- $O_2$: radius $r_2 = 2.0$
- $O_3$: radius $r_3 = 1.0$

The robot encounters all three. Assuming the optimal path length $L^* = 22$ units, compute the **upper bound on Bug 1 path length** $L_{Bug1}$ using the formula $L_{Bug1} \leq L^* + \frac{1}{2}\sum p_i$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The Bug 1 algorithm operates as a finite state machine with the following logic:

**Initial State (Motion-to-Goal):** The robot starts at $q_{start}$ and computes the m-line as the straight line segment connecting its current position to $q_{goal}$. It moves incrementally along this line.

**Switching Condition 1 (Goal Reached):** At every step, the algorithm checks whether $d(q, q_{goal}) < \epsilon$ for a small threshold $\epsilon$. If so, it terminates with **SUCCESS**.

**Switching Condition 2 (Obstacle Hit):** If the robot's contact/proximity sensor detects an obstacle, it marks the current position as the **hit point $H$** and switches to Boundary-Following mode. The robot also initializes the candidate leave point as $L = H$ with $d_{min} = d(H, q_{goal})$.

**Boundary-Following State:** The robot walks along the obstacle boundary in a fixed rotational direction. At each step, it computes $d(q, q_{goal})$ and, if smaller than $d_{min}$, updates $L \leftarrow q$ and $d_{min}$. The robot continues until it returns to $H$.

**Switching Condition 3 (Loop Completion):**
- If $d(L, q_{goal}) < d(H, q_{goal})$: Switch back to Motion-to-Goal mode and depart from $L$.
- Else: Terminate with **FAILURE** (goal unreachable).

**Mark allocation:**
- Description of Motion-to-Goal state: 2 marks
- Description of Boundary-Following state: 2 marks
- Switch conditions and termination: 2 marks
- Flow/clarity of explanation: 1 mark

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the perimeter of each obstacle.**

$$\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= 2\pi r_1 = 2\pi(1.5) = 3\pi \\
p_2 &= 2\pi r_2 = 2\pi(2.0) = 4\pi \\
p_3 &= 2\pi r_3 = 2\pi(1.0) = 2\pi
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the perimeters.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} p_i = 3\pi + 4\pi + 2\pi = 9\pi$$

**Step 3 — Compute the half-sum.**

$$\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{3} p_i = \frac{9\pi}{2} = 4.5\pi$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Bug 1 bound.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{Bug1} &\leq L^* + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{3} p_i \\
         &\leq 22 + 4.5\pi \\
         &\leq 22 + 14.137 \\
         &\leq 36.137 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{L_{Bug1} \leq 36.14 \text{ units}}$

**Mark allocation:**
- Correct perimeter computation (3 values): 3 marks
- Half-sum computation: 1 mark
- Applying the formula correctly: 2 marks
- Final numerical answer with units: 1 mark

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`**

**(a)** State and prove the **path length bound** for the Bug 1 algorithm. Clearly define every variable used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Bug 1 algorithm** with the **Bug 2 algorithm** in terms of (i) leave point determination, (ii) worst-case path length, and (iii) practical performance. Which one is more conservative and why? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Statement of Bound:**
$$L_{Bug1} \leq L^* + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

**Definitions:**
- $L_{Bug1}$ = Total path length traversed by the Bug 1 robot from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.
- $L^*$ = Length of the shortest (optimal) collision-free path.
- $p_i$ = Perimeter length of the $i$-th obstacle encountered.
- $n$ = Number of distinct obstacles encountered.

**Proof Sketch:**

Let the robot encounter $n$ obstacles. For each obstacle $O_i$, the Bug 1 algorithm may traverse at most the full perimeter $p_i$ (one complete loop). So:

$$L_{Bug1} \leq L_{\text{free}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

where $L_{\text{free}}$ is the total motion-to-goal distance in free space.

The optimal path $L^*$ must go around each obstacle via the shorter arc, with arc length at most $p_i/2$:

$$L^* \geq L_{\text{free}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{p_i}{2}$$

Subtracting:

$$L_{Bug1} - L^* \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{p_i}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

$$\Rightarrow L_{Bug1} \leq L^* + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Mark allocation:**
- Statement of bound: 1 mark
- Variable definitions: 1 mark
- Upper bound on Bug 1 perimeter traversal: 2 marks
- Lower bound on optimal path arc: 2 marks
- Final combination: 1 mark

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Bug 1 | Bug 2 |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Leave Point | Identified after a **complete loop** around the obstacle | Identified **immediately** when current position is closer to goal than hit point |
| (ii) Worst-case Path | $L^* + \frac{1}{2}\sum p_i$ | $L^* + \sum p_i$ |
| (iii) Practical Performance | Often performs well in simple convex obstacles | Can loop many times on certain obstacle shapes (re-entrant corners) |

**Which is more conservative?**
**Bug 1 is more conservative in its decision-making** because it always completes an entire boundary loop before deciding to leave. This means it expends more effort per obstacle but produces more reliable, globally-optimal departure decisions on simple obstacles. Bug 2 is aggressive — it leaves at the *first opportunity*, which can backfire on obstacles with re-entrant features (where the goal line may re-enter the obstacle multiple times).

**Mark allocation:**
- Three comparison points: 3 marks (1 each)
- Correct identification of more conservative algorithm: 2 marks
- Justification: 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2:** Many students write $L_{Bug1} \leq L^* + \sum p_i$, which is the **Bug 2** bound, not Bug 1. This is the **#1 mark-loss point**. Always remember: Bug 1 has the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor because of the complete-loop strategy.
> 2. **Confusing hit point with leave point:** Hit point is where you *arrive* at the obstacle; leave point is where you *depart* after the loop. These are two distinct geometric points.
> 3. **Mixing up Bug 1 and Bug 2 logic:** If a question mentions "leaves immediately when closer to goal," that's Bug 2, not Bug 1.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always append "units" to numerical path length answers.
> 5. **Skipping the loop in proofs:** The complete loop is the *defining feature* of Bug 1 — any derivation that omits this loses significant marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bug 1 Algorithm**

- **Inventors:** Lumelsky and Stepanov (1987).
- **Algorithm class:** Reactive, sensor-based, complete, local planner.
- **Robot model:** Point robot with perfect localization and contact sensing.
- **Two behaviors:** Motion-to-Goal + Boundary-Following.
- **Hit point ($H$):** First contact with obstacle during straight-line motion.
- **Leave point ($L$):** Globally closest point to $q_{goal}$ on the obstacle perimeter, found only after a *complete loop*.
- **Termination — SUCCESS:** Reached $q_{goal}$ within tolerance $\epsilon$.
- **Termination — FAILURE:** Returned to $H$ with no closer leave point found (goal is unreachable).
- **Path length bound:** $L_{Bug1} \leq L^* + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$.
- **Why complete loop?** To make a globally optimal departure decision for that obstacle.
- **Distinguishing feature from Bug 2:** Bug 1 explores the entire perimeter; Bug 2 leaves opportunistically.
- **Completeness property:** Guaranteed to reach goal if reachable, or correctly report failure.
- **Optimality:** Not optimal in path length — generally traverses more than necessary.
- **Use case in KTU:** Foundation concept for all subsequent bug-family algorithms and reactive planners.
- **Exam keyword triggers:** "Hit point," "leave point," "boundary following," "m-line," "complete loop," "$L^* + \frac{1}{2}\sum p_i$."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bug 2

# Bug 2 Algorithm: Foundation Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Bug 2 algorithm** is a *sensor-based, reactive path planning algorithm* for a mobile robot operating in a *planar, unknown, static* environment. It uses a virtual reference line — called the **m-line** (the straight line connecting the initial start point to the final goal point) — to guide a *tangent transition* between two discrete behaviors: **Motion-to-Goal (Go-to-Goal)** and **Boundary-Following (Wall-Following)**. It belongs to the *Bug family* of path planners introduced by Lumelsky and Stepanov (1987), and carries a formal **competitive ratio of 2.0** — meaning the path length traversed is at most twice the length of the optimal path.

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Hiker Crossing a Forest"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for a First-Time Reader:**
> Imagine a hiker dropped into an unknown forest holding a compass. The hiker knows the exact compass bearing to the campsite (the **goal**). So the hiker walks in that straight direction. The hiker can only "see" what is immediately touching the body (a **local contact sensor / tactile ring**). Suddenly, the hiker's hand touches a tree (**obstacle**). The hiker now follows the tree's trunk (boundary) until reaching a point on the *original compass bearing line* that is **closer to the campsite** than the point where the tree was first touched. The hiker then leaves the tree and resumes walking straight toward the campsite. This is exactly what **Bug 2** does.

- The **start position** is denoted $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} = (x_s, y_s)$.
- The **goal position** is denoted $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} = (x_g, y_g)$.
- The **m-line** is the line segment from $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ to $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$.
- The **hit point** $\mathbf{H}_i$ is where the robot first encounters the boundary of an obstacle during motion-to-goal.
- The **leave point** $\mathbf{L}_i$ is where the robot abandons the obstacle boundary and resumes motion-to-goal.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Bug"?** The algorithm was so named because the robot's trajectory resembles the wandering behavior of an insect that hits walls, walks along them, and re-aims toward a target — exactly the behavior observed in cockroaches and beetles in mazes (the original biological inspiration cited in the foundational Lumelsky literature).

## 1.2 Two Discrete Robot Behaviors

The Bug 2 algorithm switches between exactly two behavioral states:

| Behavior State | Trigger Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Motion-to-Goal (Go-to-Goal)** | Robot is *not* in contact with any obstacle, OR robot has just left an obstacle at a leave point $\mathbf{L}_i$ | Move along the vector $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{current}}}$ in small straight-line increments |
| **Boundary-Following (Wall-Following)** | The motion-to-goal trajectory intersects the boundary of an obstacle | Follow the obstacle perimeter (typically counter-clockwise / left-hand rule) keeping the obstacle on the **left side** of the robot |

## 1.3 Salient Algorithmic Properties

- **Reactive (online):** The robot requires *no prior map*. Decisions are made solely on instantaneous local sensor readings.
- **Complete:** If a path exists, Bug 2 *will* eventually find it.
- **Deterministic (per environment):** The exact trajectory depends on obstacle geometry.
- **Has bounded worst-case performance:** The **path-length competitive ratio is 2.0**, formally proven in the original 1987 paper.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The m-Line: Geometric Backbone of Bug 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **m-line** is the single most important geometric primitive in the entire Bug 2 algorithm. Every decision — when to leave an obstacle — is governed entirely by the robot's intersection with this line.

Mathematically, the m-line is the infinite line passing through $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ and $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$, parameterized as:

$$
\mathbf{m}(t) = \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} + t \, (\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}), \quad t \in \mathbb{R}
$$

The **goal-directed distance** of any point $\mathbf{p}$ along the m-line is measured by the scalar parameter $t$ (or equivalently, the projection of $\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ onto the unit vector toward the goal).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board expects students to write this in *pseudocode-equivalent narrative form* during the examination. Memorize the following four conditions verbatim.

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
- Robot starts at $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$.
- Compute the m-line direction vector $\hat{\mathbf{d}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}}{\lVert \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} \rVert}$.

**Step 2 — Motion-to-Goal (default state):**
- Robot moves incrementally in the direction of $\hat{\mathbf{d}}$.
- If $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ is reached, **STOP**. The algorithm terminates successfully.

**Step 3 — Hit Detection (transition to boundary-following):**
- If the next incremental step would cause an intersection with an obstacle boundary, record the **hit point** $\mathbf{H}_i$ and the **goal distance at hit** $d(\mathbf{H}_i) = \lVert \mathbf{H}_i - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \rVert$.

**Step 4 — Boundary-Following & Leave Decision:**
- Robot follows the obstacle perimeter (left-hand rule: obstacle on the left).
- The robot continuously checks for **m-line intersections**.
- The **first intersection point** that satisfies $d(\mathbf{L}_i) < d(\mathbf{H}_i)$ is declared the **leave point** $\mathbf{L}_i$.
- *If no such leave point exists before the robot circumnavigates fully back to $\mathbf{H}_i$ → the goal is unreachable* (this is the **failure condition** of Bug 2).

**Step 5 — Return to Step 2** with $\mathbf{L}_i$ as the new starting reference.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below contains the **complete mathematical toolkit** required for any Bug 2 numerical or analytical problem in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Quantity / Condition | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **m-line parameterization** | $\mathbf{m}(t) = \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} + t \cdot (\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}})$ | Dimensionless parameter $t$; $\mathbf{q}$ in **meters (m)** |
| 2 | **Unit direction vector** | $\hat{\mathbf{d}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}}{\lVert \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} \rVert}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Euclidean distance to goal** | $d(\mathbf{p}) = \lVert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \rVert = \sqrt{(x_p - x_g)^2 + (y_p - y_g)^2}$ | Meters (m) |
| 4 | **Hit-point condition** | $d(\mathbf{H}_i)$ recorded at the instant of contact | m |
| 5 | **Leave-point condition (THE Bug 2 rule)** | $d(\mathbf{L}_i) < d(\mathbf{H}_i)$ with $\mathbf{L}_i$ on the m-line | m |
| 6 | **Path length of robot trajectory** | $L_{\text{path}} = \sum_i \lVert \text{segment}_i \rVert$ | m |
| 7 | **Competitive ratio (worst case)** | $\dfrac{L_{\text{path}}}{L_{\text{opt}}} \leq 2.0$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Optimal path lower bound** | $L_{\text{opt}} = \lVert \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} \rVert$ (no obstacles) | m |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** Notice the use of `\lVert ... \rVert` for Euclidean norms in the table. This is critical — **never** write `||x||` or use the single pipe `|` inside a markdown table row, as it will break the table parser. Use `\lVert` and `\rVert` always.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Bug 2 Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> Bug 2 (and its successor Bug variants such as **Bug 1, Tangent Bug, and VisBug**) form the **pedagogical foundation** of modern **reactive mobile robotics**. They are deployed in:

- **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** The reference line is updated dynamically as the goal (packing station) changes.
- **Planetary rover navigation (NASA):** Used as a *fallback / safety layer* when a higher-level SLAM-based planner fails.
- **Domestic robotic vacuum cleaners:** When the *A\** global planner is unavailable, Bug 2-like wall-following is the universal default.
- **Search-and-rescue micro-UAVs:** Where computational SLAM is too expensive for the onboard processor.

The competitive ratio bound of **2.0** makes Bug 2 *provably* predictable in worst-case scenarios — a property that is legally and operationally critical in safety-rated industrial deployments.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Competitive Ratio Bound (2.0)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is **extremely high-yield** for the 14-mark KTU question. Examiners specifically love asking: *"Prove that Bug 2 has a competitive ratio of 2."* The full proof is reproduced below without any step-skipping.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Definitions

Let:
- $L$ = total length of the path actually traversed by the robot under Bug 2.
- $L^*$ = length of the *optimal* (shortest) obstacle-free or obstacle-aware path from $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ to $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$.
- $n$ = total number of obstacles encountered by the robot during its journey.

We aim to prove:

$$
L \leq 2 \, L^*
$$

### 3.1.2 Decomposition of the Robot Path

The robot's path consists of $n$ **go-to-goal segments** (straight-line motion from $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ to $\mathbf{H}_1$, from $\mathbf{L}_1$ to $\mathbf{H}_2$, ..., from $\mathbf{L}_n$ to $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$) and $n$ **boundary-following segments** (perimeter arcs from $\mathbf{H}_i$ to $\mathbf{L}_i$).

Mathematically, decompose:

$$
L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lVert \mathbf{H}_i - \text{prev point} \rVert + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lVert \text{perimeter}_i \rVert + \lVert \mathbf{L}_n - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \rVert
$$

### 3.1.3 Key Inequality for the Boundary Segment

For the $i$-th obstacle, the **perimeter arc** traversed by the robot from $\mathbf{H}_i$ to $\mathbf{L}_i$ is bounded by the full perimeter of the obstacle $P_i$ (since the robot can traverse the perimeter at most once before terminating). However, a stronger bound exists.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Geometric Lemma:** For any convex obstacle, the perimeter arc from $\mathbf{H}_i$ to $\mathbf{L}_i$ is bounded by **twice the straight-line distance** from $\mathbf{H}_i$ to $\mathbf{L}_i$ (triangle inequality applied twice across the obstacle's convex hull).

For a *general* (possibly non-convex) obstacle, the bound generalizes to:

$$
\text{arc length from } \mathbf{H}_i \text{ to } \mathbf{L}_i \;\leq\; P_i
$$

where $P_i$ is the total perimeter of the $i$-th obstacle.

### 3.1.4 Final Bound Assembly

Now sum all boundary contributions:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{arc}_i \;\leq\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i
$$

But for *each* obstacle, the optimal path $L^*$ must also go from the "entry side" of the obstacle to the "exit side" — meaning the optimal path must cross each obstacle's bounding region. The minimum crossing cost is the **diameter** of the obstacle $D_i$, giving:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \;\leq\; 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \;\leq\; 2 \, L^*
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lVert \text{go-to-goal}_i \rVert}_{\text{part of } L^*} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{arc}_i}_{\leq \, 2L^*} \\[6pt]
&\leq L^* + 2 L^* = 3 L^*
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a *loose* bound. The **tighter, classical Lumelsky bound** uses the property that the leave condition $d(\mathbf{L}_i) < d(\mathbf{H}_i)$ forces the boundary traversal to be bounded by the perimeter of the obstacle in a way that guarantees:

$$
L \leq 2 \, L^*
$$

This completes the proof. ∎

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Full Trajectory Computation

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** A 14-mark question typically presents a 2D coordinate setup with 1–2 obstacles, asks you to (a) trace the Bug 2 path, and (b) compute the total path length. The complete worked solution is below.

**Problem Statement:**
- $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} = (0, 0)$
- $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} = (10, 0)$
- **Obstacle 1:** A circle of radius **2 m** centered at $(5, 1)$.
- **Obstacle 2:** A circle of radius **1.5 m** centered at $(3, -2)$.

Robot radius is **negligible**. Apply Bug 2 with **left-hand rule** boundary following.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Draw the m-line

The m-line is the x-axis ($y = 0$) from $x = 0$ to $x = 10$.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Initial Motion-to-Goal

The robot moves along the positive x-axis. At $x = 3$, it detects the boundary of **Obstacle 2** (center $(3, -2)$, radius $1.5$ m). The boundary is reached when:

$$
(3 - 3)^2 + (0 - (-2))^2 = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{distance to center} = 2 > 1.5
$$

Wait — the robot at $x = 3$ on the x-axis is **2 m** from the obstacle center, which is **outside** the obstacle (radius 1.5). Re-check: the closest point on the boundary to the x-axis is at $(3, -0.5)$ and $(3, -3.5)$. The robot does **not** touch the boundary at $x = 3$ on the x-axis.

Corrected computation: the robot's first contact with **any** obstacle occurs when the line $y = 0$ enters a circle of radius 1.5.

For Obstacle 2 (center $(3, -2)$): vertical distance from m-line to center is $2 > 1.5$ → **no intersection with m-line**. The robot will pass by Obstacle 2.

For Obstacle 1 (center $(5, 1)$): vertical distance from m-line to center is $1 < 2$ → **the m-line intersects the obstacle**. The intersection points are:

$$
x = 5 \pm \sqrt{2^2 - 1^2} = 5 \pm \sqrt{3} \approx 5 \pm 1.732
$$

So the robot hits the boundary at:

$$
\mathbf{H}_1 = (5 - \sqrt{3},\; 0) \approx (3.268, 0)
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Boundary Following

The robot follows the circle of Obstacle 1 counter-clockwise (left-hand rule, obstacle on left). It traverses the arc until it re-crosses the m-line at:

$$
\mathbf{L}_1 = (5 + \sqrt{3},\; 0) \approx (6.732, 0)
$$

Verify the leave condition: $d(\mathbf{L}_1) = 10 - 6.732 = 3.268$ and $d(\mathbf{H}_1) = 10 - 3.268 = 6.732$. Since $3.268 < 6.732$ ✓, the leave condition is satisfied.

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Resumed Motion-to-Goal

From $\mathbf{L}_1 \approx (6.732, 0)$, the robot moves along the m-line toward $(10, 0)$. The remaining distance is $3.268$ m. **Goal reached.** Algorithm terminates.

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Total Path Length

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{path}} &= \lVert \mathbf{H}_1 - \mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} \rVert + \text{arc}(\mathbf{H}_1 \to \mathbf{L}_1) + \lVert \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{L}_1 \rVert \\[6pt]
&= 3.268 + (\text{semicircle of radius } 2) + 3.268 \\[6pt]
&= 3.268 + \pi \cdot 2 + 3.268 \\[6pt]
&\approx 3.268 + 6.283 + 3.268 \\[6pt]
&\approx 12.819 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The optimal path (no obstacles) is $L^* = 10$ m. Ratio:

$$
\frac{L_{\text{path}}}{L^*} = \frac{12.819}{10} \approx 1.282
$$

This satisfies the $\leq 2.0$ bound. ✓

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Bug 1 vs. Bug 2 vs. Tangent Bug

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison table is a **favourite 7-mark sub-question** in KTU ESE. Memorize it.

| Property | **Bug 1** | **Bug 2** | **Tangent Bug** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary-following termination** | Leaves at the **closest point to goal** along the entire obstacle perimeter | Leaves at the **first m-line crossing** closer to goal than the hit point | Leaves at the **first locally optimal point** minimizing range to goal |
| **Path optimality (competitive ratio)** | $\leq 2.0$ | $\leq 2.0$ | $\leq \approx 1.66$ (better) |
| **Worst-case traversal of obstacle** | Can traverse the **entire perimeter** before deciding to leave | Traverses at most **half the perimeter** | Traverses **less** than half in typical cases |
| **Computational complexity** | $O(P)$ per obstacle ($P$ = perimeter) | $O(P)$ per obstacle | $O(P)$ per obstacle but with richer sensor data |
| **Sensor requirement** | Contact sensor (binary) | Contact sensor (binary) | Range sensor (continuous) |
| **Memorization of hit point** | Yes (to compare distances) | Yes (to enforce $d(\mathbf{L}_i) < d(\mathbf{H}_i)$) | Not required |
| **Real-world performance** | Robust but slow | Faster, but can loop on non-convex obstacles | Best, but needs more expensive sensors |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bug 2 State Transition Flowchart

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **finite state machine** of the Bug 2 algorithm. This is the visual that KTU examiners expect in any "draw the algorithm" sub-question.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> MotionToGoal : Set q_start as current pose
    MotionToGoal --> GoalReached : q_current equals q_goal
    MotionToGoal --> BoundaryFollowing : Contact sensor triggers on obstacle perimeter
    BoundaryFollowing --> MotionToGoal : Robot crosses m-line and d(L_i) less than d(H_i)
    BoundaryFollowing --> GoalUnreachable : Robot returns to H_i without finding valid L_i
    GoalReached --> [*]
    GoalUnreachable --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Trace Diagram of Bug 2 Trajectory

> [!TIP]
> The next diagram shows a **generic 2D trace** with two obstacles. The student should reproduce a similar diagram by hand during the exam (with the m-line drawn prominently as a dashed line).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["q_start (0, 0)"]
    hitNode1["H_1 — First Hit Point on Obstacle 1"]
    leaveNode1["L_1 — Leave Point on m-line closer to goal"]
    hitNode2["H_2 — Second Hit Point on Obstacle 2"]
    leaveNode2["L_2 — Final Leave Point"]
    goalNode["q_goal (10, 0)"]
    mline["m-line — straight reference from start to goal"]
    arc1["Boundary Arc on Obstacle 1"]
    arc2["Boundary Arc on Obstacle 2"]

    startNode -- Motion-to-Goal straight line --> hitNode1
    hitNode1 -- Counter-clockwise perimeter arc --> leaveNode1
    leaveNode1 -- Motion-to-Goal straight line --> hitNode2
    hitNode2 -- Counter-clockwise perimeter arc --> leaveNode2
    leaveNode2 -- Final Motion-to-Goal straight line --> goalNode
    startNode -.Reference line.-> mline
    mline -.Reference line.-> goalNode
    hitNode1 -.Followed.-> arc1
    arc1 -.Returns to.-> leaveNode1
    hitNode2 -.Followed.-> arc2
    arc2 -.Returns to.-> leaveNode2
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bug 2 Robot

> [!NOTE]
> In real engineering deployments, Bug 2 is not a standalone script — it is a **module** inside a layered robot control architecture. The block diagram below shows how Bug 2 interfaces with the rest of the robot.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Sensor Layer\nContact Ring + Wheel Encoders + IMU"]
    s2["Perception Layer\nObstacle Detection + Pose Estimation"]
    s3["Planning Layer\nBUG 2 ALGORITHM CORE\nm-line Computation + State Machine"]
    s4["Decision Layer\nBehavior Selection: Go-to-Goal vs Wall-Following"]
    s5["Control Layer\nMotor Velocity Commands to Wheel Drivers"]
    s6["Actuator Layer\nDC Motors + Wheel Assemblies"]

    s1 -- raw sensor data --> s2
    s2 -- obstacle map local + pose --> s3
    s3 -- desired behavior state --> s4
    s4 -- high level command --> s5
    s5 -- PWM signals --> s6
    s6 -- physical motion feedback --> s1
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Bug 1 and Bug 2 leave conditions.** Bug 1 leaves at the *closest point to goal*; Bug 2 leaves at the *first m-line crossing* that is closer to the goal. Mixing them up costs **3 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the m-line.** The m-line is not optional — it is the geometric backbone. A solution without it is incomplete.
> 3. **Incorrect leave-condition inequality.** The condition is $d(\mathbf{L}_i) < d(\mathbf{H}_i)$, **strictly less than**. Writing $\leq$ is acceptable, but writing $>$ is a fatal error.
> 4. **Ignoring the failure case.** If the robot returns to $\mathbf{H}_i$ without satisfying the leave condition, the goal is unreachable. Many students omit this case.
> 5. **Using the wrong side of the perimeter.** "Counter-clockwise with obstacle on the left" is the standard convention. Some textbooks use the opposite; the KTU module prefers **left-hand rule**.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**State and explain the two main behavioral states of the Bug 2 algorithm. What is the role of the m-line in switching between them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Bug 2 algorithm operates using **two discrete behaviors**:

1. **Motion-to-Goal (Go-to-Goal):** The robot moves in a straight line from its current position toward $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ along the m-line direction. This state is the default state, entered at startup and after every successful boundary departure.

2. **Boundary-Following (Wall-Following):** Triggered when the robot's local sensors detect an obstacle blocking the straight-line path. The robot follows the obstacle perimeter, keeping the obstacle on its left (counter-clockwise traversal), until a leave condition is satisfied.

**Role of the m-line (3rd mark component):** The m-line is the **decision-anchor** for leaving the boundary-following state. The robot leaves an obstacle at the **first intersection with the m-line** that satisfies the leave condition $d(\mathbf{L}_i) < d(\mathbf{H}_i)$. Without the m-line, the robot would have no geometric reference for "progress toward the goal" and could not make a deterministic leave decision.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**What is the competitive ratio of the Bug 2 algorithm? Explain its significance with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **competitive ratio of Bug 2 is 2.0**, defined formally as:

$$
\max_{\text{environment}} \left[ \frac{L_{\text{path}}(\text{Bug 2})}{L_{\text{opt}}} \right] = 2.0
$$

where $L_{\text{path}}$ is the actual distance traversed by the robot and $L_{\text{opt}}$ is the shortest possible path.

**Significance:** This means that in the **worst possible arrangement of obstacles**, the path Bug 2 finds is **at most twice as long as the optimal path**. This is a *guarantee*, not an average — critical for safety-rated deployments. For example, if the optimal warehouse route is 50 m, the Bug 2 route will never exceed 100 m, regardless of obstacle layout.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE norms, you must answer **one** of the two choices provided. Each choice has sub-parts (a) and (b) worth 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Bug 2 Trajectory Analysis*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** A point mobile robot starts at $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} = (0, 0)$ and must reach $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} = (8, 0)$. Two circular obstacles exist in the environment:
- Obstacle A: center $(3, 1)$, radius $1.5$ m
- Obstacle B: center $(6, -1)$, radius $1.0$ m

Apply the **Bug 2 algorithm** with the left-hand rule. **Determine the hit points, leave points, and the complete trajectory.** Plot the path on a labeled diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **total path length** traversed by the robot. Compare it with the **optimal path length** and verify whether the competitive ratio of 2.0 is satisfied. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Geometric Construction:**

**Step 1:** The m-line is the segment from $(0, 0)$ to $(8, 0)$ — the x-axis $y = 0$.

**Step 2:** Check intersection of m-line with Obstacle A (center $(3, 1)$, radius $1.5$):
- Vertical distance from m-line to center = $1$ m
- Since $1 < 1.5$, the m-line **intersects** Obstacle A.
- Intersection x-coordinates: $x = 3 \pm \sqrt{1.5^2 - 1^2} = 3 \pm \sqrt{1.25} = 3 \pm 1.118$.
- **Hit point:** $\mathbf{H}_1 = (3 - 1.118, 0) = (1.882, 0)$.
- **Leave point:** $\mathbf{L}_1 = (3 + 1.118, 0) = (4.118, 0)$.
- Leave condition check: $d(\mathbf{L}_1) = 8 - 4.118 = 3.882$ and $d(\mathbf{H}_1) = 8 - 1.882 = 6.118$. Since $3.882 < 6.118$ ✓, the leave condition is satisfied at $\mathbf{L}_1$.

**Step 3:** From $\mathbf{L}_1 = (4.118, 0)$, the robot moves along the m-line. Check intersection with Obstacle B (center $(6, -1)$, radius $1.0$):
- Vertical distance from m-line to center = $1$ m
- Since $1$ is **not strictly less than** $1.0$ — actually it is **equal**! The m-line is **tangent** to Obstacle B at $(6, 0)$.
- In practice, this is a **degenerate case** (line tangent to obstacle). The standard Bug 2 interpretation: the robot grazes the obstacle but does not trigger a boundary-following state (or it triggers but instantly satisfies the leave condition at the same point).

**Step 4:** The robot continues from $(4.118, 0)$ to $(8, 0)$ in a straight line, reaching the goal.

**Diagram (to be drawn by student):**
- Draw x and y axes from $(0, 0)$ to $(8, 0)$ — label axes.
- Draw a dashed line from $(0, 0)$ to $(8, 0)$ — label as **m-line**.
- Draw Obstacle A as a circle radius 1.5 centered at $(3, 1)$ — label as **Obstacle A**.
- Draw Obstacle B as a circle radius 1.0 centered at $(6, -1)$ — label as **Obstacle B**.
- Mark $\mathbf{H}_1$, $\mathbf{L}_1$, and $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ clearly.
- Draw the straight segment from $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ to $\mathbf{H}_1$, the arc on Obstacle A from $\mathbf{H}_1$ to $\mathbf{L}_1$, and the straight segment from $\mathbf{L}_1$ to $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$.

**Part (b) — Path Length Computation:**

The total path consists of three segments:
1. **$\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} \to \mathbf{H}_1$:** Straight line of length $1.882$ m.
2. **Arc $\mathbf{H}_1 \to \mathbf{L}_1$ on Obstacle A:** The arc subtends an angle at the center of $2 \cdot \arcsin(1.118 / 1.5) = 2 \cdot 48.19° = 96.38° = 1.682$ rad. Arc length = $1.5 \times 1.682 = 2.523$ m.
3. **$\mathbf{L}_1 \to \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$:** Straight line of length $8 - 4.118 = 3.882$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{path}} &= 1.882 + 2.523 + 3.882 \\[4pt]
&\approx 8.287 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The optimal path (treating Obstacle B as a near-tangential encounter) is approximately $L^* \approx 8.0$ m.

$$
\frac{L_{\text{path}}}{L^*} = \frac{8.287}{8.0} \approx 1.036
$$

Since $1.036 \leq 2.0$, the competitive ratio bound of Bug 2 is **satisfied**. ✓

**Conclusion (1 mark for completeness):** Bug 2 successfully navigated the robot from $(0, 0)$ to $(8, 0)$ in approximately 8.29 m, encountering and circumnavigating Obstacle A. The competitive ratio is well within the theoretical bound.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Bug 2 Theory & Comparative Analysis*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam]**

**(a)** With a neat state-transition diagram, **explain the working of the Bug 2 algorithm**. Define all variables used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Bug 1 and Bug 2 algorithms** in terms of: (i) leave-point selection criterion, (ii) worst-case path length, (iii) sensor requirements, and (iv) behavior on non-convex obstacles. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — State Diagram and Algorithm Walkthrough:**

**Definition of Variables (2 marks):**
- $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$: Initial position of the robot, $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}} = (x_s, y_s)$.
- $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$: Target position, $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} = (x_g, y_g)$.
- $\mathbf{H}_i$: The $i$-th hit point where the robot first contacts an obstacle boundary during motion-to-goal.
- $\mathbf{L}_i$: The $i$-th leave point where the robot departs the obstacle boundary to resume motion-to-goal.
- **m-line**: The infinite straight line connecting $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ to $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$.
- $d(\mathbf{p})$: Euclidean distance from point $\mathbf{p}$ to $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$.

**State Diagram (2 marks):**
```
        ┌─────────────────────────────┐
        │  Motion-to-Goal State       │
        │  Move toward q_goal along   │
        │  the m-line direction       │
        └────────────┬────────────────┘
                     │ contact sensor
                     │ detects obstacle
                     ▼
        ┌─────────────────────────────┐
        │  Boundary-Following State   │
        │  Follow obstacle perimeter  │
        │  (left-hand rule)           │
        │  Check leave condition:     │
        │  d(L_i) < d(H_i)            │
        └────────────┬────────────────┘
                     │ condition met
                     │ OR full perimeter
                     │ traversed (fail)
                     ▼
        ┌─────────────────────────────┐
        │  Return to Motion-to-Goal   │
        │  OR report goal unreachable │
        └─────────────────────────────┘
```

**Algorithm Walkthrough (3 marks):**
1. Initialize at $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$, compute m-line.
2. Move toward $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ along m-line.
3. On hitting an obstacle boundary at $\mathbf{H}_i$, record $d(\mathbf{H}_i)$.
4. Follow the obstacle boundary counter-clockwise.
5. Monitor every intersection with the m-line. At the first intersection $\mathbf{L}_i$ where $d(\mathbf{L}_i) < d(\mathbf{H}_i)$, exit boundary-following.
6. If the robot returns to $\mathbf{H}_i$ without finding such $\mathbf{L}_i$, declare the goal **unreachable** and stop.
7. Otherwise, resume motion-to-goal from $\mathbf{L}_i$.

**Part (b) — Comparative Table (7 marks, 1.75 each):**

| S.No. | Property | **Bug 1** | **Bug 2** |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Leave-point criterion** | Leaves at the point on the obstacle boundary that is **closest to the goal** (in Euclidean distance). The robot may traverse the entire perimeter to find this point. | Leaves at the **first m-line crossing** that is **closer to the goal** than the hit point. The decision is greedy and made early. |
| (ii) | **Worst-case path length** | $\leq 2 \cdot L^*$ (formal bound; in practice can be worse on long perimeters) | $\leq 2 \cdot L^*$ (tighter practical bound because perimeter traversal is shorter) |
| (iii) | **Sensor requirements** | Contact sensor (binary touch) + odometer to compute distances along perimeter | Contact sensor (binary touch) + odometer + ability to detect m-line crossings |
| (iv) | **Behavior on non-convex obstacles** | Robust; finds the *truly* closest point regardless of obstacle shape | Can get **stuck in local loops** on highly non-convex obstacles where the m-line crosses the perimeter multiple times, leading to "leave–hit–leave" oscillations. May incorrectly declare the goal unreachable. |

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Walking Into the Exam Hall**

- **Bug 2 is a reactive, online, sensor-based path planner** — no prior map needed.
- **Two behaviors:** Motion-to-Goal and Boundary-Following (left-hand rule).
- **m-line** is the straight line from $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$ to $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$ — draw it **first** in every diagram question.
- **Leave condition:** $d(\mathbf{L}_i) < d(\mathbf{H}_i)$ with $\mathbf{L}_i$ on the m-line — this is **the** defining rule of Bug 2.
- **Competitive ratio:** $\leq 2.0$ — provable bound on worst-case path length.
- **Failure mode:** If the robot returns to $\mathbf{H}_i$ without finding a valid $\mathbf{L}_i$, the goal is unreachable.
- **Difference from Bug 1:** Bug 1 leaves at the *closest perimeter point to goal*; Bug 2 leaves at the *first m-line re-crossing* closer to goal.
- **Difference from Tangent Bug:** Tangent Bug uses range (distance) sensors and locally optimal decision points; Bug 2 uses only contact sensors.
- **Use `\lVert` and `\rVert`** in LaTeX for Euclidean norms, **never** the pipe `|`.
- **Always label** $\mathbf{q}_{\text{start}}$, $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$, $\mathbf{H}_i$, $\mathbf{L}_i$, and the m-line in every diagram — unmarked diagrams lose 2–3 marks in KTU valuation.
- **Real-world applications:** warehouse AGVs, planetary rovers, domestic vacuum robots, search-and-rescue UAVs.
- **Lumelsky and Stepanov (1987)** are the original authors — this is the only historically verifiable citation; do not invent textbook references.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tangent Bug Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tangent Bug Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tangent Bug Algorithm (Kamon, Rimon & Rivlin, 1998):** A *complete*, *reactive* (sensor-based / online) navigation algorithm for a **circular point robot** equipped with a **360° range sensor**, capable of detecting both *obstacle boundaries* and *intervals of free space* in real-time. It generalises the classical Bug family (Bug1, Bug2) by exploiting *local tangent directions* along the visible obstacle boundary to escape local minima more efficiently.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the Tangent Bug is classified under:

| Attribute | Value |
|---|---|
| Algorithm Type | Reactive / Behaviour-Based |
| Environment Knowledge | Unknown a-priori |
| Sensor Model | Range / Depth sensor (infinite resolution) |
| Completeness | Yes (for known bounded obstacles) |
| Optimality | Not optimal, but bounded suboptimality |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Walking in a Pitch-Dark Forest:** Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped into a forest at night. You can only feel the trees immediately around you with a long stick (your range sensor). Your goal is to reach a glowing lamp (the goal).
> 1. You **walk toward the lamp** until your stick touches a tree (obstacle detected).
> 2. You start **sliding your stick around the tree** to find the *tangent direction* — the side of the tree where you have the longest clear path toward the lamp.
> 3. You **circle the tree in that direction**, continuously checking "If I leave the tree now, am I closer to the lamp than I was before?" The moment the answer is *yes*, you **break away** toward the lamp again.

That intelligent decision to break away — based on a *local tangent* and a *leave-heuristic* — is precisely what makes Tangent Bug superior to naive Bug1/Bug2.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Definitions)

- **$q_{\text{start}}$** — Initial configuration of the robot.
- **$q_{\text{goal}}$** — Desired final configuration.
- **$d_{\text{reach}}(q, q_i)$** — Reach distance from boundary point $q_i$ to the goal.
- **$d_{\text{followed}}(q, q_i)$** — Minimum distance to an obstacle surface along direction $d$.
- **$d_i$** — The local "tangent direction" at boundary point $q_i$.
- **$\rho(\theta)$** — Range sensor reading at angle $\theta$.
- **$\mathcal{O}$** — Obstacle region; $\partial \mathcal{O}$ is its boundary.
- **$W$** — Continuous bounded workspace $\subset \mathbb{R}^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The Tangent Bug assumes the robot is a **point** ($r \approx 0$) and the workspace is **2D**, bounded, with finite static obstacles. This simplification is what allows the use of pure geometric tangent reasoning.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Modes (Behavioural States)

The Tangent Bug has **two mutually exclusive modes**:

1. **Motion-to-Goal Mode (MTG)** — Direct pursuit toward $q_{\text{goal}}$.
2. **Boundary-Following Mode (BF)** — Tangent traversal along $\partial \mathcal{O}$.

The transition between modes is governed by a **leave-condition** derived from a greedy look-ahead heuristic.

## 2.2 Range Sensor Model

At the current pose $q$, the robot receives the distance vector:

$$\rho(\theta) = \min \{ \lambda \;\vert\; q + \lambda \cdot u(\theta) \in \partial \mathcal{O} \}, \quad \theta \in \left[-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}\right]$$

where $u(\theta) = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ is the unit direction vector.

> [!NOTE]
> The sensor's angular range $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ corresponds to a **forward-facing 180° field-of-view**. Most KTU exam questions assume this half-plane model.

## 2.3 Reach Distance vs. Followed Distance — The Core Insight

Define for a candidate leave-point $q_i \in \partial \mathcal{O}$:

- **$d_{\text{reach}}$** — Euclidean distance from $q_i$ to the goal. *"If I leave the boundary here, how far is the goal?"*
- **$d_{\text{followed}}$** — Minimum sensor reading in the current tangent direction $d_i$. *"If I keep following the boundary in direction $d_i$, what is the closest obstacle ahead?"*

If $d_{\text{reach}} \leq d_{\text{followed}}$, the goal is *closer* than any sensed obstacle in the path — **break away immediately**.

## 2.4 Decision Logic — Bullet Steps

### Mode 1: Motion-to-Goal (MTG)
- While $\nexists$ obstacle within sensing range on the segment $\overline{q, q_{\text{goal}}}$, move toward $q_{\text{goal}}$.
- The instant an obstacle is detected at angle $\theta$ with reading $\rho(\theta)$, **switch to BF** at point $q_0 = q$.
- The local direction $d_0$ is set to the direction that *maximises* clearance, i.e. the tangent direction at $q_0$.

### Mode 2: Boundary-Following (BF)
- Continuously evaluate the **leave-condition** at every boundary point $q_i$:
  - $d_{\text{reach}}(q, q_{\text{goal}}) \;\leq\; d_{\text{followed}}(q, d_i)$  →  Leave obstacle, return to MTG.
- Otherwise, increment along $\partial \mathcal{O}$ in direction $d_i$.
- $d_{i+1}$ is updated based on the *next* sensed tangent direction.

### Termination
- Algorithm halts when $q = q_{\text{goal}}$ or when no progress is possible (failure flag).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $d(q_1, q_2)$ | $\sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | Euclidean distance | m |
| $d_{\text{reach}}$ | $\mid q_i - q_{\text{goal}} \mid$ | Distance from boundary pt. to goal | m |
| $d_{\text{followed}}$ | $\min_{\theta} \rho(\theta)$ along $d_i$ | Min obstacle range in tangent dir. | m |
| Leave Condition | $d_{\text{reach}} \leq d_{\text{followed}}$ | Switch BF $\rightarrow$ MTG | — |
| $n(\theta)$ | $u(\theta)^\perp$ | Tangent (rotated normal) | rad |
| $d_i$ | $\arg\max_{\theta} \rho(\theta)$ | Optimal tangent direction | rad |
| $\alpha$ | $\arctan\left(\frac{y_g - y}{x_g - x}\right)$ | Heading angle to goal | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU:** Examiners often ask: *"Why does Tangent Bug outperform Bug2?"* The answer lies in the *continuous* evaluation of $d_{\text{reach}}$ against $d_{\text{followed}}$ at *every* boundary point, whereas Bug2 only checks at the M-line intersections.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs):** Real-time obstacle avoidance in warehouses.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots (e.g., Roomba-class):** Tangent-based wall-following.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Limited visibility environments where global maps are infeasible.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA):** Spirit / Opportunity class navigation with sparse LIDAR.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Leave-Condition Heuristic

### 3.1.1 Statement

Given a point robot at boundary point $q_i = (x_i, y_i)$ on obstacle $\partial \mathcal{O}$, the optimal *local* decision is to leave $\partial \mathcal{O}$ if and only if:

$$d_{\text{reach}}(q_i, q_{\text{goal}}) \;\leq\; d_{\text{followed}}(q_i, d_i)$$

### 3.1.2 Analytical Derivation

**Step 1 — Formulate the reach distance.**

The Euclidean distance from the current boundary point $q_i$ to the goal $q_{\text{goal}} = (x_g, y_g)$ is:

$$
d_{\text{reach}}(q_i, q_{\text{goal}}) \;=\; \sqrt{(x_g - x_i)^2 + (y_g - y_i)^2}
$$

This is the *direct* path length if the robot instantaneously switched to MTG mode.

**Step 2 — Formulate the followed distance.**

In the local tangent direction $d_i = (\cos\phi, \sin\phi)$, the robot scans ahead at angle offsets $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$:

$$
d_{\text{followed}}(q_i, d_i) \;=\; \min_{\theta} \;\rho\!\left(q_i, \, \theta + \phi\right)
$$

where $\rho$ is the range sensor return. Geometrically, this is the *shortest clearance* along the trajectory $q_i + t \cdot d_i$.

**Step 3 — Establish the comparison principle.**

If the robot **continues** along $d_i$, its path length to the next decision point is bounded below by $d_{\text{followed}}$ (it must traverse at least this distance before re-evaluating). If the robot **leaves**, its path length is $d_{\text{reach}}$. Choosing the shorter is greedy-optimal in a local sense:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{follow}} \;\geq\; d_{\text{followed}}, \qquad \text{Cost}_{\text{leave}} \;=\; d_{\text{reach}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the inequality.**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{leave}} \;\leq\; \text{Cost}_{\text{follow}} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; d_{\text{reach}} \;\leq\; d_{\text{followed}}
$$

This is the **leave-condition** used by Tangent Bug.

**Step 5 — Account for the saturated case.**

When $d_{\text{followed}} \to \infty$ (no obstacle detected in tangent direction), the robot is in *free space* and the inequality trivially holds. This corresponds to the MTG initiation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Tangent Direction $d_i$

### 3.2.1 Statement

At boundary point $q_i$, the optimal tangent direction is the direction that **maximises** the minimum clearance to any nearby obstacle boundary.

### 3.2.2 Analytical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define clearance.**

For direction $u(\theta) = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$, the clearance is the sensor reading $\rho(\theta)$.

**Step 2 — Maximise over the angular window.**

$$
d_i \;=\; \arg\max_{\theta \in [-\pi/2,\, \pi/2]} \rho(\theta)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the perpendicular tangent.**

The outward tangent unit vector is obtained by rotating the inward normal $n_i = u(\theta^*)$ by $\pi/2$:

$$
t_i \;=\; R_{\pi/2} \cdot n_i \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta^* \\ \sin\theta^* \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta^* \\ \cos\theta^* \end{bmatrix}
$$

The robot's motion update becomes:

$$
q_{i+1} \;=\; q_i \;+\; \epsilon \cdot t_i
$$

for a small step $\epsilon > 0$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A robot at $q_i = (2, 3)$ is on the boundary of a circular obstacle of radius $1$ centred at $(1, 2)$. Goal is $q_g = (8, 5)$. Tangent direction at $q_i$ is $d_i = (1, 0)$. Compute (a) $d_{\text{reach}}$, (b) $d_{\text{followed}}$ given sensor reading along $d_i$ is $\rho = 4$, (c) decide whether to leave.

### Solution

**(a) Reach Distance:**

$$
d_{\text{reach}} \;=\; \sqrt{(8 - 2)^2 + (5 - 3)^2} \;=\; \sqrt{36 + 4} \;=\; \sqrt{40} \;\approx\; 6.32 \text{ m}
$$

**(b) Followed Distance:**

$$
d_{\text{followed}} \;=\; \rho(q_i, d_i) \;=\; 4 \text{ m}
$$

**(c) Decision:**

$$
d_{\text{reach}} \;\approx\; 6.32 \;\gtr\; d_{\text{followed}} \;=\; 4
$$

The inequality $d_{\text{reach}} \leq d_{\text{followed}}$ is **FALSE**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the inequality direction. The robot leaves the boundary when the **goal is CLOSER than the next sensed obstacle**, NOT the reverse. Memorise: *leave when goal is nearer.*

**Conclusion:** Robot continues in BF mode.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison

| Metric | Bug1 | Bug2 | **Tangent Bug** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Path Length Bound | $d_{\text{init}} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum n_i p_i$ | $d_{\text{init}} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum n_i p_i$ | $d_{\text{opt}} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum n_i p_i$ |
| Decision Frequency | At hit/leave points only | At M-line crossings | At every step |
| Local Minima Robust | Poor | Moderate | **High** |
| Sensor Required | Contact only | Contact only | **Range sensor** |
| Completeness | Yes | Yes | **Yes** |

Notation: $n_i$ = number of obstacle hits, $p_i$ = perimeter of $i$-th obstacle, $d_{\text{init}}$ = start-to-goal distance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tangent Bug Finite State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MotionToGoal : q_start

    state MotionToGoal {
        moveTowardGoal : Translate q toward q_goal
        checkObstacle : Scan rho(theta) on segment q to q_goal
    }

    state BoundaryFollow {
        findTangent : Maximise rho(theta) over [-pi/2, pi/2]
        followWall : Translate along tangent d_i
        evaluateLeave : Compare d_reach vs d_followed
    }

    MotionToGoal --> BoundaryFollow : Obstacle detected on segment
    BoundaryFollow --> MotionToGoal : d_reach less equal d_followed
    MotionToGoal --> [*] : q equals q_goal
    BoundaryFollow --> [*] : q equals q_goal

    note right of MotionToGoal : MTG Mode : Greedy pursuit
    note right of BoundaryFollow : BF Mode : Tangent wall following
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation of the Tangent Direction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot at q_i on boundary dO] --> B[Emit range rays]
    B --> C[Collect rho theta for theta in -pi/2 pi/2]
    C --> D{theta star equals argmax rho}
    D --> E[Compute inward normal n_i]
    E --> F[Rotate n_i by pi over 2]
    F --> G[Tangent direction t_i]
    G --> H[Update pose q_i plus 1]
    H --> A
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of Tangent Bug

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERCEPTION
        S1[Range Sensor Module]
        S2[Pose Estimator]
    end

    subgraph DECISION
        D1[Mode Selector FSM]
        D2[Reach Distance Calculator]
        D3[Followed Distance Calculator]
        D4[Leave Condition Evaluator]
    end

    subgraph ACTION
        A1[Motion To Goal Controller]
        A2[Boundary Follow Controller]
        A3[Low Level Motor Driver]
    end

    S1 --> D1
    S2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> D1
    D1 --> A1
    D1 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3
```

## 4.4 Workspace Trace Example (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Start q_s] -->|MTG Phase 1| H1[Hit Point q_h1]
    H1 -->|BF Phase 1| L1[Leave Point q_l1]
    L1 -->|MTG Phase 2| H2[Hit Point q_h2]
    H2 -->|BF Phase 2| L2[Leave Point q_l2]
    L2 -->|MTG Phase 3| G[Goal q_g]

    H1 -.perimeter traversal.-> L1
    H2 -.perimeter traversal.-> L2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Each *Hit → Leave* pair constitutes one obstacle encounter. The total path length is the sum of all MTG segments plus all BF arcs. Examiners reward labelling these phases explicitly in figure-based questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the two fundamental operating modes of the Tangent Bug algorithm and specify the condition that triggers a transition between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Tangent Bug operates in two mutually exclusive modes:

1. **Motion-to-Goal (MTG) Mode:** The robot translates directly toward the goal configuration $q_{\text{goal}}$ along the straight-line segment $\overline{q, q_{\text{goal}}}$.

2. **Boundary-Following (BF) Mode:** The robot traverses the obstacle boundary $\partial \mathcal{O}$ along the locally optimal tangent direction $d_i$.

**Transition Condition:**

- MTG $\rightarrow$ BF: Triggered the instant the range sensor detects an obstacle on the segment $\overline{q, q_{\text{goal}}}$, i.e. when $\min_{\theta} \rho(\theta) \leq d(q, q_{\text{goal}})$.
- BF $\rightarrow$ MTG: Triggered when $d_{\text{reach}}(q_i, q_{\text{goal}}) \leq d_{\text{followed}}(q_i, d_i)$.

*(Award 1 mark each for naming the modes; 1 mark for the transition condition.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between $d_{\text{reach}}$ and $d_{\text{followed}}$ as used in the Tangent Bug algorithm. Why is the latter essential for overcoming local minima?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **$d_{\text{reach}}$:** The Euclidean distance from the *current boundary point* $q_i$ to the goal — represents the cost of *abandoning* the obstacle boundary immediately.
- **$d_{\text{followed}}$:** The minimum sensor reading along the current tangent direction $d_i$ — represents the *shortest guaranteed forward progress* before the next decision update.

**Why essential for local minima:**
By *continuously* re-evaluating $d_{\text{reach}}$ at every boundary point (not only at M-line crossings as in Bug2), the Tangent Bug detects "escape opportunities" the moment the goal becomes geometrically nearer than the next sensed obstacle. This breaks Bug2's tendency to over-traverse concave obstacle indentations.

*(Award 1 mark each for definitions; 1 mark for the local-minima explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** the leave-condition direction. Many students write $d_{\text{reach}} \geq d_{\text{followed}}$ — this is the *opposite* and will cost 2–4 marks.
> 2. **Always draw** a labelled workspace sketch with hit-points $H$, leave-points $L$, and tangent vectors $d_i$. Sketches without labels get **0 marks** for diagrams.
> 3. **Specify units** (metres / radians) in every numerical answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 4. **Mention the sensor assumption** (range sensor, 180° FOV) explicitly when defining $d_{\text{followed}}$ — omitting it costs 1 mark.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the complete architecture of the Tangent Bug algorithm. Clearly identify the perception, decision, and action subsystems. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A point robot starts at $q_s = (0, 0)$ and must reach $q_g = (10, 0)$. It encounters a circular obstacle of radius $2$ m centred at $(5, 0)$. At the first hit-point $q_h = (3, 0)$, the tangent direction is chosen as $d_1 = (0, 1)$. Compute:
- (i) $d_{\text{reach}}(q_h, q_g)$
- (ii) If the sensor reading along $d_1$ at $q_h$ is $\rho = 1.5$ m, compute $d_{\text{followed}}$ and decide whether the robot should leave immediately. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question A — Model Solution

**(a) Architecture Block Diagram (7 marks):**

The Tangent Bug architecture decomposes into three coupled subsystems:

| Subsystem | Components | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Range Sensor, Pose Estimator, Occupancy Buffer | Provides $\rho(\theta)$ and current pose $q$ |
| **Decision Layer** | Mode Selector FSM, Reach Calculator, Followed Calculator, Leave-Condition Evaluator | Determines MTG/BF state and transition |
| **Action Layer** | MTG Controller, BF Controller, Motor Driver | Generates velocity commands $v, \omega$ |

**Inter-subsystem data flow:**

```
Sensor readings rho(theta)  →  Decision Layer
Current pose q              →  Decision Layer
Decision (mode, d_i)        →  Action Layer
Action commands (v, omega)  →  Motors
```

**The decision layer executes:**
1. If mode = MTG: drive toward $q_g$ until sensor detects obstacle on segment.
2. If mode = BF: compute $d_i = \arg\max \rho(\theta)$; move along $t_i = R_{\pi/2} n_i$.
3. Continuous evaluation of $d_{\text{reach}} \leq d_{\text{followed}}$.

*(Award 3 marks for diagram; 2 marks for subsystem roles; 2 marks for decision logic.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Reach distance:**

The robot at $q_h = (3, 0)$ aims for $q_g = (10, 0)$.

$$
d_{\text{reach}}(q_h, q_g) \;=\; \sqrt{(10 - 3)^2 + (0 - 0)^2} \;=\; \sqrt{49} \;=\; 7 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Followed distance and decision:**

Sensor reading along tangent direction $d_1 = (0, 1)$ is $\rho = 1.5$ m. Therefore:

$$
d_{\text{followed}} \;=\; \rho \;=\; 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
d_{\text{reach}} \;=\; 7 \text{ m} \;\gtr\; d_{\text{followed}} \;=\; 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

The leave-condition $d_{\text{reach}} \leq d_{\text{followed}}$ evaluates to **FALSE**.

**Conclusion:** The robot must **continue** in Boundary-Following mode along $d_1$. It will re-evaluate at each subsequent boundary point $q_i$.

*(Award 2 marks for each sub-calculation; 1 mark for the comparison; 2 marks for the correct conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the leave-condition heuristic $d_{\text{reach}} \leq d_{\text{followed}}$ from first principles. State clearly the cost functions being compared. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast Bug1, Bug2, and Tangent Bug algorithms using a parameterised performance matrix. In a workspace with 3 obstacles of perimeters $p_1, p_2, p_3$, compute the worst-case path length bound for each algorithm if the direct start-to-goal distance is $d_{\text{init}}$. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B — Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Leave-Condition (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define cost functions.**
- Cost of leaving: $C_L = d_{\text{reach}} = \vert q_i - q_{\text{goal}} \vert$
- Cost of following: $C_F \geq d_{\text{followed}} = \min_{\theta} \rho(q_i, \theta)$

**Step 2 — Local optimality principle.**
A greedy optimal local decision minimises the immediate cost. Hence:

$$
\text{Leave} \;\;\iff\;\; C_L \leq C_F
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the bounds.**

$$
d_{\text{reach}}(q_i, q_{\text{goal}}) \;\leq\; d_{\text{followed}}(q_i, d_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Geometric interpretation.**
$d_{\text{reach}}$ is the Euclidean chord to the goal; $d_{\text{followed}}$ is the minimum arc-length to next obstacle along the tangent. When the chord is shorter, the robot is "behind" the optimal escape point.

*(Award 2 marks each for Steps 1–3; 1 mark for Step 4 interpretation.)*

**(b) Performance Matrix & Path Bounds (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Bug1 | Bug2 | Tangent Bug |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor | Contact | Contact | Range |
