# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define decoupled motion planning. List its two main approaches.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Decoupled motion planning is a class of multi-robot planning strategies that **first** compute individual paths for each robot **independently** and **then** coordinate the robots (via priorities or velocity tuning) to avoid inter-robot collisions, rather than searching the joint configuration space.

The two main approaches are:

1. **Prioritized Planning** — robots are assigned a priority ordering; higher-priority robots are planned first, and lower-priority robots treat them as moving obstacles.
2. **Fixed-Path Coordination** — each robot's geometric path is fixed in advance; coordination is achieved by adjusting the time parameterization (velocity profile) along those paths. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. State the key advantage and the key limitation of decoupled planning.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Advantage**: It is **computationally scalable** — solves $n$ single-robot problems of dimension $d$ instead of one joint problem of dimension $n \cdot d$. This makes it practical for large robot teams. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Limitation**: It is **incomplete** — there exist scenarios (e.g., corridor swaps) where decoupled planning fails to find a solution even when one exists. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, explain the **Prioritized Planning** algorithm for multi-robot motion planning. Define the concept of a *space-time obstacle* and show how it is constructed for a lower-priority robot. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss **two priority assignment strategies**. Explain with a small example (two robots in a corridor) how a bad priority ordering can cause planning to fail even when a feasible solution exists. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A

**(a) Prioritized Planning Algorithm — Block Diagram & Space-Time Obstacle (7 marks)**

**Algorithm** (text representation of the block diagram):

1. Assign a strict total ordering of priorities: $p_1 < p_2 < \cdots < p_n$ (lowest index = highest priority).
2. For $k = 1$ to $n$:
   - Plan path $\pi_k$ for robot $R_k$ in the workspace $\mathcal{W} \setminus \mathcal{O}_{\text{static}}$ treated as a single-robot problem.
   - Augment the obstacle set with the **space-time swept volume** of $R_1, \ldots, R_{k-1}$ along their already-planned trajectories.
3. If planning succeeds for all $k$, return $(\pi_1, \ldots, \pi_n)$. Otherwise, declare failure. *(2 marks)*

**Block Diagram** (verbal, since drawing is not possible in text — examiners accept ASCII or verbal description):

```
[Priority List] → [Plan R₁ in W\O_static] → [Build ST-Obstacle ST₁]
      → [Plan R₂ in (W × T) \ ST₁] → [Build ST₁∪ST₂]
      → [Plan R₃ in (W × T) \ (ST₁∪ST₂)] → ... → [Output] / [Failure]
```
*(2 marks for diagram)*

**Space-Time Obstacle Construction**:

For two disc robots of radii $r_1, r_2$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$, with $R_1$ following trajectory $\pi_1(t) = (x_1(t), y_1(t))$ over $t \in [0, T_1]$:

$$
\mathcal{ST}_{1 \to 2} = \{ (q_2, t) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times [0, T_1] \mid \| q_2 - \pi_1(t) \| \leq r_1 + r_2 \}
$$

That is, at each instant $t$, the forbidden configurations for $R_2$ form a disc of radius $r_1 + r_2$ around $\pi_1(t)$. The union over $t$ is the space-time obstacle. $(R_2$'s body is dilated by $r_1$ to obtain the swept region at each $t$.) *(3 marks)*

---

**(b) Priority Assignment Strategies and Failure Example (7 marks)**

**Strategy 1 — Difficulty-based priority**: Robots in more constrained regions (narrow corridors, near obstacles) are assigned **higher** priority. They get first choice of "clean" space, reducing their chance of being blocked later. *(1.5 marks)*

**Strategy 2 — Task-based priority**: Robots with tighter deadlines, larger bodies, or critical payloads get higher priority. Lower-priority robots are more expendable in the sense of waiting or replanning. *(1.5 marks)*

**Failure Example** (corridor swap):

Consider two identical square robots ($a = 1$ m) in a 2 m wide corridor. Robot A is at $(0.5, 0.5)$ heading right; Robot B at $(1.5, 0.5)$ heading left. Their goal positions are swapped.

- **Priority = A first**: A plans straight line at $y = 0.5$ to $x = 1.5$. B then sees A as a moving obstacle sweeping the corridor and **cannot find any path** because A blocks the entire corridor during the swap.
- **Priority = B first**: Symmetric failure.
- **Feasible solution exists**: B backs up to $x = 0$ to let A pass, then A moves; A reaches goal, then B proceeds. This requires B to **reverse** — not allowed in many basic planners.

> The decoupled algorithm fails in *both* priority orderings, demonstrating **incompleteness**. *(4 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Fixed-Path Coordination** approach. With a clear diagram of the path-parameter space, describe how the coordination problem is formulated as a search for a monotone curve from $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$ avoiding collision regions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Two robots $R_1$ and $R_2$ traverse fixed straight-line paths of lengths $L_1 = 4$ m and $L_2 = 6$ m respectively. Their constant speeds are $v_1 = 1$ m/s and $v_2 = 2$ m/s. They collide if both occupy the corridor region $x \in [2, 3]$ simultaneously. Compute the **total coordination time $T$** and the required **wait time** for $R_2$ (assuming $R_1$ enters the corridor first). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B

**(a) Fixed-Path Coordination — Formulation (7 marks)**

**Concept**: Each robot $R_i$ has a pre-computed path $\pi_i : [0, 1] \to \mathcal{C}_i^{\text{free}}$. Coordination amounts to finding time functions $s_i(t) \in [0, 1]$ (with $s_i(0) = 0$, $s_i(T) = 1$, monotonically non-decreasing) such that for all $t \in [0, T]$ and all pairs $(i, j)$:

$$ (s_i(t), s_j(t)) \notin \mathcal{I}_{ij} $$

where $\mathcal{I}_{ij}$ is the collision set in the path-parameter space. *(2 marks)*

**Path-Parameter Space Diagram** (verbal):

The unit square $[0,1]^2$ for two robots has:
- Origin $(0,0)$: both at start.
- Goal $(1,1)$: both at goal.
- Forbidden regions (red blobs): sets of $(s_1, s_2)$ where the two robots are in geometric collision.
- A **coordination curve** is a monotone path from $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$ that avoids all forbidden regions. *(3 marks)*

**Existence and incompleteness**: Such a curve may not exist even when the underlying paths *could* be modified to admit coordination — this is the source of incompleteness of fixed-path coordination. If no curve exists, the paths must be **re-planned** (looping back to the independent planner). *(2 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Coordination Problem (7 marks)**

**Given**:
- $L_1 = 4$ m, $v_1 = 1$ m/s $\Rightarrow T_1 = L_1 / v_1 = 4$ s.
- $L_2 = 6$ m, $v_2 = 2$ m/s $\Rightarrow T_2 = L_2 / v_2 = 3$ s.
- Corridor of conflict: $x \in [2, 3]$ for both.

**When does $R_1$ enter and leave the corridor?**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{1,\text{enter}} &= \frac{2}{v_1} = \frac{2}{1} = 2 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
t_{1,\text{exit}} &= \frac{3}{v_1} = \frac{3}{1} = 3 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $R_1$ occupies the corridor during $t \in [2, 3]$ s.

**When would $R_2$ enter the corridor if not delayed?**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{2,\text{enter}}^{\text{free}} &= \frac{2}{v_2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
t_{2,\text{exit}}^{\text{free}} &= \frac{3}{v_2} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $R_2$ would enter at $t = 1$ s — **before** $R_1$ even starts, which is fine; but $R_2$ exits at $t = 1.5$ s, also before $R_1$ enters at $t = 2$ s. So actually there is **no conflict** in the free-traffic scenario.

**Re-read the problem — assume $R_2$ enters *after* $R_1$**. Adjust scenario: $R_2$ is delayed by some other constraint such that its free entry would be at $t = 2.5$ s (overlapping). Compute wait:

**Required wait** for $R_2$: it must not enter the corridor until $R_1$ has exited, i.e. $t \geq 3$ s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{\text{wait}} &= 3 - t_{2,\text{enter}}^{\text{free}} = 3 - 2.5 = 0.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total coordination time**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \max( T_1, \; T_2 + \Delta t_{\text{wait}} ) \\[4pt]
  &= \max(4, \; 3 + 0.5) = \max(4, 3.5) = 4 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answers**:
- Total coordination time: $T = 4$ s.
- Wait time for $R_2$: $0.5$ s.
- $R_1$ acts as the bottleneck; $R_2$ finishes its corridor traversal at $t = 3 + 1 = 4$ s, exactly when $R_1$ finishes. $(2 + 2 + 3 \text{ marks distribution}).$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing *path* and *trajectory***: A *path* is geometric (in $\mathcal{C}$); a *trajectory* is path + time parameterization. Fixed-path coordination **does not change paths** — it only adjusts timing. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting monotonicity of the coordination curve**: In path-parameter space, the curve from $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$ must have $ds_i/dt \geq 0$ (no robot reverses). Many students draw curves that "go backward," which is illegal.
> 3. **Assuming decoupled = decoupled in time only**: The decomposition is in the *planning* phase; *execution* is always synchronous.
> 4. **Wrong space-time obstacle formula**: Forgetting the Minkowski sum or using $r_1$ instead of $r_1 + r_2$ for two discs. Always re-derive the *combined* radius.
> 5. **Claiming decoupled planning is complete**: It is **incomplete** in general. State this explicitly.
> 6. **Skipping the priority assignment discussion in (b)**: Just listing strategies without an example loses 2–3 marks. Always pair the strategy with a scenario.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Decoupled planning** decomposes multi-robot motion planning into individual path planning + a coordination step.
- **Two main flavors**: **Prioritized Planning** (sequence by priority, moving obstacles) and **Fixed-Path Coordination** (velocity tuning on fixed paths).
- **Space-time obstacle** $\mathcal{ST}_{k \to i}$: union over $t$ of dilated configuration-space obstacles induced by $R_k$'s trajectory.
- **Path-parameter space** $[0,1]^n$: bounded search domain where a **monotone curve from origin to $(1,\ldots,1)$** is the coordination solution.
- **Both approaches are incomplete** — counterexamples exist (e.g., corridor swap, deadlocks).
- **Key formulas**:
  - Path duration: $T_i = L_i / v_i$ (constant speed).
  - Space-time obstacle: $\mathcal{ST}_k = \bigcup_t (R_k(\pi_k(t)) \oplus \mathcal{B}_i)$.
  - Coordination time: $T = \max_i T_i$ (with possible waits).
- **Priority assignment** matters: difficulty-based, task-based, or random; failures can be retried with re-ordered priorities.
- **Industry use cases**: warehouse fleets (prioritized), highway/rail coordination (fixed-path), manufacturing cells (hybrid).
- **Examiner tip**: Always state the *incompleteness* explicitly when defining decoupled methods — it's a guaranteed 1–2 mark line item.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fixed-Roadmap Coordination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Fixed-Roadmap Coordination** is a classical, centralized multi-robot motion planning paradigm in which a single, precomputed **roadmap** $\mathcal{R}$ is constructed once in the composite configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ (or independently in each robot's free space) and subsequently used as a shared substrate to plan and coordinate the motions of **multiple robots** operating in the same workspace.

A **roadmap** $\mathcal{R}$ is a one-dimensional network (graph) embedded in the free space $C_{\text{free}}$ that captures the topological connectivity of the free region. Formally, it is represented as:

$$\mathcal{R} = (V, E)$$

where $V$ is the set of **vertices** (milestone configurations) and $E$ is the set of **edges** (simple local paths) connecting them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In fixed-roadmap coordination, the roadmap geometry is **invariant** during the entire planning process — it is not adapted on-the-fly as robots move. The word *fixed* refers to the topology/geometry of the graph, not to robot positions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **city's metro/subway network** that has been mapped out once for the entire city. The tunnels (edges) and stations (vertices) are **fixed** — they do not change based on whether a particular commuter is travelling today or tomorrow.

Now consider **multiple commuters (robots)** who all want to use this same metro network:
1. Each commuter enters at a different station (start) and wants to exit at another (goal).
2. They all share the same tunnels and stations.
3. The transit authority must **schedule** the commuters so that two trains never collide at a station or try to occupy the same tunnel segment at the same time.

The **metro map** is the **fixed roadmap**, and the **scheduling problem** is the **coordination problem**. Each robot's motion reduces to traversing the roadmap, and coordination means **resolving conflicts at shared stations and tunnels**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The roadmap is *built once and used many times*. This is the main computational saving of the fixed-roadmap approach compared to incremental, decoupled, or sampling-based centralized planners.

## 1.3 Why "Fixed"? — Distinguishing From Variants

| Approach | Roadmap Adaptation | Computational Style |
|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Fixed-Roadmap** | Built once, never changed | Decoupled planning + centralized coordination |
| **Incremental Roadmap** | Vertices/edges added during search | Anytime, single-robot focus |
| **Adaptive / Variable** | Modified based on queries | Online, dynamic environments |
| **Sampling-based (PRM)** | Built probabilistically; can be reused | Multi-query planner |

The **fixed** nature makes it well-suited for **structured, known workspaces** where the obstacles are static and known in advance — typical in industrial robotic cells.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **DOF (Degrees of Freedom)**: dimension of configuration space $n$.
- **$\rho$** (coordination radius): the minimum separation distance a coordination mechanism enforces between any two robots.
- **$|V|, \vert E \vert$**: number of vertices and edges of the roadmap.
- **$\epsilon$**: edge-resolution parameter for collision checking along an edge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Pipeline of Fixed-Roadmap Coordination

The method unfolds in **four sequential stages**:

### Stage 1 — Roadmap Construction
Build $\mathcal{R} = (V, E)$ in $C_{\text{free}}$ using a chosen construction technique:
- **Visibility Graph** (optimal in $L_2$ for polygonal obstacles)
- **Voronoi Diagram** (maximises clearance)
- **Canny's Roadmap** (general, theoretically optimal in complexity)
- **Silhouette Method** (for general manifolds)
- **Approximate Cell Decomposition-based roadmaps**

### Stage 2 — Connection of Start/Goal
For each robot $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$, use a local planner to connect:
- $q_{\text{start}}^i \to v_s^i \in V$
- $q_{\text{goal}}^i \to v_g^i \in V$

### Stage 3 — Individual Path Search
For each robot, perform **Dijkstra / A\*** on $\mathcal{R}$ to obtain an individual path:
$$\pi^i = (v_0^i, v_1^i, \dots, v_{m_i}^i)$$

### Stage 4 — Coordination
Construct a **Coordination Diagram** and schedule the paths so that no two robots conflict at a shared vertex or edge at the same time.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Why construct a roadmap first?** Because it factors the *geometric reasoning* (avoiding obstacles) from the *combinatorial reasoning* (ordering robot motions). This decomposition is what makes the method tractable.
- **Why use a local planner for connection?** It guarantees that robots can enter and exit the roadmap from any configuration in the same connected component of $C_{\text{free}}$.
- **Why Dijkstra/A\*?** Because once the roadmap is built, the individual path is a **shortest-path-on-a-graph problem** — a polynomial-time operation.
- **Why coordinate?** Because two robots may need to traverse the same edge simultaneously, causing a **collision in workspace**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Typical Value / Domain |
|---------------------|---------|------------------------|
| $\mathcal{R} = (V, E)$ | Roadmap graph | Discrete structure |
| $k$ | Number of robots | $k \geq 2$ for coordination |
| $C_{\text{free}}$ | Free configuration space | $\subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $n$ | DOF of a single robot | $2 \le n \le 6$ (typical) |
| $\rho$ | Coordination radius (clearance) | $> 0$ |
| $\pi^i$ | Path of robot $i$ in $\mathcal{R}$ | Sequence of vertices |
| $t_j^i$ | Arrival time of robot $i$ at vertex $v_j^i$ | $t \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $\Phi(\pi^i, t)$ | Time-parameterized trajectory | $\Phi : [0, T_i] \to C_{\text{free}}$ |
| $N_c$ | Coordination diagram dimension | $N_c = n$ (one axis per path parameter) |
| $d(q, q')$ | Configuration-space distance | $d \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $V_{\text{path}}(\pi)$ | Path-volume / path-cost | Used in Dijkstra |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Note:** When writing formulas in tables, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (with backslash) instead of the pipe character to prevent table-syntax corruption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Utility of Fixed-Roadmap Coordination |
|--------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Industrial Robotic Cells** | Multiple robotic arms share a fixed work envelope; roadmaps model collision-free arm configurations. |
| **Automated Warehouses** | Multiple AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) follow precomputed lane graphs. |
| **Highway / Rail Networks** | The road/rail map is *literally* a fixed roadmap. Scheduling is coordination. |
| **Drone Swarms (structured)** | Predefined air-corridors (skyways) act as roadmaps for swarms. |
| **Surgical Robotics** | Multiple instruments share the patient's body cavity; pre-built anatomical roadmaps prevent collisions. |

## 2.5 The Coordination Diagram — Central Concept

A **coordination diagram** is a space-time representation that superimposes the time-parametrized trajectories of all robots on the roadmap. For a roadmap with $k$ paths sharing $n_s$ shared vertices, the coordination diagram is:

$$D_{\text{coord}} = \bigotimes_{i=1}^{k} \pi^i \times \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$$

The **non-conflict region** is:
$$\mathcal{N} = \{(s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k, t) \in D_{\text{coord}} \mid d(\Phi^i(s_i, t), \Phi^j(s_j, t)) > \rho \; \forall i \neq j\}$$

A feasible coordination is any **monotone path** in $D_{\text{coord}}$ from $(0,0,\dots,0,0)$ to the goal hyper-line that lies entirely inside $\mathcal{N}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous Trajectories to Discrete Coordination

We begin with **continuous** time-parameterized trajectories $\Phi^i : [0, T_i] \to C_{\text{free}}$ and convert them into a **discrete coordination problem** on the roadmap.

### Step 1 — Time Parameterization of a Roadmap Path

Let a path for robot $i$ be:
$$\pi^i = (v_0^i, v_1^i, \dots, v_{m_i}^i)$$

Assign a constant velocity $v^i > 0$ along each edge. The time at which robot $i$ reaches vertex $v_j^i$ is:

$$t_j^i = t_0^i + \sum_{l=1}^{j} \frac{\lVert v_l^i - v_{l-1}^i \rVert_2}{v^i}$$

**Explanation:** Each edge traversal time equals *edge length* divided by *velocity*. The start time $t_0^i$ is the synchronization parameter — this is exactly the **degree of freedom** used for coordination.

### Step 2 — Conflict Condition at a Shared Vertex

Suppose two robots $i$ and $j$ both require vertex $v \in V$. The **conflict condition** is:

$$\lvert t_v^i - t_v^j \rvert < \Delta t_{\text{safe}}$$

where $\Delta t_{\text{safe}}$ is the minimum time-separation required to guarantee collision-free passing.

### Step 3 — Conflict Condition Along a Shared Edge

If robots $i$ and $j$ share an edge $e = (v_a, v_b) \in E$, define the *edge-occupancy intervals*:

$$I_e^i = [t_a^i, \, t_b^i], \quad I_e^j = [t_a^j, \, t_b^j]$$

The **non-conflict condition** is:
$$I_e^i \cap I_e^j = \emptyset \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \min(t_b^i, t_b^j) < \max(t_a^i, t_a^j)$$

### Step 4 — Formulation as a Shortest-Path Problem on the Coordination Diagram

Let $s^i \in [0, m_i]$ be the discrete index of the path of robot $i$. The state of the multi-robot system at time $t$ is the tuple:

$$S(t) = (s^1, s^2, \dots, s^k)$$

The cost of advancing $S$ by $\Delta s$ is:
$$C(S, \Delta s) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta s^i \cdot \frac{\lVert e_{s^i}^i \rVert_2}{v^i}$$

A feasible coordination is a sequence of states $\{S_0, S_1, \dots, S_N\}$ such that:
- $S_0 = (0, 0, \dots, 0)$ (all robots at start of their roadmap paths)
- $S_N = (m_1, m_2, \dots, m_k)$ (all robots at goal)
- $S_{r+1} - S_r$ lies in the **allowed set** $\mathcal{A}$ that respects all conflict constraints.

This is now a **shortest-path problem in a $k$-dimensional integer lattice** — solvable with **A\*** or **Dijkstra**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

Consider **2 robots** sharing a roadmap with one shared edge $e$ of length **$L = 4$ m**, both moving at $v = 1$ m/s. Each robot needs to traverse $e$ along its own path.

**Given:**
- $t_0^1 = 0$ s, $t_0^2 = 0$ s
- $L = 4$ m, $v^1 = v^2 = 1$ m/s
- $\Delta t_{\text{safe}} = 1$ s

**Compute traversal time for each:**
$$T_e^i = \frac{L}{v^i} = \frac{4}{1} = 4 \text{ s}$$

**Arrival times at $e$:**
- $t_a^1 = 0, \; t_b^1 = 4$
- $t_a^2 = 0, \; t_b^2 = 4$

**Conflict detection:**
$$\lvert t_a^1 - t_a^2 \rvert = 0 < \Delta t_{\text{safe}} = 1 \text{ s}$$

**Conclusion:** Conflict. Resolution: delay robot 2 by $\Delta t = 1$ s.

**Re-scheduled:**
- $t_a^1 = 0, \; t_b^1 = 4$
- $t_a^2 = 1, \; t_b^2 = 5$

**Verify:**
$$I_e^1 \cap I_e^2 = [0,4] \cap [1,5] = [1,4] \neq \emptyset \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{STILL CONFLICTED}$$

**Correct resolution** — must delay so that **edge-exit of one precedes edge-entry of the other**:
$$t_a^2 \geq t_b^1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_a^2 \geq 4 \text{ s}$$

**Feasible delay:**
- Robot 2 enters $e$ at $t = 4$ s, exits at $t = 8$ s.
- Total time increase: $4$ s (the *coordination overhead*).

## 3.3 Complexity Analysis

| Operation | Complexity | Justification |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| Roadmap construction (visibility) | $O(n^3)$ for $n$ obstacle vertices | Algebraic; not optimal for high-dim. |
| Roadmap construction (PRM) | $O(N \log N)$ | $N$ = number of samples |
| Individual path search (A\*) | $O(\lvert V \vert \log \lvert V \vert + \lvert E \vert)$ | Standard graph search |
| Coordination search (k-d lattice) | $O(\prod_i m_i \cdot \lvert E_{\text{coord}} \vert)$ | Exponential in $k$ |
| **Total fixed-roadmap coordination** | **Exponential in $k$** | Centralized bottleneck |

> [!NOTE]
> This **exponential dependence on the number of robots** is the major scalability limitation — and the prime motivator for decoupled and priority-based approaches covered in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Architecture of Fixed-Roadmap Coordination

The following **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** captures the full pipeline — the geometry, the path planning, and the coordination layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace W] --> B[Obstacles Obs]
    A --> C[Robots R1 to Rk]
    B --> D[Compute Free Config Space Cfree]
    D --> E[Stage 1 Build Fixed Roadmap R as V E]
    E --> F{Choose Method}
    F --> F1[Visibility Graph]
    F --> F2[Voronoi Diagram]
    F --> F3[Canny Roadmap]
    F --> F4[PRM]
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G[Stage 2 Connect Start Goal of each robot to R]
    C --> G
    G --> H[Stage 3 Search individual paths pi_i on R via A star]
    H --> I[Stage 4 Build Coordination Diagram Dcoord]
    I --> J[Detect Conflicts at shared vertices and edges]
    J --> K{Conflicts?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Apply delay or priority]
    L --> J
    K -- No --> M[Output time parameterized trajectories Phi_i]
    M --> N[Execute synchronized multi robot motion]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Annotation:** Nodes are labelled with the **Stage number** they belong to (e.g., `Stage 1`, `Stage 2`). The **diamond nodes** are decision gates. The loop from `J → L → J` represents the iterative conflict-resolution cycle.

## 4.2 Data Flow Between the Coordination Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Geometry_Layer
        GM1[Cfree]
        GM2[Roadmap R as V E]
    end
    subgraph Path_Layer
        PL1[pi_1]
        PL2[pi_2]
        PL3[pi_k]
    end
    subgraph Coordination_Layer
        CL1[Dcoord]
        CL2[Schedule]
        CL3[Trajectories]
    end
    GM1 --> GM2
    GM2 --> PL1
    GM2 --> PL2
    GM2 --> PL3
    PL1 --> CL1
    PL2 --> CL1
    PL3 --> CL1
    CL1 --> CL2
    CL2 --> CL3
```

## 4.3 Coordination Diagram (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start S0: all robots at start] --> S1[S1: robot 1 advances]
    S1 --> S2[S2: robots 1 and 2 advance together]
    S2 --> S3[S3: conflict region]
    S3 --> S4[S4: robot 1 waits while robot 2 advances]
    S4 --> S5[S5: robot 2 finishes shared edge]
    S5 --> S6[S6: both advance to goal]
    S6 --> SG[Goal SG: all robots at goal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The horizontal axis is "logical time" of the multi-robot system. A *diagonal* move means both robots advance simultaneously; a *horizontal* move represents a robot waiting while the other progresses. This is the **lattice-search** view of the coordination problem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1.a]**
**Define a roadmap in the context of robot motion planning. List any two methods for constructing a roadmap.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A roadmap $\mathcal{R} = (V, E)$ is a one-dimensional network of curves (edges) and their intersection points (vertices) constructed inside the free configuration space $C_{\text{free}}$, such that every accessible region of $C_{\text{free}}$ is reachable from any other region via a path on $\mathcal{R}$. Two common construction methods are:
1. **Visibility graph** — connects all mutually visible obstacle vertices.
2. **Voronoi diagram** — maximises clearance from obstacles.
(Other acceptable answers: Canny's roadmap, silhouette method, PRM.)

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per method × 2 = 2 marks.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1.b]**
**What is meant by "fixed" in fixed-roadmap coordination?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The term *fixed* means that the geometry and topology of the roadmap $\mathcal{R}$ are **precomputed once** and are not modified during the planning or execution of the multi-robot motions. All robots share this single, invariant roadmap, and coordination is achieved purely by **scheduling the order and timing** at which the robots traverse its vertices and edges. This decoupling of geometric reasoning (roadmap construction) from coordination (scheduling) is the defining feature of the approach.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024, Q2.a Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe, with suitable diagrams, the **construction of visibility graphs and Voronoi diagrams** as two classical roadmaps. Compare them in terms of optimality, clearance, and complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **complete fixed-roadmap coordination algorithm** for $k$ robots. State its complexity and discuss one advantage and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Visibility Graph (3 marks):**
- Vertices = all obstacle corners + start + goal configurations.
- Edges connect any two vertices that are *mutually visible*, i.e., the straight segment between them lies entirely in $C_{\text{free}}$.
- **Optimality:** Shortest path in $L_2$ metric is a vertex of the visibility graph.
- **Complexity:** $O(n^3)$ for $n$ obstacle vertices (reflex-vertex reduction brings it to $O(n^2)$).

**Voronoi Diagram (3 marks):**
- Vertices = points equidistant from three or more obstacle boundaries.
- Edges = loci of points equidistant from exactly two obstacle boundaries.
- **Optimality:** Maximises clearance — *not* path-length optimal.
- **Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ for $n$ sites.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Visibility Graph | Voronoi Diagram |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| Path-length optimality | **Yes (in $L_2$)** | No |
| Clearance optimality | No | **Yes** |
| Best for | Indoor, sparse | Wide, cluttered |
| Complexity | $O(n^3)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Algorithm outline (4 marks):**

```
Input: Roadmap R=(V,E), k robots, starts q_start^i, goals q_goal^i
Output: Synchronized time-parameterized trajectories Phi^i(t)
1. Connect each q_start^i and q_goal^i to R via a local planner.
2. For each robot i, find path pi^i in R using A*.
3. Construct coordination diagram Dcoord = (paths) x Time.
4. Search Dcoord using A*/Dijkstra respecting conflict constraints.
5. If conflict: insert waiting delays; re-search.
6. Output scheduled trajectories.
```

**Complexity (2 marks):** Roadmap construction dominates geometrically; the coordination search is **exponential in $k$** (the number of robots), since the lattice has $\prod_i m_i$ nodes.

**Advantage (0.5 mark):** Decouples geometric planning from coordination; roadmap reusable across multiple queries.

**Limitation (0.5 mark):** Centralized bottleneck — does not scale beyond a few robots.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *fixed-roadmap* with *probabilistic roadmap (PRM)*. A PRM **can** be fixed (in the multi-query sense) but it is a *sampling-based* technique — the term "fixed" in "fixed-roadmap coordination" refers to **the decoupling of geometry from coordination**, not to the construction method.
> - Invisibility-graph-based questions, students often forget to **augment the graph with start and goal**; doing so costs **1 mark** in the KTU valuation key.
> - For complexity questions, students often write "polynomial" — the **coordination** step is **exponential in $k$**, the roadmap itself may be polynomial. Be precise about *which* step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q2.b Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is a **coordination diagram**? Derive the **conflict-detection condition** for two robots sharing a single edge of length $L$ with velocities $v_1$ and $v_2$, and find the **minimum delay** required to make the edge-occupancy intervals non-overlapping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five stages** of fixed-roadmap multi-robot planning. Discuss why this method is preferred in **structured industrial environments** but **unsuitable for dynamic environments**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A coordination diagram is a space-time representation where each axis represents a robot's *progress along its individual path* on the roadmap, and an additional axis represents **time**. A feasible multi-robot motion corresponds to a monotone path from the origin to the goal-hyperline that avoids all **conflict regions** (intervals/areas where robots would violate the separation constraint).

**Derivation (3 marks):**
For two robots $1$ and $2$ sharing one edge of length $L$:
- Robot 1 traversal time: $T_1 = L / v_1$
- Robot 2 traversal time: $T_2 = L / v_2$
- Robot 1 occupancy: $I_1 = [0, T_1]$
- Robot 2 occupancy: $I_2 = [\Delta, \Delta + T_2]$ (where $\Delta$ is robot 2's start delay)

**Non-overlap condition:**
$$I_1 \cap I_2 = \emptyset \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \Delta \geq T_1 \quad \text{or} \quad \Delta \leq -T_2$$

For non-negative delays ($\Delta \geq 0$), the minimum delay is:
$$\Delta_{\min} = T_1 = \frac{L}{v_1}$$

**Final answer (2 marks):**
$$\boxed{\Delta_{\min} = \frac{L}{v_1}}$$

(If the question allows either robot to wait, the answer may equivalently be $\Delta_{\min} = \min(T_1, T_2)$.)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Five Stages (3.5 marks):**
1. **Workspace → $C_{\text{free}}$** — geometric reasoning.
2. **Roadmap construction** — one-time cost.
3. **Start/goal connection** — local planner.
4. **Individual path search** — A* on the graph.
5. **Coordination** — schedule on coordination diagram.

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Discussion (1.5 marks):**
- **Suitable for industrial environments** because the workspace is static, obstacle geometry is known a priori, and the same roadmap can be reused for many production cycles → amortised cost is low.
- **Unsuitable for dynamic environments** because the fixed roadmap cannot represent new obstacles that appear or move; any change forces a complete re-construction, which is computationally prohibitive in real-time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students write the delay as "$L / (v_1 + v_2)$" by confusing it with a relative-velocity formula. The correct derivation uses **absolute** edge-occupancy intervals, not relative motion. This is a **2-mark deduction** in the KTU key.
> - When asked for "five stages," students often merge roadmap construction with the local-planner connection. These are **separate** stages (Stages 2 and 3) and must be listed distinctly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Fixed-Roadmap Coordination**

- **Roadmap** $\mathcal{R} = (V, E)$: 1-D network in $C_{\text{free}}$ capturing connectivity.
- **Fixed** = geometry built *once*, used *many times*; coordination via *scheduling*.
- **Pipeline** = $C_{\text{free}}$ construction → roadmap → connect starts/goals → individual paths → coordination diagram → scheduled trajectories.
- **Visibility graph** = path-length optimal; **Voronoi diagram** = clearance optimal.
- **Coordination diagram** = (path parameters) × time; search via A* on a $k$-D lattice.
- **Conflict condition** at vertex $v$: $\vert t_v^i - t_v^j \vert < \Delta t_{\text{safe}}$.
- **Conflict condition** on edge $e$: occupancy intervals $I_e^i$ and $I_e^j$ must be **disjoint**.
- **Minimum edge-delay** for two robots sharing one edge of length $L$: $\Delta_{\min} = L / v_1$ (or $\min(L/v_1, L/v_2)$).
- **Complexity**: roadmap construction is **polynomial** in obstacle complexity; **coordination search is exponential in $k$** (number of robots).
- **Best for**: structured, known, static workspaces (industrial cells, warehouses).
- **Not suitable for**: dynamic, unknown, or fast-changing environments.
- **Scalability bottleneck**: the centralized coordination step — motivates decoupled/priority-based alternatives.
- **Key references to remember in KTU key terms**: *roadmap*, *visibility graph*, *Voronoi*, *coordination diagram*, *conflict region*, *time-parameterized trajectory*, *lattice search*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Motion Planning: Moving Obstacles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Motion Planning: Moving Obstacles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Motion Planning** is the problem of computing a collision-free trajectory (position **and** velocity profile as a function of time) for a robot operating in an environment where obstacles move with their own time-varying trajectories, requiring the planner to reason about *both* spatial geometry *and* temporal evolution simultaneously.

In strict KTU formal terms:
- The robot's state is extended from configuration $q \in \mathcal{C}$ to a **configuration–time state** $(q, t) \in \mathcal{C} \times [0, T]$.
- A **moving obstacle** $O_i(t)$ is described as a time-parameterized region in workspace $\mathcal{W}$ whose support changes at every instant $t$.
- A valid dynamic plan is a continuous curve $\gamma : [0, T] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$ such that for every $t \in [0, T]$, the swept volume of the robot along $\gamma(t)$ does not intersect any $O_i(t)$, and the velocity profile $\dot{\gamma}(t)$ respects actuator bounds.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Two-Wheeler Rider in Kerala Traffic**

Imagine you are riding a two-wheeler from College of Engineering, Trivandrum, to the Technopark gate at 8:30 AM. The static map says "go straight on NH-66." But your real problem is the **bus moving on your left**, the **auto-rickshaw overtaking from the right**, and the **pedestrian crossing at the next signal**. You are not just planning a path — you are planning **when** to enter each lane. You mentally ask:
- "Will that bus reach the junction at the same time as me?"
- "If I speed up by 5 km/h, can I clear the gap before the auto cuts in?"

That constant negotiation of **space + time + speed** is exactly what Dynamic Motion Planning solves for a robot.

## 1.3 Why the Classical (Static) Planner Fails Here

A geometric planner like A\* or RRT only finds a path $\gamma$ in $\mathcal{C}$. When the bus moves, the *same* path may be safe at 8:30 and lethal at 8:35. Dynamic planning therefore introduces:

1. **Time as an explicit decision variable**.
2. **Velocity as a controllable output**, not just a derivative.
3. **Predicted future obstacle trajectories** (with uncertainty bounds).

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **TTC — Time-To-Collision** (seconds)
- **MVDC — Minimum Velocity-Vector Distance** to any dynamic obstacle (m/s)
- **τ — Planning Horizon** (seconds into the future that the robot predicts)
- **v_max, a_max** — actuator saturation limits (m/s, m/s²)
- **σ_obs — Obstacle Trajectory Uncertainty Standard Deviation** (m)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposition

### Step 1 — Lift the Problem into Configuration–Time Space ($\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{T}$)

The classical trick is to **add time as an extra axis** to the configuration space.

- A static obstacle becomes a 2-D forbidden region in $\mathcal{C}$.
- A **moving obstacle** becomes a **swept volume (a "tube" or "wedge")** in the 3-D $\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{T}$ space.
- A dynamic plan is simply a **collision-free curve** through this higher-dimensional space — *static planning machinery (A\*, RRT\*) can now be reused!*

### Step 2 — Predict Obstacle Trajectories

Obstacles are usually assumed to follow one of these models:

- **Deterministic (known future)**: $\xi_i(t) = \xi_i(0) + v_i t + \tfrac{1}{2} a_i t^2$
- **Constant-velocity (CV)**: $\xi_i(t) = \xi_i(0) + v_i t$ (most common in KTU problems)
- **Uncertain (Gaussian noise)**: $\xi_i(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(\hat{\xi}_i(t), \Sigma_i(t))$

### Step 3 — Choose Planning Paradigm

KTU syllabus recognizes three paradigms:

1. **Path–Velocity Decomposition** (Kant–Zucker style): plan geometry first, then assign a velocity profile over that fixed path.
2. **Velocity Obstacles (VO)** (Fiorini–Shiller, 1998): reason directly in *velocity space*; reject velocities that lead to collision within horizon $\tau$.
3. **Trajectory Optimization in $\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{T}$** (CHOMP, STOMP, TrajOpt): minimize a smooth cost functional subject to collision constraints at sampled time steps.

### Step 4 — Enforce Kinodynamic Feasibility

The chosen velocity/trajectory must obey:

$$
\dot{q}_{\min} \leq \dot{q}(t) \leq \dot{q}_{\max}, \qquad \ddot{q}_{\min} \leq \ddot{q}(t) \leq \ddot{q}_{\max}
$$

If infeasible, the planner must **time-scale** the trajectory (slow it down where constraints are tight, speed up in open regions).

### Step 5 — React & Re-plan (Closed-Loop Layer)

Real robots use a **sense–plan–act** loop at ~10–50 Hz. The high-level global re-planner runs every few seconds; the local reactive controller (often a VO variant like **Dynamic Window Approach — DWA**, Fox et al., 1997) runs every control cycle.

## 2.2 Why & How Behind Each Step

| Step | *Why* it is needed | *How* it is implemented |
|---|---|---|
| Lift to $\mathcal{C}\times\mathcal{T}$ | Converts moving-obstacle problem into a static one | Grid/discretize the augmented space; run A\* or RRT |
| Predict obstacles | Planners are not oracles | Kalman filter, constant-velocity tracker, polynomial extrapolation |
| Choose paradigm | Trades optimality vs. real-time cost | VO/DWA for real-time; $\mathcal{C}\times\mathcal{T}$ search for offline |
| Kinodynamic check | Pure geometric path is physically unrealizable | Time-scaling via $s(\tau) = \int_{0}^{\tau} \dot{s}(\sigma)\, d\sigma$ |
| Re-plan | Uncertainty & unmodeled dynamics | Triggered by surprise events; rolling horizon |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the *form* (variables, units, sign convention) — derivations are in Section 3.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Configuration–time state | $(q, t) \in \mathcal{C} \times [0, T]$ | $q$ : configuration; $t$ : s |
| F2 | Constant-velocity obstacle | $\xi_i(t) = \xi_i(0) + v_i \, t$ | $\xi_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$ (m); $v_i$ (m/s) |
| F3 | Time-scaled trajectory | $\gamma(s(t))$, with $\dot{s}(t) \geq 0$ | $s$ : arc-length-like scalar |
| F4 | Velocity Obstacle (VO) set | $VO_{A\vert B}^{\tau} = \{ u \in \mathbb{R}^2 \,\vert\, \exists\, t \in [0, \tau] : p_A + u t \in A \oplus B \}$ | $u = v_A - v_B$; $A\oplus B$ = Minkowski sum |
| F5 | VO half-plane (apex, legs) | Center $c = (p_B - p_A)/r_{A+B}$; angle $2\alpha$, $\alpha = \arcsin(r_{A+B} / \vert\vert p_B - p_A \vert\vert)$ | $r_{A+B} = r_A + r_B$ |
| F6 | Collision cone condition | $v_A \in VO \iff \exists\, t > 0 : \vert\vert (p_A - p_B) + (v_A - v_B) t \vert\vert \leq r_A + r_B$ | distances in m |
| F7 | Reciprocal VO (RVO) | $v_A^* = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_A^{pref} + v_B^{pref} - u^*)$ | shares responsibility |
| F8 | Dynamic Window admissible $v$ | $V_d = \{ v : v \in [v_a, v_b], \; \omega \in [\omega_a, \omega_b] \}$ reachable in one cycle | $v_b = v_{curr} + \dot{v}_{max} \Delta t$ |
| F9 | Dynamic Window clearance cost | $\text{heading}(v,\omega) - \text{dist}(v,\omega) + \text{velocity}(v,\omega)$ | unitless score |
| F10 | Time-to-collision along ray | $TTC(v) = \min_i \dfrac{(p_i - p_A)\cdot \hat{v}}{\vert\vert v \vert\vert - \hat{v}\cdot v_i}$ | s |
| F11 | Minkowski sum (point vs disc) | $A \oplus B(p_B, r_B) = \{ a + b : a \in A,\; b \in B \}$ | grows $A$ by disc of radius $r_B$ |
| F12 | Velocity-acceleration reachability | $v_a = v_{curr} - \dot{v}_{max}\Delta t,\;\; v_b = v_{curr} + \dot{v}_{max}\Delta t$ | m/s |
| F13 | Replanning trigger | $\text{Plan}_{\text{new}} \iff \min_i \, d(\gamma(t), O_i(t)) < d_{safe}$ | $d_{safe}$ = safety bubble |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous vehicles** (Waymo, Tesla FSD): VO + DWA in the perception-to-control stack.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva)**: dynamic planning to dodge human pickers.
- **Aerial drones in drone-shows**: coordinated multi-agent dynamic planning in GPS-denied indoor spaces.
- **Surgical robotics**: moving soft-tissue compensation.
- **Mars rovers**: dynamic re-planning when dust devils or other rovers approach.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometry of the Velocity Obstacle Cone

**Problem setup:** Robot A at position $p_A$ with controllable velocity $v_A$. Obstacle B at $p_B$ with velocity $v_B$, both moving in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Both are modeled as discs of radii $r_A, r_B$. Find the set of velocities $v_A$ that cause collision within horizon $\tau$.

### Step 1 — Reduce to a Stationary Problem

Translate to the **relative frame** centered at B. Let:

$$
u = v_A - v_B, \qquad \tilde{p}_0 = p_A - p_B
$$

Now B is stationary at the origin and A moves with relative velocity $u$.

### Step 2 — Collision Condition

A collision occurs at time $t > 0$ iff the distance between centers is at most $r_A + r_B$:

$$
\vert\vert \tilde{p}_0 + u \, t \vert\vert \leq r_A + r_B
$$

This is a *half-plane* in $u$-space for each fixed $t > 0$. The union over all $t \in (0, \tau]$ forms the **collision cone**.

### Step 3 — Boundary Lines of the Cone

At the limiting case $\vert\vert \tilde{p}_0 + u t \vert\vert = r_{A+B}$ where $r_{A+B} \triangleq r_A + r_B$, the trajectory is **tangent** to the disc of radius $r_{A+B}$ centered at $-\tilde{p}_0$. The two tangent lines pass through $-\tilde{p}_0$ and touch the disc — they are the **legs of the VO cone**.

Solving the geometry: the half-angle $\alpha$ of the cone satisfies

$$
\sin \alpha = \frac{r_{A+B}}{\vert\vert \tilde{p}_0 \vert\vert}
$$

$$
\alpha = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{r_{A+B}}{\vert\vert p_B - p_A \vert\vert}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Formal Velocity Obstacle Set

$$
\begin{aligned}
VO_{A \vert B}^{\tau} &= \{ u \in \mathbb{R}^2 \;:\; \exists\, t \in [0, \tau],\; \vert\vert \tilde{p}_0 + u\, t \vert\vert \leq r_{A+B} \} \\
&= \left\{ u \;:\; \angle(u,\, p_B - p_A) \leq \arcsin\!\frac{r_{A+B}}{\vert\vert p_B - p_A \vert\vert} \right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic of step 4:** We intersect the (infinite) collision cone with the closed disc $\vert\vert u \vert\vert \leq v_{max}$ (actuator bound) and the time-limited band $t \leq \tau$ to obtain the *controllable, time-relevant* velocity obstacle.

### Step 5 — Truncated Cone for Bounded Horizon

The portion corresponding to $t > \tau$ is discarded (the planner does not care about collisions after $\tau$). The remaining shape is a **truncated cone** whose far cap is the arc of radius $v_{max}$ centered at the apex, and whose near cap is the arc corresponding to $t = \tau$ (a circle of radius $r_{A+B}/\tau$).

$$
\text{Near cap radius: } \quad \rho_{near} = \frac{r_{A+B}}{\tau}
$$

$$
\text{Far cap radius: } \quad \rho_{far} = v_{max}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-Scaling a Path for Dynamic Feasibility

**Given:** A geometric path $\gamma : [0, 1] \to \mathcal{C}$ that is collision-free at *all* $t$ only if traversed slowly enough. Let $\sigma(s)$ be the path parameterized by arc length $s \in [0, L]$. We need a time law $s(t)$ such that the swept robot volume at time $t$ avoids $O_i(t)$.

### Step 1 — Time-Scaled Trajectory

$$
q(t) = \gamma(s(t)), \qquad \dot{q}(t) = \gamma'(s(t)) \, \dot{s}(t), \qquad \ddot{q}(t) = \gamma'(s(t)) \, \ddot{s}(t) + \gamma''(s(t))\, \dot{s}^2(t)
$$

### Step 2 — Velocity and Acceleration Bounds

For all $t$:

$$
\vert \dot{q}(t) \vert \leq v_{max}, \qquad \vert \ddot{q}(t) \vert \leq a_{max}
$$

### Step 3 — Conservative Bound for Path with Bounded Curvature

If curvature $\kappa(s)$ is bounded by $\kappa_{max}$:

$$
\ddot{s}(t) \leq a_{max} - \kappa_{max} \, \dot{s}^2(t)
$$

### Step 4 — Maximum Safe Speed on a Segment

For a collision-free interval where minimum clearance to obstacle is $d_{min}(s)$:

$$
\dot{s}_{safe}(s) = \min\!\left\{ \sqrt{2 \, a_{max}\, d_{min}(s)},\; v_{max} \right\}
$$

> **Logic:** The robot can decelerate from $\dot{s}$ to 0 over distance $d_{min}$ using brake $a_{max}$, hence $\tfrac{1}{2} \dot{s}^2 / a_{max} \leq d_{min}$.

### Step 5 — Time-Optimal Profile

Combining forward/backward passes (Kant–Zucker algorithm):

$$
\dot{s}^*(s) = \min\!\left\{ \dot{s}_{safe}(s),\;\; \dot{s}_{fwd}(s),\;\; \dot{s}_{bwd}(s) \right\}
$$

where $\dot{s}_{fwd}, \dot{s}_{bwd}$ are limits accumulated from the start and goal ends.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reciprocal Velocity Obstacle (RVO)

### Step 1 — VO is "Too Pessimistic"

In $VO_{A\vert B}$, *all* collision avoidance responsibility falls on A. For two cooperating robots this causes **oscillations** (both dodge the same way, then re-dodge).

### Step 2 — Share the Responsibility

Assume each agent moves halfway from its preferred velocity $v^{pref}$ toward the boundary of VO:

$$
v_A^{new} = v_A^{pref} + \tfrac{1}{2}(v_B^{pref} - v_A^{*})
$$

Rearranging:

$$
v_A^{new} = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_A^{pref} + v_B^{pref}) - u^{*}
$$

where $u^*$ is the projection of $\tfrac{1}{2}(v_A^{pref} - v_B^{pref})$ onto the boundary of $VO_{A\vert B}$.

### Step 3 — Geometry of RVO

The $VO_{A\vert B}$ apex is shifted to $\tfrac{1}{2}(p_B - p_A)$. The set of admissible $v_A$ becomes $RVO_{A\vert B}$ — same cone, translated apex.

### Step 4 — Optimal Velocity Selection

$$
v_A^{opt} = \arg\min_{v \in \text{admissible}} \; \vert\vert v - v_A^{pref} \vert\vert^2
$$

> **Logic:** We pick the admissible velocity closest (in Euclidean sense) to what A would do if the world were empty. This is the **unconstrained quadratic-programming** flavor of VO.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) Score

### Step 1 — Admissible Velocities in One Control Cycle

Given current velocity $v_{curr}$ and cycle $\Delta t$:

$$
v_a = v_{curr} - \dot{v}_{max} \Delta t, \qquad v_b = v_{curr} + \dot{v}_{max} \Delta t
$$

$$
V_d = \{ (v, \omega) \;:\; v \in [v_a, v_b],\; \omega \in [\omega_a, \omega_b] \}
$$

### Step 2 — Forward Simulate

For each $(v,\omega) \in V_d$, simulate a short arc trajectory of duration $\Delta t$ and check:

- Stopping distance $\leq$ clearance to nearest obstacle.
- Goal alignment (heading).
- Forward progress (velocity magnitude).

### Step 3 — Score Function

$$
\text{score}(v,\omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v,\omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v,\omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v,\omega)
$$

Components normalized to $[0,1]$; $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ tuned per robot.

> **Logic of step 3:** Each term is a different **safety/efficiency tradeoff**. $\beta$ (distance) is the dynamic-obstacle-aware term; without moving obstacles, $\beta$ alone dominates.

### Step 4 — Argmax Selection

$$
(v^*, \omega^*) = \arg\max_{(v,\omega) \in V_d} \; \text{score}(v,\omega)
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favourite Exam Table)

| Method | Planning Horizon | Re-plans? | Multi-robot? | Optimal? | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{C}\times\mathcal{T}$ A\* | Global | No | No | Yes (within grid) | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ |
| $\mathcal{C}\times\mathcal{T}$ RRT\* | Global | Yes | Extension via $\mathcal{C}\times\mathcal{T}_i$ | Asymptotically | $O(n \log n)$ |
| VO | Local | Yes (every cycle) | Yes (with RVO/HRVO) | Local optimum | $O(N)$ where $N$ = #obstacles |
| DWA | Local | Yes (every cycle) | No (single agent) | Local optimum | $O(\vert V_d \vert)$ |
| Path–Velocity Decomp. | Global + local | Yes | Limited | Yes (for the path) | Moderate |
| MPC / Traj. Opt. | Rolling horizon | Yes | Yes (with coupling) | Receding-horizon opt. | High (QP / NLP) |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Question:** Robot A at $p_A = (0, 0)$, disc radius $r_A = 0.5$ m. Obstacle B at $p_B = (4, 0)$, disc radius $r_B = 0.5$ m, moving with $v_B = (1, 0)$ m/s. Robot's current velocity $v_A = (0, 0)$. Compute $VO_{A\vert B}$ for $\tau = 4$ s.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Effective radius:**

$$
r_{A+B} = r_A + r_B = 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Relative position:**

$$
\tilde{p}_0 = p_A - p_B = (-4, 0), \qquad \vert\vert \tilde{p}_0 \vert\vert = 4 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Cone half-angle:**

$$
\alpha = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{1.0}{4}\right) = \arcsin(0.25) \approx 14.48^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — VO cone in relative-velocity space:**

The cone has its apex at the origin of $u$-space, opens toward the direction $-\tilde{p}_0 = (4, 0)$ (i.e., toward B), and spans angles from $\pi/2 - \alpha$ to $\pi/2 + \alpha$ relative to $u$-axis. Since $v_A = (0,0)$, $u = -v_B = (-1, 0)$. The angle to $u$ from the cone axis is measured; here $u$ lies *inside* the cone — meaning the *current* relative velocity leads to collision unless corrected.

**Step 5 — Minimum clearance distance:**

Distance from A to B closes at $\vert\vert u \vert\vert = 1$ m/s. Time-to-collision = $4 / 1 = 4$ s. Boundary of $\tau$ exactly matches, so this is **critical**; A must change velocity.

**Step 6 — Recommended action (RVO):**

Pick $v_A^{pref} = (2, 0)$ (move toward goal, east). New relative velocity $\tilde{u} = (1, -0)$ with respect to $v_B$. Project to RVO boundary:

$$
v_A^{new} = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl((2,0) + (1,0)\bigr) - u^{*} = (1.5, 0) - u^{*}
$$

where $u^{*}$ is the projection that pushes $(1.5 - 0, 0)$ just outside the truncated cone.

The final answer: choose any $v_A$ such that $v_A - v_B$ points *outside* the $14.48^{\circ}$ cone — for example, $v_A = (1, 0.5)$ m/s, giving $u = (0, 0.5)$ which is orthogonal to B and clearly clears.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Functional Architecture of a Dynamic Motion Planning Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Sensors: LiDAR Camera Radar] --> A2[Perception Module]
    A2 --> A3[Obstacle Tracker: Kalman Filter]
    A3 --> A4[Trajectory Predictor: CV / CTRV model]
    A4 --> A5[Global Planner: C x T RRT*]
    A4 --> A6[Local Reactive: Velocity Obstacle / DWA]
    A5 --> A7[Trajectory Optimizer: QP / MPC]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Low-level Controller: PID / MPC]
    A8 --> A9[Robot Actuators]
    A9 --> A10[World]
    A10 --> A1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Data flows clockwise. The **dual-loop** structure (global planner + local reactive) is the industry-standard pattern for autonomous systems operating with moving obstacles (e.g., Waymo Driver stack).

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Mapping Static Planning to $\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{T}$ for a Moving Obstacle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Static C-space]
        A1[Robot q in C]
        A2[Obstacle region in C]
    end
    subgraph B[C x T space]
        B1[Robot curve gamma_t in C x T]
        B2[Moving obstacle swept volume tube]
    end
    A1 -->|Add time axis t| B1
    A2 -->|Swept over t| B2
    B1 -.collision-free.-> B2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A 2-D static obstacle becomes a 3-D **"tube"** in $\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{T}$. Planning in 3-D lets us reuse A\*/RRT\* from static planning theory.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Velocity Obstacle Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Robot pose pA velocity vA] --> R2[Compute relative position pB - pA]
    R3[Obstacle pose pB velocity vB] --> R2
    R2 --> R4[Compute Minkowski-disc radius rA plus rB]
    R4 --> R5[Compute cone half-angle arcsin]
    R5 --> R6[Construct VO cone in velocity space]
    R6 --> R7[Truncate by tau and vmax]
    R7 --> R8[Clip relative velocity vA - vB]
    R8 --> R9{Inside VO?}
    R9 -- Yes --> R10[Reject velocity]
    R9 -- No --> R11[Admissible velocity set]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is a deterministic geometric transformation. Total cost is $O(N)$ for $N$ obstacles — the *reason* VO is real-time friendly.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Flow Inside Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Current v and omega] --> S2[Compute reachable set Vd over dt]
    S2 --> S3[Sample discretized v omega from Vd]
    S3 --> S4[Forward simulate trajectory dt]
    S4 --> S5{Collision with dynamic obstacle in dt?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Discard sample]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Compute heading dist velocity scores]
    S7 --> S8[Argmax score]
    S8 --> S9[Send v star omega star to controller]
    S9 --> S1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The inner loop runs at the controller rate (typically 20–50 Hz). The **discard** branch makes DWA *probabilistically complete* only insofar as samples cover $V_d$ densely.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Path–Velocity Decomposition Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Static map] --> P2[Geometric planner: A* or RRT]
    P2 --> P3[Path gamma s]
    P3 --> P4[Dynamic sweep: assign s of t]
    P4 --> P5{Predict moving obstacles at t?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Re-time profile: time scaling]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Keep constant velocity profile]
    P6 --> P8[Output kinodynamic trajectory q of t]
    P7 --> P8
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Decouples *where* the robot goes (geometric) from *when* (velocity) — a powerful modularization that simplifies software engineering and certification.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the configuration–time space $(\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{T})$ and explain how a moving obstacle is represented in it. Why does this transformation simplify dynamic motion planning?

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
The configuration–time space is the Cartesian product $\mathcal{C} \times [0, T]$ where each point $(q, t)$ denotes the robot being at configuration $q$ at instant $t$. A moving obstacle whose support at time $t$ is the workspace region $O(t)$ corresponds, in $\mathcal{C}$, to the configuration obstacle $\mathcal{CO}(t)$; sweeping this over $t \in [0, T]$ produces a **3-D forbidden volume (tube)** in $\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{T}$. A dynamic plan becomes any curve $\gamma : [0, T] \to \mathcal{C}$ whose lifted curve $( \gamma(t), t )$ does not intersect this swept volume — *exactly* the static collision-free path problem in one extra dimension, so any static planner (A\*, RRT\*) applies directly.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for simplification argument)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State the Velocity Obstacle (VO) concept. Write the mathematical condition for a robot velocity $v_A$ to lie inside the VO induced by a moving obstacle B, and identify the role of the horizon $\tau$.

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
For robot A and moving obstacle B (radii $r_A, r_B$), the **Velocity Obstacle** $VO_{A \vert B}^{\tau}$ is the set of velocities $v_A$ that will cause a collision with B at some time $t \in [0, \tau]$ in the future.

Mathematical condition:

$$
v_A \in VO_{A \vert B}^{\tau} \iff \exists\, t \in [0, \tau] \;:\; \vert\vert (p_A - p_B) + (v_A - v_B)\, t \vert\vert \leq r_A + r_B
$$

The horizon $\tau$ **truncates** the cone: only collisions within the planning window are considered. Velocities that would collide after $\tau$ are ignored because the planner will re-plan long before then. This truncation is what makes VO real-time and prevents over-conservative avoidance.

*(1 mark concept + 1 mark formula + 1 mark role of τ)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, Module 4 questions typically appear with internal choice. Both alternatives below cover the dynamic motion planning syllabus exhaustively.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 ESE]**

**(a)** Derive the geometric construction of the **Velocity Obstacle cone** for a disc-shaped robot A of radius $r_A$ and a moving disc-shaped obstacle B of radius $r_B$. Show the half-angle expression and explain the role of the Minkowski sum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **time-optimal velocity profile** along a given path segment of length $L = 5$ m for a robot with $v_{max} = 2$ m/s and $a_{max} = 1$ m/s². The minimum clearance to a moving obstacle along the path is $d_{min} = 0.4$ m at the midpoint. Verify whether the robot can traverse the segment without violating bounds. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Reduction to relative frame:** Let $\tilde{p}_0 = p_A - p_B$ and $u = v_A - v_B$. In the relative frame, B is stationary at the origin. Collision at time $t$ iff $\vert\vert \tilde{p}_0 + u t \vert\vert \leq r_{A+B}$ where $r_{A+B} \triangleq r_A + r_B$.

**Step 2 — Minkowski sum role:** By inflating robot A by obstacle B's disc, we treat A as a **point robot**. This avoids tracking edges of polygons. The Minkowski sum of A and a disc of radius $r_B$ is simply $A \oplus \mathcal{D}(r_B)$, a "puffed up" version of A.

**Step 3 — Cone construction:** The set of $u$ leading to collision is a **cone** with apex at the origin, axis along $-\tilde{p}_0$, and half-angle $\alpha$ satisfying

$$
\sin \alpha = \frac{r_{A+B}}{\vert\vert \tilde{p}_0 \vert\vert} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \alpha = \arcsin\!\frac{r_{A+B}}{\vert\vert p_B - p_A \vert\vert}
$$

**Step 4 — Truncation:** Clip with disc $\vert\vert u \vert\vert \leq v_{max}$ (actuator limit) and with $t \leq \tau$ to get a **truncated cone**. The near cap has radius $r_{A+B} / \tau$.

**Step 5 — Velocity decision rule:** A velocity $v_A$ is *admissible* iff $u = v_A - v_B$ lies *outside* this truncated cone. (7 marks distributed: 1.5 each for Steps 1–4, 1 for Step 5)

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Maximum safe velocity at midpoint:** The robot must be able to brake from current speed to zero within clearance $d_{min}$:

$$
v_{safe} = \sqrt{2\, a_{max}\, d_{min}} = \sqrt{2 \times 1 \times 0.4} = \sqrt{0.8} \approx 0.894 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with $v_{max}$:**

$$
v_{safe} \approx 0.894 < v_{max} = 2 \text{ m/s}
$$

So the robot **cannot** traverse at full speed — it must slow to $\leq 0.894$ m/s near the midpoint.

**Step 3 — Minimum traversal time with trapezoidal profile:** Accelerate from $0$ to $v_{safe}$, cruise, decelerate back to $0$. Assuming symmetric trapezoid centered at midpoint:

- Acceleration distance: $s_1 = \tfrac{v_{safe}^2}{2 a_{max}} = 0.4$ m
- Cruise distance: $s_{cruise} = L - 2 s_1 = 5 - 0.8 = 4.2$ m
- Accel. time: $t_1 = v_{safe} / a_{max} = 0.894$ s
- Cruise time: $t_{cruise} = s_{cruise} / v_{safe} = 4.2 / 0.894 \approx 4.70$ s
- Total time: $T = 2 t_1 + t_{cruise} \approx 0.894 + 0.894 + 4.70 = 6.49$ s

**Step 4 — Verification of bounds:**

- Velocity $\in [0, 0.894]$ m/s $\leq v_{max} = 2$ ✓
- Acceleration $\leq 1$ m/s² ✓
- Clearance $d_{min} = 0.4$ m respected because braking distance from $v_{safe}$ equals exactly $d_{min}$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The robot *can* traverse safely in **6.49 s** by capping speed at 0.894 m/s. If time-critical, alternative: replan geometric path to increase $d_{min}$. (7 marks: 2 + 1 + 3 + 1)

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 ESE]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** and the **Reciprocal Velocity Obstacle (RVO)** for dynamic motion planning. Cover (i) the underlying space (velocity vs configuration–time), (ii) multi-robot handling, (iii) optimality, and (iv) typical application domain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point robot at the origin must reach goal $G = (10, 0)$ while avoiding an obstacle moving along $x(t) = 6 + 0.5 t$, $y(t) = 0$ (constant velocity). Robot max speed = 2 m/s. Using the **path–velocity decomposition** method, propose a straight-line geometric path and determine whether a constant-velocity profile is safe. If not, compute the **minimum-time velocity profile**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

| Criterion | DWA | RVO |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Underlying space | Search in **velocity space** $(v, \omega)$ over one cycle $\Delta t$ | Search in **velocity space** $v_A$, geometric construction |
| (ii) Multi-robot | Native: **single-agent**; multi-agent needs extension (e.g., centralized DWA) | Native: **multi-agent** by reciprocity |
| (iii) Optimality | Local optimum via argmax score; no global guarantee | Local optimum w.r.t. preferred velocity |
| (iv) Typical application | Mobile ground robots, TurtleBot, service robots | Multi-pedestrian / multi-robot crowd simulation, warehouse AMRs |

**Key DWA features:**
- Admissible set $V_d$ based on actuator reachability.
- Forward simulation of arc trajectories.
- Score = $\alpha \cdot$ heading $+ \beta \cdot$ clearance $+ \gamma \cdot$ velocity.

**Key RVO features:**
- Truncated cone in velocity space, *apex shifted* by half the relative position.
- Assumes **symmetric responsibility** for collision avoidance.
- Provably oscillation-free under mild conditions.

**Summary (1 mark):** DWA excels in single-agent dynamic environments with holonomic or differential-drive robots; RVO excels in dense multi-agent scenarios. (7 marks: 1+1+1+1 for table + 1+1 for features + 1 for conclusion)

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Geometric path:** Straight line from $(0,0)$ to $(10,0)$, i.e., $\gamma(s) = (s, 0)$, $s \in [0, 10]$.

**Step 2 — Constant velocity check:** If robot travels at $v_R$, it reaches obstacle's $x$-position at $t_R = 6 / v_R$. Obstacle reaches same $x$ at $t_O = (s - 6) / 0.5$. For constant $v_R = 2$ m/s: $t_R = 3$ s, $t_O = (6 - 6)/0.5 = 0$ s — wait, obstacle starts at $x=6$ already. Re-check: at $t=0$, robot is at $x=0$, obstacle at $x=6$. Robot reaches $x=6$ at $t = 6/2 = 3$ s. Obstacle is at $x = 6 + 0.5(3) = 7.5$ at that time. So at $t=3$ s, robot and obstacle are at $x_R = 6, x_O = 7.5$, both on $y = 0$, gap = 1.5 m — **safe at that instant** if radii sum to < 1.5 m.

But check the *closest approach*: minimize $\vert (s_R - x_O(t)) \vert$ with $s_R = v_R t$. Set $v_R t = 6 + 0.5 t \Rightarrow t (v_R - 0.5) = 6 \Rightarrow t = 6/(v_R - 0.5)$.

For $v_R = 2$: $t = 6/1.5 = 4$ s, $x = 8$. Distance = $8 - 8 = 0$ — **collision!** So constant 2 m/s is **unsafe**.

**Step 3 — Minimum-time safe profile:** Robot's relative speed to obstacle = $v_R - 0.5$. To avoid collision, robot must not be at same position at same time. Equivalently, robot must *finish* before obstacle catches up:

Robot finishes at $t_f = 10 / v_R$. For no collision, $t_f \leq$ time for obstacle to leave path-region. Conservative: ensure $v_R > 0.5$ so robot outruns, and choose $v_R$ such that robot passes each $x$ before obstacle:

$t_R(x) = x / v_R, \quad t_O(x) = (x - 6) / 0.5 = 2x - 12$

Require $t_R(x) < t_O(x)$:

$$
\frac{x}{v_R} < 2x - 12 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_R > \frac{x}{2x - 12}
$$

The RHS is maximized at the smallest $x > 6$ (i.e., $x \to 6^+$), giving bound $v_R > \infty$. This means **no constant velocity is safe** in the strict sense — the robot *and* obstacle converge to the same point eventually.

**Step 4 — Time-scaling solution:** Insert a wait/delay at the start so robot arrives *after* obstacle passes.

If robot waits $t_w$, then at obstacle-clear time $t_c$ when robot reaches $x=6$, obstacle is at $x_c = 6 + 0.5 (t_c + t_w)$. Need $x_c > 10$ (obstacle exits) before robot enters, OR accept narrow gap. Simplified answer: delay $t_w$ such that obstacle has cleared goal:

$t_c + t_w = (10 - 6)/0.5 = 8$ s. With $v_R = 2$, $t_c = 3$ s. So $t_w = 5$ s.

**Total minimum time** = $t_w + 10/v_R = 5 + 5 = 10$ s.

**Conclusion:** Constant velocity **not safe**; minimum-time profile: wait 5 s, then travel at 2 m/s — total **10 s**. (7 marks: 1 path + 2 collision check + 2 safety analysis + 1 delay computation + 1 conclusion)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students LOSE MARKS on Dynamic Motion Planning:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Minkowski sum** in VO construction — examiner expects $r_A + r_B$ explicitly. Writing just $r_A$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up VO with RVO** — VO assigns full avoidance to robot A; RVO shares responsibility (apex shift). Examiners check this distinction in part (a).
> 3. **Sign error in relative velocity**: $u = v_A - v_B$, not $v_A + v_B$. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{T}$ with workspace-time** — $\mathcal{C}$ is *configuration* space, NOT workspace. Examiner will deduct marks for sloppy notation.
> 5. **Skipping the role of horizon $\tau$** in VO truncation — required for full marks.
> 6. **In path–velocity decomposition**, students often forget that the *geometric path* must first be re-checked against **future** obstacle positions; only then assign velocity profile.
> 7. **Numerical errors** in Time-to-Collision (TTC) computations — keep units consistent (m/s for velocity, s for time, m for distance).
> 8. **Not drawing the truncated VO cone** when asked "show geometrically" — a labeled diagram is worth 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Points — Dynamic Motion Planning: Moving Obstacles**

- ⭐ Moving-obstacle problem is solved by lifting to **$\mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{T}$** — turning dynamic into static.
- ⭐ **Velocity Obstacle (VO)** = cone in velocity space; condition: $\exists\, t \in [0, \tau]$ with $\vert\vert \tilde{p}_0 + u t \vert\vert \leq r_{A+B}$.
- ⭐ Half-angle: $\alpha = \arcsin(r_{A+B} / \vert\vert p_B - p_A \vert\vert)$.
- ⭐ **RVO** = apex-shifted VO for reciprocal avoidance in multi-agent settings.
- ⭐ **DWA** = search admissible $V_d$, forward-simulate, score = heading + clearance + velocity, argmax.
- ⭐ **Path–Velocity Decomposition**: plan path geometrically, then time-scale for dynamic feasibility.
- ⭐ Minkowski sum $A \oplus \mathcal{D}(r_B)$ turns "shape vs. shape" into "point vs. shape".
- ⭐ Time scaling constraint: $\dot{s}_{safe}(s) = \sqrt{2 a_{max} d_{min}(s)}$ along the path.
- ⭐ TTC = $(p_i - p_A) \cdot \hat{v} \;/\; (\vert\vert v \vert\vert - \hat{v} \cdot v_i)$.
- ⭐ Replanning trigger: $\min_i d(\gamma(t), O_i(t)) < d_{safe}$.
- ⭐ Industry stack = (Perception → Tracker → Predictor → Global $\mathcal{C}\times\mathcal{T}$ planner) **plus** (Local Reactive VO/DWA) **plus** (Trajectory Optimizer MPC) **plus** (Low-level Controller).
- ⭐ Computational cost: VO/DWA are $O(N)$ per cycle → real-time feasible.
- ⭐ Multi-robot dynamic planning uses **RVO / HRVO / ORCA** (Optimal Reciprocal Collision Avoidance).
- ⭐ Uncertainty in obstacle motion is handled via **probabilistic VO** (pVO) with chance constraints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration Time Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Configuration Time Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Configuration Time Space (CT-space)** is the augmented state-space used in robot motion planning that combines the robot's **Configuration Space (C-space)** with the **temporal dimension (T)**. Mathematically, it is defined as the Cartesian product:

$$CT = C \times T$$

where $C \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is the n-dimensional configuration manifold (each point representing a unique joint configuration of the robot) and $T \subset \mathbb{R}^+$ is the time interval over which the motion is planned. Every point $(q, t) \in CT$ specifies **both where the robot is (configuration $q$) and when it is there (time $t$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental shift from **Configuration Space** to **Configuration Time Space** converts the geometric problem of *path planning* into the dynamic problem of *trajectory planning*. A path becomes a trajectory only when a time law $q(t)$ is explicitly associated with it.

A feasible trajectory in CT-space is a continuous, time-parametrized curve:

$$\tau : [t_0, t_f] \rightarrow C, \quad \tau(t) = q(t)$$

subject to **kinematic constraints** (collision avoidance), **dynamic constraints** (velocity, acceleration, jerk limits), and **boundary conditions** (initial and final configurations at specified times).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car from your home to your office:

- **Configuration Space alone** is like a **static road map**. It tells you *which roads* you can take and *which intersections to avoid*. A pure path planner says: "Take MG Road, then turn left at the second signal."
- **Configuration Time Space** is like a **live GPS navigation system**. It does not just tell you *which road*, but also *when* you will reach each intersection, *how fast* you can drive on each segment, and whether there is a parade (a *moving obstacle*) blocking the road at 9 AM that was not there at 7 AM.

In robotics terms:
- **C-space** answers: "What configurations can the robot pass through?"
- **CT-space** answers: "At what time, with what velocity, and in what sequence can the robot pass through those configurations?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** If C-space is the *map*, CT-space is the *map plus the clock*. Adding time transforms an abstract geometric path into an executable, real-world trajectory.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following quantities are conventionally treated as fixed design parameters in CT-space planning:

- **Maximum joint velocity:** $\dot{q}_{i,\max}$ (typically in **rad/s** for revolute joints or **m/s** for prismatic joints)
- **Maximum joint acceleration:** $\ddot{q}_{i,\max}$ (typically in **rad/s²** or **m/s²**)
- **Maximum Cartesian speed:** $v_{\max}$ (typically in **m/s**)
- **Sampling period:** $\Delta t$ (typically in **seconds**, often **0.001 s to 0.01 s**)
- **Trajectory duration:** $t_f - t_0$ (in **seconds**)

---

## 1.4 Why CT-Space is Needed in Robot Motion Planning

| Limitation of Pure C-Space | How CT-Space Resolves It |
|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Cannot express velocity/acceleration limits | Adds explicit time axis and dynamic constraints |
| Treats obstacles as static | Allows **time-varying obstacles** (C-obstacles become time-dependent) |
| Produces only paths, not trajectories | Yields a complete **time-parametrized trajectory** |
| No notion of synchronization with other agents | Enables **coordination, scheduling, and multi-robot planning** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In real industrial environments, obstacles move (humans, conveyor belts, mobile platforms). Without the time dimension, the planner cannot guarantee collision-free execution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of CT-Space

### 2.1.1 The Configuration Space $C$

For a robot with $n$ degrees of freedom (DOF), the configuration space is an $n$-dimensional manifold:

$$C = C_{\text{free}} \cup C_{\text{obs}}$$

where:
- $C_{\text{free}} = \{q \in C \mid A(q) \cap O = \emptyset\}$ (collision-free configurations)
- $C_{\text{obs}} = \{q \in C \mid A(q) \cap O \neq \emptyset\}$ (configurations in collision)
- $A(q)$ is the subset of the workspace occupied by the robot at configuration $q$
- $O$ represents the obstacles in the workspace

### 2.1.2 The Time Component $T$

The time axis is a one-dimensional continuum, typically bounded:

$$T = [t_0, t_f] \subset \mathbb{R}^+$$

### 2.1.3 The Combined CT-Space

$$CT = C \times T$$

The dimension of CT-space is therefore $(n+1)$, where $n$ is the number of DOF of the robot.

---

## 2.2 Time-Parameterized Obstacles (C-obstacles with Time)

In a dynamic environment, the obstacle region in the workspace becomes a function of time: $O(t)$. Consequently, the C-obstacle region is also time-dependent:

$$C_{\text{obs}}(t) = \{q \in C \mid A(q) \cap O(t) \neq \emptyset\}$$

A feasible trajectory must satisfy:

$$\forall t \in [t_0, t_f] : q(t) \in C_{\text{free}}(t)$$

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

**Why time as a separate dimension?**  
A purely geometric path can be traced at any speed, including an infinite one. Real robots have finite actuator torques and cannot achieve infinite velocity. Adding time converts the *feasibility* question from a static geometric one to a dynamic one.

**Why does $C_{\text{obs}}$ depend on $t$?**  
Moving obstacles (humans, vehicles, other robots) change the forbidden region continuously. A configuration that is safe at $t=2$ s may be unsafe at $t=3$ s.

**Why enforce continuity of $\dot{q}$ and $\ddot{q}$?**  
Discontinuities in velocity cause infinite accelerations (impulsive torques), which damage actuators and excite structural vibrations. Trajectory planners ensure at least $C^2$ continuity (continuous up to the second derivative).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary Conditions | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | CT-space definition | $CT = C \times T$ | $T = [t_0, t_f]$, $C \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ | mixed |
| 2 | Trajectory map | $\tau: T \to C$, $\tau(t) = q(t)$ | $q(t_0) = q_{\text{start}}$, $q(t_f) = q_{\text{goal}}$ | rad or m |
| 3 | Time-varying C-obstacle | $C_{\text{obs}}(t) = \{q \mid A(q) \cap O(t) \neq \emptyset\}$ | — | — |
| 4 | Feasibility condition | $q(t) \in C_{\text{free}}(t), \forall t$ | $\tau \in C^2$ continuity | — |
| 5 | Joint velocity bound | $\vert \dot{q}_i(t) \vert \leq \dot{q}_{i,\max}$ | $\forall i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Joint acceleration bound | $\vert \ddot{q}_i(t) \vert \leq \ddot{q}_{i,\max}$ | $\forall i$ | rad/s² |
| 7 | Trapezoidal velocity profile (total time) | $t_f = \dfrac{d}{v_{\max}} + \dfrac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}}$ | $d$ = path length | s |
| 8 | Triangular velocity peak velocity | $v_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{d \cdot a_{\max}}$ | when $t_a = t_d$ | m/s |
| 9 | Cubic polynomial segment | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | continuous $q, \dot{q}$ | rad |
| 10 | Quintic polynomial segment | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | continuous $q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q}$ | rad |
| 11 | Time-optimal duration (linear segment) | $t^* = \dfrac{6d}{4v_{\max} + \sqrt{4v_{\max}^2 + 12 a_{\max} d}}$ | bounded accel. | s |
| 12 | Swept volume integral | $V_{\text{swept}} = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} V(q(t))\, dt$ | volumetric check | m³·s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examiner Note:** The distinction between $\vert \dot{q}_i \vert \leq \dot{q}_{i,\max}$ (joint-space bound) and $\Vert \dot{x} \Vert \leq v_{\max}$ (task-space bound) is a frequent KTU exam trap. Always specify which space the bound applies to.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CT-space planning is foundational in:

- **Industrial robotic welding arms** that must synchronize with a moving conveyor (moving obstacle in workspace).
- **Autonomous vehicles** navigating traffic with time-dependent lane closures.
- **Surgical robots** (e.g., da Vinci system) where the moving tissue creates dynamic C-obstacles.
- **Collaborative robots (cobots)** that must slow down or stop when a human enters a safety zone (time-varying forbidden region).
- **Spacecraft rendezvous and docking** where every motion is governed by fuel-optimal time-parameterized trajectories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time Required for Trapezoidal Velocity Profile on a Straight-Line Path Segment

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A robot end-effector must traverse a straight-line path segment of length $d$ in joint space (after projection). The maximum velocity is $v_{\max}$ and the maximum acceleration is $a_{\max}$.
