2. A *cylindrical body* of radius $R$ around the obstacle's world-line.
3. A *rear* spherical cap where the obstacle *catches up* from behind if the robot is too slow.

The resulting solid — rendered with the obstacle's world-line as its axis — visually resembles a lobed celestial body, hence the term *asteroid*.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Reduction Methods

| Method | Dimensionality | Exactness | Computational Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct 3D $(x,y,t)$ search | 3D | Approximate | High | Few obstacles, low speeds |
| Slice + A* on grid | 2D per slice | Discrete | Medium | Standard textbook problems |
| Slice + Visibility Graph | 2D per slice | Exact (within slice) | Medium | Polygonal obstacles |
| Slice + RRT | 2D per slice | Probabilistic | Variable | High-dimensional extensions |
| Direct trajectory optimization | 2D trajectory | Continuous | High | Smooth, fuel-optimal paths |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Most KTU questions ask for the *slice reduction* approach because it is the cleanest and most conceptually transparent. Practice writing down $F(p_k)$ explicitly.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Illustration

Suppose:
- Start $q_0 = (0, 0)$ at $t_0 = 0$
- Goal $q_g = (10, 0)$
- Single asteroid at $o_0 = (5, 0)$ moving with $v_o = (-1, 0)$ m/s
- $V_{max} = 2$ m/s, $R = 0.5$ m, $\Delta t = 1$ s

**Step 1 — Asteroid trajectory:**
$$o(t) = (5 - t,\; 0)$$

At $t = 0$: $o = (5, 0)$; at $t = 1$: $o = (4, 0)$; at $t = 2$: $o = (3, 0)$.

**Step 2 — Reach radius per slice:**
$$r_{reach} = V_{max}\,\Delta t = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Search:**
From $(0,0)$ at $t=0$, the robot can reach any point within 2 m, but must stay $\geq 0.5$ m from $(5,0)$. To reach the goal at $(10,0)$ in 5 slices (5 s), an average speed of 2 m/s is required — exactly the limit. The robot must curve *around* the asteroid (e.g., $y > 0.5$ at slice 2) before straightening out.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Planning with Velocity Bound

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: q_s, t_0] --> B1[Discretize time axis into slices of width Delta_t]
    B1 --> C1[For each slice t_k, compute obstacle positions o_i of t_k]
    C1 --> D1[Build reachable disc D of p_k, V_max Delta t]
    D1 --> E1[Subtract forbidden discs D of o_i of t_k, R]
    E1 --> F1[Feasible region F of p_k for slice t_k+1]
    F1 --> G1{Goal reached?}
    G1 -- No --> H1[Add nodes and edges to graph]
    H1 --> I1[Run A* or Dijkstra search]
    I1 --> J1[Output optimal velocity-bounded trajectory q of t]
    G1 -- Yes --> J1
    J1 --> K0[End: Collision-free trajectory respecting V_max]
```

### 4.2 Configuration Space-Time Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Start q_s at t_0 in 3D x,y,t space] --> nodeTube[Moving obstacle becomes static 3D tube of radius R along world-line]
    nodeTube --> nodeCone[Velocity bound V_max becomes cone constraint on trajectory slope]
    nodeCone --> nodeGoal[Goal q_g at t_f reachable via A* search through free space]
```

### 4.3 Slice-Based Graph Search Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    slice0[Slice k=0: Robot at p_0] --> discReach[Reachable disc radius V_max Delta t]
    discReach --> obsCheck[Check collision with all obstacles at slice k+1]
    obsCheck --> valid[Valid next positions: free and reachable]
    valid --> edgeAdd[Add directed edges in time]
    edgeAdd --> astar[A* search minimizing time or path length]
    astar --> pathOut[Optimal velocity-bounded path]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Asteroid Shape in $(x, y, t)$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obsCenter[Obstacle world-line in 3D x,y,t] --> frontCap[Front spherical cap: obstacle cannot be outrun]
    obsCenter --> bodyTube[Cylindrical body of radius R]
    obsCenter --> rearCap[Rear spherical cap: obstacle catches up if robot too slow]
    frontCap --> asteroidShape[Combined forbidden solid = Asteroid shape]
    bodyTube --> asteroidShape
    rearCap --> asteroidShape
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT632]**
*State the two fundamental constraints that govern the asteroid avoidance problem with velocity bound.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two constraints are:
1. **Kinematic velocity bound:** $\sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2} \leq V_{max}$ for all time $t$.
2. **Collision avoidance:** $\|q(t) - o_i(t)\| \geq R$ for all $t$ and all obstacles $i$, where $o_i(t) = o_i(0) + v_{o_i} t$ is the obstacle's predicted position.

The first restricts the *slope* of the trajectory in $(x, y, t)$ space; the second restricts the trajectory to remain outside the 3D swept volumes of the moving obstacles.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT632]**
*Why is the configuration space-time formulation preferred for the asteroid avoidance problem?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The configuration space–time $(x, y, t)$ formulation converts the *dynamic* planning problem (moving obstacles, velocity-limited robot) into an equivalent *static* planning problem. The moving obstacles become stationary 3D tubes, and the velocity bound becomes a geometric cone constraint on the trajectory. This allows the use of classical static-path-planning algorithms (visibility graphs, A*, RRT) on a single static free-space, dramatically simplifying the problem.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PEBRT632]`

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Explain in detail the reduction of the asteroid avoidance problem with velocity bound to a planning problem in configuration space-time. Clearly show how the velocity bound becomes a cone constraint and how each moving obstacle becomes a static 3D solid.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* A robot starts at $(0, 0)$ at $t = 0$ and must reach the goal at $(8, 0)$. A single asteroid is initially at $(4, 0)$ and moves with constant velocity $(-0.5, 0)$ m/s. The robot has maximum speed $V_{max} = 1$ m/s, and the clearance radius is $R = 0.5$ m. Using $\Delta t = 1$ s, list the candidate reachable positions at slice $k = 1$ and $k = 2$ that remain collision-free.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Configuration Space-Time Reduction:*

1. **Embedding time as a coordinate:** Lift the 2D configuration $(x, y)$ into the 3D space $(x, y, t)$. The robot becomes a point; its trajectory is a 3D curve.

2. **Static obstacles:** Each moving obstacle $i$ at $o_i(0)$ with velocity $v_{o_i}$ traces the line $o_i(t) = o_i(0) + v_{o_i}\,t$. The obstacle's swept volume — a tube of radius $R$ around this line — becomes a *static* forbidden region in $(x, y, t)$.

3. **Velocity cone:** The bound $\|\dot{q}\| \leq V_{max}$ translates to the geometric condition that the trajectory's tangent vector at any point must lie within a double cone of half-angle $\alpha$ where $\tan\alpha = V_{max}$. This restricts how steeply the 3D trajectory can climb in the time direction.

4. **Slice discretization:** Time is sliced into intervals $\Delta t$. At each slice $k$, the feasible next positions form the disc $D(p_k, V_{max}\Delta t)$ minus all obstacle discs $D(o_i(t_{k+1}), R)$.

5. **Graph search:** The reduced problem is solved by constructing a graph (visibility graph, grid, or RRT) over the feasible positions at each slice and running A* to find the optimal collision-free velocity-bounded trajectory.

**(b)** *Numerical Slice Computation:*

Asteroid trajectory: $o(t) = (4 - 0.5t,\; 0)$.

Reach radius: $r_{reach} = V_{max}\Delta t = 1 \times 1 = 1$ m.

**Slice $k = 0 \rightarrow k = 1$:**
- Robot at $p_0 = (0, 0)$.
- Reachable disc: all $(x, y)$ with $x^2 + y^2 \leq 1$.
- Asteroid at $t_1 = 1$: $o(1) = (3.5, 0)$. Forbidden disc: $(x - 3.5)^2 + y^2 \leq 0.25$.
- Sample feasible candidates on grid: $(1, 0)$, $(0.5, 0.5)$, $(-0.5, 0.5)$, $(0, 1)$, etc. All these satisfy $\|p_1 - o(1)\| > 0.5$.

**Slice $k = 1 \rightarrow k = 2$:**
- Asteroid at $t_2 = 2$: $o(2) = (3, 0)$. Forbidden disc: $(x - 3)^2 + y^2 \leq 0.25$.
- From each $p_1$, the reachable set is again a disc of radius 1.
- Example: from $p_1 = (1, 0)$, candidates include $(2, 0)$, $(1.5, 0.5)$, $(0.5, 0.7)$, etc. Check $\|p_2 - (3, 0)\| \geq 0.5$: $(2, 0)$ gives distance $1 > 0.5$ ✓; $(1.5, 0.5)$ gives $\sqrt{2.25 + 0.25} > 0.5$ ✓.
- The planner would prefer $y > 0.5$ to safely curve around the asteroid.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT632]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Derive the reachable region $F(p_k)$ of the robot at time slice $k+1$ given the velocity bound $V_{max}$ and time step $\Delta t$. Show explicitly how the obstacle positions are computed.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* With a labelled diagram in $(x, y, t)$ space, explain the geometric meaning of the asteroid shape and identify its three constituent parts.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Derivation of Reachable Region:*

Let $p_k$ be the robot position at slice $t_k$. Over the interval $[t_k, t_{k+1}]$ of length $\Delta t$, the maximum distance covered under the velocity bound is:

$$
d_{max} = \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} V_{max}\, d\tau = V_{max}\,\Delta t
$$

Therefore the reachable set is the closed disc:

$$D(p_k,\; V_{max}\Delta t) = \{p \in \mathbb{R}^2 : \|p - p_k\| \leq V_{max}\Delta t\}$$

The predicted obstacle position at slice $k+1$ is:

$$o_i(t_{k+1}) = o_i(0) + v_{o_i}\,(k+1)\Delta t$$

The collision-free reachable set is therefore:

$$F(p_k) = D(p_k,\; V_{max}\Delta t) \;\setminus\; \bigcup_i D(o_i(t_{k+1}),\; R)$$

Only positions in $F(p_k)$ become valid next nodes in the planning graph.

**(b)** *Asteroid Shape Anatomy:*

In the 3D $(x, y, t)$ space, the asteroid-shaped forbidden region around a single moving obstacle consists of three parts:

1. **Front Cap (Head):** The leading spherical surface, where the obstacle moves *toward* the robot faster than $V_{max}$, making collision unavoidable unless the robot is sufficiently far in space-time.
2. **Cylindrical Body:** The tube of radius $R$ wrapped around the obstacle's straight-line world-line $o(t) = o(0) + v_o t$.
3. **Rear Cap (Tail):** The trailing spherical surface, where the obstacle catches up from behind if the robot is too slow.

The trajectory of the robot must thread between these regions without intersecting any part, while maintaining a slope whose magnitude never exceeds $V_{max}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting to subtract obstacle discs from the reachable disc:** Students often list only the reachable set $D(p_k, V_{max}\Delta t)$ and forget the collision check $\|p_{k+1} - o_i(t_{k+1})\| \geq R$. This costs up to **3 marks**.
> - **Using obstacle position at the wrong slice:** A frequent error is using $o_i(t_k)$ (current) instead of $o_i(t_{k+1})$ (future) when computing feasibility. The future position must be used because the robot will *arrive* at slice $k+1$.
> - **Confusing reach radius with robot radius:** $V_{max}\Delta t$ is the maximum *step length*, *not* a physical dimension of the robot. Mixing these loses **2 marks**.
> - **Ignoring units in numerical answers:** Always state m, m/s, or s explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Velocity bound** $\Rightarrow$ limits the slope of any trajectory in $(x, y, t)$ space.
- ⭐ **Moving obstacles** $\Rightarrow$ become *static 3D tubes* after embedding time.
- ⭐ **Asteroid shape** = front cap + cylindrical body + rear cap.
- ⭐ **Reach radius per slice:** $r_{reach} = V_{max}\Delta t$.
- ⭐ **Obstacle trajectory:** $o(t) = o(0) + v_o t$ (linear for constant velocity).
- ⭐ **Feasibility formula:** $F(p_k) = D(p_k, V_{max}\Delta t) \setminus \bigcup_i D(o_i(t_{k+1}), R)$.
- ⭐ **Cone half-angle:** $\tan\alpha = V_{max}$ encodes the velocity limit geometrically.
- ⭐ **Reduction strategy:** dynamic 2D problem $\xrightarrow{\text{embed }t}$ static 3D problem $\xrightarrow{\text{slice}}$ graph search.
- ⭐ **Algorithm choice:** A* on a slice graph is the canonical solver; visibility graph or RRT are valid alternatives.
- ⭐ **Real-world domains:** spacecraft, autonomous cars, drones, marine vessels.
- ⭐ **Examiner traps:** wrong time slice for obstacle, forgetting collision check, confusing reach radius with robot radius, missing units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approximate Cell Decomposition
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Approximate Cell Decomposition

# 📘 APPROXIMATE CELL DECOMPOSITION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Approximate Cell Decomposition (ACD)** is a classical, discretization-based motion planning technique in which the robot's **Configuration Space (C-space)** is partitioned into a finite collection of regular-shaped (typically axis-aligned square or rectangular) cells of uniform or non-uniform size, and a path is sought through a **connectivity graph** built over the *free* cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition (Verbatim from Module 4):**
> *Approximate cell decomposition partitions the free space into cells of simple, regular shape (e.g., squares). Each cell is classified as either FREE or OBSTACLE. A path is then searched through the adjacency graph of free cells. The method is **resolution-complete**: if a solution path exists and the resolution is fine enough, the planner will find it.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Honeycomb Rescue"

Imagine you are lost inside a large **honeycomb**. You cannot see the walls of the maze in detail, but the floor has been pre-painted with a **grid of square tiles**, each tile colored either **GREEN (safe to step on)** or **RED (blocked by a wall)**. You find the start tile, look at all **four neighboring green tiles**, and hop from green tile to green tile until you reach the goal.

That is exactly what an Approximate Cell Decomposition planner does to a robot:

- 🟩 **Green tile** → A *free cell* the robot's footprint can occupy.
- 🟥 **Red tile** → A *blocked cell* (intersects an obstacle).
- 🟦 **Hop adjacency** → A graph edge connecting two free cells sharing a side or corner.
- 🧭 **Goal-seeking hops** → A graph search (Dijkstra, A*, BFS) over this adjacency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for First-Time Learners:**
> Approximate Cell Decomposition sacrifices the **exactness** of free-space boundaries (it approximates obstacles by snapping them to a grid) in exchange for **simplicity, speed, and guaranteed resolution-completeness**.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board Must-Know Terms)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Configuration Space (C-space)** | The space of all possible robot configurations $\mathcal{C}$ |
| **Free Space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$** | Subset of C-space not in collision with obstacles |
| **Cell Resolution $\delta$** | Side length of each square cell in the grid |
| **Free Cell** | A cell $\in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$ entirely free of obstacles |
| **Mixed Cell** | A cell partially occupied by an obstacle (problematic!) |
| **Obstacle Cell** | A cell completely inside an obstacle |
| **Connectivity Graph $G$** | Graph whose nodes are free cells and edges connect adjacent free cells |
| **Resolution Completeness** | Planner finds a path *if one exists* at resolution $\delta$ fine enough |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mixed cells are the Achilles' heel of ACD.** If a cell is *partly* blocked, naive ACD marks it as OBSTACLE → **free space is lost** → planner may report "no path" even when one exists. This trade-off is what makes the decomposition *approximate*, not *exact*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow — 6 Logical Steps

1. **Define the C-space** $\mathcal{C} = [x_{\min}, x_{\max}] \times [y_{\min}, y_{\max}]$.
2. **Choose a resolution** $\delta > 0$ (cell side length).
3. **Tile the C-space** with $N_x \times N_y$ cells, where:
   - $N_x = \left\lceil \dfrac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{\delta} \right\rceil$
   - $N_y = \left\lceil \dfrac{y_{\max} - y_{\min}}{\delta} \right\rceil$
4. **Classify each cell** as `FREE` or `BLOCKED` by checking collision against the robot's swept volume and the obstacle map.
5. **Build the adjacency graph** $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = set of free cell indices and $E$ = edges between 4-connected (or 8-connected) free neighbors.
6. **Run a graph search** (A*, BFS, Dijkstra) from start cell $s$ to goal cell $g$.

### 2.2 Variants of Approximate Cell Decomposition

#### (a) Uniform Grid Decomposition
- All cells have the **same size** $\delta$.
- Simplest, easiest to implement.
- Memory cost: $\mathcal{O}(N_x N_y)$.

#### (b) Non-Uniform / Adaptive Grid Decomposition
- Cell size varies: **large cells in open regions**, **small cells near obstacles**.
- Implemented via **Quadtrees** (2D) or **Octrees** (3D).
- Memory-efficient, but more complex.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Variant:** The most common adaptive form is the **Quadtree Decomposition**, where a cell that contains mixed free/obstacle space is recursively subdivided into **four equal quadrants** until each leaf cell is either fully free or fully blocked.

#### (c) Framed Quadtree
- Each subdivided quadrant shares boundaries with siblings to ensure topological consistency.
- Critical for guaranteeing **graph connectivity** between parent and child cells.

### 2.3 The Mixed-Cell Problem and Solutions

| Strategy | Description | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Conservative Marking** | Mark mixed cell as `BLOCKED` | Safest; may lose valid paths |
| **Optimistic Marking** | Mark mixed cell as `FREE` | May produce **infeasible paths** through obstacles |
| **Recursive Subdivision** | Split mixed cell into smaller cells (Quadtree) | Preserves resolution completeness |
| **Boundary Tracing** | Treat mixed cell boundary as a navigable edge | Complex; used in exact decompositions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, students **must** be able to explain why Approximate Cell Decomposition is *not* complete in the strict sense, but is *resolution-complete*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of cells along X | $N_x = \left\lceil \dfrac{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}{\delta} \right\rceil$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Number of cells along Y | $N_y = \left\lceil \dfrac{y_{\max} - y_{\min}}{\delta} \right\rceil$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Total cells | $N = N_x \cdot N_y$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Memory footprint | $M = N \cdot b$ bits, $b$ = bits/cell | bits |
| 5 | Coverage ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Volume of approximate free space}}{\text{Volume of true free space}}$ | ratio (ideally $\to 1$) |
| 6 | Narrow-passage miss probability | $P_{\text{miss}} \propto \dfrac{\delta}{w_{\text{passage}}}$ | $w_{\text{passage}}$ = passage width |
| 7 | Graph search complexity (A*) | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ | for uniform grid |
| 8 | Time to classify all cells | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot T_{\text{col}})$ | $T_{\text{col}}$ = collision check time |
| 9 | Path length bound | $L_{\text{path}} \le L_{\text{opt}} + N \cdot \delta$ | additive error in path length |
| 10 | Cell index (row-major) | $i = j \cdot N_x + k$ | $j$ = row, $k$ = column |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Do not use the vertical bar `|` inside markdown tables** (e.g., for absolute value). It will break the table renderer. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX mode instead. Example: write $\lvert x \rvert$, not `|x|`.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva)**: ACD with fixed grid for shelf-to-shelf navigation.
- **Autonomous AGVs in factories**: Pre-mapped factory floor decomposed into uniform grid cells.
- **Robot Vacuum Cleaners (Roomba)**: Uses grid-based decomposition for room coverage.
- **Game AI**: Tile-based pathfinding in RTS games (StarCraft).
- **Drone Swarms**: ACD in 3D (Octree) for indoor flight corridors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Cell Count in a Bounded C-space

**Given:** A 2D configuration space $\mathcal{C} = [x_{\min}, x_{\max}] \times [y_{\min}, y_{\max}]$ decomposed using cells of side length $\delta$.

**Step 1 — Define the lengths of the C-space along each axis:**

$$L_x \triangleq x_{\max} - x_{\min}$$

$$L_y \triangleq y_{\max} - y_{\min}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the *ideal* (non-integer) number of cells that would perfectly tile the space:**

$$N_x^{\text{ideal}} = \frac{L_x}{\delta}, \qquad N_y^{\text{ideal}} = \frac{L_y}{\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Round up using the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ to ensure full coverage (the last row/column may be partially outside $\mathcal{C}$):**

$$N_x = \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{\delta} \right\rceil, \qquad N_y = \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{\delta} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4 — Total number of cells in the decomposition:**

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{total}} = N_x \cdot N_y = \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{\delta} \right\rceil \cdot \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{\delta} \right\rceil\,}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The ceiling operation guarantees that every point of the bounded C-space lies within *at least one* cell, even when the dimensions are not exact integer multiples of $\delta$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Resolution-Complete Path Length Bound

**Statement:** For a uniform grid ACD with cell side $\delta$, any path $P$ found by graph search satisfies the following bound relative to the optimal path $P^*$:

$$L(P) \;\le\; L(P^*) \;+\; N_{\text{hops}} \cdot \delta$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Decompose the optimal path $P^*$ into cell-to-cell hops.** A path through the grid crosses at most one cell boundary per hop. If the optimal path visits $H$ cells (the number of cells along the optimal route through the grid), then:

$$H \;\le\; N_{\text{hops}}^{\max} = N_x + N_y - 2$$

*Conversion logic:* In the worst case, the optimal path must traverse the grid from one corner to the opposite corner, requiring one hop per row plus one per column.

**Step 2 — Each hop has a maximum geometric error of $\delta$:** Because the planner picks a representative point (e.g., the cell center) rather than following the exact optimal trajectory, the path deviates by at most one cell-diagonal per hop:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{per hop}} \;\le\; \delta \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case total deviation accumulates over $H$ hops:**

$$L(P) \;\le\; L(P^*) \;+\; H \cdot \delta\sqrt{2}$$

$$\Longrightarrow \quad L(P) \;\le\; L(P^*) \;+\; (N_x + N_y - 2)\cdot \delta\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 4 — As $\delta \to 0$, the bound $L(P) \to L(P^*)$:**

$$\lim_{\delta \to 0} L(P) = L(P^*)$$

> *Conversion logic:* This limit proves that **finer resolution asymptotically recovers the optimal path**, which is the formal justification of *resolution-completeness* in ACD.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Narrow-Passage Detection Probability

**Problem:** How likely is ACD with cell size $\delta$ to detect a corridor of width $w$ between two obstacles?

**Step 1 — A corridor of width $w$ is *detectable* if and only if at least one grid cell fits entirely inside it:**

$$w \;\ge\; \delta$$

**Step 2 — Probability that a randomly aligned grid has a cell centered inside the corridor:**

$$P_{\text{detect}} = \min\!\left(1, \frac{w}{\delta}\right), \quad \text{when } w < \delta$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the *miss* probability:**

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{miss}} = 1 - \min\!\left(1, \frac{w}{\delta}\right) = \max\!\left(0, 1 - \frac{w}{\delta}\right)\,}$$

> *Conversion logic:* If the corridor is narrower than the cell ($\delta > w$), uniform grids fail to represent the passage. This is the principal failure mode of ACD and the motivation for **adaptive (quadtree) decomposition**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board-Ready)

| Property | Uniform Grid ACD | Adaptive (Quadtree) ACD |
|---|---|---|
| Cell shape | All squares, equal size | Variable-size squares |
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ worst-case | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ |
| Narrow passages | Often missed | Detected via subdivision |
| Implementation complexity | Low | High |
| Resolution completeness | Yes (as $\delta \to 0$) | Yes (with bounded depth) |
| Path optimality | Bounded by $\delta$ | Bounded by min $\delta_{\text{leaf}}$ |
| Time complexity | $\mathcal{O}(N + E)$ for BFS | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of Approximate Cell Decomposition

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the end-to-end pipeline of a typical ACD motion planner, mapping the flow from raw C-space input to the final executed robot trajectory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CSPACE INPUT: Bounded Configuration Space C"] --> B["RESOLUTION SELECT: Choose cell side delta"]
    B --> C["TILE GRID: Partition C into Nx by Ny cells"]
    C --> D["CELL CLASSIFY: For each cell check collision with obstacles"]
    D --> E{"CELL TYPE"}
    E --> F["FREE CELL: mark as traversable node"]
    E --> G["OBSTACLE CELL: mark as blocked node"]
    E --> H["MIXED CELL: subdivide quadtree or mark blocked"]
    F --> I["BUILD GRAPH G: Nodes V = free cells, Edges E = adjacency"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["GRAPH SEARCH: A star or BFS from start s to goal g"]
    J --> K{"PATH FOUND"}
    K --> L["OUTPUT: Sequence of cell centers forming path"]
    K --> M["OUTPUT: Failure: no path at this resolution"]
    L --> N["PATH SMOOTH: Optional shortcutting and interpolation"]
    N --> O["EXECUTE: Send trajectory to robot controller"]
    M --> P["REFINE: Reduce delta and replan"]
    P --> B
```

### 4.2 Quadtree Recursive Subdivision (Mixed-Cell Handling)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["ROOT CELL: Mixed obstacle boundary"] --> Q1["SUBQ1 NW: Free"]
    R --> Q2["SUBQ2 NE: Mixed"]
    R --> Q3["SUBQ3 SW: Obstacle"]
    R --> Q4["SUBQ4 SE: Free"]
    Q2 --> S1["LEAF A: Free"]
    Q2 --> S2["LEAF B: Obstacle"]
    Q2 --> S3["LEAF C: Free"]
    Q2 --> S4["LEAF D: Mixed small corridor"]
    S4 --> T1["LEAF T1: Free"]
    S4 --> T2["LEAF T2: Free"]
```

> *Interpretation:* The diagram shows a **quadtree decomposition** where a root cell with a mixed obstacle is recursively subdivided. The terminal leaf `T1` and `T2` finally resolve the narrow corridor that the coarse grid would have missed.

### 4.3 Resolution-Complete Coverage Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Coarse delta: misses corridor, reports no path"] --> M1["Medium delta: corridor appears, planner succeeds"]
    M1 --> F1["Fine delta: path converges to optimal solution"]
    F1 --> ZF["Limit delta to 0: exact optimal path"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram reinforces the analytical result derived in §3.2: the planner's solution quality **monotonically improves** as $\delta$ decreases, justifying resolution-completeness.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Approximate Cell Decomposition. Why is it called "approximate"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Approximate Cell Decomposition is a motion planning technique that partitions the robot's **Configuration Space (C-space)** into regular-shaped cells (typically squares) of side length $\delta$, classifies each cell as **FREE** or **BLOCKED** based on obstacle occupancy, and searches a graph over the free cells for a path from start to goal.

**(1 mark)** *Definition of ACD as grid-based decomposition of C-space.*

It is called *"approximate"* **(1 mark)** because the boundaries of the free space are not represented exactly — they are **snapped to the grid resolution**, and any *mixed cell* (partly free, partly blocked) must be classified by a heuristic rule, thereby losing some precision in the obstacle boundary.

**(1 mark)** *Identification of mixed-cell handling as the source of approximation error.*

> 🎯 **Examiner Note:** Award full 3 marks only if both **definition** and **the reason it is approximate** are explicitly mentioned. Vague answers like *"it is not exact"* alone earn only 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by resolution-completeness in the context of Approximate Cell Decomposition?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Resolution-completeness means that **if a solution path exists, the planner is guaranteed to find it provided the cell resolution $\delta$ is fine enough** (sufficiently small).

**(1 mark)** *Definition: planner finds a path if one exists at sufficient resolution.*

If the cell size is too large, narrow passages or tight maneuvers may be **discretized away**, causing the planner to incorrectly report failure.

**(1 mark)** *Explanation: coarse grids can lose narrow free-space corridors.*

As $\delta \to 0$, the discretized free space approaches the true continuous free space, and the planner's solution converges to the true optimal path.

**(1 mark)** *Limit condition: $\delta \to 0$ gives convergence to true solution.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of **Approximate Cell Decomposition** for robot motion planning. Discuss how mixed cells are handled. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D C-space of dimensions $12\,\text{m} \times 8\,\text{m}$ is decomposed using a uniform grid with cell side $\delta = 0.5\,\text{m}$. Calculate the total number of cells, the worst-case path length bound, and discuss the narrow-passage detection capability for a corridor of width $w = 0.3\,\text{m}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

##### Part (a) — Working Principle and Mixed-Cell Handling **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: Definition and Setup** *(1 mark)*

Approximate Cell Decomposition (ACD) discretizes the bounded Configuration Space $\mathcal{C} = [x_{\min}, x_{\max}] \times [y_{\min}, y_{\max}]$ into a finite grid of cells of side length $\delta$. The configuration space is converted into a discrete search problem.

**Step 2: Pipeline Stages** *(3 marks)*

The working pipeline consists of:
1. **Discretization** — Tile $\mathcal{C}$ into $N_x \times N_y$ cells.
2. **Cell Classification** — Each cell is collision-checked; marked as `FREE` or `BLOCKED`.
3. **Graph Construction** — Free cells become graph nodes; 4-connectivity defines edges.
4. **Graph Search** — A*, BFS, or Dijkstra finds a path from start cell to goal cell.
5. **Path Extraction** — The sequence of cell centers forms the robot trajectory.

**Step 3: Mixed-Cell Handling** *(2 marks)*

A mixed cell overlaps both free and obstacle regions. The two standard strategies are:

- **Conservative (Safe) Marking** — Mark the cell as `BLOCKED`. This guarantees that the planned path stays in collision-free cells but may **incorrectly report infeasibility** if a narrow passage passes through the cell.
- **Optimistic Marking** — Mark the cell as `FREE`. This preserves connectivity but may generate **infeasible paths** that graze obstacles.
- **Best Practice (Quadtree)** — Recursively subdivide the mixed cell into four quadrants until each leaf is purely free or purely blocked.

**Step 4: Concluding Statement** *(1 mark)*

ACD is **resolution-complete** but **not exact**: the planned path is an approximation of the true optimal trajectory, with path-length error bounded by the cell resolution $\delta$.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Analysis **[7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $L_x = 12\,\text{m}$, $L_y = 8\,\text{m}$, $\delta = 0.5\,\text{m}$, $w = 0.3\,\text{m}$.

**Step 1: Number of cells** *(2 marks)*

$$N_x = \left\lceil \frac{L_x}{\delta} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{12}{0.5} \right\rceil = 24$$

$$N_y = \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{\delta} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{8}{0.5} \right\rceil = 16$$

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{total}} = 24 \times 16 = 384 \text{ cells}\,}$$

**Step 2: Worst-case path length bound** *(3 marks)*

The maximum number of hops is the corner-to-corner traversal:

$$H_{\max} = N_x + N_y - 2 = 24 + 16 - 2 = 38 \text{ hops}$$

The worst-case path length is:

$$L_{\text{max}} = L_{\text{opt}} + H_{\max} \cdot \delta\sqrt{2} = L_{\text{opt}} + 38 \times 0.5 \times \sqrt{2}$$

$$L_{\text{max}} = L_{\text{opt}} + 19\sqrt{2} = L_{\text{opt}} + 26.87\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\,L_{\text{max}} \approx L_{\text{opt}} + 26.87\,\text{m}\,}$$

**Step 3: Narrow-passage detection for $w = 0.3\,\text{m}$** *(2 marks)*

Since $w = 0.3\,\text{m} < \delta = 0.5\,\text{m}$, the corridor is **narrower than one cell**.

$$P_{\text{miss}} = 1 - \frac{w}{\delta} = 1 - \frac{0.3}{0.5} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{\text{miss}} = 40\%\,}$$

> **Conclusion:** With this grid resolution, there is a **40% chance** the corridor is misclassified as blocked. The planner would benefit from **adaptive (quadtree) subdivision** or a finer resolution $\delta \le 0.3\,\text{m}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $L_{\max} = L_{\text{opt}} + \delta$ (forgetting to multiply by hops $H$). Loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Reporting $N = 12 \times 8 = 96$ without applying the ceiling function with $\delta$. Loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Not mentioning the $\sqrt{2}$ diagonal hop distance. Loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Computing $P_{\text{miss}}$ as a percentage but writing 0.4 instead of 40%. Always include the **%** symbol.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Uniform Grid Decomposition** with **Adaptive (Quadtree) Decomposition**. Use a labeled diagram of the quadtree subdivision process in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **path length error bound** for an ACD planner with cell side $\delta$, and show that the planner is **resolution-complete** by taking the limit $\delta \to 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

##### Part (a) — Uniform vs Adaptive (Quadtree) Decomposition **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: Uniform Grid — Definition and Properties** *(2 marks)*

A **uniform grid** partitions the C-space into $N_x \times N_y$ cells of identical size $\delta$. Each cell is independently classified as free or blocked. A graph is built over the free cells, and a shortest-path search (A*, BFS, Dijkstra) is performed.

*Advantages:* Simplicity, predictable memory, easy parallelization.
*Disadvantages:* Wastes resolution in open regions, fails to detect narrow passages smaller than $\delta$.

**Step 2: Quadtree Decomposition — Definition and Properties** *(2 marks)*

A **quadtree** recursively subdivides any cell that is *mixed* (partially free, partially blocked) into four equal quadrants (NW, NE, SW, SE). The recursion continues until either (i) a maximum depth is reached, or (ii) every leaf cell is purely free or purely blocked.

*Advantages:* Adaptive resolution; narrow passages are detected via deep subdivision near obstacles. Memory-efficient: open regions are stored as coarse leaves.
*Disadvantages:* Higher implementation complexity; path-finding graph requires careful sibling/parent edge construction (the *framed quadtree* property).

**Step 3: Comparative Table** *(2 marks)*

| Aspect | Uniform Grid | Quadtree (Adaptive) |
|---|---|---|
| Cell size | Constant $\delta$ | Variable, $\delta / 2^k$ at depth $k$ |
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ typical |
| Narrow-passage handling | Misses if $w < \delta$ | Detects via subdivision |
| Build time | Fast | Slower due to recursion |
| Path optimality | Bounded by $\delta$ | Bounded by $\min \delta_{\text{leaf}}$ |

**Step 4: Labeled Quadtree Diagram** *(1 mark)*

> (Refer to Section 4.2 — Quadtree Recursive Subdivision Diagram in the notes.)

The diagram shows a root mixed cell subdivided into 4 quadrants. The NE quadrant, itself mixed, is further subdivided into 4 leaves, two of which finally resolve a narrow corridor.

##### Part (b) — Path Length Error Bound and Resolution-Completeness Proof **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: Setup and Definitions** *(1 mark)*

Let $P^*$ be the optimal (shortest) collision-free path in continuous C-space $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$, with length $L(P^*)$. Let $P$ be the path returned by the ACD planner traversing the centers of free cells from $s$ to $g$. Each cell has side length $\delta$.

**Step 2: Bound on the number of hops** *(2 marks)*

In a grid of $N_x \times N_y$ cells, any path that crosses from one corner to the opposite corner requires at most:

$$H \le (N_x - 1) + (N_y - 1) = N_x + N_y - 2$$

*Conversion logic:* Each hop advances the path by one cell in either the X or Y direction; the maximum number of such hops is the L1 (Manhattan) distance between opposite corners.

**Step 3: Per-hop geometric error bound** *(2 marks)*

The shortest distance between the centers of two adjacent cells is the cell side $\delta$ (4-connectivity), or $\delta\sqrt{2}$ (8-connectivity, diagonal hop). The true optimal path, if it must pass through both cells, deviates from the straight cell-to-cell line by at most $\delta\sqrt{2}$ per hop. Hence:

$$L(P) \le L(P^*) + H \cdot \delta\sqrt{2} \le L(P^*) + (N_x + N_y - 2)\cdot \delta\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 4: Resolution-completeness by limit** *(2 marks)*

Taking the limit as $\delta \to 0$:

$$\lim_{\delta \to 0} L(P) = L(P^*) + 0 = L(P^*)$$

*Conversion logic:* Since the bound on the gap between the planner's path and the optimal path vanishes as the cell size shrinks, the planner's output **converges to the true optimal solution**. Combined with the fact that ACD correctly classifies any *sufficiently resolved* corridor (via the narrow-passage condition $w \ge \delta$), we conclude:

$$\boxed{\,\text{Approximate Cell Decomposition is resolution-complete.}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Stating "ACD is complete" without the qualifier *"resolution-"*. This is **factually wrong** and loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to specify **8-connectivity** vs **4-connectivity** in the per-hop error. Either is acceptable if justified.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Writing $L(P) \le L(P^*) + \delta$ instead of the full $H \cdot \delta\sqrt{2}$ term. Loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Omitting the final limit step, which is the core of the resolution-completeness argument.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Approximate Cell Decomposition**

- 🟢 **Definition:** Discretize C-space into regular (typically square) cells of side $\delta$; classify each as FREE / BLOCKED; search a graph over free cells.
- 🟢 **Three Pillars:**
  1. *Discretization* (grid tiling)
  2. *Classification* (collision check)
  3. *Search* (graph algorithm)
- 🟢 **Total cell count:** $N = \lceil L_x / \delta \rceil \cdot \lceil L_y / \delta \rceil$.
- 🟢 **Mixed-cell problem:** The defining weakness of ACD; handled via conservative marking, optimistic marking, or quadtree subdivision.
- 🟢 **Resolution-completeness:** Yes, *if* $\delta$ is small enough; **NOT** strict completeness.
- 🟢 **Path length bound:** $L(P) \le L(P^*) + H \cdot \delta\sqrt{2}$, where $H = N_x + N_y - 2$ worst-case.
- 🟢 **Narrow-passage detection:** Fails when $w < \delta$ → $P_{\text{miss}} = 1 - w/\delta$.
- 🟢 **Variants:** Uniform grid (simple), Quadtree (adaptive), Octree (3D extension).
- 🟢 **Complexity:** Build $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot T_{\text{col}})$, search $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ for A*.
- 🟢 **Real-world uses:** AGVs, vacuum robots, warehouse bots, game AI, drone swarms.
- 🟢 **Examiner triggers:** Always mention *"resolution-complete, not exact"*, *"mixed-cell handling"*, and *"graph search over free cells"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:** Always draw the **discretized grid figure** whenever you are asked to *explain* ACD in Part B. A neat diagram with labeled free / blocked / mixed cells is worth at least **2 marks by itself**, even before any text is written.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT632 - ROBOT MOTION PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Velocity Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Tuning** is the post-processing stage in robot motion planning where a pre-computed geometric path (in either joint space or Cartesian space) is parameterized by time so that the resulting trajectory satisfies all kinematic and dynamic limits of the manipulator — namely **maximum joint velocities**, **maximum joint accelerations**, and **maximum joint jerks** — while simultaneously attempting to minimize the total traversal time (time-optimality) or track a user-specified time law.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, velocity tuning sits between **path planning** (purely geometric, time-independent) and **trajectory generation** (time-parameterized output). Its sole objective is to assign a scalar time-scaling function $s(t)$ (with $s(0)=0$, $s(T)=1$) to the geometric path $q(s)$ so that the commanded joint motion

$$q(t) = q\bigl(s(t)\bigr), \quad \dot{q}(t) = q'(s)\,\dot{s}(t), \quad \ddot{q}(t) = q''(s)\,\dot{s}^{2}(t) + q'(s)\,\ddot{s}(t)$$

never violates any actuator-level constraint.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Road Driver

Imagine you are driving along a pre-decided mountain route (the **path**). The road is fixed — every curve, slope, and hairpin bend is already chosen. Your job as a driver is to **decide how fast to go at every point**. You:

1. Accelerate on straight, flat sections.
2. Decelerate before sharp bends.
3. Never exceed the speed limit, the engine's red-line, or the brake's thermal capacity.

Velocity tuning is exactly this: the **path** is the road, the **robot dynamics + actuator limits** are the speed-limit signs, and the **time-scaling function $s(t)$** is the accelerator-pedal command. The geometric path is untouched; only *when* the robot reaches each waypoint is changed.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Geometric Path | $q(s)$ | Joint configuration as a function of path parameter $s \in [0,1]$ |
| Time Scaling | $s(t)$ | Monotone map from time $t \in [0,T]$ to $s \in [0,1]$ |
| Path Velocity Bound | $\dot{s}_{\max}(s)$ | Maximum admissible $\dot{s}$ at parameter $s$ |
| Path Acceleration Bound | $\ddot{s}_{\max}(s)$ | Maximum admissible $\ddot{s}$ at parameter $s$ |
| Synchronization Time | $T_{\mathrm{sync}}$ | Common time at which all axes finish the move |
| Velocity Ratio | $\lambda_i$ | Per-axis scale factor, $0 < \lambda_i \le 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Velocity tuning is classified as a *constraint-satisfying trajectory refinement* technique. It is an integral part of the pipeline *Planner → Path Interpolator → Velocity Tuner → Servo Command*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Canonical Velocity Profiles

### 2.1.1 Trapezoidal Velocity Profile (Constant Acceleration)

The simplest admissible time-law. The acceleration is **piecewise constant**, producing a velocity profile shaped like a trapezoid: ramp-up → cruise → ramp-down.

- **Phase 1 (Acceleration):** $\ddot{s} = a_{\max}$, $0 \le t \le t_a$
- **Phase 2 (Cruise):** $\ddot{s} = 0$, $t_a \le t \le t_a + t_c$
- **Phase 3 (Deceleration):** $\ddot{s} = -a_{\max}$, $t_a + t_c \le t \le T$

**Why it is used:** Trapezoidal profiles are computationally trivial, easy to implement on industrial controllers, and satisfy simple velocity/acceleration bounds. **How it fails:** They contain infinite jerk (acceleration discontinuities at $t_a$ and $t_a+t_c$), which excites mechanical resonances and reduces tracking accuracy on high-precision robots.

### 2.1.2 S-Curve Velocity Profile (Jerk-Limited)

The acceleration itself is ramped, producing a smooth S-shaped velocity curve. This requires bounding the **jerk** $j = \dddot{s}$ in addition to $\dot{s}$ and $\ddot{s}$.

- **Phase 1:** $j = j_{\max}$, acceleration builds linearly.
- **Phase 2:** $\ddot{s} = a_{\max}$, acceleration held constant.
- **Phase 3:** $j = -j_{\max}$, acceleration ramped down.
- (Symmetric deceleration block follows.)

**Why it is used:** Bounded jerk eliminates mechanical vibration, reduces wear on gearboxes, and is mandatory for **high-speed pick-and-place** and **collaborative robots**. **How it is costlier:** Requires seven-segment planning and more online computation.

## 2.2 The Path-Velocity Constraint Surface

For each joint $i$ ($i = 1,\dots,n$) and for each point $s$ along the geometric path, the joint velocity limit $V_i^{\max}$ imposes a constraint on the admissible scalar $\dot{s}$:

$$\vert q_i'(s) \vert \, \dot{s}(t) \;\le\; V_i^{\max}$$

Rearranging gives the **path-dependent velocity ceiling**:

$$\dot{s}_{\max}(s) \;=\; \min_{i}\left\{\dfrac{V_i^{\max}}{\max\bigl(\epsilon,\;\vert q_i'(s)\vert\bigr)}\right\}$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small positive constant that prevents division by zero at singular configurations. The acceleration ceiling is similarly derived:

$$\ddot{s}_{\max}(s) \;=\; \min_{i}\left\{\dfrac{A_i^{\max} - \bigl(\text{centripetal term from } q_i''\bigr)\,\dot{s}^{2}}{\vert q_i'(s)\vert}\right\}$$

## 2.3 Synchronization of Multi-Axis Motion

When each joint is independently commanded using its own trapezoidal profile, the joints typically arrive at the goal at **different times**. This is unacceptable — it causes path deviation. Velocity tuning therefore introduces a **master cycle time $T_{\mathrm{sync}}$** and per-joint scaling factors $\lambda_i \in (0,1]$ such that every joint's profile is uniformly stretched to occupy exactly $T_{\mathrm{sync}}$ seconds.

The classical Holz-Walter / Kahan synchronization rule states:

$$\lambda_i \;=\; \dfrac{T_i^{\min}}{T_{\mathrm{sync}}}, \quad T_{\mathrm{sync}} \;=\; \max_i T_i^{\min}$$

where $T_i^{\min}$ is the minimum time joint $i$ would need if it moved alone. The slowest joint is given $\lambda = 1$ (no scaling); every other joint is slowed down proportionally.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Variable Meaning | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trapezoidal cruise time | $t_c = T - 2t_a$ | $T$ = total time, $t_a$ = accel ramp | $t_c \ge 0$ |
| Trapezoidal accel time | $t_a = \dot{s}_{\max} / a_{\max}$ | $a_{\max}$ = max path accel | $\dot{s}_{\max} \le \tfrac{a_{\max}T}{2}$ |
| Minimum trapezoidal time | $T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{\Delta s / a_{\max}}$ | $\Delta s$ = path length | cruise vanishes when $T=T_{\min}$ |
| Path-velocity ceiling | $\dot{s}_{\max}(s) = \min_i \bigl(V_i^{\max} / \vert q_i'(s)\vert\bigr)$ | per-joint limit $V_i^{\max}$ | $\dot{s} > 0$ always |
| S-curve jerk time | $t_j = a_{\max} / j_{\max}$ | $j_{\max}$ = max jerk | bounded |
| Synchronization scale | $\lambda_i = T_i^{\min} / T_{\mathrm{sync}}$ | per-axis | $\lambda_i \le 1$ |
| Joint velocity | $\dot{q}_i(t) = q_i'(s)\,\dot{s}(t)$ | product rule on path | $\le V_i^{\max}$ |
| Joint acceleration | $\ddot{q}_i = q_i''\dot{s}^2 + q_i'\ddot{s}$ | path + time components | $\le A_i^{\max}$ |
| Total traversal time | $T = \int_0^1 \dfrac{ds}{\dot{s}_{\max}(s)}$ | integral of ceiling | time-optimal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Velocity tuning is the de-facto bridge between offline path planners (RRT*, PRM) and real-time industrial controllers (FANUC, KUKA, ABB). It guarantees that theoretically smooth paths remain physically executable on hardware with hard torque, velocity, and jerk ceilings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Trapezoidal Profile Time

**Goal:** Given path length $\Delta s$, peak admissible path-velocity $v_p$, and peak admissible path-acceleration $a_p$, find the smallest total time $T_{\min}$ such that $s(t)$ goes from $0$ to $\Delta s$ without exceeding $v_p$ or $a_p$.

### Step 1 — Set up the kinematic assumption

We adopt a symmetric trapezoid: acceleration $a_p$ for time $t_a$, cruise at $v_p$ for time $t_c$, deceleration $-a_p$ for time $t_a$.

$$\dot{s}(t) = \begin{cases} a_p\,t & 0 \le t \le t_a \\ v_p & t_a \le t \le t_a+t_c \\ v_p - a_p\,(t-t_a-t_c) & t_a+t_c \le t \le T \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Impose velocity continuity

Velocity must rise monotonically to $v_p$ during phase 1:

$$v_p = a_p\,t_a \;\Longrightarrow\; t_a = \dfrac{v_p}{a_p}$$

### Step 3 — Express total time

$$T = 2t_a + t_c = \dfrac{2v_p}{a_p} + t_c$$

### Step 4 — Express total displacement

$$\Delta s = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\,v_p\,t_a}_{\text{accel triangle}} + \underbrace{v_p\,t_c}_{\text{cruise rectangle}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\,v_p\,t_a}_{\text{decel triangle}} = v_p\,t_c + \dfrac{v_p^2}{a_p}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $t_c$

$$t_c = \dfrac{\Delta s}{v_p} - \dfrac{v_p}{a_p}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute back to get $T$

$$T = \dfrac{2v_p}{a_p} + \dfrac{\Delta s}{v_p} - \dfrac{v_p}{a_p} = \dfrac{\Delta s}{v_p} + \dfrac{v_p}{a_p}$$

This is the standard trapezoidal total-time formula.

### Step 7 — Minimize $T$ with respect to $v_p$

Treating $v_p$ as a free variable and minimizing:

$$\dfrac{dT}{dv_p} = -\dfrac{\Delta s}{v_p^2} + \dfrac{1}{a_p} = 0$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta s}{v_p^2} = \dfrac{1}{a_p} \;\Longrightarrow\; v_p^{*} = \sqrt{a_p\,\Delta s}$$

### Step 8 — Insert $v_p^{*}$ back into $T$

$$T_{\min} = \dfrac{\Delta s}{\sqrt{a_p\,\Delta s}} + \dfrac{\sqrt{a_p\,\Delta s}}{a_p} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta s}{a_p}} + \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta s}{a_p}}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{\min} \;=\; 2\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta s}{a_p}}\,}$$

At this optimum, $t_c = 0$, so the trapezoid degenerates into a **triangle** (no cruise phase). **Interpretation:** for the shortest possible move, accelerate at the maximum rate the entire first half and decelerate at the maximum rate the entire second half.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Synchronization Scale Factor

**Goal:** Show that choosing $T_{\mathrm{sync}} = \max_i T_i^{\min}$ and $\lambda_i = T_i^{\min} / T_{\mathrm{sync}}$ produces a common arrival time without violating any per-joint bound.

### Step 1 — Define the unconstrained single-joint time

If joint $i$ moved independently with its own trapezoidal profile from start $q_i^s$ to goal $q_i^g$:

$$T_i^{\min} = 2\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\vert q_i^g - q_i^s\vert}{a_i^{\max}}}$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the scale factor $\lambda_i$

A scale factor $\lambda_i$ uniformly stretches the velocity and acceleration of joint $i$:

$$v_i^{\mathrm{new}} = \lambda_i\,v_i^{\min}, \quad a_i^{\mathrm{new}} = \lambda_i^2\,a_i^{\min}$$

(This follows because accelerating for longer by factor $1/\lambda_i$ requires smaller acceleration by $\lambda_i^2$.)

### Step 3 — New traversal time of joint $i$

$$T_i^{\mathrm{new}} = \dfrac{T_i^{\min}}{\lambda_i}$$

### Step 4 — Enforce common arrival

Set $T_i^{\mathrm{new}} = T_{\mathrm{sync}}$ for every $i$:

$$\lambda_i = \dfrac{T_i^{\min}}{T_{\mathrm{sync}}}$$

### Step 5 — Choose the longest time as the master clock

To avoid asking any joint to move faster than its solo minimum:

$$T_{\mathrm{sync}} \;\ge\; \max_i T_i^{\min}$$

Choosing equality gives:

$$\boxed{\,\lambda_i = \dfrac{T_i^{\min}}{\max_k T_k^{\min}}, \quad T_{\mathrm{sync}} = \max_i T_i^{\min}\,}$$

The slowest joint retains $\lambda = 1$; faster joints are decelerated. The geometric path is exactly preserved because all joints reach their intermediate path-points at the *same* normalized parameter $s$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 2-DOF robot must move joint 1 by $\Delta q_1 = 60° = \pi/3$ rad and joint 2 by $\Delta q_2 = 90° = \pi/2$ rad. Limits: $a_1^{\max} = a_2^{\max} = 2$ rad/s².

**Step A — Compute solo minimum times.**

$$T_1^{\min} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi/3}{2}} = 2\sqrt{0.5236} = 2 \times 0.7235 = 1.4470 \text{ s}$$

$$T_2^{\min} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi/2}{2}} = 2\sqrt{0.7854} = 2 \times 0.8862 = 1.7724 \text{ s}$$

**Step B — Identify the slowest joint.**

$$T_{\mathrm{sync}} = \max(1.4470,\;1.7724) = 1.7724 \text{ s}$$

**Step C — Compute scale factors.**

$$\lambda_1 = \dfrac{1.4470}{1.7724} = 0.8164, \quad \lambda_2 = \dfrac{1.7724}{1.7724} = 1.0000$$

**Step D — Compute new velocities and accelerations.**

Let original $v_1^{\min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \pi/3} = 1.4470$ rad/s, $v_2^{\min} = 1.7724$ rad/s.

$$v_1^{\mathrm{new}} = 0.8164 \times 1.4470 = 1.1813 \text{ rad/s}, \quad v_2^{\mathrm{new}} = 1.7724 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$a_1^{\mathrm{new}} = (0.8164)^2 \times 2 = 1.3333 \text{ rad/s}^2, \quad a_2^{\mathrm{new}} = 2.0000 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

**Step E — Verify joint 1.**

$$T_1^{\mathrm{new}} = \dfrac{2\,\Delta q_1}{v_1^{\mathrm{new}}} = \dfrac{2 \times \pi/3}{1.1813} = 1.7724 \text{ s} \;\checkmark$$

Both joints now arrive in exactly $1.7724$ s while staying inside their acceleration bounds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Velocity-Tuning Functional Architecture

The diagram below captures the full data-flow inside a velocity-tuning module: starting from a pre-computed geometric path and ending at servo-level joint commands. Every node is given an alphanumeric identifier and a clean text label (no inline markdown) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Geometric Path q of s] --> A1[Compute Path Derivatives q prime and q double prime]
    A1 --> A2[Read Joint Limits V max and A max]
    A2 --> B0[Build Path Velocity Ceiling s dot max of s]
    B0 --> B1[Build Path Acceleration Ceiling s double dot max of s]
    B1 --> C0[Select Profile Type Trapezoidal or S Curve]
    C0 --> C1[Profile Generator Initial Guess of v peak]
    C1 --> C2[Constraint Checker compare against s dot max and s double dot max]
    C2 --> C3{Violation Detected}
    C3 -- Yes --> C4[Reduce v peak by scale lambda]
    C4 --> C2
    C3 -- No --> D0[Compute Per Joint Scale Factors lambda i]
    D0 --> D1[Synchronization Block compute T sync]
    D1 --> D2[Stretch Profiles to Common Time]
    D2 --> E0[Output Time Parameterized Trajectory q of t and dq of t and ddq of t]
    E0 --> E1[Downstream Servo Controller]

    style A0 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style C3 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
    style D1 fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Trapezoidal Profile State Machine

This second diagram shows the internal state transitions of a trapezoidal generator. Each block is a phase of motion.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AccelPhase
    AccelPhase --> CruisePhase: when s dot reaches v peak
    CruisePhase --> DecelPhase: when remaining path requires braking
    DecelPhase --> DonePhase: when s equals one
    AccelPhase --> DonePhase: triangular case no cruise
    DecelPhase --> DonePhase
    DonePhase --> [*]

    note right of AccelPhase: s double dot equals plus a max
    note right of CruisePhase: s double dot equals zero
    note right of DecelPhase: s double dot equals minus a max
```

## 4.3 Reading the Diagrams

- The **blue input node `A0`** is the only entry point; it carries the *time-independent* geometric path produced upstream by the path planner.
- The **red decision node `C3`** is the iterative refinement loop. Velocity tuning is rarely a one-shot computation — the profile generator proposes a peak velocity, the constraint checker rejects it if it exceeds $\dot{s}_{\max}(s)$ or $\ddot{s}_{\max}(s)$ anywhere along the path, and the scale factor is reduced until feasibility is achieved.
- The **orange synchronization block `D1`** transforms the per-axis independent times into a single common motion duration.
- The **green output node `E1`** is the consumer — a low-level servo loop that tracks $q(t)$, $\dot{q}(t)$, $\ddot{q}(t)$ at a typical rate of 1 kHz.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define velocity tuning in robot motion planning. Why is it necessary even after a collision-free geometric path has been obtained?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Velocity tuning is the process of assigning a time-scaling function $s(t)$ to a given geometric path $q(s)$ such that the resulting joint trajectories $\dot{q}(t)$ and $\ddot{q}(t)$ remain within the actuator limits $V_i^{\max}$ and $A_i^{\max}$ of all joints, while attempting to minimize the total traversal time $T$.

It is necessary because a geometric path alone specifies **where** the robot goes but not **how fast**. Without tuning, the commanded velocities and accelerations may exceed joint motor ratings, triggering protective stops, mechanical vibrations, or servo tracking errors. Velocity tuning thus guarantees that the planned path is also *physically executable* on the real hardware. (3 marks)

### Question A2  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare trapezoidal and S-curve velocity profiles. State one merit and one demerit of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Trapezoidal | S-Curve |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Acceleration | Piecewise constant (step changes) | Linearly ramped, jerk-bounded |
| Merits | Simple to compute, easy real-time implementation, minimal memory | Smooth motion, suppresses vibration, protects gearboxes |
| Demerits | Infinite jerk at transitions, excites resonances | More complex, requires jerk limit, higher online computation |

(3 marks — 1.5 for merits, 1.5 for demerits.)

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Derive the minimum traversal time $T_{\min}$ for a trapezoidal velocity profile over a path of length $\Delta s$ subject to maximum path acceleration $a_p$. Show that at the optimum the cruise phase vanishes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-DOF Cartesian robot must translate by $\Delta x = 0.5$ m, $\Delta y = 0.3$ m, $\Delta z = 0.4$ m simultaneously along a straight-line path. Maximum joint accelerations are $a_x^{\max} = 1$ m/s², $a_y^{\max} = 0.5$ m/s², $a_z^{\max} = 2$ m/s². Using the synchronization rule, compute the per-axis scale factors and the common motion time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Symmetric trapezoidal profile gives total time $T = \dfrac{\Delta s}{v_p} + \dfrac{v_p}{a_p}$. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Differentiating with respect to $v_p$:

$$\dfrac{dT}{dv_p} = -\dfrac{\Delta s}{v_p^2} + \dfrac{1}{a_p} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; v_p^{*} = \sqrt{a_p\,\Delta s}$$ (3 marks)

**Step 3:** Substituting back:

$$T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta s}{a_p}}$$ (2 marks)

**Step 4:** At this optimum, cruise time $t_c = \dfrac{\Delta s}{v_p^{*}} - \dfrac{v_p^{*}}{a_p} = \sqrt{\Delta s/a_p} - \sqrt{\Delta s/a_p} = 0$. Hence the trapezoid degenerates into a triangle. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute solo minimum times.**

$$T_x^{\min} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{0.5}{1}} = 2 \times 0.7071 = 1.4142 \text{ s}$$

$$T_y^{\min} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{0.3}{0.5}} = 2 \times 0.7746 = 1.5492 \text{ s}$$

$$T_z^{\min} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{0.4}{2}} = 2 \times 0.4472 = 0.8944 \text{ s}$$

(3 marks)

**Step 2 — Synchronization clock.**

$$T_{\mathrm{sync}} = \max(1.4142,\;1.5492,\;0.8944) = 1.5492 \text{ s}$$

The y-axis is the slowest. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Scale factors.**

$$\lambda_x = \dfrac{1.4142}{1.5492} = 0.9129$$

$$\lambda_y = \dfrac{1.5492}{1.5492} = 1.0000$$

$$\lambda_z = \dfrac{0.8944}{1.5492} = 0.5774$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Final values of acceleration for x and z.**

$$a_x^{\mathrm{new}} = (0.9129)^2 \times 1 = 0.8334 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$a_z^{\mathrm{new}} = (0.5774)^2 \times 2 = 0.6667 \text{ m/s}^2$$

All axes now finish in exactly $1.5492$ s while remaining inside their original limits. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common mistake is to apply the scale factor to **time** directly (i.e., to write $T_i^{\mathrm{new}} = \lambda_i \, T_i^{\min}$). This is **wrong**. The scale factor multiplies the **inverse of time**, so the correct relation is $T_i^{\mathrm{new}} = T_i^{\min} / \lambda_i$. Losing this sign costs a full mark.

---

### Question B-B  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`  *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the concept of path-velocity bounds. Derive the expression for the maximum admissible path-velocity $\dot{s}_{\max}(s)$ given per-joint velocity limits $V_i^{\max}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of jerk-limited (S-curve) profiles in velocity tuning. List the seven phases of a symmetric S-curve and state one application where it is mandatory. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** The geometric path gives joint positions $q_i(s)$. Differentiating with respect to $s$ yields $q_i'(s) = dq_i/ds$. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Under time-scaling $s(t)$, the actual joint velocity is:

$$\dot{q}_i(t) = q_i'(s)\,\dot{s}(t)$$

**Step 3:** Enforce the per-joint limit $\vert \dot{q}_i(t) \vert \le V_i^{\max}$:

$$\vert q_i'(s) \vert \,\dot{s}(t) \le V_i^{\max} \;\Longrightarrow\; \dot{s}(t) \le \dfrac{V_i^{\max}}{\vert q_i'(s) \vert}$$

(3 marks)

**Step 4:** Combining all $n$ joints through a min-operator gives the *path-dependent velocity ceiling*:

$$\boxed{\;\dot{s}_{\max}(s) = \min_{i=1,\dots,n} \dfrac{V_i^{\max}}{\max(\epsilon,\;\vert q_i'(s)\vert)}\;}$$

The $\epsilon$ guard prevents division by zero near kinematic singularities where $q_i'(s) \to 0$. (3 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Why jerk-limited?** Real motors, gearboxes, and link structures cannot tolerate infinite acceleration jumps. A bounded jerk $j_{\max} = \dddot{s}_{\max}$ produces smooth, vibration-free motion, reduces settling time at the goal, and lengthens mechanical life. (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Seven phases of symmetric S-curve.**

1. **Jerk-up:** $j = +j_{\max}$, acceleration rises linearly from $0$ to $+a_{\max}$.
2. **Constant accel:** $j = 0$, acceleration held at $+a_{\max}$.
3. **Jerk-down:** $j = -j_{\max}$, acceleration falls linearly to $0$.
4. **Cruise:** $j = 0$, $\ddot{s} = 0$, velocity held at $v_p$.
5. **Jerk-down (decel):** $j = -j_{\max}$, acceleration becomes negative.
6. **Constant decel:** $j = 0$, acceleration held at $-a_{\max}$.
7. **Jerk-up (decel end):** $j = +j_{\max}$, acceleration returns to $0$.

(3 marks)

**Step 3 — Mandatory application.** S-curve profiles are mandatory in **collaborative robots (cobots)** that share workspace with humans, because ISO/TS 15066 specifies maximum permissible jerk and quasi-static contact forces. Industrial pick-and-place at high cycle rates (e.g., SCARA in electronics assembly) is another canonical application. (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to mention that the **seven phases collapse to fewer when the path is short**. If the path is so short that the velocity never reaches $v_p$, the cruise phase (4) disappears and one or two of the accel/decel phases also vanish. Examiners often award a follow-up mark for recognizing this degenerate case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Velocity tuning ≠ path planning.** The geometry is frozen; only the time-law is adjusted.
- **Master formula:** $\dot{q}_i(t) = q_i'(s)\,\dot{s}(t)$. Every constraint chain begins here.
- **Path-velocity ceiling** $\dot{s}_{\max}(s)$ is the bottleneck of all joints at every $s$.
- **Minimum trapezoidal time** is $T_{\min} = 2\sqrt{\Delta s / a_p}$ — always a triangle at optimum.
- **S-curve** trades extra computation for bounded jerk and vibration-free motion.
- **Synchronization rule:** $T_{\mathrm{sync}} = \max_i T_i^{\min}$, $\lambda_i = T_i^{\min}/T_{\mathrm{sync}}$.
- **Numerical guards:** always include a small $\epsilon$ in the denominator of $\dot{s}_{\max}(s)$ to handle singularities safely.
- **Order of operations:** Path → Derivatives → Path Ceilings → Profile Generator → Constraint Check → Sync → Servo.
- **Industrial relevance:** velocity tuning is what allows RRT\*-planned paths to actually run on a KUKA or FANUC controller without tripping protective stops.
- **Examination favourites:** trapezoidal derivation, synchronization numerical, comparison table of profile types, identification of the slowest (master) joint.
